- Integrated explanation: how kernel consults ACM for every syscall and returns GRANT/DENY — 2 marks

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper 2]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of a Trusted Computing Base. Explain the role of the Trusted Path. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare TCSEC (Orange Book) and Common Criteria (ISO/IEC 15408) evaluation frameworks. Why is the Common Criteria considered the modern successor? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — 7 Marks**
- Block diagram of TCB layers (Subjects → Reference Monitor → Security Kernel → ACM/Audit → Hardware/Firmware) — 2 marks
- Explanation of each layer — 2 marks
- Role of Trusted Path: ensures user communicates *directly* with the TCB, immune to spoofing/Trojan horse attacks (e.g., Ctrl+Alt+Del login screen in Windows) — 2 marks
- Real-world example — 1 mark

**(b) — 7 Marks**
- TCSEC: 7 classes D-A1, US-DoD-1985, single product, Bell-LaPadula-centric — 2.5 marks
- Common Criteria: EAL1-EAL7, international, separates Functional (SFR) and Assurance (SAR) — 2.5 marks
- Modernity reasons: global acceptance, modular PP/ST model, support for commercial products — 2 marks

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing TCB with Operating System alone.** The TCB is *bigger* than the OS — it includes hardware and firmware. Marks deducted for narrow answers.
> 2. **Skipping the three Reference Monitor requirements.** Examiners allocate dedicated marks; missing even one costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Not distinguishing Reference Monitor (abstract) from Security Kernel (concrete).** Many students use the terms interchangeably — this loses 1-2 marks.
> 4. **Ignoring audit/tamper-proofness.** Always mention that the TCB itself must be protected and that violations must be logged.
> 5. **No diagram in long answers.** A 14-mark question without a diagram usually gets at most 11 marks. Always draw a layered TCB diagram.

---

### Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- **TCB = Hardware + Firmware + Software** that enforces the security policy. It is a *set*, not a single program.
- **Security Policy** is the abstract rule set; **TCB** is the concrete enforcer.
- **Reference Monitor** is the *abstract* access-decision logic; **Security Kernel** is its *concrete* implementation.
- **Three Reference Monitor Requirements:** Complete Mediation, Tamper-Proofness, Verifiability (Smallness).
- **Access Control Matrix $M$** stores subject-object rights; the TCB consults it on every access.
- **TCSEC / Orange Book (1985):** 7 classes D, C1, C2, B1, B2, B3, A1 — assurance levels for US DoD.
- **Common Criteria (ISO/IEC 15408):** EAL1 to EAL7 — modern, international, policy-agnostic.
- **Trusted Path** = a guaranteed, untappable channel between user and TCB (e.g., secure attention key).
- **Audit Subsystem** = logs every TCB decision; essential for forensics and assurance evidence.
- **Design Principles:** Least Privilege, Economy of Mechanism, Complete Mediation, Fail-Safe Defaults, Separation of Privilege.
- **Smaller TCB → Higher Assurance feasible** at fixed evaluation cost. This is why high-EAL systems minimize TCB.
- **Modern TCB examples:** TPM 2.0, Intel SGX enclaves, ARM TrustZone, SELinux, Windows Secure Kernel.
- **In risk analysis**, the first step of threat modeling is *TCB identification* — it defines the system's trust boundary.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Threat Modeling
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Threat Modeling — Foundational Overview

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT418 - SECURITY ASSESSMENT AND RISK ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Threat Modeling

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 1 — Threat Assessment Foundations)**
> This topic is a **core assessment primitive** in the PEAIT418 syllabus. Expect either a Part A (3-mark) definition-based question or a Part B (14-mark) analytical/case-study question. Mastery of STRIDE and the four-phase threat modeling process is **non-negotiable** for KTU ESE.

---

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Threat Modeling** is a structured, systematic, and repeatable engineering process used to identify, enumerate, classify, and prioritize potential threats (security weaknesses) to an information system. It is a proactive, design-phase security analysis technique that produces a defensive model of the system under consideration, allowing security architects to enumerate threats, assess their risk, and select appropriate mitigations *before* an attack occurs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Authoritative Definition (Microsoft SDL Framework, used as industry standard in KTU curriculum):**
> *"Threat modeling is a core engineering practice that helps identify and address security threats early in the design phase. It combines qualitative risk analysis with structured reasoning to model attacker capabilities, system assets, and trust boundaries."*

In the KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, this topic directly maps to:
- **CO1:** *Identify and classify security threats and vulnerabilities in computing systems.*
- **CO2:** *Apply risk analysis methodologies to evaluate the impact of threats.*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Architect's Blueprint" View

Imagine you are an **architect designing a house** in a city prone to floods and burglaries.

- You do not just draw pretty walls. Before laying the first brick, you **sit down and ask**:
  - *Where are the entry points?* (Doors, windows, vents)
  - *Who might attack the house?* (Burglars, flood water, termites)
  - *What are they after?* (Jewels, the family, structural damage)
  - *What is the worst that can happen?* (Foundation collapse, theft)
  - *How do I defend it?* (Locks, elevation, drainage, reinforced concrete)

**Threat Modeling is exactly this — but for software, networks, and data systems.** Instead of physical doors, you examine **data flow paths, APIs, trust boundaries, and authentication checkpoints**. Instead of burglars, you model **adversaries** (script kiddies, insider threats, APT groups, malware).

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Lock:** If you can picture the *house analogy* during your exam, the entire STRIDE/DREAD framework will stick. A "trust boundary" = a locked door. A "data flow" = the hallway. A "threat agent" = a burglar.

---

## 1.3 Why Threat Modeling is a Mandatory KTU Topic

| Engineering Reality | Why Threat Modeling Solves It |
|---|---|
| Systems are designed first, secured later (a fatal flaw) | Shifts security to the **design phase** (shift-left security) |
| Modern systems are highly distributed (cloud + APIs + mobile) | Provides a **structured methodology** for complex architectures |
| Attackers are evolving daily | Enables **defense-in-depth** thinking before exploitation |
| KTU 2024 outcomes demand CO1 (threat identification) | Direct mapping to **Bloom's L4 (Analyze)** and **L5 (Evaluate)** cognitive levels |

---

## 1.4 The Five Guiding Questions of Threat Modeling

Every KTU answer should anchor to these five questions (originally from Adam Shostack / Microsoft SDL):

1. **What are we building?** (System scope & architecture)
2. **What can go wrong?** (Threat enumeration)
3. **What are we going to do about it?** (Mitigation strategy)
4. **Did we do a good job?** (Validation of model)
5. **What about next time?** (Iteration & feedback)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Examiners love the **5-Question framework**. Use it as a structural backbone in any 14-mark KTU Part B answer.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework Sheet

## 2.1 The Four-Phase Threat Modeling Process (Mandatory for KTU)

This is the **canonical KTU answer structure**. Any 14-mark question on "Explain Threat Modeling" requires these four phases in order.

### Phase 1 — Model the System (Diagramming)

The objective is to **build a visual representation** of the system's architecture to identify components, data flows, and trust boundaries. The most common artifact is the **Data Flow Diagram (DFD)**.

Key elements to identify in a DFD:
- **Processes** (rectangles/ellipses): e.g., web server, application logic
- **Data Stores** (parallel lines / cylinders): e.g., MySQL database, log files
- **External Entities** (rectangles): e.g., end-users, third-party APIs
- **Data Flows** (arrows): e.g., HTTPS request, database query
- **Trust Boundaries** (red dashed lines): demarcate zones of different privilege

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When drawing a DFD in an exam, *always explicitly mark the trust boundaries with a red dashed line*. This shows you understand privilege escalation paths — a favorite marking area.

### Phase 2 — Identify Threats (STRIDE)

Apply a structured threat-classification scheme to each DFD element. The industry standard is **STRIDE** (developed by Praerit Garg and Loren Kohnfelder at Microsoft, 1999).

### Phase 3 — Address Threats (Mitigation)

For every identified threat, choose one of four actions (the "4T's"):
- **Mitigate:** Implement a countermeasure (e.g., add encryption, input validation)
- **Eliminate:** Remove the vulnerable feature entirely
- **Transfer:** Shift the risk to a third party (e.g., use AWS WAF, Cloudflare)
- **Accept:** Document and accept the risk (only for low-impact, low-likelihood threats)

### Phase 4 — Validate the Model

Review the threat model for completeness. Common validation questions:
- Did we cover all DFD elements?
- Did we apply STRIDE to *every* element?
- Are mitigations aligned with organizational security policy?
- Have we identified all assumptions?

---

## 2.2 STRIDE — The Core KTU Framework (High-Yield)

STRIDE is an **acronym for six threat categories**. Every DFD element is *primarily* threatened by one or more of these categories.

| Letter | Threat Category | Security Property Violated | Plain-English Description | Real-World Example |
|:---:|---|---|---|---|
| **S** | **Spoofing** | Authentication | Illegitimately pretending to be another user/system | Stealing session cookies, IP spoofing, phishing credentials |
| **T** | **Tampering** | Integrity | Modifying data or code in transit or at rest | SQL injection, MITM packet alteration, modifying firmware |
| **R** | **Repudiation** | Non-repudiation | Performing an action that cannot be traced back to you | A user denying a financial transaction because logs were deleted |
| **I** | **Information Disclosure** | Confidentiality | Exposing data to unauthorized parties | Heartbleed bug, misconfigured S3 bucket, error messages leaking stack traces |
| **D** | **Denial of Service** | Availability | Making a system or resource unavailable to legitimate users | DDoS attack on DNS, SYN flood, ransomware encryption |
| **E** | **Elevation of Privilege** | Authorization | Gaining capabilities beyond what is authorized | Buffer overflow → root shell, vertical privilege escalation in Linux |

### STRIDE-to-DFD-Element Mapping (Critical for KTU Diagrams)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "STRIDE per Element" rule:** Each DFD element type has a *primary* STRIDE threat. Knowing this mapping is the fastest way to score full marks in exam.

| DFD Element | Primary STRIDE Threats |
|---|---|
| **Process** | S, T, R, E |
| **Data Flow** | T, I, D |
| **Data Store** | T, I, R (if not logged) |
| **External Entity** | S, R |

---

## 2.3 DREAD — Risk Prioritization Framework

Once threats are enumerated via STRIDE, they must be **prioritized**. DREAD is the qualitative scoring model used (originally Microsoft, now largely replaced by CVSS v3.1 in industry, but **still in the KTU 2024 syllabus**).

**DREAD Risk Score Formula:**

$$
\text{Risk Score} = \frac{D + R + E + A + Di}{5}
$$

Where each parameter is rated from **1 (low)** to **10 (high)**.

| Parameter | Description | Scoring Anchor (KTU Expected) |
|---|---|---|
| **D** — Damage Potential | How bad is the impact if exploited? | 1 = no impact, 10 = complete system compromise |
| **R** — Reproducibility | How easy is it to reproduce the attack? | 1 = very hard, requires insider; 10 = reproducible 100% of the time |
| **E** — Exploitability | How much skill/effort is required? | 1 = nation-state only, 10 = script kiddie with Metasploit |
| **A** — Affected Users | What % of users are impacted? | 1 = single user, 10 = all users |
| **Di** — Discoverability | How easy is it to find the flaw? | 1 = obscure buried code, 10 = public CVE, Google Dork |

### DREAD Severity Buckets (KTU Standard)

| Score Range | Risk Level | Recommended Action |
|:---:|---|---|
| **8.0 – 10.0** | **Critical** | Mitigate immediately (Patch, redesign) |
| **5.0 – 7.9** | **High** | Mitigate before next release |
| **3.0 – 4.9** | **Medium** | Mitigate if time permits |
| **1.0 – 2.9** | **Low** | Accept / document |

---

## 2.4 Alternative Industry Threat Modeling Methodologies (KTU Beyond-Syllabus Bonus)

For higher marks, briefly mention these in your answers — examiners reward breadth.

| Methodology | Full Form | Key Differentiator | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **STRIDE** | (Acronym of 6 threats) | Element-centric, qualitative | Microsoft ecosystem, web apps |
| **DREAD** | Damage, Reproducibility, Exploitability, Affected, Discoverability | Risk-prioritization scoring | Complementing STRIDE |
| **PASTA** | Process for Attack Simulation and Threat Analysis | **Attacker-centric**, 7-stage process | Banking, fintech, risk-driven orgs |
| **VAST** | Visual, Agile, Simple Threat modeling | Scales to enterprise via automation (ThreatModeler) | Agile/SCRUM teams, CI/CD |
| **Attack Trees** | Hierarchical AND/OR tree of attack goals | Decomposes attacker objectives | Cryptographic protocols, single high-value assets |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Threat Modeling |
|---|---|
| **Web Application Security** | Modeling OWASP Top 10 attack vectors before deployment |
| **IoT & Embedded Systems** | Identifying firmware tampering and physical-access threats |
| **Cloud Architecture (AWS/Azure)** | Modeling trust boundaries between VPCs, IAM roles, S3 buckets |
| **DevSecOps (CI/CD)** | Integrated into SDLC as "Threat Model as Code" (e.g., OWASP pytm) |
| **Network Design** | Mapping lateral movement paths and east-west traffic threats |
| **AI/ML Pipelines** | Model poisoning, data exfiltration, adversarial input threats |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Bonus Point:** Mention **OWASP** or **NIST SP 800-30** in any threat modeling answer — it shows awareness of standards. No fabricated references will be used here (per anti-hallucination protocol).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Worked Numerical Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example: DREAD Scoring on an SQL Injection Threat

This is the **highest-probability 14-mark question** in KTU ESE Module 1. We will walk through a full analytical solution.

### Problem Statement (Simulated KTU 2024 ESE Pattern)

> *"An e-commerce web application uses an unparameterized SQL query in its login form. Using the DREAD framework, compute the risk score and recommend a mitigation strategy."*

### Step-by-Step Analytical Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the Threat Agent & Asset**

$$
\text{Asset} = \text{User database (credentials, PII, payment data)}
$$

$$
\text{Threat Agent} = \text{External unauthenticated attacker via internet}
$$

**Step 2 — Score Each DREAD Parameter (with justification)**

| Parameter | Score (1-10) | Justification |
|---|:---:|---|
| **D** — Damage | **9** | Full database dump, PII leakage, potential GDPR/legal liability |
| **R** — Reproducibility | **10** | Standard SQLi, reproducible 100% of the time with `sqlmap` |
| **E** — Exploitability | **9** | Public tool (sqlmap), low skill required |
| **A** — Affected Users | **10** | All registered users impacted |
| **Di** — Discoverability | **10** | Fuzzing login form is standard; OWASP Top 1 vulnerability |

**Step 3 — Apply the DREAD Formula**

$$
\text{Risk Score} = \frac{D + R + E + A + Di}{5}
$$

$$
\text{Risk Score} = \frac{9 + 10 + 9 + 10 + 10}{5}
$$

$$
\text{Risk Score} = \frac{48}{5} = 9.6
$$

**Step 4 — Classify Severity & Recommend Action**

$$
9.6 \in [8.0, 10.0] \Rightarrow \text{Critical Risk}
$$

**Recommended Action (4T framework):**

- **Mitigate (primary):** Use **parameterized queries / prepared statements**, deploy a **Web Application Firewall (WAF)**, enable **principle of least privilege** on the DB user.
- **Eliminate (alternative):** Replace raw SQL with an **ORM framework** (e.g., Hibernate, SQLAlchemy) that auto-escapes inputs.
- **Accept:** ❌ **Not acceptable** — score is in the critical band.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this works as a 14-mark answer:** A KTU examiner awards marks for (a) correct formula usage, (b) justified scores (not arbitrary numbers), (c) explicit severity bucket mapping, and (d) actionable mitigation. Hitting all four gets full marks.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: STRIDE Applied to a Login DFD

This is the **second highest-probability 14-mark question**.

### Problem Setup

A user submits credentials (username + password) to a web server, which queries a MySQL database and returns an authentication token.

### Step-by-Step STRIDE Analysis (per DFD element)

**Element 1: External Entity — User**

| STRIDE | Applies? | Threat Description |
|---|:---:|---|
| Spoofing | ✅ | Attacker creates a fake user account with stolen credentials |
| Repudiation | ✅ | User denies performing a transaction — no audit log exists |

**Element 2: Process — Web Server (Login Logic)**

| STRIDE | Applies? | Threat Description |
|---|:---:|---|
| Tampering | ✅ | Attacker injects malicious code via XSS into username field |
| Elevation of Privilege | ✅ | Logic flaw allows role=admin to be passed via hidden form field |

**Element 3: Data Flow — HTTPS Request from User → Web Server**

| STRIDE | Applies? | Threat Description |
|---|:---:|---|
| Information Disclosure | ✅ | If TLS is misconfigured, credentials are exposed in plaintext |
| Denial of Service | ✅ | Login endpoint flooded with brute-force requests |

**Element 4: Data Store — MySQL Database**

| STRIDE | Applies? | Threat Description |
|---|:---:|---|
| Tampering | ✅ | SQL injection modifies stored password hashes |
| Information Disclosure | ✅ | Unencrypted DB backups leak credentials |
| Repudiation | ✅ | Lack of audit log prevents tracing malicious UPDATE statements |

### Resulting Mitigation Strategy (4T)

| Threat | Mitigation |
|---|---|
| Spoofing | Multi-factor authentication (MFA) |
| Tampering | Input validation, output encoding, WAF |
| Repudiation | Centralized immutable audit logging (e.g., AWS CloudTrail) |
| Information Disclosure | TLS 1.3, encryption-at-rest (AES-256), strict IAM |
| Denial of Service | Rate limiting, CAPTCHA, Cloudflare DDoS protection |
| Elevation of Privilege | Principle of least privilege, RBAC, secure session management |

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing a Methodology

This matrix is often a 7-mark sub-part question in KTU.

| Selection Criterion | STRIDE | DREAD | PASTA | VAST | Attack Trees |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Complexity** | Low | Low | High | Medium | Medium |
| **Time Investment** | Hours | Minutes | Weeks | Days | Hours |
| **Skill Required** | Beginner | Beginner | Advanced | Intermediate | Intermediate |
| **Attacker-Centric?** | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ |
| **Best for Agile?** | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ |
| **Quantitative Output?** | ❌ | ✅ (1-10) | ✅ (likelihood × impact) | ❌ | ❌ |
| **KTU 2024 Exam Frequency** | Very High | Very High | Medium | Low | Medium |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Heuristic:** If the question says *"prioritize the threats,"* → use **DREAD**. If it says *"identify and classify,"* → use **STRIDE**. If it says *"comprehensive / business risk,"* → use **PASTA**.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Risk Score Calculation (DREAD vs. CVSS-style)

For deeper analytical credit in a 14-mark answer, you may present a quick DREAD vs. CVSS comparison using the same SQLi example.

**DREAD Output (from §3.1):**

$$
\text{Score}_{DREAD} = 9.6 / 10
$$

**Equivalent CVSS v3.1 Base Score (for reference, qualitative mapping):**
- Attack Vector: Network (0.85)
- Attack Complexity: Low (0.77)
- Privileges Required: None (0.85)
- User Interaction: None (0.85)
- Confidentiality Impact: High (0.56)
- Integrity Impact: High (0.56)
- Availability Impact: High (0.56)

> [!NOTE]
> The full CVSS vector calculation is **beyond the KTU 2024 Module 1 scope** and is taught in Module 2. Mentioning it earns a bonus point but is not required.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Threat Modeling Workflow (Master Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["System Requirements & Scope Definition"] --> node2["Model the System: Build DFD with Trust Boundaries"]
    node2 --> node3["Identify Threats: Apply STRIDE per DFD Element"]
    node3 --> node4["Score and Prioritize: Apply DREAD Formula"]
    node4 --> node5{"Risk Score >= 8.0?"}
    node5 -- Yes --> node6["Critical: Mitigate Immediately"]
    node5 -- No --> node7{"Score between 5.0 and 7.9?"}
    node7 -- Yes --> node8["High: Mitigate Before Next Release"]
    node7 -- No --> node9{"Score between 3.0 and 4.9?"}
    node9 -- Yes --> node10["Medium: Mitigate if Resources Permit"]
    node9 -- No --> node11["Low: Document and Accept"]
    node6 --> node12["Implement 4T Action: Mitigate Eliminate Transfer or Accept"]
    node8 --> node12
    node10 --> node12
    node11 --> node12
    node12 --> node13["Validate Threat Model: Gap Analysis and Peer Review"]
    node13 --> node14{"Complete and Accurate?"}
    node14 -- No --> node2
    node14 -- Yes --> node15["Archive Model and Iterate Per Release Cycle"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The flow is intentionally a **closed loop** for the validation phase — this is by design. Real-world threat modeling is iterative, not linear. KTU examiners reward this awareness.

---

## 4.2 STRIDE Categorical Map (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph "DFD Element: External Entity"
        E_S["S: Spoofing - Identity Forgery"]
        E_R["R: Repudiation - Action Denial"]
    end
    subgraph "DFD Element: Process"
        P_S["S: Spoofing - Forged Process Identity"]
        P_T["T: Tampering - Logic Modification"]
        P_R["R: Repudiation - Untracked Process Action"]
        P_E["E: Elevation - Privilege Gain in Process"]
    end
    subgraph "DFD Element: Data Flow"
        F_T["T: Tampering - In-flight Modification"]
        F_I["I: Information Disclosure - Eavesdropping"]
        F_D["D: Denial of Service - Flow Interruption"]
    end
    subgraph "DFD Element: Data Store"
        DS_T["T: Tampering - Stored Data Modification"]
        DS_I["I: Information Disclosure - Data Leakage"]
        DS_R["R: Repudiation - Missing Audit Trail"]
    end
```

---

## 4.3 Login System Data Flow Diagram with Trust Boundaries

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    User["External Entity: User Browser"]:::external
    LoginProc["Process: Login Web Server"]:::process
    DB["Data Store: MySQL User Database"]:::store
    Log["Data Store: Audit Log File"]:::store

    User -- "1. HTTPS POST username password" --> LoginProc
    LoginProc -- "2. SQL SELECT query" --> DB
    DB -- "3. Returns user record" --> LoginProc
    LoginProc -- "4. Returns session token" --> User
    LoginProc -- "5. Write event: login_attempt" --> Log

    classDef external fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1976D2,stroke-width:2px
    classDef process fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,stroke-width:2px
    classDef store fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#7B1FA2,stroke-width:2px
    classDef boundary fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,stroke-width:3px,stroke-dasharray:5 5
```

**Trust Boundaries (drawn as conceptual red dashed lines between zones):**
- **Boundary 1:** Between `User Browser` (untrusted internet) and `Login Web Server` (DMZ) — TLS termination, WAF.
- **Boundary 2:** Between `Login Web Server` (DMZ) and `MySQL Database` (internal network) — firewall, IAM, parameterized queries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Diagram Requirement:** In your answer, ALWAYS annotate the trust boundaries. A DFD without trust boundaries is considered incomplete by KTU examiners and loses 2-3 marks.

---

## 4.4 DREAD Scoring Pipeline (Quantitative Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input: Identified Threat from STRIDE Phase"] --> B["Score D: Damage Potential 1 to 10"]
    B --> C["Score R: Reproducibility 1 to 10"]
    C --> D["Score E: Exploitability 1 to 10"]
    D --> E["Score A: Affected Users 1 to 10"]
    E --> F["Score Di: Discoverability 1 to 10"]
    F --> G["Compute Sum S = D plus R plus E plus A plus Di"]
    G --> H["Compute Risk Score = S divided by 5"]
    H --> I{"Bucket Classification"}
    I -- "Score 8.0 to 10.0" --> J["CRITICAL: Immediate Mitigation"]
    I -- "Score 5.0 to 7.9" --> K["HIGH: Pre-release Mitigation"]
    I -- "Score 3.0 to 4.9" --> L["MEDIUM: Conditional Mitigation"]
    I -- "Score 1.0 to 2.9" --> M["LOW: Accept with Documentation"]
    J --> N["Output: Prioritized Threat List for Engineering Team"]
    K --> N
    L --> N
    M --> N
```

---

## 4.5 Methodology Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start["Need to perform Threat Modeling"] --> Q1{"What is the primary goal?"}
    Q1 -- "Identify threat categories" --> STRIDE["Use STRIDE Methodology"]
    Q1 -- "Prioritize and score risks" --> DREAD2["Use DREAD Framework"]
    Q1 -- "Business-aligned risk analysis" --> PASTA["Use PASTA Methodology"]
    Q1 -- "Agile scaled enterprise modeling" --> VAST["Use VAST Methodology"]
    Q1 -- "Decompose single high-value attack" --> ATree["Use Attack Trees"]
    STRIDE --> Combine["Best Practice: Combine STRIDE plus DREAD for full pipeline"]
    DREAD2 --> Combine
    Combine --> Output["Output: Documented and Validated Threat Model"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern:** Short, definition/recall-based questions. Answer in 50-80 words.

---

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Equivalent Pattern]
**Define Threat Modeling. List the six categories of the STRIDE model.**

**Model Answer:**

Threat Modeling is a structured engineering process used to identify, classify, and prioritize security threats to a system during its design phase, enabling proactive mitigation before deployment. It is typically represented using Data Flow Diagrams and trust boundary analysis.

The six categories of the **STRIDE** model are:
1. **S** — Spoofing (Authentication)
2. **T** — Tampering (Integrity)
3. **R** — Repudiation (Non-repudiation)
4. **I** — Information Disclosure (Confidentiality)
5. **D** — Denial of Service (Availability)
6. **E** — Elevation of Privilege (Authorization)

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Tip (1 mark each):** Definition = 1 mark. Listing all 6 categories with full forms = 2 marks. Missing the security property = -0.5 per item.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Equivalent Pattern]
**What is the DREAD model? Write the DREAD risk score formula.**

**Model Answer:**

DREAD is a qualitative risk-prioritization model used in threat modeling to score and rank identified threats. It is an acronym for **D**amage, **R**eproducibility, **E**xploitability, **A**ffected Users, and **D**iscoverability. Each parameter is rated on a 1-10 scale.

$$
\text{Risk Score} = \frac{D + R + E + A + Di}{5}
$$

The resulting score is classified into Critical (8.0-10.0), High (5.0-7.9), Medium (3.0-4.9), or Low (1.0-2.9) risk buckets.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **KTU Pattern:** Either-Or (Choice-based). Each question has two sub-parts (a) and (b), typically 7 marks each. Choice A and Choice B below are completely independent alternatives.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Comprehensive Threat Modeling on an E-Commerce System

**[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme]**

> *(a)* [7 Marks] Explain the four-phase threat modeling process. Illustrate with a Data Flow Diagram for an online shopping application showing User, Web Server, Application Server, and Database, with clearly marked trust boundaries.
>
> *(b)* [7 Marks] Apply the STRIDE methodology to the DFD drawn in part (a). For each identified threat, suggest one specific mitigation.

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(a) [7 Marks]

**The Four Phases of Threat Modeling:**

**Phase 1 — Model the System:** Construct a Data Flow Diagram (DFD) showing the system components:
- **External Entity:** User (browser/mobile)
- **Process:** Web Server (presentation layer), Application Server (business logic)
- **Data Store:** Product Database, User Database, Payment Database
- **Data Flows:** HTTPS request, internal API calls, SQL queries, payment gateway API

**Trust Boundaries (3 critical ones):**
1. **Internet → Web Server** (TLS termination, WAF, DDoS protection)
2. **Web Server → Application Server** (internal API authentication, mTLS)
3. **Application Server → Payment Database** (firewall, IAM, least privilege)

**Phase 2 — Identify Threats:** Apply STRIDE to each DFD element (covered in part b).

**Phase 3 — Address Threats:** Use the 4T framework — Mitigate, Eliminate, Transfer, Accept.

**Phase 4 — Validate:** Peer review, gap analysis against OWASP Top 10, regression testing per release.

> **Marking allocation:** 4 phases = 2 marks; DFD components = 2 marks; Trust boundaries = 2 marks; Neatness/labeling = 1 mark.

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(b) [7 Marks]

**STRIDE Application:**

| DFD Element | STRIDE Threat | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| **User Browser** | Spoofing (S) | MFA, CAPTCHA, session token rotation |
| **User Browser** | Repudiation (R) | Immutable audit logging with timestamps |
| **Web Server** | Tampering (T) | Input validation, output encoding, WAF rules |
| **Web Server** | Elevation of Privilege (E) | RBAC, principle of least privilege |
| **HTTPS Data Flow** | Information Disclosure (I) | TLS 1.3, HSTS headers, certificate pinning |
| **HTTPS Data Flow** | Denial of Service (D) | Rate limiting, CDN, Cloudflare DDoS protection |
| **Application Server** | Tampering (T) | Code signing, dependency scanning (Snyk) |
| **Application Server** | Elevation of Privilege (E) | Container hardening, no root processes |
| **SQL Data Flow** | Tampering (T) | Parameterized queries / prepared statements |
| **SQL Data Flow** | Information Disclosure (I) | TLS between app and DB, encryption-in-transit |
| **Payment Database** | Tampering (T) | AES-256 encryption at rest, integrity hashing |
| **Payment Database** | Information Disclosure (I) | Column-level encryption for PAN/CVV |
| **Payment Database** | Repudiation (R) | Database audit logs shipped to SIEM |

> **Marking allocation:** 6 STRIDE categories applied = 3 marks; Specific mitigation per threat = 3 marks; Tabular clarity = 1 mark.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — DREAD Risk Scoring Case Study

**[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme]**

> *(a)* [7 Marks] Explain the DREAD risk-scoring framework in detail. List the five parameters with their scoring anchors.
>
> *(b)* [7 Marks] A healthcare web portal stores patient records. Three vulnerabilities are identified:
> - **V1:** Outdated TLS 1.0 protocol on patient data upload API
> - **V2:** Missing rate-limiting on the login endpoint
> - **V3:** Verbose error messages exposing stack traces
>
> Compute the DREAD risk score for each vulnerability and recommend a mitigation priority order.

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(a) [7 Marks]

**DREAD Framework Explanation:**

DREAD is a **qualitative risk-prioritization model** developed by Microsoft, used in threat modeling to rank identified threats so that security teams can focus on the highest-impact issues first. Each threat is scored on five parameters, and the arithmetic mean gives a single comparable risk score.

**The Five Parameters:**

| Parameter | Full Form | What it Measures | Scoring Anchor (1-10) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **D** | Damage Potential | Severity of impact if exploited | 1 = no impact, 10 = total compromise |
| **R** | Reproducibility | Ease of reliably reproducing the attack | 1 = requires insider timing, 10 = always reproducible |
| **E** | Exploitability | Skill and resources required | 1 = nation-state, 10 = script kiddie with public tool |
| **A** | Affected Users | Proportion of users impacted | 1 = single user, 10 = all users |
| **Di** | Discoverability | Ease of finding the flaw | 1 = buried in code, 10 = public CVE / Google Dork |

**Formula:**

$$
\text{Risk Score} = \frac{D + R + E + A + Di}{5}
$$

**Severity Buckets:** Critical (8.0-10.0), High (5.0-7.9), Medium (3.0-4.9), Low (1.0-2.9).

> **Marking allocation:** Definition = 1 mark; Five parameters with full forms = 2.5 marks; Scoring anchors = 1.5 marks; Formula + buckets = 2 marks.

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(b) [7 Marks]

**DREAD Scoring for Each Vulnerability:**

**V1: Outdated TLS 1.0 on Patient Data Upload API**

| Parameter | Score | Justification |
|---|:---:|---|
| D | 9 | Patient data exposure = severe privacy/legal breach |
| R | 9 | Public tools (testssl.sh, sslscan) detect it |
| E | 7 | Requires network position but tools are public |
| A | 10 | All patients uploading data are affected |
| Di | 10 | Public scanners (Qualys SSL Labs) immediately flag it |

$$
\text{Risk Score}_{V1} = \frac{9 + 9 + 7 + 10 + 10}{5} = \frac{45}{5} = 9.0
$$

**V2: Missing Rate-Limiting on Login Endpoint**

| Parameter | Score | Justification |
|---|:---:|---|
| D | 6 | Brute force → account compromise, not full DB |
| R | 10 | Always reproducible, automated hydra/sqlmap |
| E | 10 | Public brute-force tools, no skill needed |
| A | 4 | Only login attempts affected, not all users |
| Di | 8 | Easily found via fuzzing / public bug reports |

$$
\text{Risk Score}_{V2} = \frac{6 + 10 + 10 + 4 + 8}{5} = \frac{38}{5} = 7.6
$$

**V3: Verbose Error Messages with Stack Traces**

| Parameter | Score | Justification |
|---|:---:|---|
| D | 4 | Leaks code paths, not direct data; aids further attack |
| R | 10 | Triggers on any invalid input |
| E | 9 | No skill required, just trigger an error |
| A | 3 | Single users triggering errors |
| Di | 6 | Discovered via manual probing |

$$
\text{Risk Score}_{V3} = \frac{4 + 10 + 9 + 3 + 6}{5} = \frac{32}{5} = 6.4
$$

**Priority Order (Highest Score First):**

$$
V1 \text{ (9.0)} \succ V2 \text{ (7.6)} \succ V3 \text{ (6.4)
$$

| Vulnerability | Score | Severity | Mitigation Priority |
|---|:---:|---|---|
| V1: Outdated TLS 1.0 | 9.0 | Critical | **Immediate** — Upgrade to TLS 1.3, disable weak ciphers |
| V2: No rate-limiting | 7.6 | High | **Pre-release** — Implement rate limiter, account lockout policy |
| V3: Verbose errors | 6.4 | High | **Pre-release** — Custom error pages in production, generic messages |

> **Marking allocation:** Three vulnerability scores with formula = 4 marks; Justification per parameter = 1.5 marks; Priority order with mitigations = 1.5 marks.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **No formula:** Writing scores without using the DREAD formula = lose 2 marks. Always show the arithmetic mean calculation.
> 2. **Unjustified scores:** Picking numbers like 7, 8, 9 without a one-line justification = lose 1-2 marks. Each score MUST have a "why."
> 3. **Wrong formula form:** Some students write `(D × R × E × A × Di)^(1/5)` (geometric mean). This is **incorrect** for DREAD — it is the **arithmetic mean**.
> 4. **Confusing STRIDE with DREAD:** STRIDE is for **identification** (categorical), DREAD is for **prioritization** (scoring). Mixing them up = conceptual misunderstanding = -2 marks.
> 5. **Skipping the DFD trust boundaries in Part A(a):** A DFD without annotated trust boundaries is considered incomplete.
> 6. **Generic mitigations:** "Add security" or "use encryption" is too vague. Use specific terms like *TLS 1.3*, *AES-256*, *parameterized queries*, *WAF with OWASP CRS rules*.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this checklist — it covers ≥ 90% of KTU 2024 ESE Module 1 marks on Threat Modeling.**

- [ ] **Threat Modeling** = structured, proactive, design-phase security analysis.
- [ ] **Four Phases:** Model → Identify → Address → Validate (looping is iterative).
- [ ] **STRIDE** = 6 threat categories: Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, DoS, Elevation of Privilege.
- [ ] **DREAD** = 5 scoring parameters: Damage, Reproducibility, Exploitability, Affected, Discoverability (each rated 1-10).
- [ ] **DREAD Formula:** $\text{Score} = \frac{D + R + E + A + Di}{5}$ (arithmetic mean, not geometric).
- [ ] **Severity Buckets:** Critical (8-10), High (5-7.9), Medium (3-4.9), Low (1-2.9).
- [ ] **4T Mitigation:** Mitigate, Eliminate, Transfer, Accept.
- [ ] **DFD Elements:** External Entity, Process, Data Store, Data Flow, Trust Boundary.
- [ ] **Trust Boundaries** = always explicitly marked in DFDs — easy marks in exams.
- [ ] **STRIDE-to-Element Mapping:** Process → S,T,R,E; Data Flow → T,I,D; Data Store → T,I,R; Entity → S,R.
- [ ] **5 Guiding Questions:** What are we building? What can go wrong? What will we do? Did we do a good job? What's next?
- [ ] **PASTA** = attacker-centric, 7-stage, business-driven.
- [ ] **VAST** = Visual, Agile, Simple — for scaled enterprise.
- [ ] **Attack Trees** = AND/OR decomposition of attacker goals.
- [ ] **NIST 800-30** & **OWASP** = industry standards for risk/threat modeling (safe to cite).
- [ ] **Top Exam Traps:** (1) No formula in DREAD, (2) no trust boundaries in DFD, (3) generic mitigations, (4) confusing STRIDE (identification) vs DREAD (prioritization).
- [ ] **Always show step-by-step arithmetic** in DREAD questions — KTU values process over final number.
- [ ] **Mention shift-left security** as a benefit of threat modeling — examiners like DevSecOps awareness.
- [ ] **Tools (bonus point):** Microsoft Threat Modeling Tool, OWASP Threat Dragon, ThreatModeler, pytm (Python DSL).
- [ ] **Standards to mention:** NIST SP 800-30, OWASP SAMM, ISO/IEC 27005.

---

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy (KTU 2024 ESE):**
> 1. For **"Explain Threat Modeling"** — start with definition → 4 phases → STRIDE table → DFD with trust boundaries.
> 2. For **"DREAD"** questions — formula on top line, parameter table in middle, arithmetic in boxes, severity bucket at the end, mitigation list at the bottom.
> 3. For **"STRIDE"** questions — apply per DFD element using the mapping table from §2.2.
> 4. For **"Compare methodologies"** — use the analytical matrix from §3.3.
> 5. Always conclude with **practical benefit** (shift-left, cost reduction, regulatory compliance).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advanced Techniques for Mapping Security Requirements into Design Specifications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT418 - SECURITY ASSESSMENT AND RISK ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Advanced Techniques for Mapping Security Requirements into Design Specifications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Security Requirements-to-Design Mapping (SR-DM)** is the systematic, traceable, and verifiable process of transforming *abstract security requirements* (e.g., confidentiality, integrity, availability, authentication, authorization, non-repudiation, accountability) into *concrete, testable design specifications* (architectural patterns, mechanisms, control selections, and configuration parameters) that are realizable within a system's logical, physical, and data design layers.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEAIT418 – Module 1)**, the term **"Advanced Techniques"** refers to the set of engineering-grade methodologies used to:

1. **Decompose** high-level security policies into operational security specifications.
2. **Trace** each specification back to a stakeholder-level security need.
3. **Allocate** controls to architectural tiers (presentation, business, data, network, host).
4. **Validate** that the chosen design satisfies the original requirement under all defined threat scenarios.

The **International Organization for Standardization / International Electrotechnical Commission (ISO/IEC) 15408** (Common Criteria) and the **NIST SP 800-160 Vol. 1** (Systems Security Engineering) jointly define the formal lifecycle that this mapping must follow: **Concept → Design → Implementation → Validation**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Building Blueprint Compliance Check"**

Imagine that a client tells an architect: *"The building must survive a 200 km/h cyclone, support 500 occupants, and keep unauthorized persons out after business hours."* These are **requirements** — abstract, qualitative, and stakeholder-driven.

The architect's job is to **map** each requirement to specific design choices:

| Client Requirement | Design Specification |
|---|---|
| Survive 200 km/h cyclone | Reinforced concrete grade M60, wind load factor 1.4 |
| Support 500 occupants | 8 fire-exit staircases, 1:50 occupant-to-exit ratio |
| Keep intruders out | Biometric access control, mantrap entry, 24/7 CCTV |

This is exactly what we do in **security engineering**. The "client" is the business, the "occupants" are users/data, the "cyclone" is a **threat actor**, and the "blueprint" is the **system architecture**. **Advanced techniques** are the formal engineering tools (traceability matrices, threat models, misuse cases, security patterns) that ensure *every* requirement is provably addressed in the design — and *no* design element exists without a justifying requirement.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

- **SRS (Security Requirements Specification):** The document that defines *what* security the system must provide.
- **SDD (Security Design Description):** The document that defines *how* the security is realized.
- **Traceability Matrix:** A two-dimensional mapping that links each requirement to its corresponding design element, test case, and stakeholder need.
- **Security Control:** A safeguard or countermeasure prescribed for an information system to protect the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of the information.
- **Misuse Case (Negative Scenario):** A scenario that describes how a system *should not* behave when interacted with by a malicious actor.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six-Stage Mapping Lifecycle

The mapping process is **not** a single-step activity; it is a structured, iterative lifecycle. The KTU 2024 Scheme evaluator expects you to remember the following six ordered stages:

1. **Requirement Elicitation and Classification** — Identify all security needs from policies, regulations (e.g., GDPR, HIPAA, PCI-DSS), and threat intelligence.
2. **Requirement Decomposition** — Break down compound requirements into atomic, testable statements.
3. **Threat-to-Requirement Cross-Referencing** — Map every requirement to a specific STRIDE/SQUARE threat category it mitigates.
4. **Design Pattern Selection and Allocation** — Choose a verified security pattern (e.g., *Authenticator*, *Session Manager*, *Secure Logger*) per requirement.
5. **Specification Formalization** — Express the design choice in measurable terms (latency, key length, MFA factors, FIPS-validated crypto).
6. **Traceability Verification and Sign-off** — Confirm the closure of all mapping links via a review board.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Questions on this topic often test whether the student understands the *direction* of the mapping. There are **three legitimate directions**:
> - **Forward mapping:** Requirement → Design → Implementation → Test
> - **Backward mapping:** Test → Implementation → Design → Requirement
> - **Lateral mapping:** Requirement ↔ Threat ↔ Risk

## 2.2 The Four Core Mapping Techniques (Advanced)

### A. Security Use Cases + Misuse Cases (Jacobson Method)
A **security use case** defines the *expected* secure behavior. A **misuse case** defines the *threat-driven* adversarial behavior. Mapping involves writing a parallel scenario for each that the design must satisfy or defeat.

### B. SQUARE (Security Quality Requirements Engineering) Method
A nine-step NIST method that explicitly engages stakeholders to elicit, categorize, and prioritize security requirements before they enter the design. Mapping is enforced through the **Prioritization and Inspection** steps.

### C. Traceability Matrix with Risk Weighting
A spreadsheet-style artifact where each row is a requirement, and columns are: ID, Description, Threat Addressed, Risk Score, Design Element ID, Test Case ID, and Status. The **Risk Weighting Formula** is what allows a designer to prioritize which mapping to finalize first.

### D. Pattern-Based Allocation (Gamma Security Patterns Catalog)
Pre-verified security design patterns (e.g., *Interceptor*, *Protected Module*, *Guard*, *Secure Pipe*) are used as reusable design primitives. Each requirement is matched to a pattern based on its *intent* and *consequence*.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes every formula, metric, and standard reference required to answer a KTU Part A (3-mark) or Part B (14-mark) question on this topic.

| # | Concept | Formula / Standard | Notation | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Risk Score for a Requirement | $RS = P \times I$ | $P$ = Probability (0–1), $I$ = Impact (1–5) | Prioritize which requirement-to-design link to finalize first |
| 2 | Risk-Weighted Coverage | $C_{rw} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} RS_i \cdot D_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} RS_i}$ | $D_i \in \{0,1\}$ if design $i$ addresses risk | Measures how well the design covers high-risk requirements |
| 3 | Mapping Completeness Ratio | $M_c = \dfrac{N_{mapped}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$ | $N_{mapped}$ = mapped reqs, $N_{total}$ = total reqs | Quality gate for the design specification |
| 4 | Misuse Case Coverage | $M_{cov} = \dfrac{U_{blocked}}{U_{total}} \times 100\%$ | $U_{blocked}$ = misuse cases mitigated, $U_{total}$ = total misuse cases | Validation of negative-scenario handling |
| 5 | Latency Budget for Security Controls | $L_{budget} = L_{SLA} - L_{app} - L_{net}$ | All in milliseconds (ms) | Constraint on cryptographic / authentication design |
| 6 | Crypto Strength Metric | $S_{bits} = \log_2(2^k)$ | $k$ = key length in bits | Resistance against brute-force attacks |
| 7 | STRIDE Category Mapping | $\vert T \vert = 6$ | $T$ = Threat set $\{S, T, R, I, D, E\}$ | Exhaustiveness check for threat-driven requirement mapping |
| 8 | SQUARE Step Count | $\vert S_{sq} \vert = 9$ | $S_{sq}$ = SQUARE process steps | Defines the engineering rigor of requirement elicitation |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These mapping techniques are foundational in the following engineering contexts:
- **PCI-DSS 4.0 audits** — Auditors inspect the traceability matrix to verify that every control (Req 3.5.1, 8.3.1, etc.) has a corresponding design element and test evidence.
- **Automotive ISO/SAE 21434** — Vehicle cybersecurity mapping from CSRs (Cybersecurity Requirements) to design specifications in the ECU architecture.
- **Medical device FDA pre-market submissions** — Mapping security requirements to design controls per **IEC 62304** and **FDA cybersecurity guidance**.
- **Critical Infrastructure (IEC 62443)** — Mapping zone-and-conduit security requirements to SCADA system design.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Risk-Weighted Coverage Metric

The **Risk-Weighted Coverage (RWC)** metric quantifies the degree to which a candidate design satisfies a portfolio of security requirements, weighted by their associated risk. This is the single most-likely 7-mark sub-question for a KTU ESE.

### Step 1 — Define the universe of requirements
Let there be $n$ atomic security requirements in the specification:

$$R = \{r_1, r_2, r_3, \ldots, r_n\}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the risk score for each requirement
For each $r_i$, the risk score is defined as the product of threat probability and impact:

$$RS_i = P_i \times I_i$$

where $P_i \in [0, 1]$ is the probability of the threat being realized, and $I_i \in [1, 5]$ is the impact severity on a 5-point scale (1 = Negligible, 5 = Catastrophic).

### Step 3 — Define the design-addressing indicator
For each requirement $r_i$, define a binary indicator $D_i$ such that:

$$D_i = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if a corresponding design element addresses } r_i \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

### Step 4 — Sum the risk-weighted addressed and unaddressed contributions
The numerator aggregates the risk score of every requirement *successfully* addressed by the design:

$$N = \sum_{i=1}^{n} RS_i \cdot D_i$$

The denominator aggregates the risk score of *all* requirements, whether addressed or not:

$$D_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} RS_i$$

### Step 5 — Form the ratio and express as a percentage

$$C_{rw} = \frac{N}{D_{total}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} RS_i \cdot D_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} RS_i}$$

and equivalently as a percentage:

$$C_{rw}\% = C_{rw} \times 100\%$$

### Step 6 — Apply the engineering gate
The design is **acceptable** only if:

$$C_{rw} \geq 0.90 \quad (\text{i.e., } 90\%)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A high $C_{rw}$ ensures that the design is not merely "covering many requirements" but is covering the **most dangerous** ones. A design that addresses 100% of low-risk requirements but 0% of a critical requirement would score poorly.

### Worked Numerical Example
Suppose $n = 4$ with the following data:

| Req $r_i$ | $P_i$ | $I_i$ | $D_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $r_1$ (Data-at-rest encryption) | 0.7 | 5 | 1 |
| $r_2$ (Session timeout) | 0.4 | 2 | 1 |
| $r_3$ (Anti-CSRF token) | 0.5 | 4 | 0 |
| $r_4$ (Audit logging) | 0.6 | 4 | 1 |

**Computation:**

- $RS_1 = 0.7 \times 5 = 3.5$
- $RS_2 = 0.4 \times 2 = 0.8$
- $RS_3 = 0.5 \times 4 = 2.0$
- $RS_4 = 0.6 \times 4 = 2.4$

$$C_{rw} = \frac{(3.5 \times 1) + (0.8 \times 1) + (2.0 \times 0) + (2.4 \times 1)}{3.5 + 0.8 + 2.0 + 2.4}$$

$$C_{rw} = \frac{3.5 + 0.8 + 0 + 2.4}{8.7} = \frac{6.7}{8.7} \approx 0.7701$$

$$C_{rw}\% \approx 77.01\%$$

**Conclusion:** Since $C_{rw} < 0.90$, the design is **NOT acceptable** and the designer must add a control to address $r_3$ (Anti-CSRF token). After adding the control ($D_3 = 1$):

$$C_{rw}\% = \frac{3.5 + 0.8 + 2.0 + 2.4}{8.7} \times 100\% = \frac{8.7}{8.7} \times 100\% = 100\%$$

The design now passes the engineering gate.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Latency Budget Constraint

A common KTU numerical sub-question involves determining whether a proposed cryptographic control fits within the application's response-time SLA.

### Step 1 — Define the application latency
Let $L_{app}$ be the average end-to-end application processing time **without** the security control, measured in **milliseconds (ms)**.

### Step 2 — Define the network transit time
Let $L_{net}$ be the round-trip time between the client and the server, in **ms**.

### Step 3 — Define the SLA-imposed total time
Let $L_{SLA}$ be the maximum total time the user is willing to wait (e.g., 3000 ms for a payment transaction).

### Step 4 — Isolate the security control budget

$$L_{budget} = L_{SLA} - L_{app} - L_{net}$$

### Step 5 — Validate the proposed control
If the proposed cryptographic or authentication control has a measured latency $L_{control} \leq L_{budget}$, then the design is feasible.

**Numerical Illustration:**
- $L_{SLA} = 3000$ ms
- $L_{app} = 1800$ ms
- $L_{net} = 150$ ms
- $L_{control} = 800$ ms (RSA-2048 signature generation + AES-256-GCM encryption)

$$L_{budget} = 3000 - 1800 - 150 = 1050 \text{ ms}$$

Since $L_{control} = 800 \text{ ms} \leq 1050 \text{ ms} = L_{budget}$, the design is **feasible**. If, however, a heavier control (e.g., RSA-4096) consumed 1500 ms, the design would be **rejected**, and the designer would have to negotiate the SLA, optimize the algorithm (e.g., switch to ECC P-384), or relax the requirement.

## 3.3 The Analytical Mapping Matrix (Synthesis)

| Mapping Step | Input Artifact | Output Artifact | Tool / Method | Validation Gate |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Elicitation | Business policy, regulations | List of security needs | Interviews, SQUARE Steps 1–3 | Stakeholder sign-off |
| 2. Decomposition | Atomic security needs | Atomic testable requirements | SMART criteria | Measurability check |
| 3. Threat Cross-Ref | Atomic requirements | Threat-to-Req matrix | STRIDE, VAST | Exhaustiveness ($\vert T \vert = 6$) |
| 4. Pattern Allocation | Threat-mapped requirements | Design pattern catalog | Gamma Security Patterns | Pattern applicability matrix |
| 5. Formalization | Selected patterns | Measurable specifications | FIPS 140-3, ISO 15408 | Conformance test |
| 6. Verification | Full mapping set | Sign-off report | Risk-weighted coverage | $C_{rw} \geq 90\%$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Mapping Lifecycle (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **complete six-stage mapping lifecycle** with explicit feedback loops, which is the most commonly asked 7-mark question in KTU ESE papers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 1: Requirement Elicitation] --> B[Stage 2: Requirement Decomposition]
    B --> C[Stage 3: Threat Cross Referencing]
    C --> D[Stage 4: Design Pattern Allocation]
    D --> E[Stage 5: Specification Formalization]
    E --> F[Stage 6: Traceability Verification]
    F --> G{C_rw greater than or equal to 90 percent}
    G -->|Yes| H[Design Specification Sign Off]
    G -->|No| D
    H --> I[Implementation Phase]
    I --> J[Test Phase]
    J --> K{All requirements verified}
    K -->|Yes| L[Release]
    K -->|No| A
    C --> M[STRIDE Threat Model]
    M --> C
    D --> N[Gamma Security Patterns Catalog]
    N --> D
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **forward chain** (A → B → C → D → E → F) represents the canonical mapping pipeline.
- The **gate at F** is the risk-weighted coverage check; if it fails, the process loops back to **D (Pattern Allocation)** so that additional patterns can be added.
- The **outer loop** (K → A) represents the post-release defect-driven re-elicitation cycle.
- The **side links** to **M (STRIDE)** and **N (Patterns)** show the supporting knowledge bases that fuel stages 3 and 4.

## 4.2 Security Use Case vs Misuse Case Mapping

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **Jacobson Method** for parallel scenario-based mapping.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Actor: Legitimate User] --> B1[Use Case: Login]
    B1 --> C1[Secure Design: MFA + Hashed Password]
    A2[Actor: Attacker] --> B2[Misuse Case: Credential Stuffing]
    B2 --> C2[Design Defense: Rate Limiting + CAPTCHA + Anomaly Detection]
    C1 --> D[Security Specification Document]
    C2 --> D
    D --> E[Traceability Matrix Row]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Left branch:** The legitimate user requires login → MFA + hashed credentials (Salted Argon2).
- **Right branch:** The attacker executes credential stuffing → the design defends with rate limiting, CAPTCHA, and ML-based anomaly detection.
- **Both branches converge** at the **Security Specification Document**, which then feeds a row in the **Traceability Matrix**.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

For complex systems where a physical diagram is impractical, the following **block-level architecture** illustrates how security requirements are routed to design layers.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LayerA[Presentation Layer]
        reqA1[Req: TLS 1.3 in Transit]
        reqA2[Req: XSS Protection]
    end
    subgraph LayerB[Business Layer]
        reqB1[Req: RBAC Authorization]
        reqB2[Req: Input Validation]
    end
    subgraph LayerC[Data Layer]
        reqC1[Req: AES 256 At Rest]
        reqC2[Req: Audit Logging]
    end
    subgraph LayerD[Network Layer]
        reqD1[Req: WAF and DDoS]
        reqD2[Req: Segmentation]
    end
    subgraph LayerE[Host Layer]
        reqE1[Req: Hardened OS]
        reqE2[Req: EDR and SIEM]
    end
    LayerA --> LayerB
    LayerB --> LayerC
    LayerC --> LayerD
    LayerD --> LayerE
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Each layer (Presentation, Business, Data, Network, Host) inherits a specific set of security requirements.
- The design is **layered** — meaning a single requirement (e.g., encryption) is enforced at multiple layers (transport-layer TLS + application-layer envelope encryption + storage-layer AES-256).
- This **defense-in-depth** posture is itself a mapping pattern known as the **Layered Security Pattern**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing SRS with SDD** — A frequent 1-mark loss. The SRS defines *what* the system must do; the SDD defines *how* it does it. Mapping bridges the two.
> 2. **Forgetting STRIDE exhaustiveness** — When asked to identify threats, students often miss one or two categories. Remember: **6 threats, no more, no less**.
> 3. **Mixing up SQUARE steps** — SQUARE has **9** steps. Mis-stating the count or skipping steps loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Skipping the engineering gate** — When computing $C_{rw}$, you MUST state the threshold (typically 90%) and explicitly check it.
> 5. **Ignoring bidirectional traceability** — A design specification with no back-reference to a requirement is *untraceable* and treated as an audit failure.

---

## Part A — 3-Mark Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define the term "Security Requirements-to-Design Mapping" and list any two advanced techniques used for the same.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Security Requirements-to-Design Mapping is the systematic process of translating abstract security requirements — derived from policies, regulations, and threat models — into concrete, testable design specifications that can be implemented within a system's architecture.

Two advanced techniques used are:

1. **SQUARE (Security Quality Requirements Engineering) Method** — A nine-step NIST-defined process that elicits, categorizes, prioritizes, and inspects security requirements before allocation to design.
2. **Security Use Case + Misuse Case Mapping (Jacobson Method)** — Parallel scenario engineering where the expected secure behavior of a legitimate actor is mapped alongside the adversarial behavior of a threat actor, and the design must satisfy or defeat both.

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark each for any two techniques = 3 marks)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**What is a Traceability Matrix in the context of security design? Mention any four columns it must contain.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Traceability Matrix is a two-dimensional artifact that links each security requirement to its corresponding design element, test case, and stakeholder need, ensuring *no requirement is unaddressed* and *no design element is unjustified*.

The four essential columns are:

1. **Requirement ID** — Unique identifier of the atomic security requirement.
2. **Description** — Plain-language statement of the requirement.
3. **Design Element ID** — Identifier of the architectural component that satisfies the requirement.
4. **Test Case ID** — Identifier of the validation test that proves the design element meets the requirement.

*(1 mark for definition + ½ mark each for the four columns = 3 marks)*

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**
*(a)* Explain the **SQUARE methodology** for security requirements engineering in detail. List all nine steps and discuss the role of stakeholder prioritization. **(7 marks)**
*(b)* With a suitable block diagram, describe the **six-stage security requirements-to-design mapping lifecycle**. How does the risk-weighted coverage metric act as a quality gate? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) SQUARE Methodology (7 marks)**

SQUARE (Security Quality Requirements Engineering) is a nine-step systematic process developed by the **Software Engineering Institute (SEI) of Carnegie Mellon University** and adopted by NIST. It focuses on producing security requirements that are *agreed-upon, prioritized, and inspectable*.

The nine steps are:

1. **Identify Stakeholders** — Catalog all parties (customers, regulators, security team, auditors) who have a security interest in the system.
2. **Identify Security Goals** — Define high-level business-driven security objectives (e.g., "Protect customer PII").
3. **Identify Artifacts** — List the system elements (data, software, hardware, users) that must be protected.
4. **Perform Risk Assessment** — Identify and rank threats against each artifact.
5. **Select Elicitation Technique** — Choose a method (interviews, surveys, focus groups, JAD sessions) for gathering detailed security needs.
6. **Elicit Security Requirements** — Use the selected technique to capture atomic security requirements.
7. **Categorize Requirements** — Classify them (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability, Authentication, Authorization, Accountability).
8. **Prioritize Requirements** — Rank by risk score, business impact, and implementation cost.
9. **Inspect Requirements** — Conduct a formal review (walkthrough, inspection) to verify correctness, completeness, and testability.

**Role of Stakeholder Prioritization (Step 8):**
Stakeholders assign weights based on their business value and regulatory exposure. The output is a *ranked list* that the designer uses to allocate finite resources. Without prioritization, low-value requirements may consume engineering effort that should be reserved for high-risk gaps.

*(2 marks for listing the 9 steps + 2 marks for explaining prioritization + 3 marks for descriptive flow = 7 marks)*

**(b) Mapping Lifecycle and Risk-Weighted Coverage (7 marks)**

The **six-stage mapping lifecycle** is:

1. **Requirement Elicitation** — Gather stakeholder, regulatory, and policy needs.
2. **Requirement Decomposition** — Break compound requirements into atomic SMART statements.
3. **Threat-to-Requirement Cross-Referencing** — Map each requirement to one or more STRIDE categories.
4. **Design Pattern Allocation** — Assign a verified security pattern (e.g., *Authenticator*, *Guard*) per requirement.
5. **Specification Formalization** — Express the pattern in measurable terms (key length, factor count, latency budget).
6. **Traceability Verification** — Compute $C_{rw}$ and decide on sign-off.

**Risk-Weighted Coverage as a Quality Gate:**

The risk-weighted coverage metric is defined as:

$$C_{rw} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} RS_i \cdot D_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} RS_i}$$

where $RS_i = P_i \times I_i$ is the risk score and $D_i \in \{0,1\}$ indicates whether the design addresses the requirement.

**Role as a quality gate:** If $C_{rw} < 0.90$ (90%), the design specification is **rejected** and the designer is mandated to add controls. This prevents the common engineering failure of addressing many *low-risk* requirements while neglecting *critical* ones. It enforces a **risk-proportional** design allocation.

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes.)

*(2 marks for lifecycle explanation + 3 marks for the formula and its role + 2 marks for diagram = 7 marks)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**
*(a)* Discuss the **Security Use Case and Misuse Case** approach. Construct a small example with a banking ATM system to illustrate. **(7 marks)**
*(b)* Compute the **Risk-Weighted Coverage ($C_{rw}$)** for the following design scenario and determine whether the design passes the 90% engineering gate. Show all intermediate steps. **(7 marks)**

| Req ID | Description | Probability ($P_i$) | Impact ($I_i$, 1–5) | Design Element Exists ($D_i$) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| R-01 | Multi-factor authentication for admin | 0.8 | 5 | 1 |
| R-02 | TLS 1.3 for all API calls | 0.6 | 4 | 1 |
| R-03 | AES-256 encryption at rest | 0.7 | 5 | 1 |
| R-04 | SQL injection prevention (parameterized queries) | 0.5 | 4 | 0 |
| R-05 | Audit logging of privileged actions | 0.6 | 3 | 1 |

#### Model Solution

**(a) Security Use Case and Misuse Case Approach (7 marks)**

The **Jacobson Method** introduces the concept of *Use Cases* (positive scenarios) and *Misuse Cases* (negative scenarios) as a pair. The two are documented in parallel, ensuring that for every legitimate interaction there is a documented threat scenario, and the design must satisfy the former while defeating the latter.

**Banking ATM Example:**

- **Security Use Case 1: Legitimate Withdrawal**
  - **Actor:** Account holder.
  - **Precondition:** Valid card inserted.
  - **Flow:** User enters PIN → PIN verified → Account selected → Amount entered → Cash dispensed.
  - **Security Design Specifications:** PIN block after 3 attempts, end-to-end encryption between ATM and bank server, dual-control for vault access, secure hardware module (HSM) for PIN verification.

- **Misuse Case 1: Skimming Attack**
  - **Actor:** Attacker with skimming device.
  - **Flow:** Device reads magnetic stripe + hidden camera captures PIN.
  - **Defense Design Specifications:** EMV chip-only acceptance, anti-skimming jitter on card reader, shutter on PIN pad, tamper-evident casing.

- **Misuse Case 2: Card Trapping**
  - **Actor:** Attacker physically blocks card.
  - **Defense Design Specifications:** Anti-trap sensors, real-time alert to bank, automatic retention mechanism.

The mapping outcome is a row in the Traceability Matrix for each Use Case and each Misuse Case, linking it to its design element and test case.

*(2 marks concept + 2 marks use case + 2 marks misuse case + 1 mark mapping outcome = 7 marks)*

**(b) Risk-Weighted Coverage Computation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Compute the risk score for each requirement:**

- $RS_1 = 0.8 \times 5 = 4.0$
- $RS_2 = 0.6 \times 4 = 2.4$
- $RS_3 = 0.7 \times 5 = 3.5$
- $RS_4 = 0.5 \times 4 = 2.0$
- $RS_5 = 0.6 \times 3 = 1.8$

**Step 2 — Compute the denominator (total risk exposure):**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{5} RS_i = 4.0 + 2.4 + 3.5 + 2.0 + 1.8 = 13.7$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator (risk-weighted addressed contributions):**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{5} RS_i \cdot D_i = (4.0 \times 1) + (2.4 \times 1) + (3.5 \times 1) + (2.0 \times 0) + (1.8 \times 1)$$

$$= 4.0 + 2.4 + 3.5 + 0 + 1.8 = 11.7$$

**Step 4 — Form the ratio:**

$$C_{rw} = \frac{11.7}{13.7} \approx 0.8540$$

$$C_{rw}\% \approx 85.40\%$$

**Step 5 — Apply the engineering gate:**

Since $C_{rw}\% \approx 85.40\% < 90\%$, the design **FAILS** the engineering gate. The designer must add a control for **R-04 (SQL injection prevention)** — e.g., parameterized queries plus a Web Application Firewall (WAF) with SQL injection signatures.

**Step 6 — Re-compute after remediation:**

Setting $D_4 = 1$:

$$C_{rw}\% = \frac{4.0 + 2.4 + 3.5 + 2.0 + 1.8}{13.7} \times 100\% = \frac{13.7}{13.7} \times 100\% = 100\%$$

The remediated design now passes the gate.

*(1 mark step 1 + 1 mark step 2 + 1 mark step 3 + 1 mark step 4 + 1 mark step 5 + 2 marks step 6 = 7 marks)*

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Review)**

- **Definition (must remember):** SR-DM is the *systematic, traceable* transformation of abstract security needs into concrete, testable design specifications.
- **Three mapping directions:** Forward, Backward, Lateral. All three are required for a *complete* traceability artifact.
- **Six lifecycle stages:** Elicitation → Decomposition → Threat Cross-Reference → Pattern Allocation → Formalization → Verification.
- **Four advanced techniques:** SQUARE (9 steps), Use/Misuse Cases (Jacobson), Risk-Weighted Traceability Matrix, Pattern-Based Allocation (Gamma).
- **SQUARE has exactly 9 steps** and the **STRIDE set has exactly 6 categories**.
- **Engineering gate threshold:** $C_{rw} \geq 90\%$. Always state the threshold explicitly when solving numerical problems.
- **Risk score formula:** $RS = P \times I$, with $P \in [0,1]$ and $I \in [1,5]$.
- **Latency budget formula:** $L_{budget} = L_{SLA} - L_{app} - L_{net}$. A control is feasible only if its latency is $\leq$ the budget.
- **Misuse cases are mandatory, not optional.** For every use case, document at least one corresponding misuse case.
- **Bidirectional traceability is non-negotiable** — every design element must point back to a requirement, and every requirement must point to a design element, a test case, and a stakeholder need.
- **Common KTU buzzwords to use in answers:** *traceability, decomposition, STRIDE, SQUARE, atomic requirement, defense-in-depth, risk-weighted coverage, pattern allocation, latency budget, sign-off gate.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Secure Software Implementation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT418 - SECURITY ASSESSMENT AND RISK ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Secure Software Implementation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Secure Software Implementation** is the disciplined engineering practice of designing, coding, testing, and maintaining software systems in a manner that proactively resists malicious exploitation, preserves confidentiality, integrity, and availability (the **CIA Triad**), and conforms to established security standards throughout the **Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC)**. In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme, this concept is rooted in the **Security Development Lifecycle (SDL)** — a process introduced by Microsoft and adapted globally — which embeds security checkpoints at every phase of software construction, from requirements gathering to post-deployment maintenance.

The discipline encompasses threat modeling, secure coding standards (e.g., **OWASP Top 10**, **CERT Secure Coding Standards**), static and dynamic analysis, penetration testing, cryptographic integration, and continuous vulnerability management. The objective is the creation of **Defence-in-Depth** software — a layered security architecture where the failure of any single control does not result in total compromise.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 1 — Secure Software Implementation)**
> *Secure software implementation refers to the integration of security principles, tools, and processes into every phase of the software development life cycle, ensuring that the final product is resilient against known and emerging threats, compliant with regulatory frameworks, and aligned with industry-recognized security benchmarks such as OWASP, NIST SSDF, and ISO/IEC 27034.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Bank Vault Mental Model

Imagine you are constructing a **bank vault** to protect a nation's currency reserves.

- **Insecure Implementation**: A builder constructs the vault using only thick steel for the door but neglects to reinforce the walls, ceiling, or floor. A thief simply tunnels through the rear wall. This represents software where the *authentication layer* is hardened but the *business logic* or *database* layer is left exposed.
- **Secure Implementation**: The builder uses reinforced concrete walls, a time-locked vault door, motion-sensitive cameras, biometric access, alarm systems, and 24/7 guards. Even if one barrier is breached (e.g., the camera is disabled), the attacker still faces multiple independent layers.

In software terms, this translates to:
- **Input validation** (the reinforced walls — preventing bad data from entering)
- **Authentication & Authorization** (the biometric lock — verifying identity and permissions)
- **Encryption** (the vault door — protecting data at rest and in transit)
- **Logging & Monitoring** (the guards and cameras — detecting breaches in real-time)
- **Error Handling** (the alarm system — failing safely without leaking information)

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Principle**: Security is not a feature to be bolted on at the end of development; it is a **quality attribute** that must be engineered into the product from the very first line of code. This is the central tenet of *Shift-Left Security* in modern DevSecOps pipelines.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Inventory

| Term | Definition | KTU Significance |
|------|------------|------------------|
| **SDLC** | Software Development Life Cycle | Framework within which security is integrated |
| **SDL** | Security Development Lifecycle | Microsoft's process model; KTU reference |
| **CIA Triad** | Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability | Foundational security model |
| **STRIDE** | Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, DoS, Elevation of Privilege | Threat classification schema |
| **DREAD** | Damage, Reproducibility, Exploitability, Affected Users, Discoverability | Risk-rating model |
| **OWASP** | Open Web Application Security Project | Industry-standard secure coding knowledge base |
| **CWE** | Common Weakness Enumeration | Catalogue of software security flaws |
| **CVE** | Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures | Public database of known vulnerabilities |
| **CVSS** | Common Vulnerability Scoring System | **0.0 to 10.0** severity scoring metric |
| **SAST** | Static Application Security Testing | White-box source code analysis |
| **DAST** | Dynamic Application Security Testing | Black-box runtime analysis |
| **SCA** | Software Composition Analysis | Third-party/open-source dependency scanning |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric to Remember**: Vulnerability severity is universally measured using the **CVSS (Common Vulnerability Scoring System)** v3.1 base score, ranging from **0.0 (None)** to **10.0 (Critical)**. A score of **9.0 or above** is classified as *Critical* and demands immediate remediation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Security Development Lifecycle (SDL) — Phase-Wise Breakdown

The **Security Development Lifecycle** is the operational backbone of secure software implementation. It is a *prescriptive* process (i.e., it prescribes specific activities, unlike the *descriptive* SDLC) that mandates security tasks at every phase.

#### Phase 1: Training
- **What**: Developers, testers, and project managers undergo mandatory security training.
- **Why**: Human error accounts for an estimated **74% of data breaches** (per industry breach reports). Training reduces the cognitive gap between functional design and security awareness.
- **How**: Annual certifications (e.g., CSSLP, CEH, GWAPT), secure coding workshops, threat-modelling bootcamps.

#### Phase 2: Requirements & Use Cases
- **What**: Security requirements are captured alongside functional requirements.
- **Why**: Defining security *non-functional requirements* (NFRs) early prevents costly retrofits.
- **How**: Tools like abuse-case modelling, security user stories ("As an attacker, I want to..."), regulatory mapping (GDPR, PCI-DSS, HIPAA).

#### Phase 3: Design
- **What**: Architectural risk analysis and threat modelling are performed.
- **Why**: Design flaws are **10× to 100× more expensive** to fix post-deployment than during design.
- **How**: STRIDE-per-Element analysis, data-flow diagrams (DFDs), trust boundaries, attack-surface reduction.

#### Phase 4: Implementation
- **What**: Secure coding standards are enforced, and peer code reviews focus on security.
- **Why**: Vulnerabilities introduced at this stage propagate to production.
- **How**: Following **OWASP Secure Coding Practices**, using vetted cryptographic libraries, avoiding `strcpy`, `eval`, `sprintf` in C/C++, sanitizing SQL queries, using parameterized queries.

#### Phase 5: Verification
- **What**: Security testing is integrated into the QA pipeline.
- **Why**: Verification provides empirical evidence that security controls function as designed.
- **How**: SAST (e.g., SonarQube, Checkmarx), DAST (e.g., OWASP ZAP, Burp Suite), IAST, fuzzing, manual penetration testing.

#### Phase 6: Release
- **What**: A final security review (FSR) is conducted before public deployment.
- **Why**: Acts as the last checkpoint before the product reaches end users.
- **How**: Sign-off by security leads, executive summary, residual-risk acceptance.

#### Phase 7: Response
- **What**: A post-deployment incident response plan is established.
- **Why**: Even the most secure software may face zero-day exploits; rapid response is critical.
- **How**: 24/7 SOC (Security Operations Centre), patch management process, CVE monitoring, responsible disclosure policy.

### 2.2 Secure Coding Principles — The 10 Pillars

These are the **non-negotiable** principles every KTU student must memorize:

1. **Validate All Input**: Never trust user input. Apply *whitelisting* (allow-list) over blacklisting.
2. **Encode All Output**: Prevent XSS (Cross-Site Scripting) by context-aware output encoding.
3. **Parameterize Queries**: Use prepared statements to prevent SQL Injection.
4. **Use Strong Cryptography**: AES-256 for symmetric, RSA-2048+ or ECC for asymmetric.
5. **Implement Proper Authentication**: Multi-factor authentication (MFA), salted password hashing (bcrypt, Argon2).
6. **Enforce Least Privilege**: Grant only the minimum permissions required.
7. **Fail Securely**: Error messages must not leak stack traces, DB credentials, or system paths.
8. **Log Security Events**: Failed logins, privilege escalations, and configuration changes must be logged.
9. **Keep Components Updated**: Patch all third-party libraries; track CVEs.
10. **Apply Defence-in-Depth**: Layered security controls.

### 2.3 Threat Modelling Frameworks

#### STRIDE (Per-Element)

| Threat | Security Property Violated |
|--------|---------------------------|
| **S**poofing | Authentication |
| **T**ampering | Integrity |
| **R**epudiation | Non-repudiation / Accountability |
| **I**nformation Disclosure | Confidentiality |
| **D**enial of Service | Availability |
| **E**levation of Privilege | Authorization |

#### DREAD Risk Rating Model

Each category is rated **0 (None) to 10 (Critical)**. The total risk score is the average:

$$
\text{DREAD Score} = \frac{D + R + E + A + D_i}{5}
$$

Where:
- $D$ = Damage potential
- $R$ = Reproducibility
- $E$ = Exploitability
- $A$ = Affected users
- $D_i$ = Discoverability

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Markdown Table)

| Formula / Metric | Expression | Description / Use |
|------------------|------------|-------------------|
| **DREAD Risk Score** | $\text{Risk} = \frac{D + R + E + A + D_i}{5}$ | Average of five factors; range 0–10 |
| **CVSS Base Score** | $\text{CVSS} \in [0.0, 10.0]$ | Standardized severity rating |
| **Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE)** | $\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$ | Expected annual monetary loss from a threat |
| **Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)** | $\text{SLE} = \text{Asset Value} \times \text{Exposure Factor}$ | Loss from a single occurrence |
| **Annualized Rate of Occurrence (ARO)** | $\text{ARO} \in [0, \infty)$ | Estimated yearly frequency of a threat |
| **Return on Security Investment (ROSI)** | $\text{ROSI} = \frac{\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{after}} - \text{Cost of Control}}{\text{Cost of Control}}$ | Justification of security expenditure |
