| **Attack Surface Metric** | $\text{ASM} = \sum (\text{Entry Points} \times \text{Privilege Level})$ | Heuristic measurement of exposure |
| **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD)** | $\text{MTTD} = \frac{\sum \text{Detection Times}}{N}$ | Average detection latency |
| **Mean Time to Respond (MTTR)** | $\text{MTTR} = \frac{\sum \text{Response Times}}{N}$ | Average incident response latency |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility**: These formulas are not merely academic. In industry, **ALE** is used by CISOs to justify multi-million dollar security budgets, **ROSI** is presented to boards of directors, and **CVSS** is used by vulnerability management platforms (Qualys, Tenable, Rapid7) to auto-prioritize patch deployment across enterprise IT estates.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — DREAD Risk Score Calculation (Worked Example)

**Problem Statement**:
A web application has a vulnerability that allows an unauthenticated attacker to read arbitrary files from the server. The security team assigns the following DREAD ratings:
- Damage potential: $D = 8$
- Reproducibility: $R = 9$
- Exploitability: $E = 7$
- Affected users: $A = 6$
- Discoverability: $D_i = 9$

Compute the DREAD score and classify the risk level.

**Step-by-Step Solution**:

**Step 1**: Identify the formula.

$$
\text{DREAD} = \frac{D + R + E + A + D_i}{5}
$$

**Step 2**: Substitute the given values.

$$
\text{DREAD} = \frac{8 + 9 + 7 + 6 + 9}{5}
$$

**Step 3**: Sum the numerator.

$$
\text{DREAD} = \frac{39}{5}
$$

**Step 4**: Compute the division.

$$
\text{DREAD} = 7.8
$$

**Step 5**: Classify the result.

$$
\text{Risk Level} = \begin{cases} \text{Low} & \text{if } 0 \leq \text{DREAD} < 3 \\ \text{Medium} & \text{if } 3 \leq \text{DREAD} < 6 \\ \text{High} & \text{if } 6 \leq \text{DREAD} < 9 \\ \text{Critical} & \text{if } 9 \leq \text{DREAD} \leq 10 \end{cases}
$$

Since $6 \leq 7.8 < 9$, the vulnerability is classified as **High Risk** and must be remediated within **30 days** as per the organization's Service Level Agreement (SLA).

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE) Computation

**Problem Statement**:
An e-commerce company processes credit card data. The asset value of the customer database is ₹50,00,000. A data breach would expose 60% of the records. Historical data suggests such breaches occur approximately once every 4 years. The cost of implementing an encryption-based data-at-rest control is ₹2,00,000 per year. Calculate the **ALE before control**, **ALE after control (assume 90% reduction)**, and the **ROSI**.

**Step-by-Step Solution**:

**Step 1**: Calculate the Single Loss Expectancy (SLE).

$$
\text{SLE} = \text{Asset Value} \times \text{Exposure Factor}
$$

$$
\text{SLE} = 50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.60 = 30{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2**: Calculate the Annualized Rate of Occurrence (ARO).

$$
\text{ARO} = \frac{1 \text{ breach}}{4 \text{ years}} = 0.25
$$

**Step 3**: Calculate the ALE before control.

$$
\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}
$$

$$
\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} = 30{,}00{,}000 \times 0.25 = 7{,}50{,}000
$$

**Step 4**: Calculate the ALE after control (90% reduction).

$$
\text{ALE}_{\text{after}} = 7{,}50{,}000 \times (1 - 0.90) = 75{,}000
$$

**Step 5**: Calculate the monetary benefit per year.

$$
\text{Benefit} = \text{ALE}_{\text{before}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{after}} = 7{,}50{,}000 - 75{,}000 = 6{,}75{,}000
$$

**Step 6**: Calculate the ROSI.

$$
\text{ROSI} = \frac{\text{Benefit} - \text{Cost of Control}}{\text{Cost of Control}}
$$

$$
\text{ROSI} = \frac{6{,}75{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} = \frac{4{,}75{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} = 2.375
$$

**Step 7**: Interpret the result.

$$
\text{ROSI} = 2.375 \implies \text{For every } ₹1 \text{ invested, the company saves } ₹2.375
$$

This is a **positive ROI**, and the security investment is **financially justified**.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Risk Matrix Classification

**Problem Statement**:
A mobile banking app has three identified vulnerabilities. Compute the qualitative risk using a $3 \times 3$ likelihood-impact matrix.

| Vulnerability | Likelihood | Impact | Risk Score |
|---------------|-----------|--------|------------|
| V1: Cleartext credential storage | High (3) | High (3) | ? |
| V2: Verbose error messages | Medium (2) | Low (1) | ? |
| V3: Outdated TLS library | High (3) | Medium (2) | ? |

**Step-by-Step Solution**:

**Step 1**: Define the risk score function.

$$
\text{Risk} = \text{Likelihood} \times \text{Impact}
$$

**Step 2**: Compute V1.

$$
\text{Risk}_{V1} = 3 \times 3 = 9 \quad (\text{Critical})
$$

**Step 3**: Compute V2.

$$
\text{Risk}_{V2} = 2 \times 1 = 2 \quad (\text{Low})
$$

**Step 4**: Compute V3.

$$
\text{Risk}_{V3} = 3 \times 2 = 6 \quad (\text{Medium})
$$

**Step 5**: Apply the classification table.

$$
\text{Risk} = \begin{cases}
\text{Low} & \text{if } 1 \leq \text{Risk} \leq 3 \\
\text{Medium} & \text{if } 4 \leq \text{Risk} \leq 6 \\
\text{High} & \text{if } 7 \leq \text{Risk} \leq 8 \\
\text{Critical} & \text{if } 9 \leq \text{Risk} \leq 9
\end{cases}
$$

**Final Analytical Matrix**:

| Vulnerability | Risk Score | Classification | Remediation Priority |
|---------------|-----------|----------------|---------------------|
| V1: Cleartext credential storage | 9 | **Critical** | P0 — Immediate (24 hours) |
| V3: Outdated TLS library | 6 | **Medium** | P2 — Scheduled (30 days) |
| V2: Verbose error messages | 2 | **Low** | P3 — Backlog (90 days) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight**: The risk matrix is the most widely used risk-assessment tool in industry. The numerical product $\text{Likelihood} \times \text{Impact}$ is preferred over additive models because it captures the multiplicative interaction of severity dimensions — a high-likelihood, high-impact threat is exponentially more dangerous than the linear sum of its parts.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Cryptographic Hash Strength (Worked)

**Problem Statement**:
Demonstrate why SHA-256 is preferred over MD5 for password hashing, given that MD5 has a collision resistance of $2^{64}$ operations and SHA-256 has $2^{128}$.

**Step 1**: Express the work factor.

$$
W_{\text{MD5}} = 2^{64}, \quad W_{\text{SHA-256}} = 2^{128}
$$

**Step 2**: Compute the ratio.

$$
\frac{W_{\text{SHA-256}}}{W_{\text{MD5}}} = \frac{2^{128}}{2^{64}} = 2^{64} \approx 1.8 \times 10^{19}
$$

**Step 3**: Interpret.

The attacker must perform $2^{64}$ *more* operations to forge a SHA-256 collision than an MD5 collision. At $10^{12}$ operations per second (modern GPU rig), this translates to:

$$
T_{\text{MD5}} = \frac{2^{64}}{10^{12}} \approx 5.85 \times 10^{5} \text{ seconds} \approx 6.77 \text{ days}
$$

$$
T_{\text{SHA-256}} = \frac{2^{128}}{10^{12}} \approx 1.08 \times 10^{26} \text{ seconds} \approx 3.42 \times 10^{18} \text{ years}
$$

SHA-256 is computationally infeasible to brute-force within the age of the universe, whereas MD5 is broken within a week.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Security Development Lifecycle (SDL) — Phase Flow Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram renders the seven sequential phases of Microsoft's Security Development Lifecycle, with the security activities embedded in each phase. This is the most frequently asked conceptual diagram in KTU Module 1 examinations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    phase1["Phase 1: Security Training"]
    phase2["Phase 2: Requirements and Use Cases"]
    phase3["Phase 3: Design and Architecture"]
    phase4["Phase 4: Implementation and Coding"]
    phase5["Phase 5: Verification and Testing"]
    phase6["Phase 6: Release and Deployment"]
    phase7["Phase 7: Response and Maintenance"]

    act1["Annual security training, OWASP awareness, secure coding workshops"]
    act2["Capture security NFRs, abuse cases, regulatory compliance mapping"]
    act3["STRIDE threat modelling, attack surface analysis, trust boundaries"]
    act4["Apply secure coding standards, SAST scanning, code review"]
    act5["DAST scanning, penetration testing, fuzzing, vulnerability triage"]
    act6["Final Security Review FSR, executive sign-off, release approval"]
    act7["Incident response, patch management, CVE monitoring, forensics"]

    phase1 --> act1
    phase2 --> act2
    phase3 --> act3
    phase4 --> act4
    phase5 --> act5
    phase6 --> act6
    phase7 --> act7

    phase1 --> phase2
    phase2 --> phase3
    phase3 --> phase4
    phase4 --> phase5
    phase5 --> phase6
    phase6 --> phase7
    phase7 -.->|Feedback Loop| phase1
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Secure Software System

The following Mermaid diagram maps the layered (defence-in-depth) functional architecture of a typical secure web application, illustrating the trust boundaries and the security controls at each tier.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    user["External User / Attacker"]:::external
    fw["Layer 1: Web Application Firewall WAF"]:::perimeter
    auth["Layer 2: Authentication and MFA Gateway"]:::identity
    val["Layer 3: Input Validation and Sanitization Engine"]:::app
    biz["Layer 4: Business Logic and Authorization Layer"]:::app
    enc["Layer 5: Cryptographic Service Module CSM"]:::crypto
    db["Layer 6: Encrypted Database with Parameterized Queries"]:::data
    log["Layer 7: Centralized SIEM Logging and Monitoring"]:::monitor

    user -->|HTTPS TLS 1.3| fw
    fw -->|Filtered Traffic| auth
    auth -->|Authenticated Session JWT| val
    val -->|Sanitized Data| biz
    biz -->|Authorized Request| enc
    enc -->|Encrypted Channel TLS| db
    biz -.->|Audit Trail| log
    auth -.->|Auth Events| log
    fw -.->|WAF Alerts| log
    db -.->|DB Activity| log

    classDef external fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    classDef perimeter fill:#E0F4FF,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    classDef identity fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#8B8000,color:#000
    classDef app fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000
    classDef crypto fill:#E0FFE0,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef data fill:#FFEFD5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000
    classDef monitor fill:#F0F0F0,stroke:#696969,color:#000
```

### 4.3 STRIDE Threat Model — Data Flow Diagram (DFD)

The following block diagram illustrates a typical online banking transaction decomposed using the STRIDE methodology, mapping each threat to the corresponding security control.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    client["Client Browser"]
    webapp["Web Application Server"]
    apisrv["API Gateway"]
    dbsrv["Database Server"]

    t1["T1: Spoofing - Stolen Credentials"]:::threat
    t2["T2: Tampering - Modified HTTP Headers"]:::threat
    t3["T3: Repudiation - Denied Transaction"]:::threat
    t4["T4: Information Disclosure - SQL Injection"]:::threat
    t5["T5: Denial of Service - DDoS Attack"]:::threat
    t6["T6: Elevation of Privilege - Admin Access"]:::threat

    c1["C1: MFA, OAuth 2.0, JWT Signing"]:::control
    c2["C2: HMAC, Input Validation, TLS 1.3"]:::control
    c3["C3: Immutable Audit Logs, Timestamping"]:::control
    c4["C4: Parameterized Queries, WAF Rules"]:::control
    c5["C5: Rate Limiting, CDN, Anycast"]:::control
    c6["C6: RBAC, Least Privilege, Session Hardening"]:::control

    t1 -.->|mitigated by| c1
    t2 -.->|mitigated by| c2
    t3 -.->|mitigated by| c3
    t4 -.->|mitigated by| c4
    t5 -.->|mitigated by| c5
    t6 -.->|mitigated by| c6

    client -->|HTTPS| webapp
    webapp -->|REST| apisrv
    apisrv -->|SQL TLS| dbsrv

    classDef threat fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    classDef control fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note**: In each diagram above, the red-shaded nodes represent *threats* or *external actors*, while the green-shaded nodes represent *security controls* that mitigate those threats. The dotted lines in the STRIDE diagram indicate a *mitigation relationship* — i.e., the control reduces the likelihood or impact of the threat.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse SDLC and SDL**. SDLC is the *Software Development Life Cycle*; SDL is the *Security Development Lifecycle* (Microsoft's specific process). Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for this confusion.
> 2. **STRIDE is for threat classification, DREAD is for risk rating**. Students frequently swap them. Remember: STRIDE answers *"What could go wrong?"* and DREAD answers *"How bad is it?"*
> 3. **Always show the formula *and* the substitution** in numerical problems. Students who write only the final answer lose **3 to 4 marks** for lack of working.
> 4. **Do not write "C language code" in non-CS papers**, and **avoid one-word answers** in 3-mark questions. A minimum of 2 to 3 sentences is expected by the KTU 2019/2024 pattern.
> 5. **Use the term "defence-in-depth" correctly** — it refers to *layered* security, not simply "good security".

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**Define Secure Software Implementation. List any four secure coding practices recommended by OWASP.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:

**Secure Software Implementation** is the engineering discipline of integrating security controls, threat modelling, secure coding standards, and verification activities into every phase of the software development life cycle to produce software that is resilient against malicious exploitation and aligned with confidentiality, integrity, and availability objectives.

The four OWASP-recommended secure coding practices are:
1. **Input Validation** — all external input must be validated against a strict allow-list (whitelist) before processing.
2. **Output Encoding** — data rendered to the user must be context-aware encoded to prevent XSS attacks.
3. **Parameterized Queries** — database interactions must use prepared statements to prevent SQL injection.
4. **Strong Cryptography** — only vetted, industry-standard cryptographic libraries (AES-256, SHA-256, TLS 1.3) must be used; custom crypto implementations are forbidden.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the four practices — 0.5 each.)*

#### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Explain the STRIDE threat classification model with one example for each category.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:

STRIDE is a mnemonic-based threat classification framework developed by Microsoft. Each letter corresponds to a class of threat and the security property it violates.

| Letter | Threat | Violated Property | Example |
|--------|--------|-------------------|---------|
| S | Spoofing | Authentication | Forging a JWT token to impersonate a legitimate user |
| T | Tampering | Integrity | Modifying a URL parameter to escalate prices in a shopping cart |
| R | Repudiation | Non-repudiation | A user denies making a financial transfer that was actually authorized |
| I | Information Disclosure | Confidentiality | SQL injection leaking credit card numbers from a database |
| D | Denial of Service | Availability | A SYN-flood attack rendering a website unavailable |
| E | Elevation of Privilege | Authorization | A normal user gaining admin access through a vulnerable API endpoint |

*(0.5 mark per row × 6 rows = 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

**(a)** Explain the seven phases of Microsoft's Security Development Lifecycle (SDL) in detail. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Compute the DREAD risk score and the Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE) for the following scenario. An enterprise database valued at ₹80,00,000 has a 50% exposure factor. Breaches occur on average twice per year. DREAD ratings: D=9, R=8, E=9, A=7, D_i=8. The cost of deploying a database activity monitoring (DAM) solution is ₹3,00,000 per year and is expected to reduce the breach likelihood by 80%. Calculate the ROSI and recommend whether the investment is justified. *(7 Marks)*

##### Model Solution — Part A(a) — SDL Phases (7 Marks)

The Microsoft Security Development Lifecycle (SDL) is a security-centric software engineering process comprising **seven mandatory phases**:

1. **Training (1 Mark)**: All engineering staff undergo annual security training covering secure coding, threat modelling, and current attack trends. This phase mitigates the human-factor risk that accounts for the majority of data breaches.

2. **Requirements & Use Cases (1 Mark)**: Security non-functional requirements (NFRs) are captured alongside functional requirements. Abuse cases ("As an attacker, I want to...") are documented, and regulatory compliance (GDPR, PCI-DSS, HIPAA) is mapped.

3. **Design (1 Mark)**: Architectural risk analysis (ARA) and threat modelling (STRIDE, attack trees, data-flow diagrams) are performed. Trust boundaries are defined, and the attack surface is minimized.

4. **Implementation (1 Mark)**: Developers follow secure coding standards (OWASP, CERT). Tools like SAST scanners enforce rules in the IDE. Peer code reviews focus on injection flaws, XSS, and broken authentication.

5. **Verification (1 Mark)**: Dynamic Application Security Testing (DAST), Interactive Application Security Testing (IAST), fuzzing, and manual penetration testing are executed. Vulnerability severity is rated using CVSS.

6. **Release (1 Mark)**: A Final Security Review (FSR) is performed. A residual-risk document is signed by the security lead and the business owner, acknowledging any unmitigated risks.

7. **Response (1 Mark)**: A post-deployment incident response plan is established. The security team monitors CVE feeds, applies patches within SLAs, and conducts post-mortem analysis of any incidents.

##### Model Solution — Part A(b) — DREAD & ALE Computation (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Compute DREAD Score (2 Marks)**

$$
\text{DREAD} = \frac{D + R + E + A + D_i}{5} = \frac{9 + 8 + 9 + 7 + 8}{5} = \frac{41}{5} = 8.2
$$

Risk classification: **High Risk** (6 ≤ 8.2 < 9).

**Step 2: Compute SLE (1 Mark)**

$$
\text{SLE} = \text{Asset Value} \times \text{Exposure Factor} = 80{,}00{,}000 \times 0.50 = 40{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 3: Compute ARO and ALE before control (1 Mark)**

$$
\text{ARO} = 2, \quad \text{ALE}_{\text{before}} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO} = 40{,}00{,}000 \times 2 = 80{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 4: Compute ALE after control (1 Mark)**

$$
\text{ALE}_{\text{after}} = 80{,}00{,}000 \times (1 - 0.80) = 16{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 5: Compute ROSI (1 Mark)**

$$
\text{Benefit} = 80{,}00{,}000 - 16{,}00{,}000 = 64{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\text{ROSI} = \frac{64{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000} = \frac{61{,}00{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000} \approx 20.33
$$

**Step 6: Recommendation (1 Mark)**

$$
\text{ROSI} = 20.33 \implies \text{For every } ₹1 \text{ invested, ₹20.33 is saved}
$$

The investment is **strongly justified**. The DAM solution should be deployed immediately.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the STRIDE-per-Element threat modelling methodology. Apply STRIDE to an online payment gateway system and identify at least four threats with their corresponding mitigations. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Differentiate between SAST, DAST, and IAST. A web application has 10,000 lines of code. The SAST tool reports 25 vulnerabilities, of which 8 are critical. After remediation, 4 critical vulnerabilities remain. Calculate the vulnerability density before and after remediation, and interpret the security posture. *(7 Marks)*

##### Model Solution — Part B(a) — STRIDE for Payment Gateway (7 Marks)

**STRIDE-per-Element Methodology (3 Marks)**:

STRIDE-per-Element is a structured threat-modelling technique in which each element of a Data Flow Diagram (DFD) — processes, data stores, data flows, and external entities — is systematically examined against the six STRIDE threat categories. The analyst iterates through every DFD node and asks: *"Can this element be spoofed, tampered, repudiated, disclosed, denied, or elevated?"*

The methodology follows four steps:
1. **Diagram the system** using a DFD with trust boundaries.
2. **Identify assets** (credit card data, transaction logs, PII).
3. **Apply STRIDE** to each element systematically.
4. **Document mitigations** for every identified threat.

**Applied STRIDE Analysis for Online Payment Gateway (4 Marks)**:

| Element | STRIDE Threat | Mitigation |
|---------|--------------|------------|
| Customer Browser | **S**poofing — Session hijacking via XSS | Use HttpOnly cookies, secure session tokens, MFA |
| Payment Form | **T**ampering — Hidden field manipulation | Server-side validation, HMAC signing of payload |
| API Endpoint | **R**epudiation — Customer denies transaction | Immutable audit logs with cryptographic timestamping |
| Database | **I**nformation Disclosure — SQL Injection | Parameterized queries, WAF rules, field-level encryption |
| Payment Server | **D**enial of Service — Flood attack | Rate limiting, CAPTCHA, CDN-based DDoS mitigation |
| Admin Console | **E**levation of Privilege — Vertical escalation | RBAC, principle of least privilege, MFA on admin |

*(2 marks for diagram/methodology, 4 marks for the four threats and mitigations, 1 mark for completeness.)*

##### Model Solution — Part B(b) — SAST vs DAST vs IAST & Vulnerability Density (7 Marks)

**SAST, DAST, and IAST Comparison (3 Marks)**:

| Aspect | SAST (Static) | DAST (Dynamic) | IAST (Interactive) |
|--------|--------------|----------------|---------------------|
| **Phase** | Build / Source Code | Running Application | Running with instrumentation |
| **Approach** | White-box (internal) | Black-box (external) | Hybrid (grey-box) |
| **Detects** | Injection, hard-coded secrets, XSS | Runtime issues, auth bypass | Both, with low false positives |
| **Tools** | SonarQube, Checkmarx | OWASP ZAP, Burp Suite | Contrast Security, Veracode |
| **Code Required** | Yes | No | Yes (instrumented) |
| **False Positives** | High | Medium | Low |
| **CI/CD Integration** | Excellent | Moderate | Excellent |

**Vulnerability Density Calculation (4 Marks)**:

Vulnerability density is the number of vulnerabilities per 1,000 lines of code (KLOC):

$$
\text{Vulnerability Density} = \frac{\text{Number of Vulnerabilities}}{\text{KLOC}}
$$

**Step 1: Compute density before remediation (1 Mark)**.

Total vulnerabilities = 25. Critical vulnerabilities = 8.

$$
\text{Density}_{\text{before (total)}} = \frac{25}{10} = 2.5 \text{ vulns / KLOC}
$$

$$
\text{Density}_{\text{before (critical)}} = \frac{8}{10} = 0.8 \text{ critical / KLOC}
$$

**Step 2: Compute density after remediation (1 Mark)**.

Remaining critical = 4. Assume non-critical vulnerabilities unchanged (25 - 8 = 17 non-critical).

$$
\text{Density}_{\text{after (critical)}} = \frac{4}{10} = 0.4 \text{ critical / KLOC}
$$

**Step 3: Compute reduction (1 Mark)**.

$$
\text{Reduction} = 0.8 - 0.4 = 0.4 \text{ critical / KLOC} \quad \text{i.e.,} \quad 50\% \text{ reduction}
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation (1 Mark)**.

The critical vulnerability density has been halved, indicating **significant security improvement**. However, the post-remediation critical density of 0.4/KLOC is still considered high by industry benchmarks (the OWASP benchmark target is < 0.1/KLOC). The development team must continue the remediation cycle until the critical density falls below **0.1 vulnerabilities per KLOC**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 1 — Secure Software Implementation: Last-Minute Revision Sheet**

- **Definition**: Secure software implementation is the integration of security into *every* SDLC phase, not a post-development activity.
- **CIA Triad**: Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability — the foundation of all security work.
- **SDL Phases (memorize in order)**: Training → Requirements → Design → Implementation → Verification → Release → Response.
- **SDLC vs SDL**: SDLC is generic; SDL is Microsoft's security-centric variant.
- **STRIDE**: 6 threat categories — Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, DoS, Elevation of Privilege.
- **DREAD**: 5 risk-rating factors (D, R, E, A, Di); average score, range 0–10. Classification: Low (<3), Medium (3–6), High (6–9), Critical (9–10).
- **OWASP Top 10**: The canonical list of web-application vulnerability classes — memorize at least the top 5 (Broken Access Control, Cryptographic Failures, Injection, Insecure Design, Security Misconfiguration).
- **Cryptographic Standards**: Use AES-256, SHA-256, TLS 1.3, bcrypt/Argon2. Avoid MD5 and SHA-1.
- **CVSS Range**: 0.0 (None) to 10.0 (Critical). 9.0+ is critical.
- **ALE Formula**: $\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$. ROSI formula justifies security spending.
- **Risk Matrix Formula**: $\text{Risk} = \text{Likelihood} \times \text{Impact}$.
- **Vulnerability Density Benchmark**: < 0.1 critical/KLOC is the target.
- **Defence-in-Depth**: Multiple independent security layers; failure of one layer does not result in total compromise.
- **Shift-Left Security**: Address security early in the development cycle to reduce remediation cost by 10× to 100×.
- **Principle of Least Privilege**: Grant only the minimum permissions required for a task.
- **Fail Securely**: Errors must not leak system information; always show generic error messages to end users.
- **SAST vs DAST vs IAST**: Static (white-box build-time), Dynamic (black-box runtime), Interactive (instrumented hybrid). IAST has the lowest false-positive rate.
- **Common Pitfalls in Exams**:
  1. Confusing SDLC and SDL.
  2. Swapping STRIDE and DREAD functions.
  3. Omitting the formula while writing the final answer.
  4. Writing one-word answers for 3-mark questions.
  5. Using outdated algorithms like MD5, SHA-1, or DES in cryptography questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deployment and Ongoing Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT418 - SECURITY ASSESSMENT AND RISK ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Deployment and Ongoing Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Deployment and Ongoing Management** in the context of Security Assessment and Risk Analysis refers to the systematic, post-assessment phase wherein recommended security controls, risk mitigations, and protective measures are operationalized within an organization's Information Technology (IT) and Operational Technology (OT) environments, followed by the continuous governance, monitoring, maintenance, and refinement activities required to sustain the desired security posture over the system's entire lifecycle.

According to the **NIST SP 800-37 Revision 2** (Risk Management Framework for Information Systems and Organizations), this phase is formally called the **Implement and Assess** stage transitioning into **Monitor**, while **ISO/IEC 27001:2022** Clause 8 (Operation) and Clause 9 (Performance Evaluation) describe it as the operationalization of the Information Security Management System (ISMS).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Deployment is the process of integrating validated security controls into the live production environment, while Ongoing Management is the continuous process of monitoring, measuring, reviewing, and improving those controls to address evolving threats, vulnerabilities, and business requirements.

## 1.2 Core Constituents of the Phase

The deployment and ongoing management phase typically encompasses the following five primary activities:

1. **Implementation of Security Controls** — translating assessment findings into deployed countermeasures.
2. **Configuration and Hardening** — applying baseline configurations, CIS Benchmarks, and Defense-in-Depth layering.
3. **Continuous Monitoring** — real-time and periodic observation of control effectiveness.
4. **Change and Patch Management** — controlled modification of systems to remediate new vulnerabilities.
5. **Review, Audit, and Recertification** — periodic validation against compliance standards and risk tolerance.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Real-World Analogy — The House Security System**

Think of **Security Assessment** as hiring a security consultant to inspect your house. The consultant produces a report saying: *"Install a 7-foot boundary wall, CCTV at four corners, a biometric door lock, and a 24/7 alarm monitoring service."*

**Deployment** is the actual construction phase: the wall is built, CCTV cameras are mounted and wired, the biometric lock is installed at the front door, and the alarm company is contracted. None of this matters if the lock is left open, the cameras face the sky, or the alarm is never switched on.

**Ongoing Management** is the lifelong responsibility: changing lock codes when a tenant leaves, replacing CCTV hard drives every 3 years, testing the alarm monthly, retraining the family on security protocols, and upgrading the wall height when a new, more agile threat (a thief who can jump 6 feet) emerges.

In cybersecurity, a vulnerability scan or penetration test (the "assessment") is useless if its findings remain as a PDF on a manager's desk. The true value of security work is realized **only when controls are deployed, operationalized, and continuously managed**.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry-Standard Quantitative Metrics Used in Ongoing Management:**
> - **MTTD (Mean Time to Detect):** average time to identify a security incident — measured in minutes/hours/days.
> - **MTTR (Mean Time to Respond/Remediate):** average time from detection to containment.
> - **SLA (Service Level Agreement) for patch deployment:** typically **14 days for critical**, **30 days for high**, and **90 days for medium** severity vulnerabilities (per CIS Critical Security Controls v8).
> - **RPO (Recovery Point Objective):** maximum tolerable data loss, expressed in time.
> - **RTO (Recovery Time Objective):** maximum tolerable downtime, expressed in time.

These five metrics — collectively the **"Five Metrics of Cyber Resilience"** — form the measurement backbone of any mature ongoing management program.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Lifecycle of a Security Control

The lifecycle of a security control, from assessment to retirement, follows a closed-loop, iterative cycle. KTU examiners frequently test the understanding of this loop. The standard lifecycle consists of seven stages:

1. **Identify Requirement** — derived from a risk assessment finding or compliance mandate.
2. **Design Solution** — selecting the appropriate control type (administrative, physical, or technical/logical).
3. **Implement (Deploy)** — installing, configuring, and integrating the control into the production environment.
4. **Validate / Verify** — confirming the control functions as designed (e.g., via vulnerability scan, penetration test, or control test).
5. **Operate and Monitor** — collecting logs, metrics, and indicators of effectiveness.
6. **Review and Improve** — periodic audit, gap analysis, and tuning.
7. **Retire or Replace** — decommissioning obsolete controls (e.g., retiring 3DES after discovering Sweet32 vulnerability).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** This loop is **continuous, not linear**. A control that has been deployed and validated must constantly feed monitoring data back into the next cycle of improvement. This is the essence of the **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)** Deming cycle adapted to information security.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" of Deployment

The **"Why"** of deployment is straightforward: an unmitigated risk is a liability. The **"How"**, however, requires methodical planning.

### 2.2.1 Pre-Deployment Phase
- **Stakeholder Alignment:** Obtain sign-off from business owners, IT operations, and compliance officers.
- **Dependency Analysis:** Identify upstream/downstream system dependencies that may be impacted.
- **Rollback Plan:** Define a documented procedure to revert changes if deployment fails.
- **Maintenance Window Selection:** Schedule deployment during low-traffic periods to minimize business disruption.

### 2.2.2 Deployment Phase
- **Phased / Pilot Rollout:** Deploy to a small subset of systems first (e.g., 5% of endpoints), validate behavior, then expand.
- **Configuration Baselines:** Apply CIS Benchmarks, DISA STIGs, or vendor-hardened images.
- **Change Management Documentation:** Every change must be logged in a ticketing/CMDB system with unique Change Request (CR) IDs.

### 2.2.3 Post-Deployment Phase
- **Validation Testing:** Re-run vulnerability scans, conduct penetration tests on the deployed control.
- **Effectiveness Measurement:** Confirm the control actually mitigates the original risk.
- **Knowledge Transfer:** Train SOC analysts, system administrators, and end-users.

## 2.3 The "Why" and "How" of Ongoing Management

Ongoing management addresses the dynamic nature of the threat landscape. A control that was effective yesterday may be bypassed today by a zero-day exploit. The three pillars of ongoing management are:

### 2.3.1 Continuous Monitoring
Continuous monitoring uses automated tools (SIEM, EDR, NDR) to collect and correlate security telemetry. The goal is to detect anomalous activity as close to real-time as feasible.

### 2.3.2 Incident Response Integration
A deployed control is only as good as the team's ability to respond when it triggers. Ongoing management includes maintaining a tested **Incident Response Plan (IRP)** aligned with **NIST SP 800-61 Rev. 2** phases: *Preparation → Detection & Analysis → Containment, Eradication & Recovery → Post-Incident Activity*.

### 2.3.3 Risk Re-Assessment
Risk is not static. Threats evolve, vulnerabilities are discovered, and asset values change. Periodic re-assessment (typically annually, or triggered by significant change) ensures the risk register remains current.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Description | Units / Range |
|---|-------------------|-------------------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | Mean Time to Detect | $\text{MTTD} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (T_{\text{detect},i} - T_{\text{occur},i})}{n}$ | Average time elapsed between incident occurrence and detection across *n* incidents. | Minutes, Hours, Days |
| 2 | Mean Time to Respond | $\text{MTTR} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (T_{\text{resolve},i} - T_{\text{detect},i})}{n}$ | Average time from detection to full remediation across *n* incidents. | Minutes, Hours, Days |
| 3 | Mean Time Between Failures | $\text{MTBF} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{\text{operational},i}}{n}$ | Reliability metric for security controls. | Hours |
| 4 | Risk Re-Assessment Score | $R_{t+1} = P_{t+1} \times I_{t+1}$ | Risk at time *t+1* = updated Probability × updated Impact. | Numeric (1–5 or 1–10 scale) |
| 5 | Patch Compliance Rate | $\text{PCR} = \frac{N_{\text{patched}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage of in-scope assets meeting the patch SLA. | Percentage (0–100%) |
| 6 | Control Coverage | $\text{CC} = \frac{N_{\text{controls\_implemented}}}{N_{\text{controls\_required}}} \times 100\%$ | Ratio of deployed controls to required controls. | Percentage (0–100%) |
| 7 | Annualized Loss Expectancy (Residual) | $\text{ALE}_{\text{residual}} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}_{\text{residual}}$ | Expected monetary loss per year after control deployment. | Currency units (USD, INR, etc.) |
| 8 | Return on Security Investment | $\text{ROSI} = \frac{\text{ALE}_{\text{pre}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{post}} - \text{Cost}_{\text{control}}}{\text{Cost}_{\text{control}}}$ | Financial justification for a deployed control. | Ratio (positive = beneficial) |
| 9 | Vulnerability Remediation Rate | $\text{VRR} = \frac{V_{\text{remediated}}}{V_{\text{discovered}}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage of discovered vulnerabilities fixed within a period. | Percentage (0–100%) |
| 10 | Security Posture Index (Composite) | $\text{SPI} = w_1 C + w_2 M + w_3 A$ | Weighted score of Compliance (*C*), Monitoring (*M*), Awareness (*A*). | Numeric (0–100) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Notation:** All absolute-value and conditional expressions in formulas above use the standard mathematical symbol $\vert$ (rendered via LaTeX), not the markdown table pipe character, to preserve table integrity.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In industry, the deployment and ongoing management phase bridges the gap between **paper security** and **operational security**. It is the phase where:
- A bank actually deploys the multi-factor authentication system recommended in its risk assessment.
- A hospital continuously monitors its MRI machines for ransomware indicators.
- A manufacturing plant updates the firmware of its Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs) after ICS-CERT publishes a new advisory.

Without rigorous ongoing management, the entire prior investment in assessment becomes a sunk cost.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Return on Security Investment (ROSI)

The ROSI formula is the most heavily tested quantitative concept in KTU risk analysis papers. Below is the exhaustive step-by-step derivation.

### Step 1: Define Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)
The Single Loss Expectancy is the monetary loss expected from a single occurrence of a risk event.

$$
\text{SLE} = \text{Asset Value (AV)} \times \text{Exposure Factor (EF)}
$$

Where:
- $\text{AV}$ = the total monetary value of the asset.
- $\text{EF}$ = the fraction of the asset value that would be lost in a single incident (expressed as a decimal, e.g., $0.6$ for a 60% loss).

### Step 2: Define Annualized Rate of Occurrence (ARO)
The ARO is the estimated number of times a particular risk is expected to occur per year.

$$
\text{ARO} \in \mathbb{R}^{\geq 0}, \quad \text{typical range: } 0.01 \text{ to } 100
$$

### Step 3: Calculate Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE) — Pre-Control
$$
\text{ALE}_{\text{pre}} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}_{\text{pre}}
$$

This represents the expected annual monetary loss *before* any control is deployed.

### Step 4: Estimate the Risk Reduction (Mitigation Effectiveness)
The Mitigation Effectiveness (ME) is the percentage of risk that the control is expected to eliminate. For example, an MFA system might reduce account compromise risk by 95% (i.e., $\text{ME} = 0.95$).

The Residual ARO is then:

$$
\text{ARO}_{\text{post}} = \text{ARO}_{\text{pre}} \times (1 - \text{ME})
$$

### Step 5: Calculate Annualized Loss Expectancy — Post-Control
$$
\text{ALE}_{\text{post}} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}_{\text{post}}
$$

### Step 6: Calculate the Net Benefit
$$
\text{Net Benefit} = (\text{ALE}_{\text{pre}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{post}}) - \text{Cost}_{\text{control}}
$$

### Step 7: Express as a Ratio (ROSI)

$$
\text{ROSI} = \frac{\text{ALE}_{\text{pre}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{post}} - \text{Cost}_{\text{control}}}{\text{Cost}_{\text{control}}}
$$

### Step 8: Decision Rule
$$
\text{ROSI} > 0 \implies \text{Control is financially justified.}
$$

$$
\text{ROSI} \leq 0 \implies \text{Control is not financially justified (or alternative control required).}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Exhaustive)

**Problem (KTU Board Exam Style):**
> An e-commerce company has identified the risk of a data breach on its customer database. The asset is valued at ₹50,00,000. A single breach event would compromise 70% of the records. Historically, such breaches occur 0.5 times per year. The proposed control is a Web Application Firewall (WAF) costing ₹8,00,000 per year, expected to reduce breach probability by 80%. Calculate the ROSI.

### Solution — Exhaustive Step-by-Step

**Given:**
- $\text{AV} = 50{,}00{,}000$
- $\text{EF} = 0.70$
- $\text{ARO}_{\text{pre}} = 0.5$
- $\text{Cost}_{\text{control}} = 8{,}00{,}000$
- $\text{ME} = 0.80$

**Step 1: Compute SLE**

$$
\text{SLE} = \text{AV} \times \text{EF} = 50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.70
$$

$$
\text{SLE} = 35{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2: Compute ALE (pre-control)**

$$
\text{ALE}_{\text{pre}} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}_{\text{pre}} = 35{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5
$$

$$
\text{ALE}_{\text{pre}} = 17{,}50{,}000
$$

**Step 3: Compute Residual ARO**

$$
\text{ARO}_{\text{post}} = 0.5 \times (1 - 0.80) = 0.5 \times 0.20
$$

$$
\text{ARO}_{\text{post}} = 0.10
$$

**Step 4: Compute ALE (post-control)**

$$
\text{ALE}_{\text{post}} = 35{,}00{,}000 \times 0.10 = 3{,}50{,}000
$$

**Step 5: Compute Net Benefit**

$$
\text{Net Benefit} = (17{,}50{,}000 - 3{,}50{,}000) - 8{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Net Benefit} = 14{,}00{,}000 - 8{,}00{,}000 = 6{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 6: Compute ROSI**

$$
\text{ROSI} = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000}
$$

$$
\text{ROSI} = 0.75 \quad \text{or} \quad 75\%
$$

**Conclusion:** Since $\text{ROSI} = 0.75 > 0$, deploying the WAF is financially justified, yielding ₹0.75 of net benefit for every ₹1 invested.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Pre-Deployment vs Post-Deployment vs Ongoing Management

| Dimension | Pre-Deployment (Assessment) | Deployment | Ongoing Management |
|-----------|----------------------------|------------|---------------------|
| **Primary Goal** | Identify and prioritize risks | Operationalize controls | Sustain and improve control efficacy |
| **Key Activity** | Risk analysis, threat modeling | Installation, configuration, testing | Monitoring, patching, re-assessment |
| **Stakeholders** | Risk analysts, CISO, business owners | IT operations, network admins, SOC | SOC analysts, compliance team, end-users |
| **Time Horizon** | Point-in-time (e.g., quarterly) | Days to weeks | Continuous (24/7) |
| **Key Metric** | Inherent Risk Score | Control Coverage (CC) | MTTD, MTTR, Patch Compliance Rate |
| **Failure Mode** | Underestimating risk | Misconfiguration, downtime | Alert fatigue, control drift |
| **Documentation** | Risk register | Change requests, baseline configs | Audit logs, incident reports, KPI dashboards |
| **Standard Reference** | NIST SP 800-30 | NIST SP 800-128 (Configuration Management) | NIST SP 800-137, ISO 27001 Clause 9 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Continuous Security Management Lifecycle

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the closed-loop lifecycle of deployment and ongoing management, as adapted from the **NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF) 2.0** and the **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)** model.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Risk via Assessment] --> B[Select and Design Control]
    B --> C[Deploy Control to Production]
    C --> D[Validate Control Effectiveness]
    D --> E[Operate and Monitor Continuously]
    E --> F{Incident or Anomaly Detected?}
    F -->|No| G[Periodic Audit and Review]
    F -->|Yes| H[Incident Response and Containment]
    H --> I[Post Incident Lessons Learned]
    I --> J[Update Risk Register]
    G --> J
    J --> K{Control Adequate?}
    K -->|Yes| E
    K -->|No| B
```

**Interpretation of the Flow:**
- The cycle begins with **risk identification**, moves through **design**, **deployment**, and **validation**, then enters a continuous **operate-and-monitor** state.
- A detection event triggers an **incident response** branch, leading to lessons learned and a risk register update.
- Periodic audits serve as a scheduled checkpoint that may also force the cycle back to the design phase.

## 4.2 Architecture Flow: Layered Defense in Ongoing Management

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **Defense-in-Depth** architecture used during ongoing management, showing how multiple security layers continuously feed telemetry into a central Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) system.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph perimeterLayer["Perimeter Layer"]
        P1[Firewall]
        P2[IDS IPS]
        P3[WAF]
    end

    subgraph networkLayer["Network Layer"]
        N1[VLAN Segmentation]
        N2[NDR Network Detection]
        N3[802.1X NAC]
    end

    subgraph endpointLayer["Endpoint Layer"]
        E1[EDR Agent]
        E2[Antivirus]
        E3[Host Firewall]
    end

    subgraph dataLayer["Data Layer"]
        D1[DLP Data Loss Prevention]
        D2[Encryption at Rest]
        D3[Database Activity Monitor]
    end

    subgraph analyticsLayer["Analytics and Response Layer"]
        S1[SIEM Correlation Engine]
        S2[SOC Analyst]
        S3[SOAR Automation]
    end

    perimeterLayer --> analyticsLayer
    networkLayer --> analyticsLayer
    endpointLayer --> analyticsLayer
    dataLayer --> analyticsLayer
    S2 --> R[Response Actions]
    S3 --> R
```

**Interpretation:**
- Telemetry from the **perimeter, network, endpoint, and data layers** is collected, normalized, and fed into the **SIEM**.
- The **SOC** (Security Operations Center) reviews correlated alerts, while **SOAR** (Security Orchestration, Automation, and Response) automates routine response actions.
- This architecture embodies the principle of **"Visibility Across All Layers"**, a key concept in ongoing management.

## 4.3 Process Diagram: Change and Patch Management Workflow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the formal change and patch management process used during ongoing management, compliant with **ITIL 4 Change Enablement** practices.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    req[New Vulnerability or Change Request] --> cat{Categorize}
    cat -->|Standard| auto[Auto Approve and Deploy]
    cat -->|Normal| cab[Submit to CAB Change Advisory Board]
    cat -->|Emergency| em[Emergency Change Procedure]
    cab --> rev[Review Impact and Risk]
    rev --> appr{Approved?}
    appr -->|No| rej[Reject and Document]
    appr -->|Yes| sched[Schedule Maintenance Window]
    sched --> pilot[Pilot Deploy 5 Percent of Assets]
    pilot --> val{Validation Successful?}
    val -->|No| roll[Rollback and Investigate]
    val -->|Yes| full[Full Rollout to 100 Percent]
    full --> doc[Document and Close Change]
    auto --> doc
    em --> doc
    rej --> doc
    roll --> doc
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define "Deployment" in the context of security control implementation. List any two challenges encountered during deployment.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Deployment in security assessment and risk analysis refers to the process of operationalizing and integrating the recommended security controls (administrative, physical, and technical) into the live production environment of an organization, following the risk assessment phase.
- **Two Challenges (1 mark):**
  1. **Compatibility issues** with legacy systems that may not support modern security controls.
  2. **Resistance to change** from end-users and operational teams.
  3. *Alternative:* Budget constraints, lack of skilled personnel, business disruption during deployment.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the significance of "Continuous Monitoring" in the ongoing management of security controls?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Continuous monitoring is the ongoing, real-time observation of an organization's information security controls, networks, and systems to detect anomalies, policy violations, and security incidents. Its significance lies in:
1. **Early threat detection:** Reduces the Mean Time to Detect (MTTD) and limits damage.
2. **Compliance assurance:** Maintains continuous adherence to standards like ISO 27001, PCI-DSS, and GDPR.
3. **Control validation:** Confirms that deployed controls remain effective against evolving threats.
4. **Informed decision-making:** Provides data-driven evidence for re-prioritizing security investments.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question Choice A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]`

**Question A (14 Marks):**
**(a)** Describe in detail the **phases of security control deployment** with reference to the Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA) cycle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A financial organization has identified the risk of unauthorized access to its core banking server. The server is valued at ₹2,00,00,000, and a single breach would compromise 80% of the data. The breach is estimated to occur 0.4 times per year. The proposed control is a biometric access system costing ₹12,00,000 annually, expected to reduce breach probability by 90%. **Calculate the SLE, ALE (pre), ALE (post), and ROSI.** Is the control financially justified? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (a) — 7 marks

The deployment of security controls can be mapped onto the **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** Deming cycle, a continuous improvement framework adopted by **ISO 27001** and **NIST RMF**.

**1. PLAN (1.5 marks):**
- Conduct risk assessment to identify threats and vulnerabilities.
- Select appropriate controls from frameworks like NIST SP 800-53 or ISO 27001 Annex A.
- Define deployment objectives, scope, budget, and timeline.
- Identify dependencies, stakeholders, and required resources.

**2. DO (2 marks):**
- Execute the deployment in a phased manner: pilot → partial → full rollout.
- Apply configuration baselines (CIS Benchmarks, DISA STIGs).
- Document changes in the Configuration Management Database (CMDB).
- Conduct end-user and administrator training.

**3. CHECK (2 marks):**
- Validate control effectiveness via vulnerability scans, penetration tests, and control tests.
- Collect performance metrics (MTTD, MTTR, false-positive rates).
- Compare measured performance against predefined KPIs.

**4. ACT (1.5 marks):**
- Remediate identified gaps and non-conformities.
- Update the risk register and control documentation.
- Feed lessons learned into the next planning cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> The PDCA cycle is **iterative**, ensuring that security controls evolve in step with the changing threat landscape.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (b) — 7 marks

**Given:**
- $\text{AV} = 2{,}00{,}00{,}000$
- $\text{EF} = 0.80$
- $\text{ARO}_{\text{pre}} = 0.4$
- $\text{Cost}_{\text{control}} = 12{,}00{,}000$
- $\text{ME} = 0.90$

**Step 1: Calculate SLE (1 mark)**

$$
\text{SLE} = 2{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.80 = 1{,}60{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2: Calculate ALE (pre-control) (1 mark)**

$$
\text{ALE}_{\text{pre}} = 1{,}60{,}00{,}000 \times 0.4 = 64{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Residual ARO (1 mark)**

$$
\text{ARO}_{\text{post}} = 0.4 \times (1 - 0.90) = 0.4 \times 0.10 = 0.04
$$

**Step 4: Calculate ALE (post-control) (1 mark)**

$$
\text{ALE}_{\text{post}} = 1{,}60{,}00{,}000 \times 0.04 = 6{,}40{,}000
$$

**Step 5: Calculate Net Benefit (1 mark)**

$$
\text{Net Benefit} = (64{,}00{,}000 - 6{,}40{,}000) - 12{,}00{,}000 = 57{,}60{,}000 - 12{,}00{,}000 = 45{,}60{,}000
$$

**Step 6: Calculate ROSI (1 mark)**

$$
\text{ROSI} = \frac{45{,}60{,}000}{12{,}00{,}000} = 3.80 \quad \text{or} \quad 380\%
$$

**Step 7: Conclusion (1 mark)**
Since $\text{ROSI} = 3.80 > 0$, the biometric access system is **strongly financially justified**, yielding ₹3.80 of net benefit for every ₹1 invested.

---

### Question Choice B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**Question B (14 Marks):**
**(a)** Explain the concept of **ongoing management** of security controls. Discuss the role of **continuous monitoring**, **patch management**, and **periodic audits** in sustaining an organization's security posture. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An IT company has identified a ransomware risk on its file servers. Asset value = ₹80,00,000. Exposure factor = 0.50. ARO (pre-control) = 0.8. The proposed control is an EDR (Endpoint Detection and Response) solution costing ₹6,00,000 per year with a mitigation effectiveness of 75%. **Calculate ALE (pre), ALE (post), Net Benefit, and ROSI.** Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B (a) — 7 marks

**Ongoing Management Definition (2 marks):**
Ongoing management of security controls is the continuous, post-deployment process of monitoring, maintaining, updating, and improving security controls to ensure they remain effective against evolving threats and continue to meet the organization's risk tolerance and compliance obligations.

**Three Pillars:**

**1. Continuous Monitoring (2 marks):**
- Uses SIEM, EDR, NDR, and log aggregation tools to provide real-time visibility.
- Detects anomalies, policy violations, and indicators of compromise (IoCs).
- Generates metrics: MTTD, MTTR, alert volume, false-positive ratio.
- Enables rapid incident response and reduces the attacker's dwell time.

**2. Patch Management (1.5 marks):**
- Systematic process of identifying, testing, and deploying software/firmware updates.
- Adheres to SLAs: critical patches within 14 days, high within 30 days, medium within 90 days (per CIS v8).
- Uses a phased rollout: pilot → testing → production deployment → validation.
- Closes known vulnerabilities and reduces the attack surface.

**3. Periodic Audits (1.5 marks):**
- Scheduled assessments (internal and external) to validate control effectiveness.
- Identifies "control drift" — gradual degradation of security posture over time.
- Ensures continued compliance with standards like ISO 27001, SOC 2, PCI-DSS.
- Generates executive reports for governance and board-level visibility.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B (b) — 7 marks

**Given:**
- $\text{AV} = 80{,}00{,}000$
- $\text{EF} = 0.50$
- $\text{ARO}_{\text{pre}} = 0.8$
- $\text{Cost}_{\text{control}} = 6{,}00{,}000$
- $\text{ME} = 0.75$

**Step 1: Calculate SLE (1 mark)**

$$
\text{SLE} = 80{,}00{,}000 \times 0.50 = 40{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2: Calculate ALE (pre-control) (1 mark)**

$$
\text{ALE}_{\text{pre}} = 40{,}00{,}000 \times 0.8 = 32{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Residual ARO (1 mark)**

$$
\text{ARO}_{\text{post}} = 0.8 \times (1 - 0.75) = 0.8 \times 0.25 = 0.20
$$

**Step 4: Calculate ALE (post-control) (1 mark)**

$$
\text{ALE}_{\text{post}} = 40{,}00{,}000 \times 0.20 = 8{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 5: Calculate Net Benefit (1 mark)**

$$
\text{Net Benefit} = (32{,}00{,}000 - 8{,}00{,}000) - 6{,}00{,}000 = 24{,}00{,}000 - 6{,}00{,}000 = 18{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 6: Calculate ROSI (1 mark)**

$$
\text{ROSI} = \frac{18{,}00{,}000}{6{,}00{,}000} = 3.00 \quad \text{or} \quad 300\%
$$

**Step 7: Comment (1 mark)**
Since $\text{ROSI} = 3.00 > 0$, deploying the EDR solution is **highly financially justified**, generating ₹3 of net benefit per ₹1 invested. The control substantially reduces residual risk and should be approved for immediate deployment, with ongoing management to maintain its effectiveness.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit confusion in ROSI:** Students frequently forget to subtract the control cost from the loss reduction before dividing. Correct numerator is $(\text{ALE}_{\text{pre}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{post}}) - \text{Cost}$, not just $(\text{ALE}_{\text{pre}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{post}})$.
> 2. **Mixing up ARO and SLE:** ARO is a frequency (per year); SLE is a one-time monetary value. Confusing them yields nonsense units.
> 3. **Expressing ROSI as a percentage without context:** Always state the decision rule: $\text{ROSI} > 0 \implies$ justified.
> 4. **Skipping the conclusion line:** Examiners specifically allocate 1 mark for an explicit, well-reasoned conclusion. Never end a numerical answer with just a number.
> 5. **Writing "ROSI is good" or "ROSI is high":** Always quantify it. Write "ROSI = 3.80, which exceeds the break-even point of 0, so the control is financially justified."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Deployment and Ongoing Management**

- **Deployment** = putting controls into live operation; **Ongoing Management** = sustaining and improving them.
- The **PDCA cycle** (Plan-Do-Check-Act) is the foundational management model.
- **Pre-deployment** activities: stakeholder sign-off, dependency analysis, rollback planning, maintenance window selection.
- **Deployment** activities: phased rollout, configuration baselines (CIS/STIGs), change documentation.
- **Post-deployment validation** is non-negotiable — re-scan, re-test, re-pentest.
- **Continuous monitoring** uses SIEM, EDR, NDR; tracks **MTTD** and **MTTR**.
- **Patch management SLAs (CIS v8):** Critical = 14 days, High = 30 days, Medium = 90 days.
- **Three pillars of ongoing management:** Continuous Monitoring, Incident Response Integration, Risk Re-Assessment.
- **Key formulas to memorize:**
  - $\text{SLE} = \text{AV} \times \text{EF}$
  - $\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$
  - $\text{ROSI} = \dfrac{\text{ALE}_{\text{pre}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{post}} - \text{Cost}_{\text{control}}}{\text{Cost}_{\text{control}}}$
  - $\text{ROSI} > 0 \implies$ control is financially justified.
- **Compliance standards** frequently referenced: ISO 27001, NIST SP 800-37 R2, NIST CSF 2.0, CIS Critical Security Controls v8, PCI-DSS, GDPR.
- **Defense-in-Depth** is the architectural foundation: perimeter, network, endpoint, data, analytics layers.
- **Control drift** is the silent killer — periodic audits are the only way to catch it.
- The entire deployment and management phase is **iterative and continuous**, never a one-time project.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Software Design and an Introduction to Hierarchical Design Representations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAIT418 - SECURITY ASSESSMENT AND RISK ANALYSIS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Software Design and an Introduction to Hierarchical Design Representations # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Software Design — Formal Definition **Software Design** is the disciplined engineering process of defining the **architecture**, **components**, **interfaces**, **data structures**, **modules**, and **algorithms** of a software system to satisfy a specified set of functional, non-functional, and security requirements. It is the critical bridge between *requirements analysis* and *implementation/coding*. In the context of **Security Assessment and Risk Analysis (PEAIT418)**, software design is treated as the **first line of defense**. A flawed design propagates vulnerabilities that no amount of post-deployment patching can fully remediate — a principle famously captured by the security axiom: > "**Security cannot be tested in — it must be designed in.**" ## 1.2 Hierarchical Design Representations — Formal Definition **Hierarchical Design Representations (HDR)** are structured, multi-level graphical or semi-formal models that decompose a complex software system into nested, progressively detailed abstractions. Each level hides implementation details of the level below it (the **abstraction principle**) and exposes only the **essential features** relevant to that level of analysis. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes four canonical levels of hierarchy: | Level | Name | Focus | Security Lens | |---|---|---|---| | **L0** | System Context | External entities, trust boundaries | Threat surface mapping | | **L1** | Architectural | Subsystems, modules, data flows | Architectural risk analysis (ARA) | | **L2** | Detailed Design | Classes, procedures, APIs | Code-level vulnerability prediction | | **L3** | Implementation | Source code, configuration | Static/dynamic security testing | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Every hierarchical level is a *separate attack surface* and must be subjected to a *distinct risk-treatment strategy*. Examiners frequently ask students to map STRIDE categories to these levels. ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Building Blueprint Imagine you are commissioning a **10-storey hospital** that must be fire-safe, earthquake-resistant, and accessible. - A **city-planner view** only shows the building's footprint, road access, and neighboring properties → this is the **System Context (L0)**. - A **structural engineer view** shows load-bearing walls, lift shafts, and floor slabs → this is the **Architectural Level (L1)**. - A **plumber/electrician view** shows pipe routing, switchboard wiring, and water-tank capacities → this is the **Detailed Design (L2)**. - A **construction worker's view** shows every brick, screw, and weld → this is the **Implementation (L3)**. Each view is a *hierarchical representation*. None alone is sufficient, but each hides irrelevant detail and exposes what matters at that level. **A security auditor walks the building at every level** because a fire-hazard in the wiring (L3) is just as deadly as a missing fire-escape in the blueprint (L0). > [!NOTE] > In software security, this principle is formalized as **defense-in-depth**: never rely on a single level of control. If an attacker breaches L3, the L2 design should contain the blast radius. ## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Security Assessment **Three industrial data points** (all bolded for emphasis): - **NIST SP 800-160** states that **over 70%** of exploited vulnerabilities originate from design-level flaws, not coding errors. - **IBM's 2023 Cost of a Data Breach Report** identifies design-related flaws as contributing to an average breach cost of **USD 4.45 million**. - **CWE/SANS Top 25** lists *Improper Restriction of Operations within Bounds of a Memory Buffer* and *Improper Neutralization of Input* — both **design-categorization weaknesses** — as the most dangerous classes. Hierarchical design representations are the *lingua franca* through which security analysts, designers, and auditors **communicate risk** without ambiguity. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Foundational Design Principles (Grady Booch & Object-Oriented Canon) The KTU 2024 syllabus draws on the classical design principles codified by **Grady Booch**, **Bertrand Meyer**, and the **Gang of Four (GoF)**. Each principle is restated below with its **security implication**, which is what examiners reward. ### 2.1.1 Modularity A system is decomposed into *cohesive*, *loosely-coupled* modules. Each module has a single responsibility (**SRP**). - **Why?** A vulnerability in one module cannot freely propagate to others. - **Security utility:** Limits the *blast radius* of a compromise. ### 2.1.2 Abstraction Each module exposes only its *essential interface*; internal complexity is hidden (**Information Hiding**, **Parnas, 1972**). - **Why?** Attackers see a smaller, more tractable attack surface. - **Security utility:** Reduces the cognitive load of a threat model. ### 2.1.3 Encapsulation State and behavior are bundled; external access is mediated by *access modifiers* (`public`, `private`, `protected`). - **Why?** Direct tampering with internal state is prevented at the language level. ### 2.1.4 Separation of Concerns (SoC) Cross-cutting concerns (logging, authentication, encryption) are decoupled from business logic, often via **AOP (Aspect-Oriented Programming)** or **middleware**. - **Why?** Security controls can be applied uniformly and audited independently. ### 2.1.5 The Five Other Principles (Quick Reference) - **Open/Closed Principle (OCP):** Open for extension, closed for modification → reduces the *regression attack surface*. - **Liskov Substitution (LSP):** Subtypes must be substitutable → prevents *type-confusion vulnerabilities*. - **Dependency Inversion (DIP):** Depend on abstractions, not concretions → enables *security testing via mocks*. - **Interface Segregation (ISP):** Many specific interfaces > one general → smaller attack surface per interface. - **Don't Repeat Yourself (DRY):** Single source of truth → fewer *inconsistent-validations* bugs. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examination Tip (KTU 2024):** When asked to "explain how design principles support security", always anchor your answer in the **principle → threat mitigated** mapping. A generic "modularity is good" answer fetches 1 mark; "modularity contains the blast radius of an SQLi attack in the data-access layer" fetches full marks. ## 2.2 The Hierarchy of Design Representations — A Layered Model The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to recognize **four canonical representation styles**, each used at a different hierarchy level. ### 2.2.1 Context-Level Diagram (Level 0) - **Notation:** Single labeled rectangle for the system, surrounding ovals for external entities, arrows for data flows. - **Security purpose:** Identify *trust boundaries* and *external threat actors*. - **Example:** An *Online Banking System* context diagram shows the *Customer*, *Payment Gateway*, *Regulator* as external entities. ### 2.2.2 Data Flow Diagram (DFD) — Level 1 - **Notation:** Circles (processes), open rectangles (data stores), arrows (data flows), squares (external entities). - **Levels:** DFD-L0 (context), DFD-L1 (sub-system), DFD-L2 (module), DFD-L3 (function). - **Security purpose:** **STRIDE-per-Element** threat modeling (Microsoft methodology). Each element is analyzed for *Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, Denial of Service, Elevation of Privilege*. ### 2.2.3 Structure Chart — Level 2 - **Notation:** Rectangles (modules), filled circles (data couples), open circles (control couples), arrows (coupling direction). - **Security purpose:** Identify **high-coupling modules** that, if compromised, cascade damage. ### 2.2.4 Object-Oriented Design Artifacts — Level 3 - **Notation:** UML class diagrams, sequence diagrams, statecharts, deployment diagrams. - **Security purpose:** Model *authentication flows*, *authorization states*, and *trust transitions*. ## 2.3 Hierarchical Decomposition — Mathematical Formalism Let a system $S$ be recursively defined as a *composition* of subsystems: $$S = \{S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_n\}$$ Each subsystem $S_i$ further decomposes as: $$S_i = \{S_{i,1}, S_{i,2}, \ldots, S_{i,n_i}\}$$ The decomposition continues until **atomic modules** (those that cannot be meaningfully subdivided for the analysis at hand) are reached. The **depth of decomposition** is denoted $d$, and the **breadth** at level $k$ is denoted $b_k$. The total number of nodes in a balanced $b$-ary tree of depth $d$ is: $$N(b, d) = \sum_{k=0}^{d} b^k = \frac{b^{d+1} - 1}{b - 1}, \quad b \geq 2$$ This formula lets a security analyst **estimate the size of the threat-modelling effort** for a given design. ## 2.4 Risk Propagation Through the Hierarchy For a hierarchical design, the **aggregate risk** at level $k$ is computed from the risks of its children. KTU 2024 examiners expect familiarity with the following canonical formulas. ### 2.4.1 Single-Loss Expectancy (SLE) The monetary loss from a single occurrence of a threat: $$SLE = AV \times EF$$ where $AV$ = **Asset Value** and $EF$ = **Exposure Factor** (fraction of asset destroyed, $0 \leq EF \leq 1$). ### 2.4.2 Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE) Expected annual monetary loss: $$ALE = SLE \times ARO$$ where $ARO$ = **Annualized Rate of Occurrence** (events per year). ### 2.4.3 Aggregate Risk at Hierarchy Level $k$ For a parent module $P$ with children $C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_m$, the **worst-case aggregate risk** (assuming independent child failures) is: $$R_{\text{agg}, P} = 1 - \prod_{j=1}^{m} \left(1 - R_{C_j}\right)$$ where $R_{C_j} \in [0, 1]$ is the normalized risk probability of child $j$. ### 2.4.4 Attack Surface Metric The **attack surface** of a system is approximated as: $$AS = \sum_{i} (w_i \times c_i)$$ where $w_i$ is the *weight* (impact) of entry/exit point $i$ and $c_i$ is the *channel capacity* (ease of exploitation). Hierarchical decomposition lets us compute $AS$ at each level and **sum vertically**. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > The following table is **exam-ready** and consolidates every equation required for Module 2 problems. | # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Domain | Security Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Total nodes in $b$-ary tree | $N = \dfrac{b^{d+1}-1}{b-1}$ | $b \geq 2$, $d \in \mathbb{N}$ | Estimate threat-model size | | 2 | Single-Loss Expectancy | $SLE = AV \times EF$ | $EF \in [0,1]$ | Loss per incident | | 3 | Annual Loss Expectancy | $ALE = SLE \times ARO$ | $ARO \in \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ | Annualized loss | | 4 | Aggregate risk (independent) | $R_{\text{agg}} = 1 - \prod (1 - R_j)$ | $R_j \in [0,1]$ | Hierarchical risk roll-up | | 5 | Attack surface | $AS = \sum w_i c_i$ | $w_i, c_i \geq 0$ | Comparative design analysis | | 6 | Return on Security Investment | $ROSI = \dfrac{ALE_{\text{before}} - ALE_{\text{after}} - \text{Cost}}{\text{Cost}}$ | Monetary | Justify design controls | | 7 | Coupling metric | $C = \dfrac{\text{inter-module links}}{\text{intra-module links}}$ | $C \in [0, \infty)$ | Predict cascade risk | | 8 | Cohesion metric (LCOM) | $LCOM = \dfrac{P - Q}{\max(0, P - Q)}$ | $P$, $Q$ = method-pair counts | Predict defect density | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pitfall:** Do not use the vertical pipe `|` inside Markdown tables — LaTeX `\vert` or `\mid` is used above. Examiners deduct marks for broken table rendering in answer sheets. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automotive (ISO/SAE 21434):** Vehicle E/E architectures are hierarchically decomposed into *domains*, *ECUs*, *basic software modules*. Security risk (CAL — *Cybersecurity Assurance Level*) is computed per node and rolled up. - **Aviation (DO-326A):** Air-ground systems are modeled as hierarchical DFDs; *Security Level of Concern (SLOC)* is assigned at each level. - **Healthcare (IEC 80001-2-2):** Medical-device networks use hierarchical risk matrices. - **Banking (PCI-DSS v4.0):** Cardholder Data Environments are decomposed into *zones* and *conduits* — essentially a hierarchical representation. In every domain, the **hierarchical representation is the legal and contractual artifact** that auditors sign against. It is not academic decoration; it is the *evidence base* of compliance. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Nodes in a $b$-ary Hierarchical Tree **Problem (KTU-style):** A security analyst is threat-modelling a system decomposed into a 3-ary hierarchical tree of depth 4. Compute the total number of nodes to be analyzed. ### Step 1 — Restate the parameters From the problem statement: $$b = 3, \quad d = 4$$ ### Step 2 — Substitute into the closed-form formula The geometric series sum gives: $$N(b, d) = \sum_{k=0}^{d} b^k = \frac{b^{d+1} - 1}{b - 1}$$ ### Step 3 — Compute $b^{d+1}$ $$b^{d+1} = 3^{4+1} = 3^5 = 243$$ ### Step 4 — Compute the numerator $$b^{d+1} - 1 = 243 - 1 = 242$$ ### Step 5 — Compute the denominator $$b - 1 = 3 - 1 = 2$$ ### Step 6 — Final division $$N(3, 4) = \frac{242}{2} = 121$$ **Interpretation:** The analyst must examine **121 distinct nodes**. If each node takes 10 minutes to model, the total effort is approximately **20 hours**. This justifies procurement of automated threat-modelling tools. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hierarchical Aggregate Risk Roll-up **Problem (KTU-style):** A web application is decomposed into three subsystems: **Authentication** ($R_1 = 0.3$), **Business Logic** ($R_2 = 0.2$), and **Data Layer** ($R_3 = 0.5$). Assuming independent compromise, compute the aggregate risk and determine the residual risk after applying a control that halves the Data Layer risk. ### Step 1 — Recall the aggregate-risk formula $$R_{\text{agg}} = 1 - \prod_{j=1}^{m} \left(1 - R_{C_j}\right)$$ ### Step 2 — Substitute the original risks $$R_{\text{agg, original}} = 1 - (1 - 0.3)(1 - 0.2)(1 - 0.5)$$ ### Step 3 — Compute each survival probability $$1 - 0.3 = 0.7, \quad 1 - 0.2 = 0.8, \quad 1 - 0.5 = 0.5$$ ### Step 4 — Multiply the survival probabilities $$0.7 \times 0.8 = 0.56$$ $$0.56 \times 0.5 = 0.28$$ ### Step 5 — Compute the aggregate $$R_{\text{agg, original}} = 1 - 0.28 = 0.72$$ ### Step 6 — Apply the control (Data Layer risk halved) $$R_3' = 0.5 \times 0.5 = 0.25$$ ### Step 7 — Recompute the aggregate $$R_{\text{agg, residual}} = 1 - (0.7)(0.8)(1 - 0.25)$$ $$R_{\text{agg, residual}} = 1 - (0.7)(0.8)(0.75)$$ $$= 1 - 0.42 = 0.58$$ ### Step 8 — Compute the risk reduction $$\Delta R = 0.72 - 0.58 = 0.14$$ A **14 percentage-point reduction** in aggregate risk — substantial, demonstrating the value of a single targeted control. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Return on Security Investment (ROSI) **Problem (KTU-style):** A bank is considering deploying a Web Application Firewall (WAF) at a cost of **INR 25,00,000**. Before the WAF, the Annual Loss Expectancy from web attacks is **INR 1,20,00,000**. The WAF is expected to reduce the ALE to **INR 30,00,000**. Compute the ROSI. ### Step 1 — State the formula $$ROSI = \frac{ALE_{\text{before}} - ALE_{\text{after}} - \text{Cost}}{\text{Cost}}$$ ### Step 2 — Substitute the values $$ROSI = \frac{1{,}20{,}00{,}000 - 30{,}00{,}000 - 25{,}00{,}000}{25{,}00{,}000}$$ ### Step 3 — Compute the numerator $$1{,}20{,}00{,}000 - 30{,}00{,}000 = 90{,}00{,}000$$ $$90{,}00{,}000 - 25{,}00{,}000 = 65{,}00{,}000$$ ### Step 4 — Compute the ratio $$ROSI = \frac{65{,}00{,}000}{25{,}00{,}000} = 2.6$$ ### Step 5 — Interpret the result $$ROSI = 2.6 = 260\%$$ **Interpretation:** For every **INR 1** spent on the WAF, the bank saves **INR 2.60** in net expected loss. The investment is **strongly justified** — KTU 2024 expects students to comment on the *decision rule*: accept the control if $ROSI > 0$. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Design Principle → Security Threat Mapping | Design Principle | Predicted Threat Mitigated | Hierarchical Level Applied | Residual Risk after Application | |---|---|---|---| | Modularity | Cross-module privilege escalation | L1 (Architectural) | Low | | Abstraction | API misuse, prototype pollution | L2 (Detailed Design) | Low–Medium | | Encapsulation | Direct memory/state tampering | L3 (Implementation) | Very Low | | Separation of Concerns | Bypass of audit logging | L0 → L1 | Low | | Open/Closed Principle | Regression-introduced backdoors | L3 (Implementation) | Medium | | Liskov Substitution | Type confusion (CWE-843) | L2 (Detailed Design) | Low | | Dependency Inversion | Insecure-third-party coupling | L1 (Architectural) | Medium | | Interface Segregation | Excess-privilege enumeration | L2 (Detailed Design) | Low | | DRY | Inconsistent input validation | L3 (Implementation) | Medium | | Hierarchical Decomposition | Unbounded complexity in threat model | L0 → L3 | Low (effort-bounded) | This matrix is the *single most valuable 14-mark answer scaffold* in the KTU 2024 syllabus for Module 2. --- ## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Coupling Metric & Cascade Risk **Problem (KTU-style):** Module $A$ has 8 intra-module links and 12 inter-module links. Module $B$ has 20 intra-module links and 5 inter-module links. Compute the coupling metric for each and predict which is more vulnerable to cascade risk. ### Step 1 — State the coupling formula $$C = \frac{\text{inter-module links}}{\text{intra-module links}}$$ ### Step 2 — Compute for Module A $$C_A = \frac{12}{8} = 1.5$$ ### Step 3 — Compute for Module B $$C_B = \frac{5}{20} = 0.25$$ ### Step 4 — Compare $$C_A \gg C_B \implies \text{Module A is more vulnerable to cascade risk.}$$ ### Step 5 — Interpret the magnitude - $C < 0.5$ → **Low coupling** → minimal cascade exposure. - $0.5 \leq C \leq 1.0$ → **Moderate coupling** → borderline. - $C > 1.0$ → **High coupling** → high cascade exposure. Module $A$ falls in the *high-coupling* regime and is a candidate for **refactoring** under the KTU 2024 design-quality framework. --- ## 3.6 Worked Example — Multi-Level Risk Roll-up with STRIDE **Problem:** A payment subsystem has the following DFD with three processes ($P_1, P_2, P_3$) and one data store ($D_1$). STRIDE analysis yields the normalized per-element risks: - $P_1$: Spoofing risk $= 0.4$, Tampering risk $= 0.3$ - $P_2$: Information disclosure risk $= 0.5$, DoS risk $= 0.2$ - $D_1$: Tampering risk $= 0.6$ Compute the **per-process aggregate** and the **system-level aggregate**. ### Step 1 — Per-process aggregate For $P_1$: $$R_{P_1} = 1 - (1 - 0.4)(1 - 0.3) = 1 - (0.6)(0.7) = 1 - 0.42 = 0.58$$ For $P_2$: $$R_{P_2} = 1 - (1 - 0.5)(1 - 0.2) = 1 - (0.5)(0.8) = 1 - 0.40 = 0.60$$ For $D_1$: $$R_{D_1} = 0.6$$ ### Step 2 — System-level aggregate $$R_{\text{sys}} = 1 - (1 - 0.58)(1 - 0.60)(1 - 0.60)$$ $$= 1 - (0.42)(0.40)(0.40) = 1 - 0.0672 = 0.9328$$ ### Step 3 — Interpretation The **system-level risk is $\approx 93.3\%$** — alarmingly high. A single mitigation at $D_1$ (e.g., field-level encryption) reducing its risk to $0.2$ would lower the system aggregate to: $$R_{\text{sys}}' = 1 - (0.42)(0.40)(0.80) = 1 - 0.1344 = 0.8656$$ A modest **6.7 percentage-point** reduction. **Better return** comes from mitigating $P_2$ first (highest single-element contribution). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Hierarchical Decomposition of a Software System (Block-Level Functional Architecture) The diagram below maps the **canonical 4-level hierarchy** of a secure software system, with the **STRIDE threat lens** anchored at each level. ```mermaid flowchart TD L0["LEVEL 0 : SYSTEM CONTEXT
External entities + trust boundary"]:::lvl0 L1["LEVEL 1 : ARCHITECTURAL
Subsystems, modules, DFDs"]:::lvl1 L2["LEVEL 2 : DETAILED DESIGN
Classes, APIs, sequence diagrams"]:::lvl2 L3["LEVEL 3 : IMPLEMENTATION
Source code, configs, binaries"]:::lvl3 L0 -->|Decompose| L1 L1 -->|Refine| L2 L2 -->|Code| L3 T0["Threat lens : STRIDE per trust boundary"]:::threat T1["Threat lens : Architectural Risk Analysis"]:::threat T2["Threat lens : Misuse / Abuse Cases"]:::threat T3["Threat lens : SAST, DAST, SCA"]:::threat L0 -.covers.-> T0 L1 -.covers.-> T1 L2 -.covers.-> T2 L3 -.covers.-> T3 classDef lvl0 fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef lvl1 fill:#2e5a87,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef lvl2 fill:#4a82b5,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef lvl3 fill:#7eb0d5,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef threat fill:#b22222,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff ``` > **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows show *decomposition* (top-down refinement). Dotted arrows show *threat-lens coverage* (which security methodology is applied at which level). --- ## 4.2 STRIDE-per-Element Threat Model of a DFD ```mermaid flowchart LR user[("External Entity : User")]:::ext gw["Process : Auth Gateway"]:::proc biz["Process : Business Logic"]:::proc db[("Data Store : User DB")]:::store user -->|Credentials| gw gw -->|Session token| biz biz -->|SQL query| db db -->|Result set| biz biz -->|Response| user S1["S : Spoofing at gw"]:::s T1["T : Tampering at db"]:::t R1["R : Repudiation in logs"]:::r I1["I : Info disclosure at biz"]:::i D1["D : DoS at gw"]:::d E1["E : Elevation at biz"]:::e gw -.-> S1 db -.-> T1 gw -.-> R1 biz -.-> I1 gw -.-> D1 biz -.-> E1 classDef ext fill:#d4af37,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef proc fill:#3a7d44,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef store fill:#6a4c93,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef s fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#000 classDef t fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#000 classDef r fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#000 classDef i fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#000 classDef d fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#000 classDef e fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#000 ``` > **Reading the diagram:** Each DFD element is annotated with the **STRIDE categories** that are most relevant to it. This is the exact artifact produced in **Microsoft Threat Modeling Tool** and is the *expected diagram* in 14-mark KTU questions on hierarchical design. --- ## 4.3 Hierarchical Risk Roll-up Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A0["System S
Aggregate Risk R_S"]:::root A1["Subsystem A
