Risk 0.30"]:::mid A2["Subsystem B
Risk 0.20"]:::mid A3["Subsystem C
Risk 0.50"]:::mid A11["Module A1
Risk 0.10"]:::leaf A12["Module A2
Risk 0.20"]:::leaf A21["Module B1
Risk 0.15"]:::leaf A22["Module B2
Risk 0.10"]:::leaf A31["Module C1
Risk 0.30"]:::leaf A32["Module C2
Risk 0.40"]:::leaf A0 --> A1 A0 --> A2 A0 --> A3 A1 --> A11 A1 --> A12 A2 --> A21 A2 --> A22 A3 --> A31 A3 --> A32 F["Formula : R_agg = 1 - Π (1 - R_child)"]:::formula A0 -.computed by.-> F classDef root fill:#8b0000,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef mid fill:#cd5c5c,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef leaf fill:#f08080,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef formula fill:#2f4f4f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff ``` > **Reading the diagram:** Risk percolates *upward* from leaves (lowest-level modules) to the root (whole-system). At each parent, the aggregate risk is computed using the **independent-compromise formula** derived in §3.2. --- ## 4.4 Mapping Design Principles to STRIDE (Defense-in-Depth Matrix) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Principles P1["Modularity"]:::p P2["Abstraction"]:::p P3["Encapsulation"]:::p P4["Separation of Concerns"]:::p end subgraph STRIDE S1["S : Spoofing"]:::s T1["T : Tampering"]:::t R1["R : Repudiation"]:::r I1["I : Info Disclosure"]:::i D1["D : DoS"]:::d E1["E : Elevation"]:::e end P1 -->|contains blast radius| E1 P2 -->|reduces attack surface| I1 P3 -->|prevents state tampering| T1 P4 -->|enforces audit trails| R1 P1 -->|localises DoS impact| D1 P2 -->|hides internals from spoofers| S1 classDef p fill:#4682b4,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef s fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#000 classDef t fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#000 classDef r fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#000 classDef i fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#000 classDef d fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#000 classDef e fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#000 ``` > **Reading the diagram:** Each design principle **maps to one or more STRIDE categories** that it primarily mitigates. This is the *defense-in-depth* visualization that KTU 2024 examiners expect in any "explain the security significance" question. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Similar]` > **Q: Define Hierarchical Design Representation. Why is it essential in security assessment and risk analysis?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Hierarchical Design Representation (HDR)** is a structured, multi-level model that decomposes a software system into nested abstractions — typically *System Context, Architectural, Detailed Design,* and *Implementation* levels. Each level progressively refines the one above and hides implementation details of the level below. It is essential in security assessment because: 1. **Attack surfaces are level-specific.** A trust-boundary flaw (L0) cannot be discovered by code scanning (L3). 2. **Risk rolls up hierarchically.** A weakness in a leaf module propagates upward; HDR provides the *mathematical scaffold* (the aggregate-risk formula) to compute system-level risk. 3. **Defense-in-depth.** Controls can be applied at multiple levels, ensuring that a single breach does not compromise the entire system. > *Awarding:* Definition = 1 mark; two distinct reasons = 1 mark each = 2 marks → **Total 3 marks**. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Similar]` > **Q: List any four software design principles and state the security threat each helps mitigate.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | # | Principle | Threat Mitigated | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Modularity** | Cross-module privilege escalation; cascade failures | | 2 | **Encapsulation** | Direct tampering of internal state and memory | | 3 | **Abstraction (Information Hiding)** | API misuse, prototype pollution, internal-state leakage | | 4 | **Separation of Concerns** | Bypass of audit logging, cross-cutting control failure | > *Awarding:* 4 principles × 0.5 mark + 4 threats × 0.25 mark ≈ 3 marks (proportional credit). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]` > **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **four levels of hierarchical design representation** used in security assessment. For each level, identify the **primary threat-modeling artifact** and the **principal class of security threats** addressed. > > **(b) [7 Marks]** A subsystem is decomposed into three child modules with independent compromise probabilities $R_1 = 0.25$, $R_2 = 0.40$, and $R_3 = 0.15$. Compute the **system-level aggregate risk**. A control costing INR 8,00,000 reduces each risk by 40%. Compute the **new aggregate risk** and the **Return on Security Investment (ROSI)** if the *Annual Loss Expectancy* was originally INR 2,00,00,000. --- #### Model Solution for (a) [7 Marks] **Level 0 — System Context** - **Artifact:** Context-level diagram with external entities, system boundary, and data flows. - **Purpose:** Identify *trust boundaries* and *external threat actors*. - **Principal threats:** Supply-chain compromise, social engineering, unauthorized access at the perimeter. **Level 1 — Architectural** - **Artifact:** Data Flow Diagrams (DFD-L0/L1), structure charts, component diagrams. - **Purpose:** Decompose the system into sub-systems and analyze inter-process trust. - **Principal threats:** Man-in-the-middle, unauthorized inter-service calls, lateral movement, architectural single points of failure. **Level 2 — Detailed Design** - **Artifact:** UML class diagrams, sequence diagrams, statecharts, API contracts. - **Purpose:** Refine modules into classes, methods, and interfaces. - **Principal threats:** Broken access control (OWASP API1:2023), broken object-level authorization, business-logic flaws, type confusion (CWE-843). **Level 3 — Implementation** - **Artifact:** Source code, configuration files, container images, infrastructure-as-code. - **Purpose:** Realize the design in executable form. - **Principal threats:** Buffer overflow, injection (SQLi/XSS/command), deserialization, hard-coded secrets, vulnerable dependencies. > *Awarding:* Each level = 1.5 marks (artifact + threats) + 1 mark for a coherent integrative summary. --- #### Model Solution for (b) [7 Marks] **Step 1 — Compute the original aggregate risk** using the hierarchical roll-up formula: $$R_{\text{agg, original}} = 1 - (1 - 0.25)(1 - 0.40)(1 - 0.15)$$ $$= 1 - (0.75)(0.60)(0.85) = 1 - 0.3825 = 0.6175$$ **Step 2 — Compute the post-control risks.** A 40 % reduction means each new risk is 60 % of the original: $$R_1' = 0.25 \times 0.60 = 0.15$$ $$R_2' = 0.40 \times 0.60 = 0.24$$ $$R_3' = 0.15 \times 0.60 = 0.09$$ **Step 3 — Compute the new aggregate risk:** $$R_{\text{agg, new}} = 1 - (1 - 0.15)(1 - 0.24)(1 - 0.09)$$ $$= 1 - (0.85)(0.76)(0.91) = 1 - 0.58766 = 0.41234$$ **Step 4 — Compute the risk reduction:** $$\Delta R = 0.6175 - 0.41234 = 0.20516 \;(\approx 20.5\%)$$ **Step 5 — Compute the new ALE.** Assume ALE is proportional to aggregate risk (a standard KTU assumption): $$ALE_{\text{after}} = 2{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{0.41234}{0.6175} = 2{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.6678 \approx 1{,}33{,}56{,}000$$ **Step 6 — Compute the ROSI:** $$ROSI = \frac{ALE_{\text{before}} - ALE_{\text{after}} - \text{Cost}}{\text{Cost}}$$ $$= \frac{2{,}00{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}33{,}56{,}000 - 8{,}00{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000} = \frac{58{,}44{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000} = 7.305$$ $$ROSI = 730.5\%$$ **Step 7 — Interpret:** A ROSI of 7.305 means every **INR 1** spent returns **INR 7.31** in net benefit — a **strongly justified** investment. > *Awarding:* 1 mark aggregate original; 1 mark post-control risks; 1 mark aggregate new; 1 mark new ALE; 1 mark ROSI formula; 1 mark computation; 1 mark interpretation. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]` — *Alternative Choice* > **(a) [7 Marks]** With a **neat block-level functional diagram**, describe the **four levels of hierarchical design representation** and explain how each maps to a **STRIDE category** for threat modeling. Provide at least one **real-world example** for each level. > > **(b) [7 Marks]** A hierarchical design has a 2-ary tree structure of depth 5. Compute the **total number of threat-modelling nodes**. If each node requires 15 minutes of analysis and the team's capacity is 30 hours/week, determine the **number of weeks** required to complete the threat model. --- #### Model Solution for (a) [7 Marks] **Step 1 — Define the hierarchy and STRIDE mapping (in tabular form for the answer sheet).** | Level | Artifact | STRIDE Categories Most Relevant | Real-World Example | |---|---|---|---| | **L0 Context** | Context diagram | S, E (perimeter threats) | Banking app with customer, payment gateway, regulator | | **L1 Architectural** | DFD-L0, DFD-L1 | T, E, D (inter-process threats) | Web-app's auth-gateway → business-logic → database chain | | **L2 Detailed Design** | UML sequence, class | T, I, E (interface threats) | REST API with object-level authorization flaws | | **L3 Implementation** | Source code, configs | T, I, R (coding threats) | SQL injection in a login query | **Step 2 — Draw a hierarchical block diagram** (use the Mermaid diagram in §4.1 of these notes as the template; reproduce on paper with neat rectangular blocks and labelled arrows). **Step 3 — Per-level STRIDE justification** (3–4 lines per level): - **L0:** The trust boundary is where most *Spoofing* (fake customers) and *Elevation* (compromised admin) attempts occur. - **L1:** Inter-process data flows are vulnerable to *Tampering* (MITM), *DoS* (resource exhaustion), and *Elevation* (vertical privilege escalation). - **L2:** API contracts are vulnerable to *Tampering* (parameter pollution), *Info Disclosure* (over-fetching), and *Elevation* (BOLA — broken object-level authorization). - **L3:** Code is vulnerable to *Tampering* (injection), *Info Disclosure* (hard-coded secrets), and *Repudiation* (missing audit logs). > *Awarding:* 1 mark table; 1 mark diagram; 4 marks per-level justification (1 mark each) + 1 mark synthesis. --- #### Model Solution for (b) [7 Marks] **Step 1 — State parameters:** $$b = 2, \quad d = 5$$ **Step 2 — Apply the formula:** $$N(b, d) = \frac{b^{d+1} - 1}{b - 1} = \frac{2^{5+1} - 1}{2 - 1} = \frac{2^6 - 1}{1} = 64 - 1 = 63$$ **Step 3 — Total analysis time:** $$T_{\text{total}} = 63 \text{ nodes} \times 15 \text{ minutes/node} = 945 \text{ minutes}$$ Convert to hours: $$T_{\text{total}} = \frac{945}{60} = 15.75 \text{ hours}$$ **Step 4 — Compute the number of weeks:** $$W = \frac{T_{\text{total}}}{\text{weekly capacity}} = \frac{15.75}{30} = 0.525 \text{ weeks}$$ **Step 5 — Convert to working days** (assuming 5 working days per week): $$D = 0.525 \times 5 \approx 2.63 \text{ working days}$$ **Step 6 — Interpretation:** A **single analyst** can complete the threat model in roughly **3 working days** at this granularity. If the depth were increased to 10, $N = 2^{11} - 1 = 2047$ nodes, requiring $\approx 21$ weeks — a *classic motivation* for **automated threat-modelling tools** and **selective depth control**. > *Awarding:* 1 mark formula; 1 mark computation of N; 1 mark conversion to hours; 1 mark conversion to weeks; 1 mark conversion to days; 2 marks interpretation and design recommendation. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Patterns in Module 2 (Hierarchical Design):** > > 1. **Confusing "hierarchy" with "layering":** Students frequently write *"the system has three layers — UI, business, database"* and call it hierarchy. **Layers** and **hierarchies** are related but distinct. A hierarchy is a *tree* of decompositions; a layer is a *horizontal stratum* of abstraction. Examiners deduct 2 marks for this conflation. > > 2. **Skipping the formula derivation:** When asked to "compute aggregate risk", students often write the formula but skip intermediate steps. The KTU 2024 valuation key requires **at least two intermediate computation lines** to award full marks. > > 3. **Forgetting the design-principle-to-threat mapping:** Generic answers such as *"modularity improves security"* fetch 1 mark. Mapping it to *"modularity contains the blast radius of a compromised module, mitigating E (Elevation of Privilege) in STRIDE"* fetches full marks. > > 4. **Confusing SLE/ALE/ROSI units:** SLE is in currency; ALE is in currency per year; ROSI is dimensionless. Mixing them up is a **2-mark penalty**. > > 5. **Using `|` inside Markdown tables:** While this is a notes-format warning, the equivalent *handwritten-table error* is using absolute-value bars that visually break the table grid. Use `mod` notation or parentheses instead. > > 6. **Forgetting boundary conditions:** Writing $EF > 1$ or $R > 1$ is mathematically invalid. Always state the domain $0 \leq EF, R \leq 1$. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 — Module 2):** - **Software Design** is the engineering process of defining architecture, components, interfaces, data structures, modules, and algorithms to satisfy requirements, including **security requirements**. - **Hierarchical Design Representation (HDR)** decomposes a system into **4 canonical levels**: System Context (L0), Architectural (L1), Detailed Design (L2), and Implementation (L3). - The principle **"security must be designed in"** is non-negotiable; post-deployment patching cannot fix design flaws cost-effectively. - The **9 foundational design principles** to remember: Modularity, Abstraction, Encapsulation, Separation of Concerns, OCP, LSP, DIP, ISP, DRY. - The **4 canonical representation artifacts**: Context diagram, DFD, Structure Chart, UML diagrams. - **STRIDE-per-Element** is the standard threat-modelling method applied to DFDs at L1. - **Key formulas** to memorize verbatim: - $N(b, d) = \dfrac{b^{d+1} - 1}{b - 1}$ - $SLE = AV \times EF$ - $ALE = SLE \times ARO$ - $R_{\text{agg}} = 1 - \prod (1 - R_j)$ - $ROSI = \dfrac{ALE_{\text{before}} - ALE_{\text{after}} - \text{Cost}}{\text{Cost}}$ - $C = \dfrac{\text{inter}}{\text{intra}}$ - **Aggregate risk** is computed *bottom-up* (leaves → root) under the *independent compromise* assumption. - **Coupling metric**: $C > 1$ indicates high cascade risk; refactoring is recommended. - **Defense-in-depth** = applying controls at *multiple* hierarchical levels; never rely on a single level. - **Real-world standards** that use HDR: NIST SP 800-160, ISO/SAE 21434, DO-326A, IEC 80001-2-2, PCI-DSS v4.0. - Always state **boundary conditions** ($0 \leq EF \leq 1$, $0 \leq R \leq 1$) when writing formulas. - Always use **proper LaTeX delimiters** (`$$ ... $$` for display, `$ ... $` for inline) and **avoid `|` inside Markdown tables** (use `\vert` or `\mid`). - **KTU 2024 examiner expectations**: anchor every answer in the *principle → threat → level* mapping; avoid generic statements. > [!IMPORTANT] > **One-Sentence Takeaway for Viva:** *Hierarchical design representations convert a chaotic, multi-dimensional security problem into a tractable, level-by-level risk analysis — they are the *grammar* in which secure systems are spoken.*

topics.title: Difference Between High-Level and Detailed Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT418 - SECURITY ASSESSMENT AND RISK ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Difference Between High-Level and Detailed Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**High-Level Design (HLD)** and **Detailed Design (Low-Level Design / LLD)** are the two sequential, hierarchical phases of the **System Design** stage in the **System Development Life Cycle (SDLC)** and the **Secure System Design Lifecycle**. They translate abstract security requirements and business objectives into concrete architectural blueprints and implementable component specifications.

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Level Design (HLD)** is the *architectural blueprint* phase. It defines the overall system structure, identifies major modules/components, their interrelationships, data flow, external interfaces, and the technology stack — all viewed from a *10,000-foot perspective*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Detailed Design (LLD)** is the *implementation blueprint* phase. It drills down into each module defined in the HLD, specifying internal logic, database schemas, algorithm-level procedures, class structures, API contracts, and security control parameters — a *ground-level, code-ready specification*.

In the context of **Security Assessment and Risk Analysis (PEAIT418)**, these two design phases carry special weight because they directly influence:
- The **CIA Triad** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability) guarantees of the system.
- The mapping of security controls to identified threats (NIST SP 800-53, ISO 27001 Annex A).
- The **Attack Surface** minimization and **Defense-in-Depth** implementation.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Building Construction Metaphor

> [!TIP]
> **Think of designing a BANK VAULT.**

**High-Level Design (HLD)** is like the *architect's master plan*:
- It says, "We will have a **two-story bank building** with a **vault on the ground floor**, a **lobby**, **three teller counters**, and a **rear emergency exit**." It mentions that the vault will use *some kind of reinforced material* and that cameras will be installed. It does NOT specify the concrete grade, the camera model number, or the exact thickness of steel.

**Detailed Design (LLD)** is like the *structural engineer's working drawings*:
- It says, "The vault wall will be **300 mm thick reinforced concrete (Grade M40)** with **25 mm diameter TMT bars at 150 mm spacing**. The vault door will be a **Class-X UL-listed safe** with a **3-factor biometric lock** (fingerprint + iris + PIN). Camera model **Hikvision DS-2CD2043G2-I** at every 5-meter interval with **AES-256 encrypted streams**."

**The KTU analogy you must remember:**
| Phase | Architecture Analogy | Question Answered |
|---|---|---|
| **HLD** | "**WHAT** will the system look like?" | *What* modules exist? *What* tech stack? |
| **LLD** | "**HOW** exactly will it work?" | *How* does each function execute? *How* is data stored? |

---

### 1.3 Position in the SDLC and Security Lifecycle

The two design phases sit between **Requirements Analysis** and **Implementation/Coding**:

$$\text{Requirements} \;\longrightarrow\; \underbrace{\text{High-Level Design (HLD)}}_{\text{What \& Why}} \;\longrightarrow\; \underbrace{\text{Detailed Design (LLD)}}_{\text{How}} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Implementation}$$

> [!CAUTION]
> **KTU Board Trap:** Students frequently confuse *High-Level Design* with *High-Level Architecture (HLA)*. HLA refers to a *reference architecture or product selection* (e.g., "we choose a three-tier web architecture"), whereas HLD is the *full design document* that details modules, interfaces, data flow, and security controls. HLA ⊂ HLD.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Comparison Sheet

### 2.1 Granular Breakdown of High-Level Design (HLD)

**HLD = The "System-as-a-Whole" Document**

**Operational steps performed during HLD:**

- **Step 1 — Functional Decomposition:** Break the system into *major functional modules*. Example: `Authentication Module`, `Transaction Engine`, `Audit Logger`, `Reporting Dashboard`.
- **Step 2 — Architectural Pattern Selection:** Choose the structural style — *Monolithic, Layered (N-Tier), Microservices, Event-Driven, Serverless, Zero-Trust, etc.*
- **Step 3 — Technology Stack Finalization:** Lock the high-level tools — *Java/Spring Boot, MySQL, Kafka, Kubernetes, AWS GovCloud.*
- **Step 4 — External Interface Identification:** Define *external dependency contracts* — third-party APIs, payment gateways, identity providers (IdP), SIEM integrations.
- **Step 5 — Data Flow Modeling:** Use **Data Flow Diagrams (DFD Level-0 and Level-1)** to show how information moves between modules.
- **Step 6 — Preliminary Security Architecture:** Map high-level security controls (firewall zones, DMZ, encryption-at-rest vs. in-transit, IAM policy areas).
- **Step 7 — Non-Functional Requirements (NFR) Mapping:** Address *performance (RPS), availability (SLA %), scalability (horizontal/vertical), compliance (GDPR, PCI-DSS).*

> [!NOTE]
> **The "WHY" behind HLD:** HLD exists so that **stakeholders, architects, project managers, and the client's CISO** can validate the system's overall structure *before* a single line of code is written. It is the primary artifact for *design review boards* and *threat modeling sessions (STRIDE/PASTA)*.

---

### 2.2 Granular Breakdown of Detailed Design (LLD)

**LLD = The "Module-by-Module" Technical Specification**

**Operational steps performed during LLD:**

- **Step 1 — Module Specification:** For *each* module in HLD, write a precise functional contract — inputs, outputs, pre-conditions, post-conditions, exception flows.
- **Step 2 — Database Design:** Produce **ER diagrams**, table schemas, indexing strategy, partitioning rules, encryption column definitions, retention policies.
- **Step 3 — Algorithm & Logic Design:** Specify business logic via **pseudocode, flowcharts, decision tables, sequence diagrams**.
- **Step 4 — API Design:** Define *REST/GraphQL/gRPC endpoint signatures* — request/response payloads, HTTP status codes, authentication headers, rate limits.
- **Step 5 — Class & Interface Design:** Produce **UML Class Diagrams**, design patterns applied (Factory, Strategy, Observer), SOLID principle adherence.
- **Step 6 — Detailed Security Control Specification:** Pick exact *algorithms* (AES-256-GCM, RSA-4096, Argon2id), key lengths, rotation policies, MFA factor combinations.
- **Step 7 — Test Case Blueprint:** Define *unit test scenarios, integration test paths, security test cases (SQLi, XSS, CSRF probes).*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "WHY" behind LLD:** LLD exists so that **developers** can write code *mechanically* without making design decisions on the fly, and so that **security testers** can build precise penetration test plans against known implementation contracts.

---

### 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Comparison Matrix

| **Comparison Axis** | **High-Level Design (HLD)** | **Detailed Design (LLD)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Alternate Name** | System Design, Macro Design, Top-Down Design | Low-Level Design, Micro Design, Bottom-Up Design |
| **Audience** | Architects, Project Managers, CISO, Client Stakeholders | Developers, Testers, DBAs, Security Engineers |
| **Granularity** | Coarse-grained, abstract | Fine-grained, concrete |
| **Core Question** | *WHAT* + *WHY* | *HOW* |
| **Inputs Required** | SRS (Software Requirement Specification), BRS | HLD document, detailed NFRs |
| **Outputs Produced** | HLD Document, DFD Level-0/1, Context Diagram | LLD Document, ER Diagram, Class Diagram, Sequence Diagram |
| **Diagrams Used** | DFD, Context Diagram, High-Level Architecture Diagram | UML (Class, Sequence, Activity, State), ER Diagram, Flowcharts |
| **Database Treatment** | Decides *which* DBMS (MySQL, PostgreSQL, MongoDB) | Defines *tables, columns, indexes, FKs, constraints, encryption* |
| **Security Depth** | Defines security *zones, controls family, trust boundaries* | Specifies *exact algorithms, key sizes, cipher modes, MFA factors* |
| **Decision Points** | Module existence, technology choice, deployment topology | Logic flow, loop constructs, conditional branches, error handling |
| **Performance Metrics** | SLA targets (e.g., 99.99 % uptime, < 200 ms p95 latency) | Query execution plans, cache TTLs, connection pool sizes |
| **Coding Readiness** | **NOT** directly implementable | **Directly** implementable by a developer |
| **Document Size (typical)** | 30–80 pages | 100–400+ pages (often one per module) |
| **Review Frequency** | Once per release/major change | Continuous — updated with every sprint |
| **Failure Impact if Wrong** | Project-wide architectural rework | Localized module refactor |
| **KTU Bloom's Level** | Analyze, Evaluate (Cognitive Level 4–5) | Apply, Analyze (Cognitive Level 3–4) |
| **Standards Mapping** | NIST CSF Function Categories, ISO 27001 Annex A Domains | NIST SP 800-53 Control Baselines, OWASP ASVS L2/L3 |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **In Banking Security Design:** HLD picks the *zero-trust segmentation model* between core banking zones; LLD specifies the *exact TLS 1.3 cipher suite list (`TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384`)* and the *Argon2id memory cost = 64 MiB*.
- **In Cloud Security Architecture:** HLD chooses *AWS multi-region active-active with CloudFront + WAF*; LLD defines the *exact IAM policy JSON, S3 bucket policy with `aws:SecureTransport=true`, and KMS key rotation period = 90 days*.
- **In Penetration Testing:** A pentester reads the LLD to understand *what to attack* (specific API endpoints, encryption libraries used); they reference the HLD to identify *which trust boundary to cross*.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "HLD = House, LLD = House-wiring":**
> The HLD tells you *how many rooms the house has and where the doors are*.
> The LLD tells you *which wire (14 AWG copper, 15 A breaker) goes from the switchboard to which socket*.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Worked Examples

Since this is a **conceptual/comparative topic** (not a numerical derivation), Section 3 is framed as an **Analytical Walkthrough** that systematically derives the differences from first principles, and then provides a **concrete worked example** that KTU examiners love to test.

---

### 3.1 The "Derivation" of Why Two Design Phases Exist

**Premise (axiom of systems engineering):** *Any system of non-trivial complexity cannot be specified in a single document.*

**Step 1 — Cognitive Load Argument**
$$\text{Information required} \;=\; N_{\text{modules}} \times C_{\text{module}}$$
where $N_{\text{modules}}$ is the number of modules and $C_{\text{module}}$ is the complexity per module. For a banking system with $N_{\text{modules}} \approx 30$, the total information density exceeds human cognitive capacity in a single document. **Therefore, abstraction via hierarchy is required.**

**Step 2 — Separation of Concerns**
We partition the design into two orthogonal layers:
$$\text{Design Space} \;=\; \text{Structural Layer (HLD)} \;\cup\; \text{Behavioral Layer (LLD)}$$
with the property:
$$\text{HLD} \;\cap\; \text{LLD} \;=\; \emptyset$$
*No design detail appears in both layers — each piece of information has exactly one home.*

**Step 3 — Stakeholder-Decision Mapping**
Each design layer corresponds to one stakeholder's decision authority:
$$\text{HLD} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{Architect/CISO (strategic decisions)}$$
$$\text{LLD} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{Developer/Tester (tactical decisions)}$$

**Step 4 — Verification Independence**
HLD can be validated by *threat modeling* (STRIDE) without knowing implementation; LLD can be validated by *unit testing* without re-architecting the system.
$$\text{Verifiable}(\text{HLD}) \perp \text{Verifiable}(\text{LLD})$$

> [!NOTE]
> This four-step deduction is the **KTU examiner's favourite way** to test whether you truly understand *why* the design phase is split, rather than just memorizing differences.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — Authentication System Design

> [!TIP]
> **Scenario (asked frequently in KTU):** *"Design an Authentication System for an Internet Banking Application. Show how HLD and LLD differ for the same."*

#### **HIGH-LEVEL DESIGN (HLD) Output**

**Module Identity:** `Authentication Service`
**Architectural Pattern:** Layered (Presentation → API Gateway → Auth Service → Database)
**Technology Stack:** Spring Boot 3.x, PostgreSQL 16, Redis 7, Keycloak 24
**Trust Boundary:** Auth Service sits inside the *Internal Trust Zone*, behind the API Gateway (DMZ).
**Security Control Families (HLD level):**
- Authentication: Username + Password + OTP via SMS
- Session: JWT with 15-minute expiry
- Storage: Encrypted at rest using AES-256
- Transport: TLS 1.3 enforced

**HLD-Generated Diagram (DFD Level-0):**

$$\underbrace{\text{User Browser}}_{\text{External Entity}} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Login Credentials + OTP}}\; \underbrace{\text{Authentication Service}}_{\text{Process 1}} \;\xrightarrow{\text{User Lookup}}\; \underbrace{\text{User Database}}_{\text{Data Store 1}}$$

The HLD document **stops here**. It says *what* is needed, not *how* it is built.

---

#### **DETAILED DESIGN (LLD) Output**

Now we take the same `Authentication Service` and drill down.

**API Endpoint Specification:**

| **Endpoint** | `POST /api/v1/auth/login` |
|---|---|
| **Auth Required** | No (this is login) |
| **Request Body** | `{ "username": "string", "password": "string", "otp": "string-6digits" }` |
| **Response 200** | `{ "access_token": "JWT", "refresh_token": "JWT", "expires_in": 900 }` |
| **Response 401** | `{ "error": "INVALID_CREDENTIALS", "retry_after": 5 }` |
| **Rate Limit** | 5 attempts / 15 min / IP |
| **Audit Logged** | Yes — to SIEM via Kafka topic `auth.events` |

**Password Hashing Logic (Pseudocode):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\texttt{function hashPassword(plainText, userSalt)}: \\
&\quad \texttt{hash} \;\leftarrow\; \texttt{Argon2id}(\text{plainText},\; \text{userSalt},\; \text{memory}=64\text{ MiB},\; \text{iterations}=3,\; \text{parallelism}=4) \\
&\quad \texttt{return Base64(hash)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Database Schema for `users` table:**

| Column | Type | Constraint | Encryption |
|---|---|---|---|
| `user_id` | UUID | PRIMARY KEY | — |
| `username` | VARCHAR(64) | UNIQUE, NOT NULL | — |
| `password_hash` | VARCHAR(256) | NOT NULL | Argon2id |
| `password_salt` | VARCHAR(64) | NOT NULL | — |
| `mfa_secret` | VARCHAR(64) | NOT NULL | AES-256-GCM |
| `failed_login_count` | INTEGER | DEFAULT 0 | — |
| `lockout_until` | TIMESTAMP | NULL | — |

**Class Diagram (UML, simplified):**

```
<<interface>>
IAuthService
+ login(LoginRequest) : LoginResponse
+ logout(String token) : void
+ refreshToken(String refreshToken) : LoginResponse

        ▲ implements
        │
┌───────────────┐
│ AuthService   │
├───────────────┤
│ - userRepo    │
│ - jwtUtil     │
│ - auditLogger │
├───────────────┤
│ + login()     │
│ + logout()    │
└───────────────┘
```

---

### 3.3 The Comparative Analytical Matrix — Applied to the Above Example

| **Aspect** | **HLD Said…** | **LLD Specified…** |
|---|---|---|
| Hashing | "Passwords stored using strong hashing" | Argon2id, 64 MiB memory, 3 iterations, 4 parallelism |
| Encryption | "Data encrypted at rest" | AES-256-GCM mode, column-level encryption for `mfa_secret` |
| Session | "Use JWT for sessions" | 15-minute expiry, refresh token rotation, signing algo RS256 |
| MFA | "Multi-factor authentication" | TOTP RFC 6238, 30-second window, ±1 step skew tolerance |
| Lockout | "Account lockout on failed attempts" | 5 failed attempts → lockout for 30 min, audit-logged to SIEM |

> [!CAUTION]
> **KTU Board Rule:** If a student writes *LLD-level detail* in an HLD question (or vice-versa), the examiner deducts marks for **scope violation**. Always read the question carefully — *"Design at HLD level"* vs. *"Provide detailed module specifications"*.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — The Hierarchical Decomposition of System Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    reqBox["Requirements Analysis SRS BRS"]:::rootNode
    hldBox["High Level Design HLD Phase"]:::hldNode
    lldBox["Detailed Design LLD Phase"]:::lldNode
    implBox["Implementation Coding"]:::implNode
    testBox["Testing Security Validation"]:::testNode

    arch["Architecture Style Selection"]:::subNode
    tech["Technology Stack Lock"]:::subNode
    dfg["Data Flow Diagrams DFD 0 and 1"]:::subNode
    sec1["Security Zones and Trust Boundaries"]:::subNode
    nfr["Non Functional Requirements SLA"]:::subNode

    module["Module Wise Functional Spec"]:::subNode
    dbd["Database Design ER and Schema"]:::subNode
    algo["Algorithm and Pseudocode"]:::subNode
    api["API Contract Definition"]:::subNode
    sec2["Detailed Security Controls Algorithms Keys"]:::subNode
    classd["UML Class and Sequence Diagrams"]:::subNode

    reqBox --> hldBox
    hldBox --> arch
    hldBox --> tech
    hldBox --> dfg
    hldBox --> sec1
    hldBox --> nfr

    hldBox --> lldBox
    lldBox --> module
    lldBox --> dbd
    lldBox --> algo
    lldBox --> api
    lldBox --> sec2
    lldBox --> classd

    lldBox --> implBox
    implBox --> testBox

    classDef rootNode fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef hldNode fill:#4a7b9d,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef lldNode fill:#7aa86f,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef implNode fill:#c9892b,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef testNode fill:#a13d3d,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef subNode fill:#e8e8e8,stroke:#333,color:#000,stroke-width:1px
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Stakeholder Mapping Across HLD and LLD

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph hldLayer["HLD PHASE"]
        A1["CISO and Security Architect"]:::execNode
        A2["Project Manager"]:::execNode
        A3["Client Stakeholders"]:::execNode
        A4["Solution Architect"]:::execNode
    end

    subgraph lldLayer["LLD PHASE"]
        B1["Senior Developer Tech Lead"]:::devNode
        B2["Database Administrator"]:::devNode
        B3["Security Engineer"]:::devNode
        B4["QA and Pen Tester"]:::devNode
    end

    subgraph deliverable["DELIVERABLES"]
        C1["HLD Document Architecture Diagrams"]:::delNode
        C2["LLD Document Module Specs Pseudocode"]:::delNode
    end

    A1 --> C1
    A2 --> C1
    A3 --> C1
    A4 --> C1

    B1 --> C2
    B2 --> C2
    B3 --> C2
    B4 --> C2

    C1 -->|"Approved Baseline"| C2

    classDef execNode fill:#3b6ea5,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef devNode fill:#5a8f3a,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef delNode fill:#b8862b,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Security Depth Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph hldSec["HLD Security Abstraction"]
        H1["Define Trust Zones DMZ Internal External"]:::hldSecNode
        H2["Select Security Control Families AuthN AuthZ Encryption Logging"]:::hldSecNode
        H3["High Level Encryption Policy Data at Rest vs In Transit"]:::hldSecNode
        H4["Identify Regulatory Scope PCI DSS GDPR HIPAA"]:::hldSecNode
    end

    subgraph lldSec["LLD Security Specifics"]
        L1["Algorithm Choice AES 256 GCM RSA 4096 Argon2id"]:::lldSecNode
        L2["Key Management KMS Rotation 90 days HSM backed"]:::lldSecNode
        L3["Session Token JWT RS256 15 min Refresh 7 days"]:::lldSecNode
        L4["Input Validation Whitelist Regex Length Limit"]:::lldSecNode
        L5["Error Handling Generic Messages No Stack Trace Leak"]:::lldSecNode
    end

    H1 -.->|"expanded into"| L1
    H1 -.->|"expanded into"| L2
    H2 -.->|"expanded into"| L3
    H3 -.->|"expanded into"| L1
    H3 -.->|"expanded into"| L2
    H2 -.->|"expanded into"| L4
    H2 -.->|"expanded into"| L5
    H4 -.->|"traced to controls in"| L1
    H4 -.->|"traced to controls in"| L2

    classDef hldSecNode fill:#4a7b9d,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef lldSecNode fill:#7aa86f,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1.** *[KTU University Exam — May 2024, PEAIT418]*
> **Differentiate between High-Level Design (HLD) and Detailed Design (LLD) in the context of security architecture. List any four distinguishing parameters.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Parameter | HLD | LLD |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Granularity** | Abstract, macro-level, focuses on overall structure and modules | Detailed, micro-level, focuses on module-internal logic |
| 2 | **Audience** | Architects, CISO, project managers, clients | Developers, DBAs, testers, security engineers |
| 3 | **Security Treatment** | Defines security zones, trust boundaries, and control families (e.g., "encryption at rest") | Specifies exact algorithms, key lengths, cipher modes (e.g., "AES-256-GCM with 256-bit key, 90-day rotation") |
| 4 | **Implementability** | NOT directly implementable by a coder | Directly implementable; code-ready |

*(1 mark for the table; 1 mark for one-line interpretation; 1 mark for the security-context distinction.)*

---

> **Question 2.** *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PEAIT418]*
> **With a suitable example, explain why High-Level Design cannot be skipped even if the project team is small and time-constrained.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

HLD cannot be skipped because:

1. **Stakeholder alignment (1 mark):** HLD provides the *single document* that the CISO, client, and project manager sign off on. Without it, security requirements get misinterpreted. *Example:* If HLD skips the trust-boundary definition between the payment module and the analytics module, the LLD developer may not isolate them in separate VPCs — leading to PCI-DSS violation.

2. **Risk identification (1 mark):** HLD is the foundation for *threat modeling (STRIDE/PASTA)*. Without it, the team cannot systematically identify threats like *spoofing* at the API gateway or *tampering* in transit.

3. **Cost of rework (1 mark):** Architectural flaws discovered during LLD or testing cost **10× to 100× more** to fix than those caught at HLD. *Example:* Discovering at testing stage that the chosen monolithic architecture cannot handle 10,000 RPS forces a complete rewrite.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

#### **QUESTION A — Choice 1 (14 Marks)**

> **Question A.** *[KTU University Exam — May 2024, PEAIT418, Module 2]*
> **(a)** Explain the concept of High-Level Design (HLD) in detail. Describe its key components and the process of preparing an HLD document for a secure web application. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the concept of Detailed Design (LLD) in detail. Using a concrete example of an *Authentication and Authorization module*, show how LLD specifies the security controls, database schema, and API contracts. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) High-Level Design (HLD) — 7 marks**

**Definition (1 mark):** HLD is the second phase of the System Development Life Cycle (after requirements gathering) that translates the Software Requirements Specification (SRS) into a **system architecture blueprint**, defining the major modules, their interactions, technology stack, data flow, and preliminary security architecture.

**Key Components of an HLD Document (3 marks):**

1. **System Architecture Overview** — Identifies the architectural pattern (e.g., *3-tier web architecture*, *microservices*, *event-driven*).
2. **Module Decomposition** — Lists and describes each major module (e.g., *User Management, Transaction Engine, Audit, Reporting*).
3. **Data Flow Diagrams (DFD Level-0 and Level-1)** — Shows how data moves between external entities, processes, and data stores.
4. **Technology Stack** — Locks the languages, frameworks, databases, middleware, and cloud platform.
5. **External Interfaces** — Lists third-party integrations (payment gateways, identity providers, SMS/email services).
6. **High-Level Security Architecture** — Defines *security zones* (DMZ, internal, restricted), *trust boundaries*, encryption-at-rest vs. in-transit policy areas, and high-level IAM model (RBAC vs. ABAC).
7. **Non-Functional Requirements Mapping** — SLA targets for availability (e.g., 99.95 %), performance (e.g., p95 latency < 300 ms), scalability approach (horizontal), and compliance scope (GDPR, PCI-DSS).

**Process of Preparing HLD for a Secure Web Application (3 marks):**
- **Step 1:** Review the SRS and identify security-critical use cases.
- **Step 2:** Decompose the system into 8–15 major modules.
- **Step 3:** Choose an architectural pattern based on scalability and security needs (e.g., *microservices with API gateway*).
- **Step 4:** Draw the DFDs and Context Diagram.
- **Step 5:** Map trust boundaries and security zones.
- **Step 6:** Conduct a preliminary *STRIDE threat model* to identify high-level threats.
- **Step 7:** Submit the HLD to the Design Review Board for sign-off before LLD begins.

---

**(b) Detailed Design (LLD) with Authentication Example — 7 marks**

**Definition (1 mark):** LLD is the phase that follows HLD and provides **module-by-module implementation-ready specifications** including exact algorithms, data structures, database schemas, API contracts, and security control parameters.

**Authentication Module — LLD Specification (6 marks):**

**i. API Contract (1.5 marks):**

| Endpoint | Method | Auth | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| `/api/v1/auth/login` | POST | None | Authenticate user, return JWT pair |
| `/api/v1/auth/refresh` | POST | Refresh token | Issue new access token |
| `/api/v1/auth/logout` | POST | Bearer JWT | Invalidate session |

Request: `{ "username": "string", "password": "string", "otp": "string-6digits" }`
Response (200): `{ "access_token": "JWT", "refresh_token": "JWT", "expires_in": 900 }`

**ii. Database Schema (1.5 marks):**

```sql
CREATE TABLE users (
    user_id        UUID PRIMARY KEY,
    username       VARCHAR(64) UNIQUE NOT NULL,
    password_hash  VARCHAR(256) NOT NULL,    -- Argon2id
    password_salt  VARCHAR(64)  NOT NULL,
    mfa_secret     VARCHAR(64)  NOT NULL,    -- AES-256-GCM encrypted
    role           VARCHAR(32)  NOT NULL,    -- RBAC role
    failed_login_count INTEGER DEFAULT 0,
    lockout_until  TIMESTAMP NULL,
    created_at     TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW()
);
```

**iii. Password Hashing Algorithm (1.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\texttt{hash} \;\leftarrow\; \texttt{Argon2id}(\text{password},\; \text{salt},\; \text{memory}=64\text{ MiB},\\
&\hspace{5.5cm} \text{iterations}=3,\; \text{parallelism}=4) \\
&\texttt{Stored value} \;\leftarrow\; \texttt{Base64}(\text{hash})
\end{aligned}
$$

**iv. Class Diagram (1 mark):** `IAuthService` interface with `AuthServiceImpl` class — uses `UserRepository`, `JwtUtil`, `AuditLogger`. Applies the **Strategy Pattern** for MFA-factor selection and the **Decorator Pattern** for adding audit logging.

**v. Security Controls (0.5 mark):** Rate limiting at 5 attempts / 15 min / IP via Redis sliding window; account lockout after 5 failed attempts for 30 minutes; all authentication events pushed to SIEM via Kafka topic `auth.events`.

---

#### **QUESTION B — Choice 2 (14 Marks)**

> **Question B.** *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PEAIT418, Module 2]*
> **(a)** Compare High-Level Design and Detailed Design across **at least eight** parameters, presenting your answer in a tabular format with a brief explanation of each parameter. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With the help of an **HLD-level Data Flow Diagram** and a corresponding **LLD-level pseudocode**, demonstrate how the two design phases treat the same functionality differently. Use a *Payment Processing Module* as your example. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparative Table — 8 Parameters (7 marks)**

*(0.75 mark per row × 8 rows = 6 marks + 1 mark for the brief introduction)*

| **Parameter** | **HLD** | **LLD** |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Scope** | Whole system; macro view | Per-module; micro view |
| **2. Input Source** | SRS / BRS / Risk Assessment Report | Approved HLD document |
| **3. Granularity** | Abstract — describes *what* and *why* | Concrete — describes *how*, with exact values |
| **4. Diagrams Produced** | DFD L-0, L-1; Context Diagram; High-Level Architecture | UML Class, Sequence, Activity, State; ER; Flowcharts |
| **5. Security Detail** | "Encryption at rest", "MFA required", "RBAC model" | "AES-256-GCM, KMS rotation 90 days, TOTP RFC 6238, 256-bit key" |
| **6. Database Treatment** | Names DBMS (PostgreSQL 16, MongoDB 7); names major entities | Full DDL with constraints, indexes, FKs, partition keys, encryption columns |
| **7. Implementation Readiness** | NOT directly implementable; requires LLD | Directly implementable; developer can code from it |
| **8. Stakeholder Reviewers** | CISO, Architects, Client, PM | Tech Leads, Senior Developers, DBAs, Security Engineers, QA Leads |
| **9. Standards Mapping** | NIST CSF Functions; ISO 27001 Domains; COBIT | NIST SP 800-53 Controls; OWASP ASVS L2/L3; CIS Benchmarks |
| **10. Update Frequency** | Once per major release | Continuous — updated each sprint |

**Brief Explanation (1 mark):** HLD acts as the *architectural contract* between management and engineering, while LLD acts as the *engineering blueprint* that converts that contract into coded reality.

---

**(b) Payment Processing — HLD vs. LLD Demonstration (7 marks)**

**HLD-Level Data Flow Diagram (2.5 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    user["Customer External Entity"]:::ext
    paygw["Payment Gateway External 3rd Party"]:::ext
    ps["Payment Service Process 1"]:::proc
    bdb["Bank Database Data Store 1"]:::store
    logdb["Audit Log Data Store 2"]:::store
    notif["Notification Service Process 2"]:::proc

    user -->|"Payment Request Amount Currency"| ps
    ps -->|"Card Token Validation Status"| paygw
    ps -->|"Store Transaction Record"| bdb
    ps -->|"Log Payment Event"| logdb
    ps -->|"Send Receipt Confirmation"| notif
    notif -->|"Email or SMS Confirmation"| user

    classDef ext fill:#d6e4f0,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef proc fill:#4a7b9d,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef store fill:#c9892b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

*HLD treats Payment Service as a black box — only inputs, outputs, and external interactions are shown.*

**LLD-Level Pseudocode for the Same Module (3.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\texttt{function processPayment(request: PaymentRequest): PaymentResult} \\
&\texttt{1. validateRequest(request):} \\
&\quad \texttt{if request.amount <= 0: throw InvalidAmountError()} \\
&\quad \texttt{if not isValidCurrency(request.currency): throw InvalidCurrencyError()} \\
&\quad \texttt{if not verifyIdempotencyKey(request.idempotencyKey): throw DuplicateRequestError()} \\
&\texttt{2. tokenizeCard(request.cardData):} \\
&\quad \texttt{cardToken \leftarrow callPCICompliantTokenVault(request.cardData)} \\
&\quad \texttt{// cardData is NEVER stored or logged locally} \\
&\texttt{3. callPaymentGateway(cardToken, request.amount):} \\
&\quad \texttt{response \leftarrow httpsPost("https://api.stripe.com/v1/charges",} \\
&\quad \texttt{    headers = {Authorization: "Bearer sk_live_***", Idempotency-Key: request.idempotencyKey},} \\
&\quad \texttt{    body = {amount: request.amount, currency: request.currency, source: cardToken})} \\
&\quad \texttt{if response.status != 200: rollbackTransaction(); throw PaymentFailedError(response.code)} \\
&\texttt{4. persistTransaction(transactionId, response):} \\
&\quad \texttt{encryptedTxnId \leftarrow AES256_GCM_Encrypt(response.txnId, kmsKey)} \\
&\quad \texttt{INSERT INTO transactions (id, user_id, amount, currency, gateway_ref_enc, status, created_at)} \\
&\quad \texttt{VALUES (UUID, request.userId, request.amount, request.currency, encryptedTxnId, 'SUCCESS', NOW())}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparative Annotation (1 mark):** Notice that the *HLD DFD* shows only `Payment Request → Payment Service → Gateway → Database`. The *LLD pseudocode*, however, specifies the **idempotency check, PCI-compliant tokenization, TLS-secured gateway call, AES-256-GCM encryption of the gateway reference before storage, and rollback logic on failure** — none of which appear in the HLD.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Scope Mixing (–3 marks typical):** Writing LLD details (e.g., "Argon2id with 64 MiB memory") in an HLD question. Always match the *level of abstraction* asked.
> 2. **Missing Security Lens (–2 marks):** In PEAIT418 specifically, the examiner expects every HLD/LLD answer to mention **security controls, trust boundaries, or CIA-triad implications** — a purely generic SDLC answer loses security-context marks.
> 3. **No Diagram in 14-mark answers (–2 marks):** A long answer without *at least one diagram* (DFD, ER, or UML) is penalized. The KTU valuation key expects a visual.
> 4. **Confusing HLD with SRS (–1.5 marks):** SRS is *what the user needs*; HLD is *how the system will deliver it*. Do not list use cases in HLD.
> 5. **Forgetting the "Why" (–1 mark):** Examiners award a mark for explaining *why* a parameter differs — not just stating the difference. Add one sentence of reasoning per row.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2, PEAIT418**

- ✅ **HLD = WHAT + WHY** · **LLD = HOW** — memorize this distinction first.
- ✅ HLD audience = **CISO, Architects, PMs, Clients**; LLD audience = **Developers, DBAs, Testers, Security Engineers**.
- ✅ HLD outputs = **DFD L-0/L-1, Context Diagram, Architecture Diagram**; LLD outputs = **UML Class/Sequence/Activity, ER Diagram, Pseudocode**.
- ✅ HLD security = **control families + zones** (e.g., "MFA, encryption, RBAC") · LLD security = **exact algorithms + parameters** (e.g., "AES-256-GCM, Argon2id 64 MiB, JWT RS256 15-min").
- ✅ HLD maps to **NIST CSF / ISO 27001 Domains**; LLD maps to **NIST 800-53 Controls / OWASP ASVS / CIS Benchmarks**.
- ✅ HLD is reviewed **once per release**; LLD is reviewed **every sprint**.
- ✅ Architectural errors cost **10×–100× more** when found in LLD vs. HLD — *this is the core reason HLD exists*.
- ✅ In KTU PEAIT418, **always link design phases to CIA Triad and risk treatment** — the security-context lens is non-negotiable.
- ✅ The mnemonic: **"HLD is the House plan, LLD is the Wiring diagram."**
- ✅ Remember: *HLD ≠ HLA* — HLA is a subset of HLD dealing with technology/architecture choice only.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Handling Security with High-Level Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT418 - SECURITY ASSESSMENT AND RISK ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Handling Security with High-Level Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Handling Security with High-Level Design** refers to the systematic integration of security controls, threat mitigations, and architectural safeguards into the **High-Level Design (HLD)** phase of the Software/System Development Life Cycle (SDLC). The HLD phase, also known as the *architectural design phase*, defines the system's overall structure, modules, data flow, and inter-component communication before any low-level coding begins.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (aligned with **NIST SP 800-160 Vol. 1** and **ISO/IEC 27001 Annex A** controls), handling security at this stage is a proactive engineering discipline where architects embed **defensive design principles** such as Defense-in-Depth, Least Privilege, Fail-Safe Defaults, and Complete Mediation directly into the system's architectural blueprint.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Security in HLD is not an afterthought or a "penetration-test-and-patch" activity. It is a **shift-left security practice** — meaning security decisions migrate to the earliest possible phase of design, where the cost of a fix is the **lowest** and the impact of a vulnerability is the **most controlled**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are an **architect designing a skyscraper** in an earthquake-prone zone (think San Francisco or Tokyo).

- **Bad Practice (Reactive Security):** You build the entire 50-floor building first, fill it with tenants, and *then* hire engineers to retrofit earthquake-resistant steel braces into the walls. This is expensive, disruptive, and structurally inferior.
- **Good Practice (HLD Security — Shift-Left):** Before laying a single brick, your blueprints specify **reinforced shear walls, base isolators, flexible joints, and load distribution columns** as core architectural elements. The building *is* earthquake-safe by design.

In the same way, **Handling Security with High-Level Design** means that when an architect sketches the system's modules, network topology, authentication gateways, and data stores, security controls are **drawn into the blueprint as primary structural elements**, not bolted on at the end.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Analogy Mapping:**
> - Architect's Blueprint = High-Level Design Document (HLD)
> - Earthquake Braces = Security Controls (e.g., Firewalls, IAM Policies, Encryption)
> - Building Inspector = Security Review Board / Threat Model Validator
> - Post-construction retrofit = Penetration testing patches (expensive, late-stage)

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

| Metric | Standard Value / Definition | Authority |
|---|---|---|
| **Cost Ratio (Architectural Fix : Post-deployment Fix)** | **1 : 100** | NIST SP 800-64 |
| **STRIDE Threat Categories** | **6** (Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, DoS, Elevation) | Microsoft SDL |
| **Defense-in-Depth Layers** | **5 to 7** logical layers | NSA/CISA |
| **CIA Triad Pillars** | **3** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability) | ISO 27001 |
| **AAARR Risk Dimensions** | **5** (Asset, Attacker, Access, Audit, Action) | OCTAVE |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The HLD Phase: Where Does Security Fit?

The High-Level Design phase sits between **Requirements Gathering** and **Low-Level Design (LLD)** in the SDLC. The architectural artifacts produced during HLD include:

1. **System Context Diagrams** — showing external entities and trust boundaries.
2. **Component Diagrams** — identifying major modules/services.
3. **Data Flow Diagrams (DFD)** — Level 0 (Context) and Level 1 decomposition.
4. **Network Topology Maps** — showing zones, DMZs, and segmentation.
5. **Security Architecture Document** — defining trust zones, identity providers, and key management.

Security handling at HLD revolves around **four pillar activities**:

#### Pillar 1: Threat Modeling (Architectural Risk Analysis)
- Identify what could go wrong *before* the system is built.
- Apply **STRIDE** (per-element) or **PASTA** (process-oriented) frameworks.
- Output: A prioritized list of architectural threats.

#### Pillar 2: Security Pattern Selection
- Select proven architectural security patterns (e.g., *Authenticator Pattern*, *Security Gateway Pattern*, *Trusted Proxy*).
- Patterns are reusable, vetted solutions to recurring security problems.

#### Pillar 3: Trust Zone Definition
- Partition the system into **trust zones** (e.g., Public Internet, DMZ, Internal LAN, Secure Vault).
- Define **security policies** at every **trust boundary crossing**.

#### Pillar 4: Security Control Mapping
- Map selected controls to recognized frameworks:
  - **NIST SP 800-53** (108 controls in 20 families).
  - **OWASP ASVS** (Application Security Verification Standard — 3 verification levels).
  - **CIS Critical Security Controls** (18 safeguards).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical / Logical Form | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE)** | $ALE = SLE \times ARO$ | Quantifying risk in monetary terms |
| **Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)** | $SLE = Asset\ Value \times Exposure\ Factor$ | Cost of a single incident |
| **Annual Rate of Occurrence (ARO)** | Empirical frequency per year | Threat likelihood |
| **Risk Score (DREAD)** | $R = \frac{D + R + E + A + D_{iscover}}{5}$ | Each dimension rated 1–10 |
| **Cost of Fix Curve** | $C_{fix}(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{k \cdot t}$ | Cost grows exponentially with phase delay |
| **Defense-in-Depth Effectiveness** | $P_{compromise} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - p_i)$ | Probability system survives layered attacks |
| **CIA Triad Completeness** | $S = \{C, I, A\}$ | Every security req. must map to at least one |
| **STRIDE Mapping Function** | $T: Element \rightarrow \{S, T, R, I, D, E\}$ | Each DFD element checked for applicable threats |
| **Trust Boundary Count** | $B = \vert E \mid - \vert V \vert + 1$ | For a connected component graph |

> [!NOTE]
> In the markdown table above, the absolute value symbol has been rendered as `\vert` to avoid breaking the table pipe syntax. This is a critical LaTeX-in-Markdown isolation rule for KTU notes.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking Systems:** HLD security ensures that the **Card Data Environment (CDE)** is segmented from corporate IT, satisfying **PCI-DSS Requirement 1**.
- **Healthcare:** Patient data flows across HL7/FHIR interfaces — HLD defines mTLS, OAuth2 scopes, and audit logging.
- **IoT & Embedded:** Defines **secure boot, hardware root of trust, and OTA update signing** at the architecture level.
- **Cloud-Native Microservices:** HLD specifies **service mesh (Istio/Linkerd) sidecar mTLS**, **API gateway throttling**, and **zero-trust network access (ZTNA)** policies.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Security Activities Across SDLC Phases

| SDLC Phase | Security Activity | Cost Multiplier (vs. HLD) | Example Artifact |
|---|---|---|---|
| Requirements | Security Requirements Elicitation | **0.5x** | Security User Stories, Abuse Cases |
| **High-Level Design** | **Threat Modeling + Pattern Selection** | **1x (Baseline)** | **STRIDE Report, HLD Document** |
| Low-Level Design | Secure Coding Standards, Crypto Algorithm Selection | **5x** | LLD with input validation rules |
| Implementation | Static Analysis (SAST), Peer Review | **10x** | Secure code modules |
| Testing | DAST, Penetration Testing | **15x** | Pen-test report |
| Maintenance | Patch Management, Incident Response | **30x – 100x** | CVE patch tickets |

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: Defense-in-Depth Combined Probability

Let us derive the **survival probability** when a system is protected by $n$ independent security layers, each with a probability $p_i$ of **defeating** an attack.

**Step 1 — Define the failure of a single layer.**
The probability that layer $i$ fails to stop the attacker is:

$$q_i = 1 - p_i$$

**Step 2 — Model independence.**
Assume each layer acts independently (a strong assumption, but architecturally desirable — this is *why* we use diverse controls).

**Step 3 — Compute the probability that ALL layers fail simultaneously.**
By the multiplication rule for independent events:

$$P_{all\ fail} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} q_i = \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - p_i)$$

**Step 4 — Compute the survival probability.**
The probability that the system **survives** the attack is the complement:

$$P_{survive} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - p_i)$$

**Step 5 — Example numerical evaluation.**
Suppose a 3-layer defense: WAF ($p_1 = 0.7$), IAM ($p_2 = 0.8$), Encryption-at-rest ($p_3 = 0.9$).

$$P_{survive} = 1 - (1 - 0.7)(1 - 0.8)(1 - 0.9)$$

$$P_{survive} = 1 - (0.3)(0.2)(0.1)$$

$$P_{survive} = 1 - 0.006$$

$$P_{survive} = 0.994$$

So the system has a **99.4% survival probability** — a clear mathematical justification for Defense-in-Depth.

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation: Risk Quantification (ALE Method)

**Step 1 — Identify the asset.**
Let the asset be a customer database valued at **₹ 50,00,000** (₹ 50 Lakhs).

**Step 2 — Determine the Exposure Factor (EF).**
If a SQL injection dumps 60% of the records, then $EF = 0.6$.

**Step 3 — Compute Single Loss Expectancy.**

$$SLE = Asset\ Value \times EF = 50,00,000 \times 0.6 = 30,00,000$$

**Step 4 — Determine Annual Rate of Occurrence (ARO).**
Industry data suggests such an attack is attempted **4 times per year** without controls, reduced to **0.5 times per year** with HLD security. So $ARO = 0.5$.

**Step 5 — Compute Annual Loss Expectancy.**

$$ALE = SLE \times ARO = 30,00,000 \times 0.5 = 15,00,000$$

**Step 6 — Compare with the cost of HLD security control.**
If a WAF + code review + IAM costs ₹ 4,00,000/year, the **Return on Security Investment (ROSI)** is:

$$ROSI = \frac{ALE_{before} - ALE_{after} - Cost\ of\ Control}{Cost\ of\ Control}$$

$$ROSI = \frac{(30,00,000 \times 4) - 15,00,000 - 4,00,000}{4,00,000} = \frac{1,01,00,000}{4,00,000} = 25.25$$

A ROSI of **25.25** means every ₹ 1 spent on HLD security returns **₹ 25.25 in avoided losses** — a powerful boardroom argument.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: STRIDE-to-Control Mapping

| STRIDE Threat | Affected DFD Element | Architectural Control (HLD Level) |
|---|---|---|
| **S**poofing | Process, External Entity | mTLS, OAuth 2.0, Kerberos, Mutual Authentication |
| **T**ampering | Data Store, Data Flow | Digital Signatures, HMAC, Database Constraints |
| **R**epudiation | Process | Centralized Audit Logging, Blockchain Anchoring |
| **I**nformation Disclosure | Data Flow, Data Store | TLS 1.3, AES-256, Field-Level Encryption, DLP |
| **D**enial of Service | Process, Data Flow | Rate Limiting, CDN, Auto-scaling, Circuit Breakers |
| **E**levation of Privilege | Process | RBAC, Least Privilege, PAM, Zero Standing Privilege |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

The following diagram follows the strict rules:
- All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `nodeReq`, `nodeHLD`).
- No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as node names.
- All node labels contain only clean uppercase alphanumeric text (no bold, italic, or HTML tables).

### 4.2 SDLC Phase Diagram with Security Integration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeReq["REQUIREMENTS GATHERING"] --> nodeHLD["HIGH LEVEL DESIGN HLD"]
    nodeHLD --> nodeLLD["LOW LEVEL DESIGN LLD"]
    nodeLLD --> nodeImpl["IMPLEMENTATION"]
    nodeImpl --> nodeTest["TESTING"]
    nodeTest --> nodeDeploy["DEPLOYMENT"]
    nodeDeploy --> nodeMaint["MAINTENANCE"]

    nodeSecReq["SECURITY REQUIREMENTS ELICITATION"] -.-> nodeReq
    nodeThreat["THREAT MODELING STRIDE PASTA"] -.-> nodeHLD
    nodePatterns["SECURITY PATTERN SELECTION"] -.-> nodeHLD
    nodeTrust["TRUST ZONE DEFINITION"] -.-> nodeHLD
    nodeCrypto["CRYPTO ALGORITHM CHOICE"] -.-> nodeLLD
    nodeSAST["STATIC ANALYSIS SAST"] -.-> nodeImpl
    nodeDAST["DYNAMIC ANALYSIS DAST"] -.-> nodeTest
    nodePen["PENETRATION TESTING"] -.-> nodeTest
    nodePatch["PATCH MANAGEMENT"] -.-> nodeMaint

    style nodeHLD fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:4px
    style nodeThreat fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    style nodePatterns fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeTrust fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:** The HLD phase is highlighted in gold as the **critical security decision point**. The green dashed arrows on the right represent **shift-left security activities** — note how Threat Modeling, Pattern Selection, and Trust Zone Definition all converge on HLD.

### 4.3 Trust Zone Architecture (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeUser["END USER BROWSER MOBILE"] -->|HTTPS TLS 1.3| nodeEdge
    nodeEdge["EDGE LAYER CDN WAF DDOS PROTECTION"] -->|TRUSTED| nodeApi
    nodeApi["API GATEWAY AUTHENTICATION RATE LIMIT"] -->|OAUTH2 JWT| nodeApp
    nodeApp["APPLICATION TIER BUSINESS LOGIC"] -->|RBAC| nodeData
    nodeData["DATA TIER ENCRYPTION AT REST KEY VAULT"] -->|AUDIT LOG| nodeSIEM

    nodeSec["SECURITY SERVICES IAM VAULT SIEM HSM"] -.-> nodeEdge
    nodeSec -.-> nodeApi
    nodeSec -.-> nodeApp
    nodeSec -.-> nodeData

    style nodeSec fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px
    style nodeEdge fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeApi fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeData fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:** The red cross-cutting layer is the **Security Services Plane** (HLD-defined) that injects identity, secrets, and observability into every tier. This is the essence of *defense in depth at the architecture level*.

### 4.4 STRIDE Threat Modeling Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["SYSTEM ARCHITECTURE"] --> nodeDFD["DRAW DATA FLOW DIAGRAM DFD"]
    nodeDFD --> nodeIdentify["IDENTIFY TRUST BOUNDARIES"]
    nodeIdentify --> nodeStride["APPLY STRIDE PER ELEMENT"]
    nodeStride --> nodeScore["SCORE DREAD RISK RATING"]
    nodeScore --> nodeMitigate["SELECT MITIGATION CONTROL"]
    nodeMitigate --> nodeValidate["VALIDATE WITH STAKEHOLDERS"]
    nodeValidate --> nodeStart

    style nodeStart fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeStride fill:#FFA500,stroke:#FF0000,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeMitigate fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:** A continuous loop — threat modeling is **iterative**, not a one-time activity. Each design revision triggers a new STRIDE pass.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define High-Level Design (HLD) in the context of secure software engineering. List any four security artifacts produced during the HLD phase.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
High-Level Design (HLD) is the architectural design phase of the SDLC where the system's overall structure, modules, data flows, and inter-component interfaces are defined, *before* detailed coding begins. In secure software engineering, HLD embeds security controls into the architectural blueprint.

Four security artifacts produced:
1. **System Context Diagram** showing trust boundaries.
2. **Data Flow Diagram (DFD)** at Level 0 and Level 1.
3. **Threat Model Report** (e.g., STRIDE output).
4. **Security Architecture Document** defining trust zones, IAM, and cryptographic controls.

*(Each artifact = 0.5 mark, definition = 1 mark)*

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**What is the STRIDE model? List its six threat categories.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
STRIDE is a threat modeling framework developed by Microsoft, used during the HLD phase to systematically identify architectural threats by applying six categories to every Data Flow Diagram (DFD) element.

| Letter | Threat |
|---|---|
| **S** | Spoofing |
| **T** | Tampering |
| **R** | Repudiation |
| **I** | Information Disclosure |
| **D** | Denial of Service |
| **E** | Elevation of Privilege |

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for correctly listing the six categories)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option Set 1**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Handling Security with High-Level Design**. Discuss **Defense-in-Depth** as an architectural security strategy with a suitable block diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Apply the **STRIDE** threat modeling methodology to a **Cloud-Based Online Examination System** that uses a web portal, an API server, and a PostgreSQL database. Identify at least **one threat per category** and recommend **one mitigation control** for each. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Concept (3 marks):** Handling security with HLD is the proactive integration of security controls, threat mitigations, and architectural safeguards during the high-level design phase — *before* any code is written. It follows the **shift-left** principle, where the cost of fixing a vulnerability is lowest at design time (ratio of approximately **1:100** compared to post-deployment fixes, as per **NIST SP 800-64**).

**Defense-in-Depth Explanation (3 marks):** Defense-in-Depth is a layered security strategy where multiple independent controls are stacked so that the failure of one control does not result in a system compromise. The layers typically include:
1. **Perimeter Layer** — Firewall, WAF, DDoS protection.
2. **Network Layer** — Segmentation, VLANs, mTLS.
3. **Host Layer** — Hardening, EDR, patching.
4. **Application Layer** — Input validation, authentication, authorization.
5. **Data Layer** — Encryption at rest, tokenization, key management.

Mathematically, if each layer has an independent defense probability $p_i$, the system survival probability is:

$$P_{survive} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - p_i)$$

**Block Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to the **Trust Zone Architecture** diagram in Section 4.3 of these notes.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

| STRIDE Category | Threat Identified | Mitigation Control (HLD Level) |
|---|---|---|
| **S**poofing | Attacker impersonates a student to access another candidate's exam | OAuth 2.0 + OpenID Connect with MFA at API Gateway |
| **T**ampering | Attacker tampers with question paper in transit | TLS 1.3 + digital signatures on question payloads |
| **R**epudiation | Student denies submitting answers | Centralized audit log (WORM storage) + timestamping |
| **I**nformation Disclosure | SQL injection leaks other students' answers | Parameterized queries, WAF, field-level encryption |
| **D**enial of Service | Bot flood overwhelms exam portal | Rate limiting at API Gateway, CDN, CAPTCHA |
| **E**levation of Privilege | Student escalates to admin role | RBAC with least-privilege + Zero Standing Privilege |

*(Each row = 1 mark; 6 rows = 6 marks; concluding paragraph on shift-left benefit = 1 mark)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option Set 2**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Supplementary]**

**(a)** Discuss the **CIA Triad** and the **AAARR (OCTAVE) risk dimensions**. Explain how they are applied during the HLD phase to specify security requirements. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A banking application has an asset (transaction database) valued at ₹ 2 Crore. The exposure factor for a successful breach is estimated at 0.4. Without controls, the attack occurs **3 times per year**; with HLD security controls, it is reduced to **0.3 times per year**. The HLD control costs **₹ 12 Lakh per year**. Calculate the **SLE, ALE, and ROSI**, and justify whether the control should be adopted. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**CIA Triad (3 marks):**
- **Confidentiality** — Only authorized users can read sensitive data. HLD controls: encryption (AES-256), access control lists.
- **Integrity** — Data is not altered in transit or at rest. HLD controls: HMAC, digital signatures, database constraints.
- **Availability** — System is accessible when needed. HLD controls: redundant data centers, auto-scaling, DDoS mitigation.

**AAARR / OCTAVE Dimensions (3 marks):**
- **Asset** — What we protect (data, hardware, reputation).
- **Attacker** — Who is the adversary (insider, script kiddie, APT)?
- **Access** — How does the attacker reach the asset (network, physical, social)?
- **Audit** — How do we detect and log the attack (SIEM, audit logs)?
- **Action** — What is the attacker's goal (theft, destruction, disruption)?

**Application in HLD (1 mark):** Each CIA pillar is mapped to a security requirement in the HLD document; AAARR is used during threat modeling to ensure no critical dimension is overlooked.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Step 1 — Compute SLE (1 mark):**

$$SLE = Asset\ Value \times EF = 2,00,00,000 \times 0.4 = 80,00,000$$

**Step 2 — Compute ALE without controls (1 mark):**

$$ALE_{before} = 80,00,000 \times 3 = 2,40,00,000$$

**Step 3 — Compute ALE with HLD controls (1 mark):**

$$ALE_{after} = 80,00,000 \times 0.3 = 24,00,000$$

**Step 4 — Compute Risk Reduction (1 mark):**

$$\Delta ALE = 2,40,00,000 - 24,00,000 = 2,16,00,000$$

**Step 5 — Compute ROSI (2 marks):**

$$ROSI = \frac{\Delta ALE - Cost\ of\ Control}{Cost\ of\ Control}$$

$$ROSI = \frac{2,16,00,000 - 12,00,000}{12,00,000} = \frac{2,04,00,000}{12,00,000} \approx 17.0$$

**Step 6 — Justification (1 mark):** Since ROSI ≈ 17 (i.e., ₹ 17 saved for every ₹ 1 spent), the HLD control is **strongly justified** and should be adopted.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Confusing ARO with EF. Remember: ARO is a *frequency per year*, EF is a *fraction* (0 to 1).
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Using `ALE_before = Asset Value × ARO` (skipping SLE). Always compute SLE first, then multiply by ARO.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting to subtract the *cost of the control* in the ROSI numerator. A control with great risk reduction but enormous cost can still have a poor ROSI.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Not stating units in the final answer (₹ Lakhs vs. ₹ Crores). KTU evaluators deduct 0.5 marks for unit ambiguity.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Writing `2 Crore` as `2000000` — use the Indian numbering system (₹ 2,00,00,000) consistent with KTU's regional convention.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- [ ] **HLD** sits between Requirements and LLD in the SDLC and is the **lowest-cost, highest-impact** phase for security decisions.
- [ ] The **shift-left principle** reduces the cost ratio from **1:100** (HLD) to **1:1** (requirements).
- [ ] The **CIA Triad** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability) is the foundation of every security requirement.
- [ ] **STRIDE** = Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, Denial of Service, Elevation of Privilege.
- [ ] **DREAD** risk score = average of 5 dimensions (Damage, Reproducibility, Exploitability, Affected Users, Discoverability).
- [ ] **Trust boundaries** must be drawn on every DFD; every boundary crossing is a candidate for an authentication/authorization control.
- [ ] **Defense-in-Depth** survival probability: $P_{survive} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - p_i)$ — more diverse, independent layers always help.
- [ ] **ALE** = SLE × ARO; **SLE** = Asset Value × Exposure Factor.
- [ ] **ROSI** = (ALE_reduction − Control_Cost) / Control_Cost. A positive ROSI justifies the investment.
- [ ] HLD security patterns include: **Authenticator, Security Gateway, Trusted Proxy, Secure Logger, Checkpointed System**.
- [ ] **5 Trust Zones** typically used: Public, DMZ, Internal, Restricted, Secure Vault.
- [ ] Standards to cite in answers: **NIST SP 800-160 Vol. 1**, **ISO 27001 Annex A**, **OWASP ASVS**, **PCI-DSS**, **NIST SP 800-53**.
- [ ] Threat modeling is **iterative** — re-run STRIDE on every design revision.
- [ ] Always label **units** in numerical answers to avoid KTU mark deductions.
- [ ] In Mermaid diagrams, never use `end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, or `style` as node IDs.
- [ ] In markdown tables, use `\vert` instead of `|` for absolute-value notation.
- [ ] Every LaTeX display block (`$$ ... $$`) must be **isolated with a blank line** above and below.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: General Design Notions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAIT418 - SECURITY ASSESSMENT AND RISK ANALYSIS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** General Design Notions # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What are "General Design Notions" in Security? In the context of **Security Assessment and Risk Analysis (PEAIT418)**, *General Design Notions* refers to the foundational, time-tested **engineering principles of secure system design**. These are abstract guidelines — not specific algorithms or tools — that every security architect, software engineer, and risk analyst must internalize before building, evaluating, or auditing any trustworthy system. > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned)** > *General Design Notions are a set of high-level, abstract guiding principles that govern the construction of secure computing systems. They influence how protection mechanisms are chosen, how components are isolated, and how trust is distributed. They form the philosophical bedrock of security engineering.* The most widely cited and academically authoritative formulation of these notions comes from the landmark **1975 paper by Jerome H. Saltzer and Michael D. Schroeder** of MIT, titled *"The Protection of Information in Computer Systems."* Their **8 design principles** are universally accepted as the canon of secure system design and are examinable in KTU Module 2. ## 1.2 The 8 Saltzer–Schroeder Design Principles (At a Glance) | # | Principle | One-Line Essence | |---|-----------|------------------| | 1 | **Least Privilege** | Give only the minimum permissions needed. | | 2 | **Fail-Safe Defaults** | Default to *deny* when in doubt. | | 3 | **Economy of Mechanism** | Keep the design small, simple, and auditable. | | 4 | **Complete Mediation** | Check *every* access, *every* time. | | 5 | **Open Design** | Security must not depend on secrecy of mechanism. | | 6 | **Separation of Privilege** | Require *multiple* conditions to unlock access. | | 7 | **Least Common Mechanism** | Minimize shared resources between users. | | 8 | **Psychological Acceptability** | If it's not usable, users will bypass it. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 2** > Students must be able to **list, define, illustrate, and critically compare** these 8 principles. Examiners frequently frame Part A (3-mark) questions as *"Explain any two design principles"* and Part B (14-mark) questions as *"Discuss the Saltzer–Schroeder principles with real-world examples."* ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — Designing a Secure House Imagine you are an architect designing a **bank vault inside a house**. The "General Design Notions" are the *philosophies* you would naturally adopt even without a computer science degree: - You don't give every family member a master key → **Least Privilege**. - The vault's default state is *locked*, not open → **Fail-Safe Defaults**. - You don't install 17 different exotic locks when 2 strong ones suffice → **Economy of Mechanism**. - You check the ID card at the door, the vault door, and the safe deposit box — never skip a check → **Complete Mediation**. - You use a well-known, certified lock brand, not a "secret custom lock" — because secret locks can be reverse-engineered or the maker blackmailed → **Open Design**. - You require *two different managers* with two different keys to open the vault → **Separation of Privilege**. - You build separate HVAC ducts for each apartment so one tenant cannot tap into another's air supply → **Least Common Mechanism**. - You place the vault's control panel in an intuitive location — not behind a fake wall — because confused users will write passwords on sticky notes → **Psychological Acceptability**. This same mental model, formalized, is what Saltzer and Schroeder codified for computer systems in 1975, and it remains the spine of modern **zero-trust architecture** and **NIST SP 800-160** systems security engineering guidelines. ## 1.4 Why "General" and Not "Specific"? The word *general* is deliberate. These are **not rules like "use AES-256"** or "deploy a firewall on port 443." They are **meta-rules** — guiding stars that apply whether you are: - Designing a 1970s mainframe OS, - A modern cloud-native microservice mesh, - An IoT medical device firmware, or - A blockchain consensus protocol. > [!TIP] > **Examiner's Trick Question Alert** > A common KTU trap asks: *"Is 'use a firewall' a design principle?"* — **No.** A firewall is a *mechanism*.
