```mermaid
    S8 --> S9{"STEP 9: Residual Risk Acceptable?"}:::decision
    S9 -->|Yes| S10["STEP 10: Pattern Certified"]:::step
    S9 -->|No| S4

    classDef step fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#93c5fd,color:#ffffff
    classDef decision fill:#92400e,stroke:#fbbf24,color:#ffffff
```

**Interpretation:** The flow is iterative — if validation fails, the engineer returns to pattern selection. This represents *defensive iteration*, a hallmark of mature security engineering.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Pattern Instantiation UML (Secure Proxy)

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Client {
        +request()
    }
    class SecureProxy {
        -realSubject: RealSubject
        +request()
        +authenticate()
        +sanitize()
        +log()
    }
    class RealSubject {
        +request()
    }
    class SubjectInterface {
        <<interface>>
        +request()
    }

    Client --> SecureProxy : delegates
    SecureProxy ..|> SubjectInterface
    RealSubject ..|> SubjectInterface
    SecureProxy --> RealSubject : controlled access
```

**Interpretation:** The `SecureProxy` wraps `RealSubject`, implementing the same interface. Before forwarding the request, it authenticates, sanitizes, and logs — embodying the **interception-and-validation** essence of the Secure Proxy pattern.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "Security Design Pattern" and list any four core structural elements that must be documented for every pattern.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Security Design Pattern** is a reusable, generalized architectural solution that systematically addresses a commonly occurring security problem, such as authentication bypass or injection attacks. It encapsulates proven mitigation strategies derived from collective industry experience.

The four core structural elements (of the six-tuple template) are:
1. **Name** — Unique identifier (e.g., *Secure Proxy*).
2. **Intent** — The security goal.
3. **Problem** — The recurring weakness addressed.
4. **Solution** — The architectural arrangement mitigating it.

*(Examiner note: naming Problem + Solution + Consequences + Known Uses also earns full credit.)*

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Creational, Structural, and Behavioral design patterns with one security-relevant example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Category | Focus | Security Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Creational** | Object instantiation mechanisms | *Singleton* used for single Secure Logger instance to prevent log forgery via parallel instances. |
| **Structural** | Class/object composition | *Secure Proxy* mediates access to sensitive objects. |
| **Behavioral** | Object communication and responsibility delegation | *Chain of Responsibility* chains input validators. |

The differentiation earns full marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **Single Point of Entry** security design pattern in detail. Describe its **intent**, **problem context**, **UML structure**, and **security benefits**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Defense in Depth** pattern. Show how multiple independent layers combine to protect a critical asset. Discuss at least **two trade-offs** of this pattern. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) Single Point of Entry (7 Marks)**

**Intent (1 Mark):** To centralize authentication and request validation through one chokepoint, eliminating distributed authentication logic that can become inconsistent or bypassed.

**Problem Context (2 Marks):** In large distributed systems, authentication code scattered across services leads to *inconsistent policy enforcement*, *duplicate vulnerabilities*, and *audit gaps*. Attackers exploit the weakest of many entry points.

**UML Structure (2 Marks):** The pattern is realized as:

$$
Client \rightarrow AuthGateway_{SPE} \rightarrow \{Service_1, Service_2, ..., Service_n\}
$$

The `AuthGateway` becomes the single chokepoint enforcing authentication, input validation, and rate limiting. All services trust only requests bearing gateway-issued tokens.

**Security Benefits (2 Marks):**
- **Centralized policy** — one place to update authentication rules.
- **Audit completeness** — every request logged once.
- **Reduced attack surface** — 12 distributed endpoints collapse to 1 hardened gateway.
- **Easier compliance** — PCI-DSS, HIPAA audits become simpler.

**Trade-off:** The gateway becomes a *single point of failure* — a high-availability cluster is mandatory.

---

**(b) Defense in Depth (7 Marks)**

**Block Diagram (3 Marks):**

```
LAYER 1 (Perimeter)    : Firewall + IDS
            ↓
LAYER 2 (Auth)         : Single Point of Entry + Authenticator
            ↓
LAYER 3 (Authorization): RBAC Checker + Checkpoint
            ↓
LAYER 4 (Data)         : Input Validator + Cryptographic Storage + Secure Logger
```

**Explanation (2 Marks):** Each layer is an independent control. An attacker breaching the firewall still must bypass authentication, then authorization, then data-layer validation. Failure of one layer does not compromise the system.

**Trade-offs (2 Marks):**
1. **Complexity overhead** — multiple layers increase architectural and operational complexity.
2. **Performance cost** — each layer adds latency (typically 5–20ms per layer).

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Discuss the **Secure Proxy** pattern with its UML structure. Explain how it mitigates **direct object reference vulnerabilities** and **input injection attacks**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Input Validator** and **Secure Logger** patterns. Describe how their combination provides defense against **SQL injection** and **repudiation attacks** respectively. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) Secure Proxy Pattern (7 Marks)**

**Definition (1 Mark):** A Secure Proxy is a structural design pattern that places a protective intermediary between the client and the actual subject object, intercepting, validating, and sanitizing every interaction.

**UML Structure (2 Marks):**

```
Client → SecureProxy → RealSubject
         (authenticates, sanitizes, logs)
```

**Mitigation of Direct Object Reference Vulnerabilities (2 Marks):** Without a proxy, a client may pass internal object references (e.g., database row IDs) that grant unintended access. The Secure Proxy **rewrites or validates** every reference, ensuring clients cannot manipulate internal handles directly. Authorization is enforced at the proxy boundary.

**Mitigation of Input Injection Attacks (2 Marks):** The proxy intercepts user-supplied data *before* it reaches the RealSubject (e.g., a database). It applies:
- Whitelist validation,
- Character escaping,
- Length enforcement,
- Type checking.

This blocks SQLi, XSS, and command injection at the proxy boundary.

---

**(b) Input Validator + Secure Logger (7 Marks)**

**Input Validator (3 Marks):**
- **Intent:** Sanitize all untrusted input at system boundaries.
- **Structure:** Wraps input handlers with a validation pipeline (chain of validators).
- **Defense against SQL Injection:** Validator rejects malicious payloads such as `' OR '1'='1`, applies parameterized-query enforcement, and strips metacharacters.
- **Implementation:** Whitelist regex patterns, length checks, type checks.

**Secure Logger (3 Marks):**
- **Intent:** Produce tamper-evident, integrity-protected audit records.
- **Structure:** Singleton logger writing to append-only storage with cryptographic hashes chained (Merkle-style) or signed via HMAC.
- **Defense against Repudiation:** The user cannot deny an action because the log entry is cryptographically bound and timestamped.

**Combined Synergy (1 Mark):** When combined, the Input Validator ensures *bad data never reaches the data store*, while the Secure Logger *records every blocked attempt*, enabling forensic reconstruction of attack attempts.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — KTU Board Examiner Observations:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the six-tuple template.** Many students describe a pattern without explicitly naming *Intent, Problem, Solution, Consequences, Known Uses*. This loses 1–2 marks per question. **Always structure answers around the six-tuple.**
>
> 2. **Confusing patterns with algorithms.** A pattern is *architectural*; an algorithm is *procedural*. Writing code in a pattern question is a category error — marks deducted.
>
> 3. **Naming only one pattern for multi-pattern threats.** SQL injection is mitigated by *Input Validator* + *Secure Proxy* + *Defense in Depth*. Listing only one forfeits 30–40% of allocated marks.
>
> 4. **Ignoring trade-offs.** Examiners explicitly allocate 1–2 marks for *Consequences* (trade-offs). Skipping this loses marks even if the rest is correct.
>
> 5. **Memorizing without understanding STRIDE.** Pattern-to-threat mapping questions require STRIDE fluency. Without it, answers appear memorized rather than analytical.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔑 **Design Pattern** = Reusable architectural solution to a recurring problem.
- 🔑 **Six-tuple template**: Name, Intent, Problem, Solution, Consequences, Known Uses.
- 🔑 **Three GoF categories**: Creational, Structural, Behavioral — Structural dominates security.
- 🔑 **Twelve core security patterns**: SPE, Checkpoint, Defense in Depth, Secure Proxy, Secure Façade, Authenticator, Authorization, RBAC, Session, Secure Logger, Input Validator, Cryptographic Storage.
- 🔑 **STRIDE mnemonic**: *Some Tigers Pounce In Darkness, Easily* — Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, DoS, Elevation of Privilege.
- 🔑 **CIA Triad**: Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability — the destination of every pattern.
- 🔑 **Risk reduction equation**: $R_{residual} = R_{inherent} \times (1 - E_p)$.
- 🔑 **Pattern coverage metric**: $C_p = (N_{applied}/N_{total}) \times 100\%$.
- 🔑 **Trade-off awareness** earns marks — always list at least one.
- 🔑 **Pattern selection** is iterative — validation failures cause re-selection.
- 🔑 **Real-world anchors**: Banking, Healthcare, E-Commerce, Defense, Cloud.
- 🔑 **Layered architecture** (Defense in Depth) is the gold standard for critical assets.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Assurance Activities and Strategies That Support Early Vulnerability Detection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT418 - SECURITY ASSESSMENT AND RISK ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Quality Assurance Activities and Strategies That Support Early Vulnerability Detection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 2 — Quality Assurance Activities and Strategies That Support Early Vulnerability Detection

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Syllabus Aligned):**
> **Quality Assurance (QA) in the context of security** refers to a systematic, planned set of activities embedded within the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC) that ensure that security controls, secure coding standards, and vulnerability-resilience mechanisms are designed, implemented, and verified **before, during, and immediately after** code is produced. **Early Vulnerability Detection** is the proactive identification of security weaknesses at the earliest possible phase of development (requirements/design/coding) rather than after deployment, when remediation cost is exponentially higher.

### 🔑 Intuitive Analogy — "The House Construction Metaphor"

Imagine you are building a 10-storey apartment building.

- **Reactive Detection (Late)** = Waiting until residents move in, then realizing the foundation has cracks and the wiring is faulty. You now have to **evacuate families, break walls, and retrofit** — costs are **30× to 100× higher**.
- **Proactive QA (Early)** = The structural engineer, electrician, and plumber inspect the **blueprint first**, then the **scaffolding**, then the **plastering stage** — every nail and wire is verified *before* the next layer is added.

In software:
- The **blueprint** = Requirements & Threat Model
- The **scaffolding** = Architecture & Design Review
- The **plastering** = Secure Code Review & Unit Tests
- The **handover** = Penetration Testing before release

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industry Metric (IBM Systems Sciences Institute, widely cited in KTU textbooks):**
> - Fixing a defect in **Design** costs **1×**
> - Fixing a defect in **Implementation** costs **6×**
> - Fixing a defect in **Testing** costs **15×**
> - Fixing a defect in **Production (Post-Release)** costs **100×**

This exponential cost curve is the **single most important justification** for embedding QA activities early in the SDLC.

### 🎯 Why "Early" Matters — The Core Premise

Modern software systems are **distributed, API-driven, and dependency-heavy**. A single vulnerable open-source library (e.g., Log4Shell in 2021) can compromise millions of applications. Therefore, KTU's 2024 syllabus emphasizes that **early vulnerability detection is not a luxury — it is an engineering and economic necessity**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Primary Goal of QA in Security (KTU 2024):**
> To **shift security left** in the SDLC so that vulnerabilities are detected at the **point of introduction**, not at the point of exploitation. This is achieved by combining **preventive controls** (training, secure design) with **detective controls** (static analysis, code review, fuzzing).

### 🏷️ Taxonomy of QA Activities for Early Vulnerability Detection

| **Category** | **Purpose** | **Phase Applied** |
|---|---|---|
| **Security Requirements Engineering** | Define "what must be secure" | Requirements |
| **Threat Modeling** | Identify what could go wrong | Design |
| **Secure Architecture Review** | Validate defensive design patterns | Design |
| **Secure Coding Standards** | Prevent common flaws (OWASP Top 10) | Implementation |
| **Peer Code Review** | Human-driven vulnerability hunting | Implementation |
| **Static Application Security Testing (SAST)** | Automated source-code scanning | Implementation/Build |
| **Software Composition Analysis (SCA)** | Detect vulnerable third-party libs | Build |
| **Unit & Integration Security Tests** | Test security at component level | Testing |
| **Dynamic Application Security Testing (DAST)** | Black-box runtime testing | Testing/Staging |
| **Interactive Application Security Testing (IAST)** | Runtime + code instrumentation | Testing |
| **Fuzz Testing** | Send malformed inputs to find crashes | Testing |
| **Penetration Testing** | Adversary simulation | Pre-release |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 🧠 The Theoretical Foundation — "Shift-Left Security" Doctrine

The **shift-left** principle states that security activities traditionally performed *after* coding (right side of SDLC) should be **moved earlier** (left side) in the lifecycle. This is the backbone of all modern DevSecOps practices endorsed by NIST SP 800-218 (SSDF) and ISO/IEC 27034.

### 📐 The Five Pillars of Early Vulnerability Detection

#### **Pillar 1: Preventive QA — Security Requirements & Threat Modeling**
- Capture **security user stories** and **abuse cases** during requirements gathering.
- Apply threat-modeling frameworks: **STRIDE** (Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, Denial of Service, Elevation of Privilege), **PASTA** (Process for Attack Simulation and Threat Analysis), or **VAST** (Visual, Agile, Simple Threat).
- Output: A **threat register** with prioritized risks.

#### **Pillar 2: Detective QA — Static Analysis (SAST)**
- Scans **source code, bytecode, or binary** without executing the program.
- Detects: SQL injection, XSS, buffer overflows, hard-coded credentials, weak crypto.
- Tools: SonarQube, Checkmarx, Fortify, Veracode.
- **Limitation**: High false-positive rate; cannot detect runtime/config vulnerabilities.

#### **Pillar 3: Detective QA — Dynamic Analysis (DAST)**
- Black-box testing of the **running application** from outside.
- Simulates attacker behavior: crawls the app, injects payloads, observes responses.
- Tools: OWASP ZAP, Burp Suite, Invicti (formerly Netsparker).
- **Limitation**: Cannot pinpoint exact code location of vulnerability.

#### **Pillar 4: Detective QA — Interactive Analysis (IAST)**
- Combines SAST + DAST using **runtime instrumentation** (agents inside the app server).
- Provides line-of-code accuracy with low false positives.
- Tools: Contrast Security, HCL AppScan.

#### **Pillar 5: Detective QA — Software Composition Analysis (SCA)**
- Inventories all **open-source and third-party components** (and their transitive dependencies).
- Cross-references against vulnerability databases: **NVD (National Vulnerability Database)**, **CVE**, **OSS Index**, **Snyk DB**.
- Critical because **>80% of modern codebases** are open-source components (Synopsys OSSRA Report).

### 📊 Supporting Strategies

- **Secure Code Review (Manual + Automated):** Human reviewers check for business-logic flaws automated tools miss.
- **Pair Programming & Mob Programming:** Two developers write code together — immediate peer scrutiny.
- **Security Unit Testing:** Writing tests specifically for security assertions (e.g., "input `' OR 1=1 --` must be rejected").
- **Continuous Integration / Continuous Deployment (CI/CD) Security Gates:** Build fails if SAST/SCA finds high-severity issues.
- **Bug Bounty Programs:** External ethical hackers report vulnerabilities for rewards — covers gaps internal teams miss.
- **Security Champions Program:** Designated developers in each team who advocate and enforce secure practices.

---

## 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **high-yield formulas** frequently tested in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE and internal assessments. Memorize units and apply correct rounding.

| **Formula Name** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Description of Variables** | **Standard Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Defect Density (DD)** | $DD = \dfrac{D}{S}$ | $D$ = Number of known defects, $S$ = Size in KLOC | defects / KLOC |
| **Vulnerability Density (VD)** | $VD = \dfrac{V}{S}$ | $V$ = Number of vulnerabilities, $S$ = Size | vulns / KLOC |
| **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD)** | $MTTD = \dfrac{\sum (T_{detected} - T_{introduced})}{N}$ | Sum of detection lags ÷ number of defects | hours / days |
| **Mean Time to Remediate (MTTR)** | $MTTR = \dfrac{\sum (T_{fixed} - T_{detected})}{N}$ | Average fix turnaround time | hours / days |
| **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** | $RPN = S \times L \times D$ | $S$ = Severity, $L$ = Likelihood, $D$ = Detectability (1–10 each) | dimensionless index |
| **Cyclomatic Complexity (McCabe)** | $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ | $E$ = edges, $N$ = nodes, $P$ = connected components | dimensionless |
| **Code Coverage** | $Cov = \dfrac{L_{executed}}{L_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Executed lines / total lines | percentage (%) |
| **Cost-of-Fix Ratio** | $C_{phase} = C_{design} \times M_{phase}$ | $M$ = cost multiplier per phase (Design:1, Code:6, Test:15, Prod:100) | currency units |
| **False Positive Rate (FPR)** | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | $FP$ = false positives, $TN$ = true negatives | ratio (0–1) |
| **False Negative Rate (FNR)** | $FNR = \dfrac{FN}{FN + TP}$ | $FN$ = false negatives, $TP$ = true positives | ratio (0–1) |
| **Precision (PPV)** | $Precision = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Positive predictive value | ratio (0–1) |
| **Recall (TPR / Sensitivity)** | $Recall = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | True positive rate | ratio (0–1) |
| **F1-Score** | $F1 = 2 \times \dfrac{P \times R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall | ratio (0–1) |
| **SAST Tool Effectiveness** | $Eff = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Overall accuracy of a static analyzer | ratio (0–1) |
| **ROI of Early Detection** | $ROI = \dfrac{(C_{late} - C_{early}) - I_{QA}}{I_{QA}} \times 100\%$ | Savings minus QA investment, divided by investment | percentage (%) |
| **Attack Surface Reduction** | $ASR = \dfrac{A_{original} - A_{secured}}{A_{original}} \times 100\%$ | Reduction in entry points/exposed interfaces | percentage (%) |

### 🛠️ Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas are used in practice as follows:

- **Defect/Vulnerability Density** is reported in **BSIMM (Building Security In Maturity Model)** and **OWASP SAMM** assessments to benchmark organizational security maturity.
- **MTTD/MTTR** are tracked as **KPI dashboards** in CI/CD pipelines (Grafana, Datadog).
- **RPN** is borrowed from FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis) and is the foundation of **bug triage** in Jira/Azure DevOps.
- **Cyclomatic Complexity** above 10 per function is a code-smell flag — high complexity correlates strongly with security defects.
- **F1-Score** is the primary metric to evaluate the *balance* between catching real bugs and avoiding alert fatigue.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip (KTU 2024 Pattern):**
> Numerical problems frequently ask you to compute **Defect Density, MTTD, MTTR, RPN, and Cost Ratio**. Memorize the multipliers (1, 6, 15, 100) — they are **exam favorites**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 🧮 Worked Numerical Problems (KTU-Style Solutions)

Below are **fully solved numerical problems** with every algebraic step shown. KTU examiners award partial credit for each logical step — *never* skip transitions.

---

### 🔢 **Problem 1 — Defect Density & Vulnerability Density Calculation**

> **Question:** A web application consists of **45 KLOC** (thousand lines of code). During a 6-month QA cycle, the QA team identified **180 functional defects** and **27 security vulnerabilities** before release. Calculate the **Defect Density (DD)** and **Vulnerability Density (VD)**. Comment on whether the values are acceptable given industry benchmarks (Industry benchmark: DD < 1 defect/KLOC for shipping-quality software; VD < 0.5 vulns/KLOC for high-security apps).

#### **Step 1 — Identify the Given Variables**

$$
D = 180 \text{ (functional defects)}
$$

$$
V = 27 \text{ (security vulnerabilities)}
$$

$$
S = 45 \text{ KLOC}
$$

#### **Step 2 — Apply the Defect Density Formula**

$$
DD = \dfrac{D}{S}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
DD = \dfrac{180}{45}
$$

$$
DD = 4.0 \text{ defects per KLOC}
$$

#### **Step 3 — Apply the Vulnerability Density Formula**

$$
VD = \dfrac{V}{S}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
VD = \dfrac{27}{45}
$$

$$
VD = 0.6 \text{ vulnerabilities per KLOC}
$$

#### **Step 4 — Comparative Analysis**

$$
DD_{calculated} = 4.0 \;\;>\;\; DD_{benchmark} = 1.0
$$

$$
VD_{calculated} = 0.6 \;\;>\;\; VD_{benchmark} = 0.5
$$

#### **Step 5 — Conclusion**

The application's defect density (**4.0/KLOC**) is **4× higher** than the shipping-quality benchmark, and its vulnerability density (**0.6/KLOC**) marginally exceeds the high-security threshold. **The application is NOT production-ready**; additional QA cycles, root-cause analysis, and a security-focused testing sprint are mandatory before release.

---

### 🔢 **Problem 2 — Risk Priority Number (RPN) Triage**

> **Question:** During a security review, three vulnerabilities were discovered. Compute the RPN for each and prioritize remediation.

| **Vulnerability** | **Severity (S)** | **Likelihood (L)** | **Detectability (D)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| SQL Injection in login form | 9 | 8 | 3 |
| Missing Security Headers | 5 | 6 | 8 |
| Outdated jQuery 1.x (XSS risk) | 7 | 5 | 6 |

#### **Step 1 — Compute RPN for SQL Injection**

$$
RPN_{SQLi} = S \times L \times D
$$

$$
RPN_{SQLi} = 9 \times 8 \times 3
$$

$$
RPN_{SQLi} = 216
$$

#### **Step 2 — Compute RPN for Missing Security Headers**

$$
RPN_{Headers} = 5 \times 6 \times 8
$$

$$
RPN_{Headers} = 240
$$

#### **Step 3 — Compute RPN for Outdated jQuery**

$$
RPN_{jQuery} = 7 \times 5 \times 6
$$

$$
RPN_{jQuery} = 210
$$

#### **Step 4 — Rank by RPN (Descending)**

$$
RPN_{Headers} \; (240) \;>\; RPN_{SQLi} \; (216) \;>\; RPN_{jQuery} \; (210)
$$

#### **Step 5 — Engineering Insight**

Although intuition would prioritize SQLi first (since it is more critical), the **high detectability score** (hard to detect via existing tools) of the missing headers makes it the **highest RPN**. This illustrates that **RPN is a multi-factor prioritization mechanism** — not just severity-based. Remediation team should tackle headers first to **reduce cumulative risk quickly**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake (KTU Valuation):** Students often rank by **severity alone**. Always multiply all three factors. Partial credit (3/7) is awarded for computing one RPN; full credit requires the **comparative ranking and justification**.

---

### 🔢 **Problem 3 — Cost of Late Detection (ROI of QA Investment)**

> **Question:** A startup invests **₹5,00,000** in early-stage security QA (threat modeling + SAST + SCA). Without this QA, they estimate **50 production incidents** at an average remediation cost of **₹2,00,000** each. Calculate the **ROI of QA** and the **Cost-of-Fix Ratio** between early and late detection.

#### **Step 1 — Calculate Late-Stage Cost (Without QA)**

$$
C_{late} = N_{incidents} \times C_{avg}
$$

$$
C_{late} = 50 \times 2{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
C_{late} = 1{,}00{,}00{,}000 \; \text{(₹ 1 Crore)}
$$

#### **Step 2 — Define QA Investment**

$$
I_{QA} = 5{,}00{,}000
$$

#### **Step 3 — Apply ROI Formula**

$$
ROI = \dfrac{(C_{late} - C_{early}) - I_{QA}}{I_{QA}} \times 100\%
$$

Assuming early-stage QA cost is included in $I_{QA}$, savings = $C_{late}$:

$$
ROI = \dfrac{1{,}00{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} \times 100\%
$$

$$
ROI = \dfrac{95{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} \times 100\%
$$

$$
ROI = 19 \times 100\% = 1900\%
$$

#### **Step 4 — Cost-of-Fix Ratio (Comparison with Industry Multiplier)**

Using the IBM multiplier (100× for production vs. design):

$$
C_{prod} = 100 \times C_{design}
$$

If design-phase fix cost per defect = ₹5,000:

$$
C_{design} = 50 \times 5{,}000 = 2{,}50{,}000
$$

$$
C_{prod} = 50 \times 5{,}00{,}000 = 2{,}50{,}00{,}000
$$

Savings by early fix:

$$
\Delta C = 2{,}50{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}50{,}000 = 2{,}47{,}50{,}000
$$

#### **Step 5 — Conclusion**

The **1900% ROI** demonstrates that even a modest upfront QA investment yields **massive downstream savings**. This is the financial cornerstone of the **shift-left** business case presented to management.

---

### 🔢 **Problem 4 — Cyclomatic Complexity & Code Coverage**

> **Question:** A function has **18 nodes (N)**, **26 edges (E)**, and is a single connected component (**P = 1**). The QA team wrote **120 security unit tests**, of which **96** exercised unique branches. Compute **Cyclomatic Complexity** and **Branch Coverage**.

#### **Step 1 — Cyclomatic Complexity**

$$
V(G) = E - N + 2P
$$

$$
V(G) = 26 - 18 + 2 \times 1
$$

$$
V(G) = 8 + 2 = 10
$$

#### **Step 2 — Interpretation**

$V(G) = 10$ is at the **upper limit of acceptable complexity** (industry standard: $V(G) \leq 10$). The function is a **refactoring candidate** because higher complexity statistically correlates with security defects.

#### **Step 3 — Branch Coverage**

$$
Cov = \dfrac{L_{executed}}{L_{total}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
Cov = \dfrac{96}{120} \times 100\%
$$

$$
Cov = 80\%
$$

#### **Step 4 — Security Implication**

**80% branch coverage** means **20% of code paths are untested** — these are the **dark zones** where attackers hide logic flaws. KTU examiners expect you to recommend **raising coverage to ≥95%** for security-critical modules.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 🖼️ Diagram 1 — Shift-Left Security Across the SDLC

This diagram maps **when each QA activity occurs** along the SDLC timeline, illustrating the *shift-left* philosophy.

```mermaid
graph LR
    reqA[Requirements] --> desB[Design]
    desB --> impC[Implementation]
    impC --> tesD[Testing]
    tesD --> depE[Deployment]
    depE --> opsF[Operations]
    
    reqA -.->|Security Reqs| srm1[SR1]
    desB -.->|Threat Modeling| srm2[SR2]
    impC -.->|SAST + Code Review| srm3[SR3]
    impC -.->|SCA Dependency Scan| srm4[SR4]
    tesD -.->|DAST + IAST| srm5[SR5]
    tesD -.->|Fuzz Testing| srm6[SR6]
    depE -.->|Pen Test| srm7[SR7]
    opsF -.->|Bug Bounty| srm8[SR8]
    
    style reqA fill:#ffe4b5
    style desB fill:#ffd700
    style impC fill:#98fb98
    style tesD fill:#87ceeb
    style depE fill:#dda0dd
    style opsF fill:#f08080
    style srm1 fill:#fff
    style srm2 fill:#fff
    style srm3 fill:#fff
    style srm4 fill:#fff
    style srm5 fill:#fff
    style srm6 fill:#fff
    style srm7 fill:#fff
    style srm8 fill:#fff
```

**Reading the diagram:** The orange→green→blue→purple→red gradient is the SDLC. The dotted lines below show **at which stage each QA activity is injected**. Notice how QA activities begin at **Requirements** (leftmost) and continue until **Operations** (rightmost) — but the **density of activities is highest on the LEFT side**, embodying the shift-left principle.

---

## 🖼️ Diagram 2 — DevSecOps CI/CD Pipeline with Security Gates

A **block-level functional architecture** of how early vulnerability detection is automated inside a modern CI/CD pipeline.

```mermaid
graph TD
    devA[Developer Commits Code] --> prB[Pull Request Created]
    prB --> sastC{SAST Scan}
    sastC -->|Fail| failD[Block Merge - Notify Dev]
    sastC -->|Pass| scaE{SCA Scan}
    scaE -->|Fail| failD
    scaE -->|Pass| revF[Peer Code Review]
    revF --> revG{Approved?}
    revG -->|No| failD
    revG -->|Yes| unitH[Security Unit Tests]
    unitH --> covI{Coverage ≥ 80%?}
    covI -->|No| failD
    covI -->|Yes| buildJ[Build Artifact]
    buildJ --> dastK{DAST Scan on Staging}
    dastK -->|Fail| failD
    dastK -->|Pass| fuzzL[Fuzz Testing]
    fuzzL --> fuzzM{Crashes Found?}
    fuzzM -->|Yes| failD
    fuzzM -->|No| deployN[Deploy to Production]
    deployN --> monO[Continuous Monitoring + Bug Bounty]
    
    style devA fill:#90ee90
    style failD fill:#ff7f7f
    style deployN fill:#87ceeb
    style monO fill:#dda0dd
```

**Reading the diagram:** Every diamond (`{}`) is a **security gate**. The build **cannot progress** to the next stage unless it passes. This is the engineering embodiment of the principle **"security must be automated and continuous, not manual and periodic."**

---

## 🖼️ Diagram 3 — QA Activity Classification (Preventive vs Detective)

```mermaid
graph TB
    qaRoot[QA Activities for Security]
    qaRoot --> prev[Preventive Controls]
    qaRoot --> det[Detective Controls]
    qaRoot --> corr[Corrective Controls]
    
    prev --> p1[Security Training]
    prev --> p2[Secure Coding Standards]
    prev --> p3[Threat Modeling]
    prev --> p4[Security Requirements]
    
    det --> d1[SAST]
    det --> d2[DAST]
    det --> d3[IAST]
    det --> d4[SCA]
    det --> d5[Fuzz Testing]
    det --> d6[Code Review]
    
    corr --> c1[Patch Management]
    corr --> c2[Incident Response]
    corr --> c3[Root Cause Analysis]
    
    style qaRoot fill:#ffd700
    style prev fill:#98fb98
    style det fill:#87ceeb
    style corr fill:#f08080
```

**Reading the diagram:** A clean classification. **Preventive** stops vulnerabilities from being introduced, **Detective** finds them after introduction, **Corrective** fixes and learns from them. Effective early-detection programs emphasize **Preventive + Detective** (left two branches).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **\[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style\]**

### **Q1. Define "Shift-Left Security" and list FOUR QA activities that support early vulnerability detection.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 Marks):** Shift-left security is the practice of moving security testing and verification activities to the *earlier* phases of the SDLC (requirements, design, coding) rather than performing them only at the testing or post-release stage. It is driven by the principle that **the cost of fixing a defect grows exponentially** with each phase it travels undetected.
- **Four Activities (1.5 Marks — 0.375 each):**
  1. **Security Requirements Engineering** — defining security user stories.
  2. **Threat Modeling** — using STRIDE/PASTA to identify attack vectors.
  3. **Static Application Security Testing (SAST)** — automated source-code scanning.
  4. **Peer Code Review** — manual inspection by a second developer.

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between SAST and DAST. Why are both needed?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **SAST (Static Application Security Testing):** *(1 Mark)*
  - White-box technique; analyzes **source code, bytecode, or binary** *without execution*.
  - Detects: SQL injection, XSS, hard-coded secrets.
  - Tools: SonarQube, Checkmarx.
  - **Limitation:** Cannot find runtime/configuration issues; high false positives.
- **DAST (Dynamic Application Security Testing):** *(1 Mark)*
  - Black-box technique; tests the **running application** from outside.
  - Detects: Authentication flaws, server misconfigurations, injection in real runtime.
  - Tools: OWASP ZAP, Burp Suite.
  - **Limitation:** Cannot pinpoint exact code location.
- **Why both? (1 Mark):** SAST and DAST are **complementary** — SAST catches flaws early in code, DAST validates them in a live environment. Together they cover the **full vulnerability surface** (code + runtime).

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### ✏️ **Question A (14 Marks) — Full Question with Sub-parts**

> **\[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Style\]**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain in detail the Quality Assurance activities that support early vulnerability detection across the SDLC. For each activity, specify the SDLC phase and the type of vulnerability it primarily detects.**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

| **#** | **QA Activity** | **SDLC Phase** | **Vulnerability Detected** | **Marks** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Security Requirements Engineering** | Requirements | Missing access-control rules, weak password policies | 1 |
| 2 | **Threat Modeling (STRIDE)** | Design | Architectural flaws, missing trust boundaries | 1 |
| 3 | **Secure Code Review (manual)** | Implementation | Business-logic flaws, race conditions, auth bypass | 1 |
| 4 | **SAST (e.g., SonarQube)** | Implementation/Build | SQLi, XSS, hard-coded keys, buffer overflow | 1 |
| 5 | **SCA (e.g., Snyk, OWASP Dependency-Check)** | Build | CVEs in open-source libs (e.g., Log4Shell) | 1 |
| 6 | **DAST (e.g., OWASP ZAP)** | Testing/Staging | Runtime injection, session mgmt flaws | 1 |
| 7 | **Fuzz Testing** | Testing | Crashes from malformed input, memory corruption | 1 |

**Concluding statement (1 mark implicit in above):** The combination ensures **layered defense-in-depth** — even if one activity misses a vulnerability, another catches it.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**A software project of 80 KLOC recorded 240 functional defects and 32 security vulnerabilities. Compute (i) Defect Density, (ii) Vulnerability Density, and (iii) MTTD if the sum of detection lags is 768 days across all 32 vulnerabilities. Comment on the results against industry benchmarks (DD ≤ 1/KLOC, VD ≤ 0.5/KLOC, MTTD ≤ 30 days for security bugs).**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Given Data (1 Mark):**
$S = 80$ KLOC, $D = 240$, $V = 32$, $\sum (T_{detected} - T_{introduced}) = 768$ days.

**Step 2 — Defect Density (2 Marks):**
$$
DD = \dfrac{D}{S} = \dfrac{240}{80} = 3.0 \text{ defects/KLOC}
$$

**Step 3 — Vulnerability Density (1 Mark):**
$$
VD = \dfrac{V}{S} = \dfrac{32}{80} = 0.4 \text{ vulns/KLOC}
$$

**Step 4 — MTTD (2 Marks):**
$$
MTTD = \dfrac{768}{32} = 24 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 5 — Comparative Analysis (1 Mark):**
- $DD = 3.0$ vs benchmark $\leq 1.0$ → **Fails** (3× too high).
- $VD = 0.4$ vs benchmark $\leq 0.5$ → **Passes** (acceptable).
- $MTTD = 24$ days vs benchmark $\leq 30$ days → **Passes** (acceptable).

**Conclusion (Implicit):** Functional quality is poor; security maturity is acceptable. Recommend **root-cause analysis on functional defects** and continued investment in early SAST.

---

### ✏️ **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **\[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style\]**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Discuss the DevSecOps approach to early vulnerability detection. How do CI/CD security gates operationalize the shift-left principle? Illustrate with a suitable diagram.**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

- **DevSecOps Definition (1.5 Marks):** DevSecOps integrates security practices into every phase of the DevOps pipeline, making security a shared responsibility of development, security, and operations teams rather than a final-stage gate. It is the *operational manifestation* of shift-left.
- **Key Practices (3 Marks — 0.5 each):**
  1. **SAST in pre-commit hooks** — catches issues before code is even pushed.
  2. **SCA in build pipeline** — flags vulnerable dependencies.
  3. **DAST in staging** — black-box validation of deployed app.
  4. **Container scanning (e.g., Trivy)** — finds OS-level CVEs in Docker images.
  5. **Infrastructure-as-Code (IaC) scanning** — Terraform/CloudFormation misconfigurations.
  6. **Continuous monitoring + observability** — runtime anomaly detection.
- **CI/CD Security Gates (1.5 Marks):** Gates are automated checkpoints (illustrated in Diagram 2 in Section 4) — if SAST finds critical issues, **the build is blocked** from reaching production. This enforces security at machine speed, removing human bottleneck.
- **Benefits (1 Mark):** Faster feedback, fewer escaped defects, lower MTTR, cultural alignment.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Three vulnerabilities in a web application are rated as follows. Compute the Risk Priority Number (RPN) and rank them. (i) Stored XSS in user profile: S=8, L=7, D=4. (ii) Open redirect: S=4, L=6, D=9. (iii) Insecure direct object reference (IDOR): S=9, L=5, D=5.**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — RPN for Stored XSS (2 Marks):**
$$
RPN_{XSS} = 8 \times 7 \times 4 = 224
$$

**Step 2 — RPN for Open Redirect (2 Marks):**
$$
RPN_{Redirect} = 4 \times 6 \times 9 = 216
$$

**Step 3 — RPN for IDOR (2 Marks):**
$$
RPN_{IDOR} = 9 \times 5 \times 5 = 225
$$

**Step 4 — Ranking (1 Mark):**
$$
IDOR \; (225) \;>\; XSS \; (224) \;>\; OpenRedirect \; (216)
$$

**Conclusion:** IDOR has the highest RPN despite having lower likelihood than XSS, because of its high severity. Remediation team should patch IDOR first, then XSS, then redirect.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in this Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing SAST vs DAST** — Mixing up "white-box vs black-box" loses 1 mark per occurrence.
> 2. **Forgetting units in Defect Density** — Always write "defects/KLOC"; bare numbers lose 0.5 mark.
> 3. **Using the wrong RPN formula** — Some students write $S + L + D$ (sum) instead of $S \times L \times D$ (product). The product is the **industry and KTU standard**.
> 4. **Skipping the comparative benchmark** in numerical problems — KTU examiners specifically award marks for **comparing results against industry standards** (e.g., "DD = 3.0 > benchmark 1.0").
> 5. **Not drawing the diagram** in 7-mark DevSecOps questions — KTU valuation scheme allots **1–2 marks** specifically for a labeled SDLC/CI-CD diagram. Always include one.
> 6. **Listing tools without explaining phases** — Naming "SonarQube" without saying "used in implementation phase" loses the activity-phase mapping mark.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Shift-Left Security** = move security activities to the earliest possible SDLC phase.
- ✅ **QA in security** = preventive + detective + corrective controls.
- ✅ **SDLC Phases & Corresponding QA Activities:**
  - Requirements → Security Requirements
  - Design → Threat Modeling (STRIDE/PASTA)
  - Implementation → SAST, Secure Code Review, SCA
  - Testing → DAST, IAST, Fuzz Testing
  - Deployment → Penetration Testing
  - Operations → Bug Bounty, Continuous Monitoring
- ✅ **SAST** = White-box, no execution, source-code level.
- ✅ **DAST** = Black-box, runtime, outside-in view.
- ✅ **IAST** = Hybrid, agent-based, low false-positives.
- ✅ **SCA** = Open-source / third-party vulnerability detection.
- ✅ **Cost Multipliers (IBM):** 1× (design) → 6× (code) → 15× (test) → 100× (production).
- ✅ **Key Formulas:** $DD = D/S$, $VD = V/S$, $RPN = S \times L \times D$, $V(G) = E - N + 2P$, $MTTD = \sum \Delta T / N$, $F1 = 2PR/(P+R)$.
- ✅ **Cyclomatic Complexity > 10** = refactoring candidate (security risk).
- ✅ **Code Coverage < 80%** for security modules is unacceptable.
- ✅ **RPN ranking** = product of severity, likelihood, detectability (each 1–10).
- ✅ **DevSecOps** = cultural + technical integration of security into DevOps.
- ✅ **CI/CD Security Gates** = automated checkpoints that block unsafe builds.
- ✅ **Industry Benchmarks:** DD < 1/KLOC, VD < 0.5/KLOC, MTTD < 30 days.
- ✅ **Always** include a labeled diagram in 7-mark answers.
- ✅ **Always** compare numerical results against industry benchmarks for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT418 - SECURITY ASSESSMENT AND RISK ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Trust Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trust Model (Security Engineering Definition)**
> A **Trust Model** is a formalized framework that defines the rules, relationships, and cryptographic mechanisms by which entities (users, systems, certificates, or domains) establish, evaluate, propagate, and revoke **trust relationships** in a distributed computing environment. It specifies *who trusts whom*, *under what conditions*, and *how trust assertions are validated* to enable secure interactions such as authentication, authorization, and data integrity assurance.

In the context of **Security Assessment and Risk Analysis (PEAIT418)**, a trust model is the foundational layer upon which all subsequent risk evaluation, threat modeling, and vulnerability scoring depends. Without a clearly defined trust model, an organization cannot determine its *trust boundary*, and therefore cannot perform a meaningful risk assessment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Snapshot (Module 2 Anchor Terms)**
> - **Trustor:** The entity that places trust in another entity.
> - **Trustee:** The entity that is being trusted.
> - **Trust Anchor:** A pre-configured, inherently trusted authority (root of trust).
> - **Trust Domain:** A collection of entities that share a common security policy and trust infrastructure.
> - **Trust Path / Chain:** The sequence of trust relationships connecting a trustor to a remote trustee.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Passport & Embassy" Model

Imagine you are traveling from Kerala to Japan. At immigration, the Japanese officer does not know you personally. However, he trusts the **Embassy of Japan in Delhi**, which in turn trusts the **Indian Ministry of External Affairs**, which in turn trusts the **Kerala State Government**, which in turn issued your **Passport**.

> This chain of endorsements is exactly what a **Trust Model** formalizes in computing.

| Real World Element | Security Domain Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Embassy (root authority) | **Root Certificate Authority (CA)** |
| Passport issued by Kerala Govt | **Digital Certificate** |
| Officer trusting the chain | **Relying Party (Verifying Entity)** |
| Stamped visa on passport | **Digital Signature** |
| Forged passport detection | **Certificate Revocation Check (CRL/OCSP)** |

The officer doesn't blindly trust *you* — he trusts a **chain of valid endorsements** rooted in a known, authoritative body. This is the essence of every trust model in information security.

## 1.3 Why Trust Models Are Pivotal in Risk Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PEAIT418 — Module 2 Learning Outcome):**
> *Students must be able to evaluate trust assumptions, identify implicit trust boundaries in system architectures, and reason about cascading failures when trust is misplaced.*

A flawed or implicit trust model is the **root cause of approximately 70% of major security breaches** (industry estimate cited in NIST SP 800-39 risk frameworks). Examples:

- **SolarWinds (2020):** Implicit trust between software build servers allowed lateral compromise.
- **Equifax (2017):** Failure to revoke an expired digital certificate (broken trust chain).
- **Target Breach (2013):** Excessive trust granted to a third-party HVAC vendor (trust over-provisioning).

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Trust Relationship

A trust relationship, formally, is a **directed binary relation** between two entities. Let:

- $T$ = the trust relationship operator
- $A$ = the trusting entity (trustor)
- $B$ = the trusted entity (trustee)

Then a trust relationship is expressed as:

$$A \xrightarrow{\;\text{trust level } \tau\;} B$$

where $\tau \in [0, 1]$ is the **trust level** (or trust value) that $A$ assigns to $B$.

### 2.1.1 Properties of Trust Relationships

| Property | Description | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **Asymmetry** | $A \xrightarrow{\text{trust}} B$ does NOT imply $B \xrightarrow{\text{trust}} A$ | Trust must be assessed bidirectionally in risk audits |
| **Transitivity** | If $A$ trusts $B$ and $B$ trusts $C$, then $A$ may trust $C$ | Foundation of PKI chains and Web of Trust |
| **Context-Dependence** | $A$ may trust $B$ for confidentiality but not for integrity | Single trust level is insufficient in real systems |
| **Monotonicity (ideal)** | Once trust is granted, it does not weaken over time | Real systems violate this — dynamic trust models are needed |
| **Revocability** | Trust can be withdrawn if evidence of compromise emerges | Implemented via Certificate Revocation Lists (CRLs) |

## 2.2 Taxonomy of Trust Models (Core KTU Module 2 Content)

The module distinguishes between **four principal trust models** used in modern information security:

### 2.2.1 Hierarchical Trust Model (PKI / X.509)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A single, centralized **Root Certificate Authority (CA)** sits at the top of a tree-like trust hierarchy. All entities ultimately derive trust from this root.

- **Structure:** Tree (inverted) — Root CA → Intermediate CAs → End-entity certificates.
- **Trust Path:** Unidirectional, always pointing upward to the root.
- **Used in:** HTTPS, S/MIME, e-Governance, banking PKI.
- **Strength:** Predictable, auditable, single root of trust.
- **Weakness:** Root CA compromise = total system collapse. Single point of failure.

### 2.2.2 Distributed Trust Model (Web of Trust — PGP)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Trust is decentralized. Each user acts as a mini-certification authority, signing the public keys of other users they personally vouch for.

- **Structure:** Graph (mesh) — no central authority.
- **Trust Path:** Computed dynamically by traversing the signature graph.
- **Used in:** PGP email encryption, GNU Privacy Guard, decentralized identity (DID).
- **Strength:** No single point of failure; resilient to isolated compromise.
- **Weakness:** Trust path discovery is computationally hard (NP-hard in general); key signing parties introduce social engineering risk.

### 2.2.3 Hybrid Trust Model (Bridge CA / Cross-Certification)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Combines elements of hierarchical and distributed models. Multiple roots interconnect through **bridge CAs** or **cross-certificates** to enable interoperability between organizations.

- **Used in:** Government federations (e.g., US FBCA), large enterprise mergers, cross-border e-commerce.
- **Strength:** Scalable interoperability; localized compromise containment.
- **Weakness:** Complex governance; policy mapping is non-trivial.

### 2.2.4 Trust List / Anchor Model (TCG / TPM)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A device or system maintains an explicit list of pre-approved trust anchors (public key hashes). Only certificates chaining back to a listed anchor are accepted.

- **Used in:** TPM-based platform integrity, DNSSEC, mobile device management (MDM).
- **Strength:** Hardware-rooted, hard to tamper with.
- **Weakness:** Static; cannot accommodate dynamic trust decisions easily.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Metric Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is a CHEAT SHEET for ESE (End Semester Examination) answers. Memorize the structure; write the equations correctly to score full marks.**

| # | Concept / Metric | Mathematical Form | Variables & Domain | Real-World Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Trust Level Assignment | $\tau_{A \to B} \in [0, 1]$ | $\tau = 0$ = no trust, $\tau = 1$ = absolute | PKI policy files, PGP trust databases |
| 2 | Trust Transitivity (Conjunctive) | $\tau_{A \to C} = \min(\tau_{A \to B},\; \tau_{B \to C})$ | Conservative chaining (weakest link wins) | PKI chain validation |
| 3 | Trust Transitivity (Disjunctive) | $\tau_{A \to C} = \max(\tau_{A \to B_i},\; \tau_{B_i \to C})$ | Take strongest available path | Routing trust in distributed systems |
| 4 | Trust Transitivity (Multiplicative) | $\tau_{A \to C} = \tau_{A \to B} \cdot \tau_{B \to C}$ | Probabilistic interpretation | Bayesian trust networks |
| 5 | Composite Trust (Weighted) | $T_{A \to C} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot \tau_{B_i \to C}$ | $\sum w_i = 1$ | Multi-attribute trust (reputation + cert + history) |
| 6 | Risk Exposure (Trust-Related) | $R = (1 - \tau) \cdot V$ | $V$ = value of asset, $R$ = residual risk | ISO 27005 risk scoring |
| 7 | Trust Decay (Time-based) | $\tau(t) = \tau_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\lambda$ = decay constant, $t$ = elapsed time | Session trust, token expiration |
| 8 | Certification Authority Failure Rate | $P_{CA} = 1 - (1 - p)^n$ | $p$ = per-key compromise probability, $n$ = keys issued | PKI sizing decisions |
| 9 | Trust Graph Connectivity | $C = \dfrac{2 \cdot \vert E \vert}{\vert V \vert(\vert V \vert - 1)}$ | $\vert V \vert$ = entities, $\vert E \vert$ = trust edges | Web of Trust resilience analysis |
| 10 | Revocation Lag Penalty | $R_{L} = N_{comp} \cdot t_{revoke} \cdot V_{per\,sec}$ | $N_{comp}$ = compromised certs, $t_{revoke}$ = time to revoke | SLA design for CRL/OCSP |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL: Do NOT use the vertical pipe `|` symbol inside KTU answer sheets' tables. Use `\\vert` or `\\mid` in LaTeX. The above table shows raw notation; in your written exam, write it as `\\vert E \\vert` for cardinality.**

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of Trust Models

1. **Web Browsing (HTTPS):** Hierarchical trust — your browser has ~150 root CAs pre-installed. Any HTTPS connection you make chains to one of these roots.
2. **Email Encryption (PGP):** Distributed trust — you decide whose signatures you trust, and the Web of Trust figures out the rest.
3. **Cloud Federation (AWS, Azure):** Hybrid trust — federated identity (SAML, OIDC) bridges different organizational PKIs.
4. **IoT Device Authentication:** Anchor trust — TPM chips ship with manufacturer-embedded trust anchors.
5. **Risk Assessment Audit:** Every asset's trust assumptions must be enumerated; this becomes the **Trust Boundary** for STRIDE/PASTA threat modeling.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Problems

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Trust Transitivity in a Three-Node Chain

**Problem:** A system administrator $A$ issues a digital certificate to intermediate server $B$, and $B$ in turn signs a document for end-user $C$. If $A$ assigns $\tau_{A \to B} = 0.9$ and $B$ assigns $\tau_{B \to C} = 0.8$, compute the transitive trust $\tau_{A \to C}$ under three different propagation models.

### Step 1 — Identify the propagation rule

We have three candidate rules. The choice of rule depends on the **security policy** of the organization. We will compute all three and discuss.

### Step 2 — Conjunctive (Weakest Link) Propagation

$$ \tau_{A \to C}^{(conj)} = \min(\tau_{A \to B},\; \tau_{B \to C}) $$

Substituting the given values:

$$ \tau_{A \to C}^{(conj)} = \min(0.9,\; 0.8) = 0.8 $$

> **Interpretation:** The chain is only as strong as its weakest link. This is the *most conservative* model, preferred in high-assurance PKI (e.g., government, defense).

### Step 3 — Disjunctive (Strongest Path) Propagation

$$ \tau_{A \to C}^{(disj)} = \max(\tau_{A \to B},\; \tau_{B \to C}) $$

Substituting the given values:

$$ \tau_{A \to C}^{(disj)} = \max(0.9,\; 0.8) = 0.9 $$

> **Interpretation:** Optimistic — the trust is taken from the strongest available link. Used in loosely coupled, fault-tolerant networks (e.g., ad-hoc mesh networks).

### Step 4 — Multiplicative (Probabilistic) Propagation

$$ \tau_{A \to C}^{(mult)} = \tau_{A \to B} \cdot \tau_{B \to C} $$

Substituting the given values:

$$ \tau_{A \to C}^{(mult)} = 0.9 \times 0.8 = 0.72 $$

> **Interpretation:** Models trust as the *joint probability of both endorsements being valid*. Each hop erodes trust multiplicatively. This is the *most pessimistic* model for long chains.

### Step 5 — Comparative Analysis

| Propagation Model | Computed $\tau_{A \to C}$ | Trust Erosion | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|---|
| Conjunctive | 0.80 | None (preserved) | PKI, banking |
| Disjunctive | 0.90 | None (preserved) | Mesh networks, routing |
| Multiplicative | 0.72 | Significant (0.18 drop) | Bayesian trust, risk scoring |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always state *which* model you are using before computing. Marks are lost when students compute without justification.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Trust Decay and Risk Exposure Over Time

**Problem:** A mobile banking app grants a session token with initial trust $\tau_0 = 1.0$. The token is used for $t$ minutes with decay constant $\lambda = 0.05 \; \text{min}^{-1}$. A user is transferring funds of value $V = \text{₹}50{,}000$. Derive the risk exposure $R(t)$ as a function of time and identify the maximum safe session duration if the bank's risk policy mandates $R(t) \le \text{₹}5{,}000$.

### Step 1 — Apply the exponential trust decay equation

The trust value at time $t$ is given by:

$$ \tau(t) = \tau_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t} $$

Substituting $\tau_0 = 1.0$:

$$ \tau(t) = 1.0 \cdot e^{-0.05\,t} = e^{-0.05\,t} $$

### Step 2 — Express residual risk as a function of time

Recall the risk exposure formula from the cheat sheet:

$$ R = (1 - \tau) \cdot V $$

Substituting $\tau(t)$:

$$ R(t) = (1 - e^{-0.05\,t}) \cdot 50{,}000 $$

### Step 3 — Expand for clarity

$$ R(t) = 50{,}000 \cdot (1 - e^{-0.05\,t}) $$

This is a monotonically **increasing** function of $t$ — as time passes, trust decays and risk accumulates.

### Step 4 — Solve for the maximum safe session duration

Set $R(t) \le 5{,}000$:

$$ 50{,}000 \cdot (1 - e^{-0.05\,t}) \le 5{,}000 $$

Divide both sides by $50{,}000$:

$$ 1 - e^{-0.05\,t} \le \frac{5{,}000}{50{,}000} = 0.1 $$

Rearrange:

$$ e^{-0.05\,t} \ge 1 - 0.1 = 0.9 $$

Take the natural logarithm of both sides (note: $\ln$ is a monotonically increasing function, so the inequality direction is preserved):

$$ -0.05\,t \ge \ln(0.9) $$

Substitute the numerical value $\ln(0.9) \approx -0.10536$:

$$ -0.05\,t \ge -0.10536 $$

Multiply both sides by $-1$ (inequality direction **flips**):

$$ 0.05\,t \le 0.10536 $$

Divide by $0.05$:

$$ t \le \frac{0.10536}{0.05} = 2.1072 \; \text{minutes} $$

### Step 5 — Final Answer and Interpretation

$$ \boxed{t_{\max} \approx 2.11 \; \text{minutes} \;\approx\; 127 \; \text{seconds}} $$

> **Interpretation:** Under the stated policy, the session token must expire within approximately **2.1 minutes** to keep residual risk under ₹5,000. Real banking systems use additional controls (MFA, transaction signing) to extend this safely.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Web of Trust Path Discovery

**Problem:** In a PGP Web of Trust with 5 users (Alice, Bob, Carol, Dave, Eve), the following signed trust edges exist (signer → signee):

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Alice} \to \text{Bob} \quad (\tau = 0.9) \\
&\text{Bob} \to \text{Carol} \quad (\tau = 0.7) \\
&\text{Alice} \to \text{Dave} \quad (\tau = 0.6) \\
&\text{Dave} \to \text{Eve} \quad (\tau = 0.8) \\
&\text{Carol} \to \text{Eve} \quad (\tau = 0.5)
\end{aligned}
$$

Find all trust paths from Alice to Eve and compute the multiplicative trust for each.

### Step 1 — Enumerate all simple paths from Alice to Eve

A simple path does not repeat a node. We perform a graph traversal:

- **Path 1:** Alice → Bob → Carol → Eve
- **Path 2:** Alice → Dave → Eve

### Step 2 — Compute multiplicative trust for each path

For **Path 1**:

$$ \tau_{A \to E}^{(1)} = \tau_{A \to B} \cdot \tau_{B \to C} \cdot \tau_{C \to E} = 0.9 \times 0.7 \times 0.5 = 0.315 $$

For **Path 2**:

$$ \tau_{A \to E}^{(2)} = \tau_{A \to D} \cdot \tau_{D \to E} = 0.6 \times 0.8 = 0.480 $$

### Step 3 — Select the best path

Using the **disjunctive (strongest path)** rule:

$$ \tau_{A \to E}^{best} = \max(0.315,\; 0.480) = 0.480 $$

The optimal path is **Alice → Dave → Eve** with trust value $0.480$.

### Step 4 — Decision

If the threshold for accepting a trust assertion is $\tau_{min} = 0.5$, then **neither path is sufficient**. Alice would need to either (a) obtain a direct signature from Eve, or (b) request an additional signatory to strengthen the chain.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Note:** The act of comparing paths and selecting the optimal one is the core competency being tested. Marks are awarded for *enumeration* of paths, *correct multiplication*, and *final selection logic*.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Trust Model (PKI / X.509) — Inverted Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[Root CA]:::root
    intB[Intermediate CA 1]:::int
    intC[Intermediate CA 2]:::int
    eeD[End Entity Cert 1]:::ee
    eeE[End Entity Cert 2]:::ee
    eeF[End Entity Cert 3]:::ee
    eeG[End Entity Cert 4]:::ee
    rpH[Relying Party Browser]:::rp

    rootA --- intB
    rootA --- intC
    intB --- eeD
    intB --- eeE
    intC --- eeF
    intC --- eeG
    eeD --- rpH
    eeF --- rpH

    classDef root fill:#1f1f1f,stroke:#ff6b6b,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    classDef int fill:#2d3a4a,stroke:#4ecdc4,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef ee fill:#1a3a3a,stroke:#ffe66d,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
    classDef rp fill:#3a1a3a,stroke:#a8e6cf,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Root CA** at the top is the single trust anchor.
- Trust flows *downward* through the hierarchy.
- The **Relying Party** (browser) at the bottom trusts certificates that chain back to a root CA in its trust store.

## 4.2 Distributed Trust Model (Web of Trust — PGP)

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1[Alice]:::user
    node2[Bob]:::user
    node3[Carol]:::user
    node4[David]:::user
    node5[Eve]:::user
    node6[Frank]:::user

    node1 --- node2
    node2 --- node3
    node3 --- node1
    node4 --- node5
    node1 --- node4
    node2 --- node6
    node6 --- node5

    classDef user fill:#1a2a3a,stroke:#ffa07a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- No central authority. Every user is both a *trustor* and a *trustee*.
- Trust paths are computed dynamically by traversing the mesh.
- A path from Alice to Eve may go through Bob-Carol or David, or both.

## 4.3 Hybrid Trust Model (Bridge CA Federation)

```mermaid
graph TD
    orgA[Organization A Root CA]:::orgA
    orgB[Organization B Root CA]:::orgB
    orgC[Organization C Root CA]:::orgC
    bridgeX[Bridge CA Federation]:::bridge
    usr1[Employee of A]:::user
    usr2[Employee of B]:::user
    usr3[Employee of C]:::user

    orgA --- bridgeX
    orgB --- bridgeX
    orgC --- bridgeX
    orgA --- usr1
    orgB --- usr2
    orgC --- usr3

    classDef orgA fill:#2a1a1a,stroke:#ff6b6b,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef orgB fill:#1a2a1a,stroke:#4ecdc4,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef orgC fill:#1a1a2a,stroke:#ffe66d,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef bridge fill:#3a3a1a,stroke:#a8e6cf,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    classDef user fill:#2a2a2a,stroke:#cccccc,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Each organization retains its **own root CA** (preserves autonomy).
- A central **Bridge CA** issues cross-certificates linking the roots.
- An employee of Org A can be verified by Org B via the bridge.

## 4.4 Functional Block Diagram — Trust Model Decision Flow in Risk Assessment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    phaseA[Identify System Components]:::phase --> phaseB[Map Trust Relationships Between Components]:::phase
    phaseB --> phaseC{Trust Model Classification}:::decision
    phaseC -->|Centralized| phaseD[Apply Hierarchical Trust Analysis]:::phase
    phaseC -->|Decentralized| phaseE[Apply Web of Trust Graph Analysis]:::phase
    phaseC -->|Federated| phaseF[Apply Bridge CA Policy Mapping]:::phase
    phaseC -->|Embedded Devices| phaseG[Apply Anchor Trust List Audit]:::phase
    phaseD --> phaseH[Compute Trust Values and Chain Length]:::phase

    phaseF --> phaseH
    phaseG --> phaseH
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- This is the **methodological workflow** a risk analyst follows.
- The system components are mapped, the trust model is classified, trust values are computed, and the residual risk feeds into remediation.
