# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**Define a trust model. List any four properties that a trust relationship may exhibit in a security context.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Trust Model** is a formalized specification of the rules, relationships, and validation mechanisms that govern how entities establish and propagate trust in a distributed environment. It answers: *who trusts whom, under what conditions, and on the basis of what evidence?*

Four key properties of a trust relationship:

1. **Asymmetry** — Trust is directional. $A$ trusting $B$ does not imply $B$ trusts $A$.
2. **Transitivity** — Trust can be chained: $A \to B$ and $B \to C$ may imply $A \to C$.
3. **Context-Dependence** — Trust may hold for one purpose (e.g., confidentiality) but not another (e.g., authorization).
4. **Revocability** — Trust can be withdrawn, e.g., via certificate revocation lists (CRLs) or OCSP responses.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 0.5 × 4 for properties.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Compare the Hierarchical Trust Model and the Web of Trust Model on the basis of: (i) structure, (ii) single point of failure, and (iii) trust path discovery complexity.**

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | Hierarchical Trust Model (PKI) | Web of Trust Model (PGP) |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Structure | Inverted tree with a single root CA | Decentralized mesh graph of user signatures |
| (ii) Single point of failure | **Yes** — root CA compromise is catastrophic | **No** — distributed, no central authority |
| (iii) Trust path discovery | Trivial — walk up to the root | **NP-hard** in general — must traverse graph |

*(3 marks: 1 per row of comparison.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Module carries a 14-mark question with **internal choice** between two alternatives. Both are given below.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **Hierarchical Trust Model (PKI)** in detail with a suitable diagram. Discuss its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A three-level certificate chain has the following trust assignments: Root to Intermediate $= 0.95$, Intermediate to End-Entity $= 0.85$. Compute the transitive trust from Root to End-Entity using (i) conjunctive, (ii) multiplicative, and (iii) disjunctive models. Comment on which model is most appropriate for a banking PKI. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

A **Hierarchical Trust Model (PKI)** organizes trust in a tree-like structure with a single **Root Certificate Authority (CA)** at the apex. Intermediate CAs branch from the root, and end-entity certificates are signed by these intermediates.

**Architecture:**

1. **Root CA** — Self-signed, pre-installed in all clients' trust stores.
2. **Intermediate CAs** — Cross-signed by the root; can be revoked independently.
3. **End-Entity Certificates** — Issued to users, servers, or devices.

```
Root CA
 ├── Intermediate CA (Web Server)
 │     ├── *.ktu.ac.in
 │     └── mail.ktu.ac.in
 └── Intermediate CA (Code Signing)
       └── vendor-acme.crt
```

**Advantages:**
- Simple, predictable trust path (always upward to root).
- Easy revocation via CRLs/OCSP.
- Standardized (X.509, RFC 5280) — broad tool support.
- Auditable: clear chain of custody.

**Limitations:**
- **Root CA compromise = total system failure** (single point of failure).
- Cross-organizational trust requires cross-certification or bridge CAs.
- Certificate lifecycle management is operationally heavy.
- Implicit trust in ~150 root CAs pre-installed in browsers is a systemic risk.

*(7 marks: 2 for architecture, 2 for diagram, 1.5 for advantages, 1.5 for limitations.)*

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Given:**
- $\tau_{R \to I} = 0.95$
- $\tau_{I \to E} = 0.85$

**(i) Conjunctive (Min):**
$$ \tau_{R \to E}^{(conj)} = \min(0.95,\; 0.85) = 0.85 $$

**(ii) Multiplicative:**
$$ \tau_{R \to E}^{(mult)} = 0.95 \times 0.85 = 0.8075 $$

**(iii) Disjunctive (Max):**
$$ \tau_{R \to E}^{(disj)} = \max(0.95,\; 0.85) = 0.95 $$

**Comment on the best model for banking PKI:**

For a **banking PKI**, the **Conjunctive (Min) model** is most appropriate. Banking systems are **high-assurance** environments where any weakness in the chain can lead to financial fraud. The min model ensures that the chain is only as strong as its weakest link, providing a conservative, pessimistic risk posture. The multiplicative model (0.8075) is acceptable as a secondary check. The disjunctive model is unsuitable for banking because it grants excessive trust based on a single strong link, ignoring weak intermediate CAs.

*(7 marks: 1 per computation, 4 for the comment with justification.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Describe the **Web of Trust Model** as used in PGP. With a small graph of your own design (at least 5 users), demonstrate how trust propagates from user $A$ to user $F$ over multiple paths. Compute the multiplicative trust for each path and identify the strongest path. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a session management system, a token is issued with initial trust $\tau_0 = 1.0$ and decay constant $\lambda = 0.03 \; \text{s}^{-1}$. The protected asset has value $V = \text{₹}2{,}00{,}000$. If the organization mandates that residual risk must remain below ₹10,000, derive the maximum safe session duration. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**The Web of Trust Model:**

The **Web of Trust** is a **decentralized trust model** popularized by Pretty Good Privacy (PGP). In this model, there is no central authority. Each user acts as a mini-certification authority: when user $X$ personally verifies user $Y$'s identity, $X$ signs $Y$'s public key. Other users, upon seeing $X$'s signature, may extend trust to $Y$ if they trust $X$ — this is the basis of **trust transitivity** in a Web of Trust.

**Example Graph (5 users, signed edges):**

| Edge (Signer → Signee) | Trust Value |
|---|---|
| A → B | 0.9 |
| B → C | 0.8 |
| A → D | 0.7 |
| D → F | 0.9 |
| C → F | 0.6 |

**Trust Path Enumeration from A to F:**

- **Path 1:** A → D → F
- **Path 2:** A → B → C → F

**Multiplicative Trust Computation:**

- **Path 1:** $\tau_1 = 0.7 \times 0.9 = 0.63$
- **Path 2:** $\tau_2 = 0.9 \times 0.8 \times 0.6 = 0.432$

**Strongest Path:** Path 1 (A → D → F) with trust value **0.63**.

**Comment:** Path 1 wins despite the lower initial trust from A to D, because the shorter chain avoids multiplicative erosion. This illustrates a key principle in Web of Trust analysis: **shorter chains are generally stronger than longer chains with high individual endorsements.**

*(7 marks: 2 for theory, 2 for graph, 2 for computation, 1 for analysis.)*

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1 — Write the trust decay function.**

$$ \tau(t) = \tau_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t} = 1.0 \cdot e^{-0.03\,t} = e^{-0.03\,t} $$

**Step 2 — Write the risk exposure function.**

$$ R(t) = (1 - \tau(t)) \cdot V = (1 - e^{-0.03\,t}) \cdot 200{,}000 $$

**Step 3 — Apply the policy constraint $R(t) \le 10{,}000$.**

$$ (1 - e^{-0.03\,t}) \cdot 200{,}000 \le 10{,}000 $$

$$ 1 - e^{-0.03\,t} \le \frac{10{,}000}{200{,}000} = 0.05 $$

$$ e^{-0.03\,t} \ge 0.95 $$

**Step 4 — Solve for $t$.**

$$ -0.03\,t \ge \ln(0.95) \approx -0.05129 $$

Multiply both sides by $-1$ (flip inequality):

$$ 0.03\,t \le 0.05129 $$

$$ t \le \frac{0.05129}{0.03} \approx 1.7097 \; \text{seconds} $$

**Final Answer:**

$$ \boxed{t_{\max} \approx 1.71 \; \text{seconds}} $$

**Interpretation:** Without additional controls, the session is viable for barely 1.7 seconds. This extreme value highlights the importance of layering controls: **short-lived tokens (e.g., JWT with refresh), multi-factor authentication, and transaction signing** are essential to safely extend banking sessions.

*(7 marks: 1 for trust equation, 1 for risk equation, 3 for algebraic solution, 2 for interpretation and control recommendations.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to flip the inequality** when multiplying/dividing by a negative number (Step 4 of decay problems) — costs 1 full mark.
> 2. **Not specifying which propagation model is used** (conjunctive, multiplicative, disjunctive) before computing — costs 0.5 to 1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing the trustor and trustee direction** when drawing diagrams — costs 0.5 to 1 mark.
> 4. **Omitting the "comment/justification"** after a numerical answer — examiners specifically look for engineering judgment, not just arithmetic. Costs 1 to 2 marks.
> 5. **Using the vertical pipe `|` inside answer tables** — this breaks the table structure. Use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX, or write "abs(...)" or "card(...)" in plain text.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 2 — Trust Models)**

- [x] **Trust Model** = formalized rules for establishing, evaluating, and propagating trust among entities.
- [x] **Trustor** trusts **Trustee** via a **Trust Relationship**; trust level $\tau \in [0, 1]$.
- [x] **Four canonical trust models:** Hierarchical (PKI), Distributed (Web of Trust), Hybrid (Bridge CA), Anchor (TPM/Trust List).
- [x] **Hierarchical (PKI):** Tree structure, single root CA, predictable path, single point of failure.
- [x] **Web of Trust (PGP):** Mesh structure, no root, resilient but NP-hard path discovery.
- [x] **Hybrid (Bridge CA):** Federation pattern; multiple roots interconnected for inter-org trust.
- [x] **Anchor Model:** Pre-approved trust list; used in TPM, DNSSEC, MDM.
- [x] **Five properties of trust:** Asymmetry, Transitivity, Context-Dependence, Monotonicity, Revocability.
- [x] **Three transitivity formulas to memorize:**
  - Conjunctive: $\tau_{A \to C} = \min(\tau_{A \to B}, \tau_{B \to C})$
  - Disjunctive: $\tau_{A \to C} = \max(\tau_{A \to B}, \tau_{B \to C})$
  - Multiplicative: $\tau_{A \to C} = \tau_{A \to B} \cdot \tau_{B \to C}$
- [x] **Trust decay equation:** $\tau(t) = \tau_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$
- [x] **Risk exposure equation:** $R = (1 - \tau) \cdot V$
- [x] **In Web of Trust, shorter chains beat longer chains** — multiplicative erosion makes deep chains fragile.
- [x] **Banking / high-assurance PKI → Conjunctive (min) model.** Loosely coupled networks → Disjunctive (max) model. Probabilistic / Bayesian systems → Multiplicative model.
- [x] **Trust boundary identification** is the first step in any risk assessment methodology (STRIDE, PASTA, NIST RMF).
- [x] **Real-world breach patterns:** SolarWinds (implicit trust), Equifax (broken revocation), Target (over-trusting third party) — all are trust model failures.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Security Architecture & Design Reviews
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT418 - SECURITY ASSESSMENT AND RISK ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Security Architecture & Design Reviews

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Security Architecture** is the unified design artifact that defines how security controls (technical, procedural, and physical) are organized, integrated, and enforced across an enterprise's IT infrastructure to protect assets and support the organization's mission. It bridges business requirements with technical implementation, providing a coherent blueprint for confidentiality, integrity, and availability (the **CIA Triad**).

A **Security Design Review (SDR)** is a formal, systematic, and documented evaluation of a system's architectural and detailed design against established security principles, standards, threat models, and regulatory requirements. The objective is to identify design-level vulnerabilities *before* code is written or systems are deployed, thereby reducing the cost of remediation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Security Architecture and Design Reviews sit at the intersection of **Module 2 (Security Assessment & Methodologies)** and the broader lifecycle of the *NIST Cybersecurity Framework (Identify → Protect → Detect → Respond → Recover)*. Expect exam questions mapping architecture principles to risk reduction.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are building a **fortified palace** for a king.

- The **Security Architecture** is the *master blueprint* drawn by the royal engineer — it shows the moat's depth, the thickness of the outer wall, the position of the watchtowers, the layout of the inner courtyard, and the secret escape tunnel. Nothing is built until the blueprint is approved.
- The **Security Design Review** is the *inspection* done by the King's independent military advisors. Before any stone is laid, they walk through the blueprint and ask: *"What if the enemy brings ladders? What if they tunnel under? What if a guard is bribed?"* If the blueprint fails their scrutiny, the engineer must revise it.

In software/IT terms:
- The **Architecture** is your *blueprint* of firewalls, identity providers, network segmentation, encryption layers, and access control lists.
- The **Design Review** is the *peer review meeting* where security architects, developers, and auditors challenge that blueprint to find weaknesses *before* the system goes live.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** Catching a flaw at the *design stage* is roughly **10× to 100× cheaper** to fix than catching it after deployment. This is the central economic argument for Security Architecture Reviews (a principle from NIST SP 800-64 and Microsoft's SDL).

## 1.3 Core Security Design Principles (Saltzer & Schroeder + Modern Additions)

The foundational principles every KTU student must memorize:

1. **Least Privilege** — Grant only the minimum permissions necessary.
2. **Defense in Depth** — Multiple overlapping layers of controls.
3. **Fail-Safe Defaults** — Default to "deny" when a control fails.
4. **Separation of Duties (SoD)** — No single individual should control an entire critical process.
5. **Complete Mediation** — Every access must be checked against access rules.
6. **Open Design** — Security should not depend on secrecy of mechanism (Kerckhoffs's principle).
7. **Economy of Mechanism** — Keep the design small and simple.
8. **Psychological Acceptability** — Security mechanisms should be user-friendly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric to Remember:** The industry-accepted cost ratio is **1 : 10 : 100 : 1000** for fixing a defect at *requirements → design → coding → testing → production* respectively (IBM Systems Sciences Institute, frequently cited in KTU reference materials).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Security Architecture

A robust Security Architecture consists of **five inter-locking layers**:

| Layer | Purpose | Example Controls |
|---|---|---|
| **Business / Governance Layer** | Policies, risk appetite, compliance | ISO 27001, NIST CSF, PCI-DSS |
| **Application Layer** | Secure SDLC, input validation, authN/Z | OAuth 2.0, OWASP ASVS |
| **Data Layer** | Encryption, DLP, key management | AES-256, tokenization, HSM |
| **Host / Endpoint Layer** | OS hardening, EDR, patching | CIS Benchmarks, SIEM agents |
| **Network / Perimeter Layer** | Segmentation, firewalls, IDS/IPS | Zero Trust, NAC, WAF |

## 2.2 Why & How — The Logic Behind the Layers

- **Why Business first?** Because security without business context is just paranoia. The architecture must reflect the organization's **risk appetite** (often quantified in monetary terms).
- **Why Network last?** The traditional "castle-and-moat" model has failed. Modern architectures use **Zero Trust** ("never trust, always verify") which inverts this layer's importance.
- **How do the layers interact?** Through a **control mapping matrix** where each threat is traced to the specific layer(s) that mitigate it. This traceability is what a Design Review verifies.

## 2.3 The Security Design Review (SDR) Process — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Charter & Scope Definition** — Define system boundaries, data classification, and review objectives.
2. **Artifact Collection** — Gather architecture diagrams, data flow diagrams (DFDs), threat models, requirements specs.
3. **Baseline Standard Selection** — Pick the standard(s) to evaluate against (e.g., NIST SP 800-53, CIS Controls v8, OWASP ASVS).
4. **Threat Modeling** — Apply STRIDE, PASTA, or VAST to enumerate threats.
5. **Control Mapping & Gap Analysis** — Map existing/planned controls to the baseline; identify gaps.
6. **Findings Classification** — Categorize each finding by severity (Critical / High / Medium / Low / Informational).
7. **Remediation Tracking** — Assign owners, deadlines, and re-test schedules.
8. **Sign-off & Risk Acceptance** — Formal acceptance by stakeholders if residual risk is within appetite.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> While Security Architecture is qualitative, several **quantitative models** are used during design reviews to justify control investments. These formulas are examinable in KTU ESE.

| Concept | Formula / Metric | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|
| Annual Loss Expectancy | $ALE = SLE \times ARO$ | $SLE$: Single Loss Expectancy (₹), $ARO$: Annualized Rate of Occurrence (events/year) |
| Single Loss Expectancy | $SLE = AV \times EF$ | $AV$: Asset Value (₹), $EF$: Exposure Factor (0 to 1) |
| Return on Security Investment | $ROSI = \dfrac{(ALE_{\text{pre}} - ALE_{\text{post}}) - Cost\ of\ Control}{Cost\ of\ Control}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| Cost-Benefit Ratio | $CBR = \dfrac{ALE - Cost\ of\ Control}{Cost\ of\ Control}$ | Used to decide *go/no-go* on a control |
| Risk Score (Qualitative) | $Risk = Likelihood \times Impact$ | Likelihood, Impact: scored 1–5 |
| Defense-in-Depth Effectiveness | $P_{\text{breach}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} p_i$ | $p_i$: probability each layer fails (independent) |
| Mean Time to Detect | $MTTD$ | Time (hours) — lower is better |
| Mean Time to Respond | $MTTR$ | Time (hours) — lower is better |

**Critical boundary conditions:**
- $0 \le EF \le 1$ (Exposure Factor is a fraction, never a currency value).
- $ARO \ge 0$ (integer events per year).
- $P_{\text{breach}} \rightarrow 0$ as $n \rightarrow \infty$ *only if* layers are truly independent (a key KTU trap question — layers in a poorly designed architecture are often correlated, so the product overstates protection).

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Banking & FinTech:** Security Architecture Reviews are mandatory before any new payment API is launched, regulated by **RBI Cyber Security Framework** in India.
- **Healthcare:** HIPAA and ABDM (Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission) require design reviews of any system touching patient data.
- **IoT & Embedded Systems:** Automotive (ISO/SAE 21434) and Industrial Control Systems (IEC 62443) mandate security-by-design reviews.
- **Cloud Migrations:** Every workload moving to AWS/Azure/GCP undergoes a *Well-Architected Review* with a dedicated "Security Pillar."

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Computing ALE & ROSI for a Design Review Decision

A KTU case-study type question. A bank is considering a new **Web Application Firewall (WAF)** for its internet banking portal.

**Given Data:**
- Asset Value of the portal: $AV = ₹\,5,00,00,000$ (5 Crore)
- Exposure Factor if an SQL injection succeeds: $EF = 0.40$
- Estimated Annual Rate of such attacks succeeding: $ARO = 2$ per year
- Cost of the WAF (control): $₹\,20,00,000$ per year
- After deploying WAF, expected $EF$ reduces to $0.04$ and $ARO$ becomes $0.5$ per year

**Step 1 — Calculate $SLE_{\text{pre}}$ (before control).**

$$SLE_{\text{pre}} = AV \times EF_{\text{pre}} = 5,00,00,000 \times 0.40 = 2,00,00,000 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $ALE_{\text{pre}}$.**

$$ALE_{\text{pre}} = SLE_{\text{pre}} \times ARO_{\text{pre}} = 2,00,00,000 \times 2 = 4,00,00,000 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate $SLE_{\text{post}}$ (after WAF).**

$$SLE_{\text{post}} = 5,00,00,000 \times 0.04 = 20,00,000 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate $ALE_{\text{post}}$.**

$$ALE_{\text{post}} = 20,00,000 \times 0.5 = 10,00,000 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate the Annual Savings from Risk Reduction.**

$$\text{Risk Reduction} = ALE_{\text{pre}} - ALE_{\text{post}} = 4,00,00,000 - 10,00,000 = 3,90,00,000 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 6 — Calculate $ROSI$.**

$$ROSI = \frac{\text{Risk Reduction} - \text{Cost of Control}}{\text{Cost of Control}} = \frac{3,90,00,000 - 20,00,000}{20,00,000}$$

$$ROSI = \frac{3,70,00,000}{20,00,000} = 18.5$$

**Interpretation:** $ROSI = 18.5$ means every ₹1 spent on the WAF returns ₹18.5 in avoided loss. The design review should **recommend approval**.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Defense-in-Depth Probability

A system has 3 independent security layers with failure probabilities $p_1 = 0.1$, $p_2 = 0.2$, $p_3 = 0.05$.

**Probability all three fail (breach):**

$$P_{\text{breach}} = p_1 \times p_2 \times p_3 = 0.1 \times 0.2 \times 0.05 = 0.001 = 0.1\%$$

**Adding a 4th layer with $p_4 = 0.1$:**

$$P_{\text{breach,new}} = 0.001 \times 0.1 = 0.0001 = 0.01\%$$

**KTU Trap:** If the 4th layer is *correlated* with $p_1$ (e.g., both depend on the same authentication server), the effective $p_4$ might equal $p_1$, and the new breach probability becomes:

$$P_{\text{breach,correlated}} = 0.001 \times 0.1 = 0.0001 \text{ (numerically same, but logically flawed)}$$

The correct correlated model is $P_{\text{joint}} = \max(p_1, p_4)$ for fully correlated events, not the product. **Always state the independence assumption in your KTU answers.**

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Threats to Architecture Layers

This is a high-yield KTU 14-mark answer structure. The student must learn to construct such a matrix during design reviews.

| STRIDE Threat | Business | Application | Data | Host | Network |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **S**poofing | Identity Governance Policy | Mutual TLS, MFA | Signed tokens | Kerberos | 802.1X, IPsec |
| **T**ampering | Audit policy | Input validation, WAF | Hashing, MACs | File integrity monitoring | TLS in transit |
| **R**epudiation | Legal hold | Non-repudiation logs | WORM storage | Sysmon, auditd | NTP-synced logs |
| **I**nfo Disclosure | Data classification | Output encoding, ASVS | AES-256, tokenization | DLP agents | Network segmentation |
| **D**enial of Service | BCP/DR plan | Rate limiting | Replication | Resource quotas | DDoS scrubbing, CDN |
| **E**lev. of Privilege | Least privilege policy | RBAC, parameterization | Key vault isolation | sudo policies, LAPS | Microsegmentation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for the exam:** Reviewers score heavily on *coverage completeness*. An empty cell in your matrix is an automatic half-mark deduction because it indicates an unmapped, unmitigated threat.

## 3.4 Risk-Scoring Worksheet Used During SDR

| Asset | Threat | Likelihood (1–5) | Impact (1–5) | Risk Score | Residual Score after Control | Accepted? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Customer DB | SQL Injection | 4 | 5 | 20 (High) | 4 (Low) | Yes |
| Internal Wiki | Defacement | 2 | 2 | 4 (Low) | 2 (Low) | Yes |
| Payment API | DDoS | 5 | 5 | 25 (Critical) | 8 (Medium) | No – escalate |

Residual risk above the organization's appetite (commonly set at score 8 in a 1–25 scheme) must trigger **re-design**, not just re-test.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between Security Architecture and Security Design Review.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Security Architecture** is the *forward-looking, prescriptive blueprint* that defines how security controls are arranged across all layers (business, application, data, host, network) of an enterprise system to meet confidentiality, integrity, and availability goals.
- **Security Design Review (SDR)** is the *retrospective, evaluative process* that scrutinizes an existing or proposed architecture against standards (NIST, ISO, OWASP) to identify gaps, assign risk scores, and mandate remediation.
- **Key distinction:** Architecture *creates* the design; SDR *challenges* the design. Architecture is proactive; SDR is a quality gate.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**List any six Saltzer-Schroeder security design principles.**

**Model Answer:**
1. Least Privilege
2. Defense in Depth
3. Fail-Safe Defaults
4. Separation of Duties
5. Complete Mediation
6. Economy of Mechanism
*(Additional acceptable: Open Design, Psychological Acceptability, Least Common Mechanism, Weakest Link.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]

**(a)** Explain the **layered Security Architecture** in detail with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of each layer in mitigating modern cyber threats. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A payment gateway company wants to deploy a new **DDoS protection service**. The unprotected $ALE$ is estimated at ₹ 2,00,00,000 and the cost of the service is ₹ 15,00,000 per year. If the service reduces the $ALE$ to ₹ 30,00,000, compute the **ROSI** and recommend whether to proceed. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution A

**(a) Layered Security Architecture — 7 marks**

1. **Business/Governance Layer (1 mark):** Sets the policy, risk appetite, and compliance baseline. Anchored in ISO 27001, NIST CSF, and local regulations (e.g., DPDP Act 2023, RBI guidelines). Without this layer, technical controls have no business justification.

2. **Application Layer (1.5 marks):** Embeds security into the SDLC through secure coding, input validation, OAuth 2.0/OIDC, and OWASP ASVS verification. Mitigates Injection, XSS, and IDOR threats.

3. **Data Layer (1.5 marks):** Protects data at rest (AES-256), in transit (TLS 1.3), and in use (confidential computing). Implements tokenization, DLP, and HSM-backed key management.

4. **Host/Endpoint Layer (1 mark):** Hardens OS via CIS Benchmarks, deploys EDR, enforces patch SLAs, and centralises logging via Sysmon/auditd.

5. **Network/Perimeter Layer (1.5 marks):** Implements Zero Trust segmentation, WAF, IDS/IPS, and DDoS scrubbing. Modern architectures invert the "castle-moat" model.

6. **Synthesis (0.5 mark):** Each layer must be *complementary* and *independently auditable*; this is the essence of Defense-in-Depth.

*(Reference Diagram: Use the block diagram from SECTION 4.1.)*

**(b) ROSI Calculation — 7 marks**

**Step 1:** Identify variables.

$$ALE_{\text{pre}} = ₹\,2,00,00,000,\quad ALE_{\text{post}} = ₹\,30,00,000,\quad C = ₹\,15,00,000$$

**Step 2:** Compute Risk Reduction.

$$\text{Risk Reduction} = ALE_{\text{pre}} - ALE_{\text{post}} = 2,00,00,000 - 30,00,000 = 1,70,00,000$$

**Step 3:** Apply ROSI formula.

$$ROSI = \frac{1,70,00,000 - 15,00,000}{15,00,000} = \frac{1,55,00,000}{15,00,000} = 10.33$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation and Recommendation (1 mark).
$ROSI = 10.33$ means every ₹1 spent on DDoS protection saves ₹10.33 in expected losses. Since $ROSI > 1$, the **control is strongly justified**, and the design review should **recommend approval** with quarterly re-validation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** Describe the **Security Design Review (SDR) process** in detail. Highlight the importance of threat modeling using the **STRIDE** methodology. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During an SDR, a system has three independent security layers with failure probabilities $p_1 = 0.15$, $p_2 = 0.10$, and $p_3 = 0.20$. Calculate (i) the probability of a successful breach, and (ii) the new breach probability if a 4th layer with $p_4 = 0.10$ is added. Comment on the **independence assumption**. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution B

**(a) SDR Process with STRIDE — 7 marks**

The SDR is a structured, multi-stage process:

1. **Charter and Scope (0.5):** Define system boundaries, data classification, and review goals.
2. **Artifact Collection (0.5):** Gather architecture diagrams, DFDs, requirements, and existing controls.
3. **Baseline Selection (0.5):** Choose reference standards (NIST SP 800-53, CIS Controls v8, OWASP ASVS).
4. **Threat Modeling with STRIDE (2.5):**
   - **S**poofing — Identity attacks (mitigated by MFA, mutual TLS).
   - **T**ampering — Data modification (mitigated by digital signatures, WAF).
   - **R**epudiation — Denial of action (mitigated by tamper-evident logs).
   - **I**nformation Disclosure — Data leakage (mitigated by encryption, DLP).
   - **D**enial of Service — Availability attacks (mitigated by rate limiting, CDN).
   - **E**levation of Privilege — Privilege escalation (mitigated by RBAC, least privilege).
5. **Control Mapping & Gap Analysis (1):** Build a threat-to-control matrix; flag unmapped threats.
6. **Findings Classification (1):** Tag each finding as Critical/High/Medium/Low; assign owners.
7. **Sign-off (1):** Management accepts residual risk or mandates re-design.

**(b) Defense-in-Depth Probability — 7 marks**

**Step 1:** Compute baseline breach probability (3 layers).

$$P_{\text{breach,3}} = p_1 \times p_2 \times p_3 = 0.15 \times 0.10 \times 0.20 = 0.003 = 0.3\%$$

**Step 2:** Add the 4th layer.

$$P_{\text{breach,4}} = P_{\text{breach,3}} \times p_4 = 0.003 \times 0.10 = 0.0003 = 0.03\%$$

**Step 3:** Reduction factor (1 mark).

$$\text{Reduction} = \frac{0.003 - 0.0003}{0.003} = 0.90 \text{ (i.e., 90\% reduction in breach probability)}$$

**Step 4:** Independence Assumption — Critical Discussion (2 marks).

The product-rule calculation assumes **statistical independence** of the layers. In practice:

- Layers often share a **common dependency** (e.g., a single Active Directory for authentication in layers 1, 2, and 4), making them *positively correlated*.
- A compromise of the common dependency collapses multiple layers simultaneously, so the **true breach probability is higher** than the product estimate.
- The correct model for fully dependent layers is $P_{\text{joint}} = \max(p_i)$, not the product.
- **Conclusion:** Independence must be explicitly verified; otherwise, the SDR overstates the architecture's resilience.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse Architecture with Design Review.** Architecture = *blueprint*; SDR = *inspection*. Examiners deduct 2–3 marks for conflating them.
> 2. **Always show units in ROSI/ALE calculations** (₹ for currency, % for probabilities). A bare number without units loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Independence assumption is mandatory.** Multiplying failure probabilities without stating the independence caveat is a classic 2-mark deduction in KTU papers.
> 4. **Threat-coverage matrices must be exhaustive.** Empty cells in your STRIDE-vs-Layer table are interpreted as "unmitigated threat" and cost 1 mark per gap.
> 5. **In Part A, do not write essays.** Three crisp sentences per definition are worth more than a rambling paragraph that misses the examiner's keyword.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Security Architecture** = the layered, *prescriptive* blueprint covering Business, Application, Data, Host, and Network layers.
- **Security Design Review (SDR)** = the *evaluative, formal process* that inspects the architecture against standards.
- **Foundational Principles (Saltzer-Schroeder):** Least Privilege, Defense in Depth, Fail-Safe Defaults, SoD, Complete Mediation, Open Design, Economy of Mechanism, Psychological Acceptability.
- **Modern Additions:** Zero Trust, Privacy-by-Design, Secure-by-Default, DevSecOps.
- **STRIDE Threat Model:** Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Info Disclosure, DoS, Elevation of Privilege.
- **Key Quantitative Metrics:** $SLE = AV \times EF$, $ALE = SLE \times ARO$, $ROSI = \dfrac{(ALE_{\text{pre}} - ALE_{\text{post}}) - C}{C}$.
- **Defense-in-Depth Formula:** $P_{\text{breach}} = \prod p_i$ (only under **independence**).
- **Reference Standards to Memorize:** ISO 27001, NIST SP 800-53, NIST CSF, CIS Controls v8, OWASP ASVS, PCI-DSS, IEC 62443.
- **Cost-of-Fix Curve:** 1 : 10 : 100 : 1000 across requirements → design → code → test → production.
- **Mandatory Exam Keywords:** Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability, Risk Appetite, Residual Risk, Threat Modeling, Control Mapping, Sign-off, Re-design.
- **Common Pitfalls:** Conflating architecture with review, ignoring independence assumption, leaving STRIDE cells empty, omitting units in calculations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Identify Project Security Risks & Selecting Risk Management Strategies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT418 - SECURITY ASSESSMENT AND RISK ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Identify Project Security Risks & Selecting Risk Management Strategies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Identifying Project Security Risks

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Project Security Risk Identification** is the systematic process of determining which risks are likely to affect a project, documenting each risk's characteristics, and establishing the project's exposure to uncertainty. It is the **first and most critical** step of the Project Risk Management lifecycle as defined in the NIST SP 800-30 Rev. 1 and PMBOK-aligned security frameworks adopted in KTU's 2024 scheme.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, this aligns with **Course Outcome CO3**: *Identify and document security risks in IT projects and recommend appropriate mitigation strategies.*

The formal inputs to this process are:
- **Enterprise Environmental Factors (EEFs)**
- **Organizational Process Assets (OPAs)**
- **Project Scope Statement / Statement of Work (SOW)**
- **Project Documents** (Risk Management Plan, Cost Baseline, Schedule Baseline)
- **Risk Categories / Risk Breakdown Structure (RBS)**

The outputs are:
- **Project Risk Register** (the master document)
- **Risk Report**
- **Updated Project Documents**

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Doctor's Check-Up"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine you are a doctor performing a full body check-up on a patient. Before you prescribe medicine (risk response), you MUST first identify all symptoms (risks). You check blood pressure, sugar levels, X-rays, etc. If you miss a symptom, the patient could collapse later. Similarly, in project security, if you miss identifying a risk (e.g., unpatched server), it becomes a vulnerability that attackers will exploit. **Risk Identification is the diagnostic phase; Risk Response is the treatment phase.**

### Key Terms You MUST Memorize (Board Exam Gold)

- **Asset**: Anything of value to the organization (data, hardware, software, people, reputation).
- **Threat**: Any potential cause of an undesirable event that may harm a system or organization.
- **Vulnerability**: A weakness in a system that can be exploited by a threat.
- **Impact**: The consequence or damage if a threat exploits a vulnerability.
- **Likelihood (Probability)**: The chance that a given threat will exploit a vulnerability.
- **Risk** = **Threat × Vulnerability × Impact** (The classic three-variable model)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 scheme, the **Risk Equation** is a 2-mark guaranteed question. Memorize: **Risk = f(Threat, Vulnerability, Asset, Impact, Likelihood)**.

---

## 1.2 Selecting Risk Management Strategies

### Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Risk Management Strategy Selection** is the process of choosing one or more strategies to reduce, transfer, avoid, or accept identified risks. The KTU 2024 syllabus, mapped to NIST SP 800-39 and ISO 31000, recognizes **four primary response strategies** plus two modern additions: **Risk Avoidance, Risk Transfer, Risk Mitigation, Risk Acceptance, Risk Exploitation, and Risk Sharing.**

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Travel Insurance" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** You are planning a trip to the mountains. The risks are: *bad weather, flight cancellation, theft, illness*. Your risk strategies are:
> - **Avoidance** → Cancel the trip (don't go).
> - **Transfer** → Buy travel insurance (let the insurer bear the cost).
> - **Mitigation** → Pack warm clothes, carry first-aid, lock luggage.
> - **Acceptance** → Accept the small risk of a rainy day and just carry an umbrella.
>
> In IT projects, we do exactly the same with security risks. Each risk gets a tailored response based on **cost-benefit analysis**.

### Mandatory Industry Standards (Highlight for 2024 Scheme)

- **NIST SP 800-30 Rev. 1** — Guide for Conducting Risk Assessments.
- **NIST SP 800-39** — Managing Information Security Risk.
- **ISO 31000:2018** — Risk Management Guidelines.
- **PMBOK 6th/7th Edition** — Project Risk Management Framework.
- **OCTAVE** (Operationally Critical Threat, Asset, and Vulnerability Evaluation) — CMU SEI.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Project Security Risk Identification Process

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Step 1 — Establish Context**
   - Define the scope of the project (IT infrastructure, application, network).
   - Identify boundaries: What is in-scope vs out-of-scope?
   - Identify stakeholders, regulatory requirements (GDPR, DPDP Act 2023, HIPAA).

2. **Step 2 — Asset Identification & Classification**
   - Enumerate all assets: hardware, software, data, services, people, facilities.
   - Classify by **CIA Triad** importance: **Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability**.
   - Assign asset valuation (quantitative ₹ value or qualitative rating).

3. **Step 3 — Threat Identification**
   - Identify natural threats (floods, earthquakes, fire).
   - Identify human threats (insider threats, hackers, nation-states, hacktivists).
   - Identify environmental threats (power failure, hardware failure, software bugs).

4. **Step 4 — Vulnerability Identification**
   - Use vulnerability scanners: **Nessus, Qualys, OpenVAS, Nikto**.
   - Perform penetration testing.
   - Review configuration baselines, patch levels, code reviews.

5. **Step 5 — Determine Likelihood & Impact**
   - Use qualitative scales: Low / Medium / High.
   - Use quantitative scales: Probability 0.0 to 1.0.

6. **Step 6 — Risk Determination & Register Creation**
   - Combine Threat + Vulnerability + Asset + Impact + Likelihood.
   - Populate the **Risk Register** (master deliverable).
   - Categorize risks using a **Risk Breakdown Structure (RBS)**.

7. **Step 7 — Risk Communication & Documentation**
   - Brief stakeholders, update the risk management plan.
   - Obtain sign-off from the project sponsor and security officer.

### Tools & Techniques for Risk Identification

| Technique | Description | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Brainstorming** | Team-based ideation of risks | Highly tested in Part A |
| **Delphi Technique** | Anonymous expert iteration | Tested as 3-mark question |
| **SWOT Analysis** | Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, Threats | Common in ESE |
| **Checklists** | Pre-defined risk lists | Quick reference tool |
| **Assumption Analysis** | Validating project assumptions | PMBOK standard |
| **Diagramming Techniques** | Fishbone (Ishikawa), Flowcharts, Influence Diagrams | Visual identification |
| **SWOT + RBS** | Combined structured approach | End-of-module favourite |
| **Expert Judgment** | Subject Matter Expert consultation | High-utility strategy |

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **The following formulas, scales, and tables are the most heavily tested in KTU 2024 ESE. Memorize the column headers and the qualitative mappings exactly.**

### Table 2.1 — Risk Score Formula (Quantitative)

$$
\text{Risk Score} \;=\; \text{Likelihood} \times \text{Impact}
$$

Where Likelihood and Impact are rated on a **1 to 5** scale (or 1 to 3 in simple models).

### Table 2.2 — Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE) — Quantitative Risk

$$
\text{ALE} \;=\; \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}
$$

$$
\text{SLE} \;=\; \text{Asset Value (AV)} \times \text{Exposure Factor (EF)}
$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $AV$ | Asset Value | ₹ (Rupees) |
| $EF$ | Exposure Factor (percentage of asset loss) | Decimal (0.0 to 1.0) |
| $SLE$ | Single Loss Expectancy | ₹ (Rupees) |
| $ARO$ | Annualized Rate of Occurrence | Number of times/year |
| $ALE$ | Annualized Loss Expectancy | ₹ (Rupees) |
| $CBA$ | Cost-Benefit Analysis ratio | Unitless |
| $ROS$ | Return on Security Investment | Percentage |

### Table 2.3 — Cost-Benefit Analysis (Mitigation Justification)

$$
CBA \;=\; \frac{ALE_{\text{before}} \;-\; ALE_{\text{after}}}{Cost \; of \; Control}
$$

**Decision Rule**: Implement the control if $CBA \;>\; 1$.

### Table 2.4 — Qualitative Risk Matrix (KTU's Most Tested Table)

| Likelihood \ Impact | **Low (1)** | **Medium (2)** | **High (3)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **High (3)** | Medium (3) | High (6) | Critical (9) |
| **Medium (2)** | Low (2) | Medium (4) | High (6) |
| **Low (1)** | Low (1) | Low (2) | Medium (3) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Examiner Tip:** The risk score is the **product of row × column**. The cell value determines the response priority. KTU 2024 scheme has frequently asked students to *"Plot the risk matrix and identify which risks are Critical."* This is a 7-mark sure-shot.

### Table 2.5 — Risk Appetite vs Risk Tolerance

| Term | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Risk Appetite** | Total amount of risk an organization is willing to accept | "We accept ₹50 lakhs of annual cyber risk" |
| **Risk Tolerance** | Acceptable deviation around objectives | "Uptime must be ≥ 99.5%" |
| **Risk Threshold** | Specific level triggering action | "Patch within 72 hours of CVE" |

---

## 2.3 The Four (Plus Two) Risk Management Strategies

### 2.3.1 Risk Avoidance

- **Definition**: Eliminate the risk entirely by removing the cause or the exposure.
- **When to use**: Risk score is **CRITICAL**, no mitigation is cost-effective.
- **Example**: Discontinuing a project feature that requires untrusted third-party code; using a different cloud provider with stronger SLAs.
- **Cost**: High opportunity cost — you lose the benefit of the activity.

### 2.3.2 Risk Transfer

- **Definition**: Shift the impact of the risk to a third party.
- **Mechanisms**: Cyber insurance, outsourcing, Service Level Agreements (SLAs), contracts, indemnification clauses.
- **Example**: Buying a cyber insurance policy that covers data breach costs.
- **Important**: Transfer does NOT eliminate the risk; it shifts the **financial impact**.

### 2.3.3 Risk Mitigation (Reduction)

- **Definition**: Reduce the likelihood and/or impact to an acceptable level.
- **Most common strategy** in real-world projects.
- **Examples**: Patching vulnerabilities, implementing firewalls, MFA, encryption, employee training, access controls, network segmentation.
- **Cost-Benefit Analysis** is performed before selection.

### 2.3.4 Risk Acceptance (Retention)

- **Definition**: Consciously accept the risk as it falls within tolerance.
- **Active vs Passive Acceptance**:
  - **Active**: A formal sign-off acknowledging the risk (e.g., management approval).
  - **Passive**: No action taken (lazy acceptance — bad practice).
- **When to use**: Risk is **Low**, mitigation cost > potential impact.

### 2.3.5 Risk Exploitation (Positive Risk)

- **Definition**: Seize an opportunity to gain positive impact.
- **Example**: Adopting a new technology ahead of competitors to gain market share.

### 2.3.6 Risk Sharing / Enhancement

- **Definition**: Allocate ownership to a third party best able to handle it.
- **Example**: Joint ventures, partnerships.

### Real-World Utility

| Industry | How They Apply It |
|---|---|
| Banking | Transfer (cyber insurance) + Mitigation (FIM, MFA) |
| Healthcare | Avoidance (no PHI on cloud) + Mitigation (HIPAA controls) |
| E-commerce | Mitigation (WAF, bot management) + Transfer (PCI-DSS insurer) |
| Government | Avoidance (no foreign cloud) + Acceptance (low-priority risks) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Quantitative Risk Calculation (ALE)

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** A small e-commerce company in Kerala has an online database server valued at **₹10,00,000**. A SQL injection vulnerability could expose **60%** of the data. Historically, such attacks occur **3 times per year**. Calculate the **ALE before mitigation**. After implementing a Web Application Firewall (WAF) costing **₹2,00,000/year**, the ARO drops to **0.5/year** and EF to **10%**. Should the company invest in the WAF?

### Step 1 — Identify Given Variables

$$
AV = 10{,}00{,}000 \; \text{INR} \quad \text{(Asset Value)}
$$

$$
EF_{\text{before}} = 0.60 \quad \text{(60\% loss per incident)}
$$

$$
ARO_{\text{before}} = 3 \; \text{(incidents per year)}
$$

$$
EF_{\text{after}} = 0.10 \quad \text{(10\% loss per incident post-WAF)}
$$

$$
ARO_{\text{after}} = 0.5 \quad \text{(incidents per year post-WAF)}
$$

$$
\text{Cost of Control} = 2{,}00{,}000 \; \text{INR per year}
$$

### Step 2 — Calculate SLE Before Mitigation

$$
SLE_{\text{before}} = AV \times EF_{\text{before}}
$$

$$
SLE_{\text{before}} = 10{,}00{,}000 \times 0.60
$$

$$
SLE_{\text{before}} = 6{,}00{,}000 \; \text{INR}
$$

### Step 3 — Calculate ALE Before Mitigation

$$
ALE_{\text{before}} = SLE_{\text{before}} \times ARO_{\text{before}}
$$

$$
ALE_{\text{before}} = 6{,}00{,}000 \times 3
$$

$$
ALE_{\text{before}} = 18{,}00{,}000 \; \text{INR per year}
$$

### Step 4 — Calculate SLE After Mitigation

$$
SLE_{\text{after}} = AV \times EF_{\text{after}}
$$

$$
SLE_{\text{after}} = 10{,}00{,}000 \times 0.10
$$

$$
SLE_{\text{after}} = 1{,}00{,}000 \; \text{INR}
$$

### Step 5 — Calculate ALE After Mitigation

$$
ALE_{\text{after}} = SLE_{\text{after}} \times ARO_{\text{after}}
$$

$$
ALE_{\text{after}} = 1{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5
$$

$$
ALE_{\text{after}} = 50{,}000 \; \text{INR per year}
$$

### Step 6 — Cost-Benefit Analysis (CBA)

$$
\text{Risk Reduction} = ALE_{\text{before}} - ALE_{\text{after}}
$$

$$
\text{Risk Reduction} = 18{,}00{,}000 - 50{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Risk Reduction} = 17{,}50{,}000 \; \text{INR per year}
$$

$$
CBA = \frac{\text{Risk Reduction}}{\text{Cost of Control}}
$$

$$
CBA = \frac{17{,}50{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000}
$$

$$
CBA = 8.75
$$

### Step 7 — Decision

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since $CBA = 8.75 \gg 1$, the WAF investment is **highly justified**. The company saves ₹8.75 in avoided losses for every ₹1 spent on the control. This is **Risk Mitigation** as the chosen strategy.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Qualitative Risk Matrix Construction

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** A bank identifies 5 risks for its mobile banking app. Rate each on Likelihood (1-3) and Impact (1-3), compute the Risk Score, and recommend a strategy.

### Step 1 — Tabulate the Risks

| ID | Risk Description | Likelihood (L) | Impact (I) | Score = L × I | Risk Level | Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| R1 | Phishing attack on customers | 3 | 3 | 9 | **Critical** | Mitigation + Transfer |
| R2 | Server downtime (1 hour/year) | 1 | 2 | 2 | Low | Acceptance |
| R3 | Insider data theft | 2 | 3 | 6 | High | Mitigation + Transfer |
| R4 | Mobile app reverse engineering | 3 | 2 | 6 | High | Mitigation |
| R5 | Vendor lock-in with cloud | 1 | 1 | 1 | Low | Acceptance |

### Step 2 — Visualize on Risk Matrix

| L \ I | 1 (Low) | 2 (Medium) | 3 (High) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **3 (High)** | Med (3) | **High (6)** R4 | **Critical (9)** R1 |
| **2 (Medium)** | Low (2) | Med (4) | **High (6)** R3 |
| **1 (Low)** | **Low (1)** R5 | **Low (2)** R2 | Med (3) |

### Step 3 — Strategy Mapping (Analytical Matrix)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rule of Thumb for Strategy Mapping:**
>
> - Score **1–3** → **Acceptance** (low risk, low priority).
> - Score **4–6** → **Mitigation** (medium risk, plan controls).
> - Score **7–9** → **Avoidance or Transfer + Mitigation** (high risk, immediate action).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Tree — Selecting a Strategy

The following logical decision tree maps the complete selection process. Memorize this — it is a 14-mark full question in the KTU ESE.

```
START
  │
  ▼
Is the risk score ≥ 7 (Critical)?
  │
  ├── YES ──► Can the activity be eliminated?
  │             │
  │             ├── YES ──► Risk AVOIDANCE
  │             │
  │             └── NO  ──► Is mitigation cost-effective?
  │                          │
  │                          ├── YES ──► MITIGATION (+ transfer residual)
  │                          │
  │                          └── NO  ──► TRANSFER (Cyber Insurance)
  │
  └── NO (score 4-6) ──► Is mitigation cheaper than impact?
                          │
                          ├── YES ──► MITIGATION
                          │
                          └── NO  ──► ACCEPTANCE (documented)

  Score 1-3 ──────────────────► ACCEPTANCE (with monitoring)
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Risk Identification Workflow (End-to-End Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Establish Project Context] --> B0[Identify Assets and Classify by CIA]
    B0 --> C0[Identify Threats Natural Human Environmental]
    C0 --> D0[Identify Vulnerabilities via Scanners and Pen Test]
    D0 --> E0[Assess Likelihood and Impact]
    E0 --> F0[Compute Risk Score Likelihood times Impact]
    F0 --> G0[Plot on Qualitative Risk Matrix]
    G0 --> H0[Populate Risk Register]
    H0 --> I0[Communicate to Stakeholders]
    I0 --> J0{Is Risk Score Critical or High}
    J0 --> K0[Select Response Strategy]
    K0 --> L0[Monitor and Review Periodically]
```

## 4.2 Risk Management Strategy Selection Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Identified Risk in Register] --> B1{Can We Eliminate the Activity}
    B1 -- Yes --> C1[AVOIDANCE]
    B1 -- No --> D1{Is Mitigation Cost Effective}
    D1 -- Yes --> E1[MITIGATION]
    D1 -- No --> F1{Is Insurance Available}
    F1 -- Yes --> G1[TRANSFER]
    F1 -- No --> H1{Is Impact Low}
    H1 -- Yes --> I1[ACCEPTANCE with Sign off]
    H1 -- No --> J1[Escalate to Senior Management]
```

## 4.3 Risk Register Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RR[Project Risk Register Document]
        A2[Risk ID R001]
        B2[Description Phishing attack]
        C2[Category Technical Human]
        D2[Asset Affected Customer Database]
        E2[Threat External Attacker]
        F2[Vulnerability Lack of MFA]
        G2[Likelihood 3 High]
        H2[Impact 3 High]
        I2[Score 9 Critical]
        J2[Strategy Mitigation plus Transfer]
        K2[Owner CISO]
        L2[Status Open]
    end
```

## 4.4 Risk Breakdown Structure (RBS) — Hierarchical Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0[All Project Security Risks] --> R1[Technical Risks]
    R0 --> R2[Organizational Risks]
    R0 --> R3[External Risks]
    R0 --> R4[Project Management Risks]
    R1 --> R1a[Software Bugs]
    R1 --> R1b[Hardware Failures]
    R1 --> R1c[Network Outages]
    R2 --> R2a[Insider Threats]
    R2 --> R2b[Skill Gaps]
    R2 --> R2c[Policy Violations]
    R3 --> R3a[Natural Disasters]
    R3 --> R3b[Supply Chain]
    R3 --> R3c[Regulatory Changes]
    R4 --> R4a[Schedule Slippage]
    R4 --> R4b[Budget Overrun]
    R4 --> R4c[Scope Creep]
```

## 4.5 Strategy Selection — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Input[Identified Risks] --> Pre[Pre processing Scoring and Prioritization]
    Pre --> Decision[Strategy Decision Engine]
    Decision --> Str1[AVOIDANCE]
    Decision --> Str2[TRANSFER]
    Decision --> Str3[MITIGATION]
    Decision --> Str4[ACCEPTANCE]
    Str1 --> Out[Documented Risk Response Plan]
    Str2 --> Out
    Str3 --> Out
    Str4 --> Out
    Out --> Feedback[Continuous Monitoring Loop]
    Feedback --> Pre
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define Project Security Risk. List any four techniques used to identify project security risks.

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Project Security Risk is an uncertain event or condition that, if it occurs, has a positive or negative effect on a project's security objectives (confidentiality, integrity, or availability of project assets). It is the function of threat, vulnerability, and impact.
>
> **Risk = Threat × Vulnerability × Impact**

**Four Risk Identification Techniques (any four to be listed):**

1. **Brainstorming** — Structured team session to generate risk ideas.
2. **Delphi Technique** — Iterative anonymous expert consultation.
3. **SWOT Analysis** — Internal Strengths/Weaknesses, external Opportunities/Threats.
4. **Checklist Analysis** — Use of pre-defined risk lists from past projects.
5. **Assumption Analysis** — Validating assumptions documented in the project plan.
6. **Diagramming** — Cause-and-effect (Ishikawa/Fishbone) diagrams.
7. **Expert Judgment** — Consultation with subject matter experts.

**(End of Answer — 3 marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Differentiate between Risk Avoidance and Risk Acceptance with suitable examples.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Risk Avoidance | Risk Acceptance |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Eliminating the risk by removing the cause | Consciously tolerating the risk within limits |
| **Effect on Risk** | Risk is reduced to **zero** | Risk remains **unchanged** |
| **Cost Implication** | High opportunity cost (lose benefits) | Low cost (no extra control) |
| **When Used** | Risk score is critical and unavoidable by mitigation | Risk is low and mitigation is not cost-effective |
| **Example** | Discontinuing a feature using untrusted third-party SDK | Accepting that a server may go down for 30 minutes/year |
| **Documentation** | Project plan update | Formal sign-off in Risk Register |

**(End of Answer — 3 marks)**

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (With Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3, Q8(a)+Q8(b)]**

> **(a)** Explain in detail the **process of identifying project security risks**, listing the inputs, tools & techniques, and outputs. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** For a small e-commerce project, an asset (customer database) is valued at **₹20,00,000**. A ransomware attack could lose **50%** of the data, and the **ARO is 2 per year**. After deploying an EDR (Endpoint Detection and Response) solution costing **₹3,00,000/year**, the ARO drops to **0.3 per year** and EF to **5%**. Calculate SLE, ALE before & after, and the CBA. Recommend a strategy. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

1. **Inputs of Risk Identification** (1 mark)
   - Risk Management Plan
   - Project Scope Statement / SOW
   - Cost & Schedule Baselines
   - Organizational Process Assets (OPAs)
   - Enterprise Environmental Factors (EEFs)

2. **Tools & Techniques** (3 marks)
   - **Brainstorming**, **Delphi Technique**, **SWOT**, **Checklists**, **Assumption Analysis**, **Diagramming** (Fishbone, Flow), **Expert Judgment**, **Risk Breakdown Structure (RBS)**.

3. **Process Steps** (2 marks)
   - Establish context → Identify assets → Identify threats → Identify vulnerabilities → Determine likelihood & impact → Populate risk register → Communicate.

4. **Outputs** (1 mark)
   - **Project Risk Register**, **Risk Report**, **Updated Project Documents**.

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks (Exhaustive Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Identify given values**

$$
AV = 20{,}00{,}000 \; \text{INR}, \quad EF_{\text{before}} = 0.50, \quad ARO_{\text{before}} = 2
$$

$$
EF_{\text{after}} = 0.05, \quad ARO_{\text{after}} = 0.3
$$

$$
\text{Cost of Control} = 3{,}00{,}000 \; \text{INR/year}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute SLE Before**

$$
SLE_{\text{before}} = AV \times EF_{\text{before}} = 20{,}00{,}000 \times 0.50 = 10{,}00{,}000 \; \text{INR}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute ALE Before**

$$
ALE_{\text{before}} = SLE_{\text{before}} \times ARO_{\text{before}} = 10{,}00{,}000 \times 2 = 20{,}00{,}000 \; \text{INR/year}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute SLE After**

$$
SLE_{\text{after}} = AV \times EF_{\text{after}} = 20{,}00{,}000 \times 0.05 = 1{,}00{,}000 \; \text{INR}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute ALE After**

$$
ALE_{\text{after}} = SLE_{\text{after}} \times ARO_{\text{after}} = 1{,}00{,}000 \times 0.3 = 30{,}000 \; \text{INR/year}
$$

**Step 6 — CBA Calculation**

$$
\text{Risk Reduction} = 20{,}00{,}000 - 30{,}000 = 19{,}70{,}000
$$

$$
CBA = \frac{19{,}70{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000} = 6.57
$$

**Step 7 — Recommendation**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since $CBA = 6.57 \gg 1$, the EDR investment is **financially justified**. The recommended strategy is **Risk Mitigation** (deploying the EDR) with possible **Risk Transfer** (cyber insurance) for residual risk. **Score: 7/7**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3, Internal Choice]**

> **(a)** Describe the **four major risk management strategies** (Avoidance, Transfer, Mitigation, Acceptance) with one real-world example for each in the context of an IT project. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Construct a **qualitative risk matrix** for the following four risks identified in a hospital information system project and recommend a strategy for each. **(7 marks)**
>
> | Risk | Likelihood | Impact |
> |---|---|---|
> | R1: Unauthorized access to patient records | High | High |
> | R2: Server disk failure | Low | Medium |
> | R3: Vendor product discontinuation | Medium | High |
> | R4: Power outage in the data center | Medium | High |

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Risk Avoidance** (1.5 marks) — Removing the risk source. *Example: Deciding not to use a public cloud to store PHI (Personal Health Information) and instead choosing a private on-premise deployment to avoid external attack vectors.*

**2. Risk Transfer** (1.5 marks) — Shifting impact to a third party. *Example: Buying a cyber insurance policy that covers HIPAA violation penalties, or outsourcing payment processing to a PCI-DSS compliant vendor.*

**3. Risk Mitigation** (2 marks) — Reducing likelihood and impact. *Example: Implementing Role-Based Access Control (RBAC), Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA), encryption-at-rest, and regular vulnerability scanning to reduce the impact of unauthorized access.*

**4. Risk Acceptance** (2 marks) — Consciously accepting low-priority risks. *Example: Accepting that a single printer in a remote branch office may fail once a year, as the cost of a redundant printer exceeds the impact of a one-day downtime.*

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Risk Matrix Construction** (3 marks)

| L \ I | Low (1) | Medium (2) | High (3) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **High (3)** | Med (3) | High (6) | **Critical (9)** R1 |
| **Medium (2)** | Low (2) | Med (4) | **High (6)** R3, R4 |
| **Low (1)** | Low (1) | **Low (2)** R2 | Med (3) |

**Step 2 — Risk Score Computation** (2 marks)

- **R1** = 3 × 3 = **9 → Critical**
- **R2** = 1 × 2 = **2 → Low**
- **R3** = 2 × 3 = **6 → High**
- **R4** = 2 × 3 = **6 → High**

**Step 3 — Strategy Mapping** (2 marks)

| Risk | Score | Recommended Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| **R1** | 9 | **Avoidance** (air-gapped storage) or **Mitigation + Transfer** (MFA + encryption + cyber insurance) |
| **R2** | 2 | **Acceptance** (low impact) |
| **R3** | 6 | **Mitigation** (multi-vendor strategy) + **Transfer** (contractual SLA) |
| **R4** | 6 | **Mitigation** (UPS, redundant power, generator) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Score: 7/7**.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes That Cost Marks (Read Before Exam):**
>
> 1. **Confusing SLE with ALE** — SLE is *per incident*; ALE is *per year*. Many students write them interchangeably. **-2 marks** deduction.
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply EF and ARO** — A common error is calculating only one term.
> 3. **Wrong risk score formula** — Some students **add** L and I; the correct method is to **multiply**. **-1 mark**.
> 4. **Not documenting Acceptance** — A risk that is accepted must have a **sign-off**. A passive acceptance without documentation will be marked as **incomplete**.
> 5. **Strategy Selection without Justification** — Always state the **CBA result or risk score** before selecting the strategy. Writing "Mitigation" without justification = **partial marks only**.
> 6. **Confusing Threats and Vulnerabilities** — A *threat* is the actor/event; a *vulnerability* is the weakness. Mixing them up = **-1 mark**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Read the night before the exam):**
>
> - **Risk Equation:** Risk = Threat × Vulnerability × Impact.
> - **Risk Score (Qualitative):** Likelihood × Impact (1-3 scale or 1-5 scale).
> - **ALE Formula:** $ALE = SLE \times ARO = (AV \times EF) \times ARO$.
> - **CBA Formula:** $(ALE_{before} - ALE_{after}) / Cost \; of \; Control$.
> - **Decision Rule:** Accept if $CBA \le 1$; Mitigate if $CBA > 1$.
> - **Four Core Strategies:** Avoidance, Transfer, Mitigation, Acceptance.
> - **Two Bonus Strategies (Positive Risks):** Exploit, Share/Enhance.
> - **RBS** = Risk Breakdown Structure — hierarchical decomposition of risks.
> - **Risk Register** = The single most important output document.
> - **Techniques:** Brainstorming, Delphi, SWOT, Checklist, RBS, Diagramming, Expert Judgment.
> - **Standards:** NIST SP 800-30, NIST SP 800-39, ISO 31000, PMBOK, OCTAVE.
> - **Always** justify the chosen strategy with either a CBA or a Risk Score.
> - **Always** document passive vs active acceptance explicitly.
> - **Avoidance** is rare and costly; **Mitigation** is the most common real-world strategy.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Risk Management Framework
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT418 - SECURITY ASSESSMENT AND RISK ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Risk Management Framework

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Risk Management Framework (RMF)** is a structured, multi-disciplinary, and iterative process promulgated by the **National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)** under **NIST SP 800-37 Rev. 2** that integrates information security, privacy, and risk management activities into the system development life cycle (SDLC). It provides a disciplined, comprehensive, and continuous process for managing organizational risk resulting from the operation and use of information systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The RMF transforms the traditional Certification & Accreditation (C&A) paradigm into a six-step **Continuous Authorization** model, emphasizing near-real-time risk management rather than point-in-time approvals.

The six core functions/steps of the RMF, as adapted from the NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF) and RMF, are:

1. **Categorize** the information system and the information processed, stored, and transmitted.
2. **Select** an initial set of baseline security controls.
3. **Implement** the security controls and document the implementation.
4. **Assess** the security controls to determine extent of correct implementation.
5. **Authorize** the information system for operation based on residual risk.
6. **Monitor** the security controls on an ongoing basis.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **The "House Construction & Living" Analogy**
>
> Think of Risk Management Framework like building and maintaining a secure house:
>
> - **Categorize** = Deciding whether you need a small shed, a family home, or a fortress (based on what you store inside).
> - **Select** = Picking the right locks, alarms, walls, and cameras from a standard catalog.
> - **Implement** = Actually installing those locks, alarms, and cameras.
> - **Assess** = Hiring a security inspector to test if the locks actually work and the cameras record.
> - **Authorize** = The insurance company agreeing to insure the house after reviewing the inspection.
> - **Monitor** = Continuous 24/7 surveillance, periodic maintenance, and updating the system when new threats emerge.
>
> The house isn't "secured forever" after one inspection — security is a **lifecycle**, not a one-time event. This is the essence of the RMF.

### 1.3 Key Standardized Metrics & Terminology

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **FIPS 199** = Standards for Security Categorization of Federal Information and Information Systems.
> - **FIPS 200** = Minimum Security Requirements for Federal Information and Information Systems.
> - **SP 800-53** = Security and Privacy Controls for Information Systems and Organizations.
> - **SP 800-37 Rev. 2** = Risk Management Framework for Information Systems and Organizations.
> - **SP 800-30 Rev. 1** = Guide for Conducting Risk Assessments.
> - **ATO (Authority To Operate)** = Formal authorization granted by a senior official.
> - **SLA (Service Level Agreement)** = Defined performance threshold, e.g., **99.9%** uptime.
> - **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair)** = Average time to restore a failed component, typically measured in **hours**.
> - **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** = Predicted elapsed time between inherent failures of a system, measured in **hours**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Seven-Step RMF Lifecycle (NIST SP 800-37 Rev. 2)

The RMF is decomposed into a **Prepare** step plus the **Six Core Steps**, executed across **System Level** and (in updated Rev. 2) **Organization Level**:

#### Step 0: Prepare (Organization Level)
- **Why:** Establishes the context, risk tolerance, and strategy.
- **How:** Identify mandates, assets, governance structures, and risk assessment methodology.

#### Step 1: Categorize
- Categorize the system based on impact: **LOW**, **MODERATE**, or **HIGH**.
- Use the formula: $C = \{C_I, C_A, C_A_v\}$ representing Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability.

#### Step 2: Select
- Select baseline controls from **NIST SP 800-53** (e.g., AC-2, AU-12, SC-7).
- Tailor and supplement controls based on risk assessment.

#### Step 3: Implement
- Deploy controls in operational systems; document in the **System Security Plan (SSP)**.

#### Step 4: Assess
- Determine if controls are implemented correctly, operating as intended, and producing the desired outcome.
- Produce a **Security Assessment Report (SAR)**.

#### Step 5: Authorize
- Senior official reviews SAR + SSP and grants an **ATO**, **DATO (Denial)**, or **IATO (Interim)**.

#### Step 6: Monitor
- Continuous monitoring, configuration management, incident response integration.

### 2.2 Quantitative Risk Formulas (KTU Formula Sheet)

The table below summarizes all critical formulas used in RMF risk calculations:

| **Formula Name** | **Equation** | **Parameters** | **Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Single Loss Expectancy | $SLE = AV \times EF$ | $AV$ = Asset Value, $EF$ = Exposure Factor | Currency (e.g., ₹) |
| Annual Loss Expectancy | $ALE = SLE \times ARO$ | $ARO$ = Annualized Rate of Occurrence | Currency/Year |
| Annualized Rate of Occurrence | $ARO = \frac{\text{Number of occurrences}}{\text{Time period (years)}}$ | Empirical estimation | occurrences/year |
| Risk Magnitude | $R = \{T, V, P, I\}$ | Threat, Vulnerability, Probability, Impact | Dimensionless score |
| Residual Risk | $R_{\text{residual}} = R_{\text{total}} - R_{\text{mitigated}}$ | $R_{\text{total}}$ = Total Risk, $R_{\text{mitigated}}$ = Mitigated | Risk score |
| Cost-Benefit Ratio | $CBA = \frac{ALE_{\text{before}} - ALE_{\text{after}}}{Cost\ of\ Control}$ | Comparison metric | Ratio (unitless) |
| Return on Security Investment | $ROSI = \frac{(ALE_{\text{before}} - ALE_{\text{after}}) - Cost\ of\ Control}{Cost\ of\ Control}$ | Cost-benefit indicator | Percentage (%) |
| FIPS 199 Impact Aggregation | $C_{\text{sys}} = \max(C_I, C_A, C_{A_v})$ | Maximum of three CIA dimensions | Categorical: L, M, H |
| Mean Time To Repair | $MTTR = \frac{\sum T_{\text{repair}}}{N_{\text{failures}}}$ | Average repair time | Hours |
| Mean Time Between Failures | $MTBF = \frac{\sum T_{\text{operational}}}{N_{\text{failures}}}$ | System reliability | Hours |
| System Availability | $A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Availability ratio | Percentage (%) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** $\vert ALE \vert$ style absolute value notation **must** be written using LaTeX `\vert` in markdown to prevent table corruption.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Defense & Aerospace:** Used in **DO-326A** Airworthiness Security Process for aircraft systems.
- **Healthcare:** Underpins **HIPAA Security Rule** assessments for Electronic Health Records.
- **Financial Sector:** Drives **PCI-DSS** compliance audits for payment systems.
- **Smart Grid:** Integral to **NERC CIP** for critical infrastructure protection.
- **Cloud Computing:** Forms the basis of **FedRAMP** (Federal Risk and Authorization Management Program) for cloud service providers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Residual Risk Calculation (Step-by-Step)

Consider an organization with the following risk profile:

- Asset Value (sensitive customer database) = **₹10,00,000**
- Exposure Factor (data breach destroys 60% of data value) = **0.60**
- Annualized Rate of Occurrence (without controls) = **0.5** (once every 2 years)
- Cost of new firewall control = **₹1,50,000**
- After control: ARO drops to **0.1**, EF drops to **0.20**

**Step 1:** Compute the original **Single Loss Expectancy**.

$$
SLE_{\text{before}} = AV \times EF_{\text{before}}
$$

$$
SLE_{\text{before}} = 10{,}00{,}000 \times 0.60
$$

$$
SLE_{\text{before}} = 6{,}00{,}000\ \text{INR}
$$

*Explanation:* A single breach would cost the organization ₹6,00,000 based on 60% destruction of the ₹10 lakh asset.

**Step 2:** Compute the original **Annual Loss Expectancy**.

$$
ALE_{\text{before}} = SLE_{\text{before}} \times ARO_{\text{before}}
$$

$$
ALE_{\text{before}} = 6{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5
$$

$$
ALE_{\text{before}} = 3{,}00{,}000\ \text{INR/year}
$$

*Explanation:* Yearly, the organization is expected to lose ₹3,00,000 without the firewall.

**Step 3:** Compute the post-control **Single Loss Expectancy**.

$$
SLE_{\text{after}} = AV \times EF_{\text{after}}
$$

$$
SLE_{\text{after}} = 10{,}00{,}000 \times 0.20
$$

$$
SLE_{\text{after}} = 2{,}00{,}000\ \text{INR}
$$

*Explanation:* The firewall reduces the damage per breach from 60% to 20% exposure.

**Step 4:** Compute the post-control **Annual Loss Expectancy**.

$$
ALE_{\text{after}} = SLE_{\text{after}} \times ARO_{\text{after}}
$$

$$
ALE_{\text{after}} = 2{,}00{,}000 \times 0.1
$$

$$
ALE_{\text{after}} = 20{,}000\ \text{INR/year}
$$

*Explanation:* Yearly expected loss with the firewall is only ₹20,000.

**Step 5:** Compute the **Total Risk Reduction** (benefit of the control).

$$
\Delta ALE = ALE_{\text{before}} - ALE_{\text{after}}
$$

$$
\Delta ALE = 3{,}00{,}000 - 20{,}000
$$

$$
\Delta ALE = 2{,}80{,}000\ \text{INR/year}
$$

*Explanation:* The control saves ₹2,80,000 per year in expected losses.

**Step 6:** Compute the **Return on Security Investment (ROSI)**.

$$
ROSI = \frac{\Delta ALE - Cost\ of\ Control}{Cost\ of\ Control}
$$

$$
ROSI = \frac{2{,}80{,}000 - 1{,}50{,}000}{1{,}50{,}000}
$$

$$
ROSI = \frac{1{,}30{,}000}{1{,}50{,}000}
$$

$$
ROSI = 0.8667 \approx 86.67\%
$$

*Explanation:* The control yields an **86.67%** return on security investment in the first year.

**Step 7:** Compute the **Residual Risk** as the post-control ALE.

$$
R_{\text{residual}} = ALE_{\text{after}} = 20{,}000\ \text{INR/year}
$$

*Explanation:* This is the risk the organization must accept after mitigation.

### 3.2 Derivation: FIPS 199 System Security Categorization

For a system with the following impact levels:

- Confidentiality = **MODERATE**
- Integrity = **HIGH**
- Availability = **MODERATE**

Apply the **waterfall (maximum)** formula:

$$
C_{\text{sys}} = \max(C_I, C_A, C_{A_v})
$$

$$
C_{\text{sys}} = \max(M, H, M)
$$

$$
C_{\text{sys}} = HIGH
$$

*Explanation:* The overall system categorization is **HIGH** because the highest impact level across CIA dimensions governs the baseline control selection.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Control Selection by FIPS 199 Category

| **FIPS 199 Category** | **Baseline Control Set (NIST SP 800-53)** | **Number of Controls** | **Example Control IDs** |
|---|---|---|---|
| LOW | Low baseline | ~114 | AC-1, AU-1, SC-1 |
| MODERATE | Moderate baseline | ~250 | AC-2(1), AU-12, SC-7(1) |
| HIGH | High baseline | ~400+ | AC-2(7), AU-12(1), SC-7(14) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 NIST RMF Lifecycle (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step0["STEP 0 PREPARE ORGANIZATION LEVEL"]:::prep
    step1["STEP 1 CATEGORIZE SYSTEM AND DATA"]:::core
    step2["STEP 2 SELECT BASELINE CONTROLS"]:::core
    step3["STEP 3 IMPLEMENT CONTROLS IN SYSTEM"]:::core
    step4["STEP 4 ASSESS CONTROL EFFECTIVENESS"]:::core
    step5["STEP 5 AUTHORIZE SYSTEM ATO DECISION"]:::core
    step6["STEP 6 MONITOR CONTINUOUSLY"]:::core
    feedback["FEEDBACK LOOP TO PREPARE"]:::fb

    step0 --> step1
    step1 --> step2
    step2 --> step3
    step3 --> step4
    step4 --> step5
    step5 --> step6
    step6 --> feedback
    feedback --> step0

    classDef prep fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608,color:#000
    classDef core fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1b4f72,color:#000
    classDef fb fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#922b21,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Risk Treatment Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    risk["IDENTIFIED RISK"]:::node
    avoid["AVOID ELIMINATE ACTIVITY"]:::node
    mitigate["MITIGATE IMPLEMENT CONTROLS"]:::node
    transfer["TRANSFER INSURANCE OR OUTSOURCE"]:::node
    accept["ACCEPT FORMAL SIGN OFF"]:::node
    review["RESIDUAL RISK REVIEW"]:::node

    risk --> avoid
    risk --> mitigate
    risk --> transfer
    risk --> accept
    avoid --> review
    mitigate --> review
    transfer --> review
    accept --> review

    classDef node fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#196f3d,color:#000
```

### 4.3 FIPS 199 Categorization Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["SYSTEM TO CATEGORIZE"]:::n
    ci["CONFIDENTIALITY IMPACT"]:::n
    in["INTEGRITY IMPACT"]:::n
    av["AVAILABILITY IMPACT"]:::n
    max["APPLY MAX FUNCTION"]:::n
    out["OVERALL SYSTEM CATEGORY LOW MODERATE HIGH"]:::n

    start --> ci
    start --> in
    start --> av
    ci --> max
    in --> max
    av --> max
    max --> out

    classDef n fill:#fdebd0,stroke:#a04000,color:#000
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Define the Risk Management Framework (RMF) and list its six core steps.

**Model Answer:**

The **Risk Management Framework (RMF)** is a structured, NIST-defined process (SP 800-37 Rev. 2) that provides a disciplined, continuous approach to managing organizational risk across the system development life cycle.

The six core steps are:

1. **Categorize** the system and its information.
2. **Select** an initial set of baseline security controls.
3. **Implement** the selected controls.
4. **Assess** the controls for correctness and effectiveness.
5. **Authorize** the system to operate.
6. **Monitor** the controls on an ongoing basis.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** Distinguish between **SLE** and **ALE** with formulas.

**Model Answer:**

- **Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)** represents the expected monetary loss from a **single** occurrence of a risk. Formula:
$$SLE = AV \times EF$$

- **Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE)** represents the expected monetary loss **per year** due to a risk. Formula:
$$ALE = SLE \times ARO$$

**Key Difference:** SLE measures loss per incident; ALE multiplies SLE by the expected annual frequency to give a yearly cost of risk.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Style]*

**(a)** Explain in detail the **six steps of the RMF** as defined in NIST SP 800-37 Rev. 2, with one sentence for each step. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A company has an asset worth **₹5,00,000**. The exposure factor is **0.4**, and the ARO is **0.25**. Calculate **SLE**, **ALE**, and the **residual risk** after a control costing **₹40,000** reduces ARO to **0.05** and EF to **0.1**. Determine if the control is justified. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) Six Steps of RMF:**

1. **Categorize** the information system based on the impact level (Low, Moderate, High) for confidentiality, integrity, and availability per FIPS 199.
2. **Select** an initial baseline set of security controls from NIST SP 800-53 and tailor them to the system's needs.
3. **Implement** the selected security controls and document their deployment in the System Security Plan (SSP).
4. **Assess** the controls to verify correct implementation, intended operation, and satisfaction of security requirements; produce a Security Assessment Report (SAR).
5. **Authorize** system operation by a senior official who accepts residual risk and issues an Authority to Operate (ATO).
6. **Monitor** the security controls continuously, updating the SSP, SAR, and risk assessment as the system and threats evolve.

**(b) Quantitative Calculation:**

**Step 1:** Compute the original SLE.

$$
SLE_{\text{before}} = AV \times EF_{\text{before}} = 5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.4
$$

$$
SLE_{\text{before}} = 2{,}00{,}000\ \text{INR}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the original ALE.

$$
ALE_{\text{before}} = SLE_{\text{before}} \times ARO_{\text{before}} = 2{,}00{,}000 \times 0.25
$$

$$
ALE_{\text{before}} = 50{,}000\ \text{INR/year}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the post-control SLE.

$$
SLE_{\text{after}} = AV \times EF_{\text{after}} = 5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.1 = 50{,}000\ \text{INR}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the post-control ALE (Residual Risk).

$$
ALE_{\text{after}} = SLE_{\text{after}} \times ARO_{\text{after}} = 50{,}000 \times 0.05 = 2{,}500\ \text{INR/year}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the ROSI.

$$
\Delta ALE = 50{,}000 - 2{,}500 = 47{,}500
$$

$$
ROSI = \frac{47{,}500 - 40{,}000}{40{,}000} = \frac{7{,}500}{40{,}000} = 0.1875 = 18.75\%
$$

**Conclusion:** The control is **justified** because it yields a positive ROSI of **18.75%**, meaning the savings exceed the control cost.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Describe the **FIPS 199** categorization process. Categorize a payroll processing system with the following impacts: Confidentiality = **HIGH**, Integrity = **HIGH**, Availability = **MODERATE**. Justify the baseline control set chosen. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the four **Risk Treatment Strategies** with a real-world example for each. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) FIPS 199 Categorization:**
