FIPS 199 categorizes information systems by assessing the **potential impact** (LOW, MODERATE, HIGH) on organizational operations, assets, or individuals if confidentiality, integrity, or availability is compromised.

For the **payroll system**:

$$
C_{\text{sys}} = \max(C_I, C_A, C_{A_v}) = \max(H, H, M) = HIGH
$$

**Justification of Baseline:** A **HIGH** impact system requires the **HIGH baseline** of NIST SP 800-53 (~400+ controls), because payroll data contains personally identifiable information (PII) and financial records whose unauthorized disclosure or modification would cause severe harm to employees and the organization.

**Examples of High-baseline controls selected:**

- **AC-2(7):** Account management with privileged user monitoring.
- **AU-12:** Audit record generation for all financial transactions.
- **SC-7(14):** Boundary protection with internal network segmentation.
- **IA-2(5):** Multi-factor authentication for payroll administrators.

**(b) Four Risk Treatment Strategies:**

1. **Risk Avoidance:** Eliminate the risk by removing the activity that causes it. *Example:* An organization discontinues a vulnerable legacy web service rather than patching it.

2. **Risk Mitigation (Reduction):** Implement controls to reduce the likelihood or impact. *Example:* Deploying a Web Application Firewall (WAF) to reduce SQL injection impact.

3. **Risk Transfer (Sharing):** Shift the financial impact to a third party. *Example:* Purchasing cyber insurance to cover breach costs or outsourcing data processing to a cloud provider under a contractual SLA.

4. **Risk Acceptance:** Formally acknowledge and retain the residual risk. *Example:* A small business accepts the risk of rare DDoS attacks of short duration because the cost of mitigation exceeds potential loss.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often write "Categorize" and "Select" steps without referencing the specific standards (**FIPS 199** and **NIST SP 800-53**). Always cite the standard number to secure full marks.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** In quantitative problems, units (₹/year) are frequently omitted. **Always** include units.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting to apply the **maximum** function in FIPS 199 — taking the *average* instead of the *maximum* is a critical error that leads to wrong categorization.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Writing "Risk Transfer" as "Risk Avoidance." These are distinct: transfer **shares** the impact; avoidance **eliminates** the cause.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Confusing **SLE** with **ALE** — remember, SLE is per incident, ALE is per year.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **RMF (NIST SP 800-37 Rev. 2)** = **Prepare + 6 Steps**: Categorize → Select → Implement → Assess → Authorize → Monitor.
- **FIPS 199** uses the **maximum (waterfall) function** of CIA impact levels for system categorization.
- **FIPS 200** mandates minimum security requirements; **SP 800-53** provides the control catalog (~1000+ controls in Rev. 5).
- **SLE** = $AV \times EF$; **ALE** = $SLE \times ARO$.
- **Residual Risk** = Risk that remains after control implementation.
- **ROSI > 0** ⇒ Control is economically justified.
- **Four Risk Treatments:** Avoid, Mitigate, Transfer, Accept.
- **ATO** (Authority to Operate) is the formal authorization decision; alternatives are **DATO** (Denial) and **IATO** (Interim).
- **Continuous Monitoring** is the key differentiator between legacy C&A and modern RMF.
- **MTBF** and **MTTR** are system reliability metrics; higher MTBF and lower MTTR imply higher availability.
- **System Availability** $A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ — always expressed as a percentage.
- **Common exam focus areas:** Numerical problems on SLE/ALE/ROSI, step-wise explanation of RMF, and application of FIPS 199.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Security Best Practices / Known Security Flaws
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAIT418 - SECURITY ASSESSMENT AND RISK ANALYSIS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Security Best Practices / Known Security Flaws # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Security Best Practices — Formal Definition **Security Best Practices** are a curated set of well-established principles, guidelines, controls, and configurations that, when systematically applied, harden an information system against known and emerging threats. They emerge from collective post-incident analysis, vulnerability research, and standards bodies (NIST, ISO/IEC 27001, OWASP, SANS, CIS). In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, security best practices form the **defensive baseline** of the Risk Management Framework (RMF) and the **Secure Configuration** domain of the assessment lifecycle. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (KTU Standard):** *Security best practices are a documented set of preventive, detective, and corrective controls derived from empirical evidence and industry standards, designed to mitigate the probability and impact of known attack vectors.* ## 1.2 Known Security Flaws — Formal Definition A **Known Security Flaw** (vulnerability/weakness) is a publicly disclosed defect in software, hardware, or configuration logic that can be exploited to violate one or more pillars of the **CIA Triad** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability). These flaws are catalogued in databases such as: - **CVE** — Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures (MITRE-maintained unique identifiers) - **CWE** — Common Weakness Enumeration (root-cause classification) - **NVD** — National Vulnerability Database (NIST-maintained, CVSS-scored) ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Bank Vault"** > > Think of your IT system as a **bank vault**: > - **Known Security Flaws** = documented weak points like a paper-thin back wall, a rusted combination dial, or a vent large enough to crawl through. They are *publicly known* because robbers (attackers) share them. > - **Security Best Practices** = the standard operating procedures of a professional bank — install reinforced steel doors, use dual-key locks, rotate combinations monthly, install CCTV, run background checks on employees, perform monthly drills. > > A bank doesn't wait for a heist to learn these lessons; it studies **every known bank robbery** in history and patches its vulnerabilities accordingly. That collective wisdom IS best practice. ## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations | Aspect | KTU Relevance | |---|---| | **CIA Triad** | Every flaw violates one or more pillars — direct CO1/CO2 mapping | | **CVSS Scoring** | Numerical risk quantification — frequently asked in Part A (3 marks) | | **OWASP Top 10** | Web application vulnerabilities — high-yield for Part B (14 marks) | | **Defense in Depth** | Architectural principle — often appears as 7-mark sub-question | | **Patch Management** | Operational security — common short-answer topic | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Constants / Standard Metrics to Memorize:** > - **CVSS v3.1 Base Score Range:** 0.0 – 10.0 > - **OWASP Top 10** is updated roughly every 3–4 years (latest 2021) > - **CIA Triad** pillars: **Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability** # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The CIA Triad — The Foundation of All Security Every security flaw and every best practice is ultimately measured against the three pillars: $$\text{Security Posture} = f(\text{Confidentiality}, \text{Integrity}, \text{Availability})$$ - **Confidentiality (C):** Only authorized users can read sensitive data. *Flaws: data leakage, eavesdropping, weak encryption.* - **Integrity (I):** Data is not tampered with, altered, or destroyed by unauthorized actors. *Flaws: man-in-the-middle, SQL injection (data modification).* - **Availability (A):** Systems and data are accessible when needed. *Flaws: DoS/DDoS, ransomware, hardware failure.* ## 2.2 The AAA Framework (Authentication, Authorization, Accounting) | Pillar | Definition | Common Flaw | |---|---|---| | **Authentication** | *Who are you?* (Identity verification) | Weak passwords, credential stuffing | | **Authorization** | *What can you do?* (Permission grants) | Privilege escalation, IDOR | | **Accounting** | *What did you do?* (Audit logging) | Insufficient logging, log tampering | ## 2.3 OWASP Top 10 (2021 Edition) — The Industry Bible The **Open Web Application Security Project (OWASP)** Top 10 is the most widely adopted awareness document representing the most critical web application security risks. The 2021 edition comprises: 1. **A01:2021 – Broken Access Control** 2. **A02:2021 – Cryptographic Failures** 3. **A03:2021 – Injection** (SQL, NoSQL, OS command, LDAP) 4. **A04:2021 – Insecure Design** 5. **A05:2021 – Security Misconfiguration** 6. **A06:2021 – Vulnerable and Outdated Components** 7. **A07:2021 – Identification and Authentication Failures** 8. **A08:2021 – Software and Data Integrity Failures** 9. **A09:2021 – Security Logging and Monitoring Failures** 10. **A10:2021 – Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)** > [!NOTE] > **KTU Exam Tip:** Memorize at least the **Top 5** with one-line descriptions and one mitigation each. Examiner's love asking "Explain any 5 OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities." ## 2.4 SANS Top 25 Most Dangerous Software Errors The **SANS Institute**, in coordination with MITRE CWE, publishes the Top 25 most dangerous software errors. These are categorized into: - **Insecure Interaction Between Components** (9 entries) — e.g., SQL injection, XSS, CSRF - **Risky Resource Management** (9 entries) — e.g., buffer overflow, path traversal - **Porous Defenses** (7 entries) — e.g., missing authentication, hard-coded credentials ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL KTU Formulas — Memorize This Table** | Concept | Formula / Standard | Purpose / Application | |---|---|---| | **CVSS Base Score Severity** | $S_{cvss} \in [0.0, 10.0]$ | Quantify exploit severity | | **CVSS Severity Rating** | $0.0 \leq S < 4.0$ → **Low** | Risk classification | | | $4.0 \leq S < 7.0$ → **Medium** | | | | $7.0 \leq S < 9.0$ → **High** | | | | $9.0 \leq S \leq 10.0$ → **Critical** | | | **Risk Calculation** | $\text{Risk} = \text{Threat} \times \text{Vulnerability} \times \text{Impact}$ | NIST SP 800-30 standard | | **Annual Loss Expectancy** | $\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$ | Quantitative risk analysis | | **Single Loss Expectancy** | $\text{SLE} = \text{Asset Value} \times \text{EF}$ | Per-incident loss | | **Exposure Factor** | $\text{EF} \in [0, 1]$ (0% to 100%) | Percentage of asset lost | | **Annualized Rate of Occurrence** | $\text{ARO} \in \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ | Frequency per year | | **Defense in Depth Layers** | $n \geq 3$ independent controls | Redundancy principle | | **MFA Entropy** | $H = \log_2(\prod_{i=1}^{n} C_i)$ bits | Combined factor strength | | **Password Entropy** | $H = L \times \log_2(C)$ bits | $L$=length, $C$=charset size | | **Annualized ROI (Security)** | $\text{ROSI} = \frac{\text{ALE}_{before} - \text{ALE}_{after} - \text{ACS}}{\text{ACS}}$ | Investment justification | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility These formulas and standards are used by: - **Penetration Testers** — to score and prioritize findings - **Security Architects** — to justify budget and controls - **GRC Teams** — for ISO 27001 / SOC 2 compliance audits - **DevSecOps Engineers** — for continuous vulnerability management ## 2.7 Principles of Security Best Practices (The "Golden Rules") > [!IMPORTANT] > **The 10 Immutable Principles (Frequently Asked in KTU):** 1. **Least Privilege** — Grant the *minimum* permissions necessary. 2. **Defense in Depth** — Multiple overlapping layers of controls. 3. **Fail Securely / Fail Safe** — On failure, deny access (not grant it). 4. **Separation of Duties** — No single person controls a critical process alone. 5. **Complete Mediation** — Every access is checked every time (no cached authorizations). 6. **Open Design** — Security should not depend on secrecy of mechanism (Kerckhoffs's principle). 7. **Psychological Acceptability** — Security mechanisms must be user-friendly. 8. **Economy of Mechanism** — Keep security simple; complexity is the enemy of security. 9. **Weakest Link** — A chain is only as strong as its weakest component. 10. **Zero Trust** — Never trust, always verify (NIST SP 800-207, modern standard). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Detailed Walkthrough — CVSS Risk Calculation The **Common Vulnerability Scoring System (CVSS)** v3.1 produces a Base Score from three metric groups: **Impact**, **Exploitability**, and **Scope**. The KTU exam frequently asks students to interpret CVSS, so understanding its derivation is essential. ### 3.1.1 Impact Sub-Score (ISC) — Step-by-Step Derivation $$\text{ISC}_{Base} = 1 - [(1 - I_C) \times (1 - I_I) \times (1 - I_A)]$$ Where $I_C, I_I, I_A \in \{0.0, 0.22, 0.56\}$ represent the impact on Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability respectively. > **Step 1:** Define the impact values > - **None** = $0.0$ > - **Low** = $0.22$ > - **High** = $0.56$ > **Step 2:** Compute the multiplicative complement > For each CIA component, compute $(1 - I_x)$, which represents the *unaffected* fraction. > **Step 3:** Multiply all three complements together > The product gives the probability that **no** significant impact occurs. > **Step 4:** Subtract from 1 > The result is the probability that **at least one** CIA component is significantly affected. ### 3.1.2 Worked Numerical Example **Scenario:** A vulnerability allows an attacker to read (Confidentiality=High) and modify (Integrity=High) a database, but not disrupt service (Availability=None). > **Step 1:** Assign values > $$I_C = 0.56, \quad I_I = 0.56, \quad I_A = 0.00$$ > **Step 2:** Compute complements > $$1 - I_C = 1 - 0.56 = 0.44$$ > $$1 - I_I = 1 - 0.56 = 0.44$$ > $$1 - I_A = 1 - 0.00 = 1.00$$ > **Step 3:** Multiply > $$0.44 \times 0.44 \times 1.00 = 0.1936$$ > **Step 4:** Subtract from 1 > $$\text{ISC}_{Base} = 1 - 0.1936 = 0.8064$$ ### 3.1.3 Full CVSS Base Score Formula For **Scope = Unchanged (U)**: $$\text{BaseScore} = \text{roundup}\left(\min\left[\text{Impact} + \text{Exploitability}, 10\right] \times \text{BaseScoreMultiplier}\right)$$ Where: $$\text{Impact} = \begin{cases} 6.42 \times \text{ISC}_{Base} & \text{if Scope} = U \\ 7.52 \times (\text{ISC}_{Base} - 0.029) - 3.25 \times (\text{ISC}_{Base} - 0.02)^{15} & \text{if Scope} = C \end{cases}$$ ## 3.2 Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE) — Full Derivation The ALE is a cornerstone of **quantitative risk analysis** and a guaranteed KTU Part B question. ### 3.2.1 Derivation Chain $$\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$$ > **Step 1:** Define Asset Value (AV) > The monetary value of the asset in Rupees (₹). Example: A customer database valued at **₹50,00,000**. > **Step 2:** Determine Exposure Factor (EF) > The percentage of asset value lost in a single incident. For a database breach, EF might be **60%** = 0.60. > **Step 3:** Compute Single Loss Expectancy (SLE) > $$\text{SLE} = \text{AV} \times \text{EF} = 50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.60 = 30{,}00{,}000$$ > **Step 4:** Determine Annualized Rate of Occurrence (ARO) > The expected number of incidents per year. Example: **0.5** (once every 2 years). > **Step 5:** Compute ALE > $$\text{ALE} = 30{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5 = 15{,}00{,}000 \text{ per year}$$ ### 3.2.2 Worked Example — Cost-Benefit of a Firewall > **Given:** > - Asset: E-commerce website = **₹10,00,000** > - EF of DDoS attack = **0.80** > - ARO without firewall = **3** per year > - Firewall cost (ACS) = **₹5,00,000/year** > - ARO with firewall = **0.1** per year > **Step 1:** Compute ALE *before* firewall > $$\text{SLE}_{before} = 10{,}00{,}000 \times 0.80 = 8{,}00{,}000$$ > $$\text{ALE}_{before} = 8{,}00{,}000 \times 3 = 24{,}00{,}000$$ > **Step 2:** Compute ALE *after* firewall > $$\text{ALE}_{after} = 8{,}00{,}000 \times 0.1 = 80{,}000$$ > **Step 3:** Compute Annualized ROSI > $$\text{ROSI} = \frac{24{,}00{,}000 - 80{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} = \frac{18{,}20{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} = 3.64$$ > **Interpretation:** A ROSI of **3.64** means every ₹1 spent on the firewall saves ₹3.64 in expected losses — **strong positive ROI**, control is justified. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Flaws vs. CIA Pillar Violated | Vulnerability | C | I | A | OWASP 2021 Rank | Best Practice Mitigation | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | SQL Injection | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | A03 | Parameterized queries, input validation | | XSS (Cross-Site Scripting) | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | A03 | Output encoding, CSP headers | | CSRF | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ | (WASC) | Anti-CSRF tokens, SameSite cookies | | Broken Access Control | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | A01 | RBAC, deny-by-default | | Cryptographic Failures | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | A02 | TLS 1.3, AES-256, bcrypt | | Security Misconfig | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | A05 | Hardened baselines, automated scans | | Buffer Overflow | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | CWE-120 | ASLR, DEP, safe languages | | DoS / DDoS | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | (CWE-400) | Rate limiting, CDNs, WAF | | Hard-coded Credentials | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | CWE-798 | Vault systems, env variables | | SSRF | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | A10 | Allowlist URLs, network segmentation | > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Matrix:** A ✓ in column C means the flaw can violate Confidentiality, and so on. This matrix is a high-yield visual aid for KTU 14-mark questions. ## 3.4 Password Entropy — Step-by-Step Password strength is measured in **bits of entropy**. Higher entropy = exponentially harder to brute-force. > **Formula:** > $$H = L \times \log_2(C)$$ Where $L$ = password length, $C$ = size of character set. > **Step 1:** Identify character set > Lowercase + uppercase + digits + symbols → $C = 26 + 26 + 10 + 32 = 94$ > **Step 2:** Set length > For an 8-character password: $L = 8$ > **Step 3:** Compute entropy > $$H = 8 \times \log_2(94) = 8 \times 6.55 = 52.4 \text{ bits}$$ > **Step 4:** Interpret > 52 bits of entropy is **moderately strong** but vulnerable to offline GPU brute-force. NIST SP 800-63B recommends **≥ 64 bits** for human-chosen passwords. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Defense in Depth — Layered Security Architecture The **Defense in Depth** model is a foundational KTU concept. The diagram below shows the seven canonical layers, from outermost (perimeter) to innermost (data). ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA["Layer 1: Physical Security\nLocks, CCTV, Biometric Access"] nodeB["Layer 2: Perimeter Security\nFirewalls, DMZ, IDS/IPS"] nodeC["Layer 3: Network Security\nVLANs, NAC, TLS, VPN"] nodeD["Layer 4: Host Security\nAntivirus, HIDS, Patch Management"] nodeE["Layer 5: Application Security\nSAST, DAST, WAF, Secure SDLC"] nodeF["Layer 6: Data Security\nEncryption at rest, Tokenization, DLP"] nodeG["Layer 7: Policies and Procedures\nAwareness, IRP, BCP, Compliance"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeG style nodeA fill:#1f2937,stroke:#fbbf24,color:#ffffff style nodeB fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#60a5fa,color:#ffffff style nodeC fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#60a5fa,color:#ffffff style nodeD fill:#312e81,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#ffffff style nodeE fill:#312e81,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#ffffff style nodeF fill:#581c87,stroke:#f0abfc,color:#ffffff style nodeG fill:#831843,stroke:#fda4af,color:#ffffff ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** An attacker must defeat *all seven* layers in sequence. Even if one control fails (e.g., firewall bypassed), the next layer (e.g., host-based IDS) provides another chance to detect and block the intrusion. This is the essence of **redundancy** in security. ## 4.2 Vulnerability Management Lifecycle This flowchart depicts the standard, cyclical process for managing known security flaws — a frequent KTU Part B question. ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["Step 1: Identify\nVulnerability Scans, Pen Tests, CVE Feeds"] nodeB["Step 2: Analyze\nCVSS Scoring, Contextual Risk Analysis"] nodeC["Step 3: Prioritize\nPatch Critical, Schedule Others"] nodeD["Step 4: Remediate\nApply Patch, Config Change, Compensating Control"] nodeE["Step 5: Verify\nRe-scan, Validate Fix, Regression Test"] nodeF["Step 6: Report\nMetrics, KPIs, Lessons Learned"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeF nodeF -.Feedback Loop.-> nodeA style nodeA fill:#0c4a6e,stroke:#38bdf8,color:#ffffff style nodeB fill:#0c4a6e,stroke:#38bdf8,color:#ffffff style nodeC fill:#713f12,stroke:#fbbf24,color:#ffffff style nodeD fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#fca5a5,color:#ffffff style nodeE fill:#14532d,stroke:#86efac,color:#ffffff style nodeF fill:#4c1d95,stroke:#c4b5fd,color:#ffffff ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** The dashed feedback loop from "Report" back to "Identify" represents **continuous improvement**. Vulnerability management is NOT a one-time project — it is a perpetual cycle aligned with the **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** model of ISO 27001. ## 4.3 SQL Injection Attack Flow A detailed step-by-step visualization of how an SQL injection flaw is exploited, mapping the attacker's path through the system's defenses. ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant attacker as Attacker participant browser as Web Browser participant webapp as Web Application participant db as Database Server attacker->>browser: Craft malicious input
e.g. 1 OR 1=1 browser->>webapp: HTTP GET request
with unsanitized input Note over webapp: Vulnerable Code:
SELECT FROM users WHERE id = input webapp->>db: Executes tainted SQL query db-->>webapp: Returns ALL user records webapp-->>browser: Renders sensitive data browser-->>attacker: Data exfiltrated Note over attacker,db: CIA Triad Violated:
Confidentiality (data leak)
Integrity (data modification) ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mitigation Mapping:** The fix is to use **parameterized queries (prepared statements)**, which separate SQL logic from user data, making injection structurally impossible. ## 4.4 Zero Trust Architecture (NIST SP 800-207) The modern evolution of the perimeter model — explicitly required for KTU 2024 scheme awareness. ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA["User Device\nUntrusted by Default"] nodeB["Identity Provider\nMFA, SSO Verification"] nodeC["Policy Engine\nPEP and PDP combined"] nodeD["Resource Server\nMicrosegmented"] nodeE["Continuous Monitoring\nSIEM, UEBA, Telemetry"] nodeA -->|Authenticate| nodeB nodeB -->|Token + Context| nodeC nodeC -->|Authorize per request| nodeD nodeE -->|Real-time signals| nodeC nodeD -->|Logs and metrics| nodeE style nodeA fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#fb923c,color:#ffffff style nodeB fill:#1e40af,stroke:#93c5fd,color:#ffffff style nodeC fill:#581c87,stroke:#d8b4fe,color:#ffffff style nodeD fill:#064e3b,stroke:#6ee7b7,color:#ffffff style nodeE fill:#0f766e,stroke:#5eead4,color:#ffffff ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Zero Trust Core Tenet:** *"Never trust, always verify."* Every request is authenticated, authorized, and encrypted — regardless of whether it originates from inside or outside the network perimeter. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pattern:** Part A questions are direct, definition-based, and test rote understanding. Answers should be **3–5 lines maximum** with one example. ### Question 1: Define the CIA Triad. [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** **Model Answer:** The **CIA Triad** is the foundational model of information security comprising three pillars: - **Confidentiality:** Ensuring that sensitive data is accessible only to authorized users (e.g., encryption enforces confidentiality). - **Integrity:** Guaranteeing that data is not altered or tampered with during storage or transmission (e.g., hash functions like SHA-256 ensure integrity). - **Availability:** Ensuring that systems and data are accessible to authorized users when needed (e.g., redundant servers and DDoS protection ensure availability). Every security flaw and best practice is evaluated against these three pillars. **(3 marks)** --- ### Question 2: List any three OWASP Top 10 (2021) vulnerabilities with one-line descriptions. [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** **Model Answer:** 1. **A01:2021 – Broken Access Control:** Users can act outside their intended permissions (e.g., accessing another user's data via URL manipulation). 2. **A02:2021 – Cryptographic Failures:** Weak or missing encryption exposes sensitive data (e.g., storing passwords in plain text or using deprecated MD5). 3. **A03:2021 – Injection:** Untrusted input is sent to an interpreter as part of a command or query (e.g., SQL Injection allows data theft or destruction). **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pattern:** Part B questions carry 14 marks and present an **internal choice**. Each question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Provide exhaustive, structured answers with diagrams where relevant. --- ### QUESTION A — 14 Marks (Choice 1) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** **(a) Explain the concept of "Defense in Depth" with a layered architecture diagram. Discuss any four layers in detail. [7 Marks]** **Model Answer:** **Definition (2 marks):** **Defense in Depth (DiD)** is a security strategy that employs multiple overlapping layers of controls, so that the failure of a single control does not result in a complete security breach. It follows the principle of redundancy inspired by medieval castle design (moat + walls + archers + vault). **Layered Architecture (3 marks):** The seven canonical layers are: | Layer | Control Type | Example | |---|---|---| | 1. Physical | Locks, CCTV, biometrics | Mantrap entry | | 2. Perimeter | Firewall, IDS/IPS | Cisco ASA | | 3. Network | VPN, VLAN, TLS | IPsec tunnel | | 4. Host | Antivirus, HIDS, patching | Windows Defender | | 5. Application | WAF, SAST/DAST | OWASP ZAP scan | | 6. Data | Encryption, DLP, tokenization | AES-256 at rest | | 7. Policy | Awareness, IRP, BCP | Annual training | **Detailed Discussion of Four Layers (2 marks):** - **Perimeter Layer:** First line of defense; firewalls filter traffic based on rules, IDS detects signatures, IPS actively blocks. - **Network Layer:** VPN encrypts traffic in transit; VLANs segment broadcast domains; NAC verifies device posture. - **Application Layer:** WAF (Web Application Firewall) blocks SQLi/XSS; SAST/DAST tools find code-level bugs. - **Data Layer:** AES-256 encryption at rest; DLP prevents sensitive data exfiltration; tokenization replaces PII with non-sensitive tokens. > [!NOTE] > **Examiner Expectation:** Include a labeled diagram of the layered model. The 7 marks are split: 2 for definition, 3 for diagram, 2 for detailed layer explanation. --- **(b) Describe the Common Vulnerability Scoring System (CVSS) v3.1. Compute the CVSS Base Score for a vulnerability with the following metrics: AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:N. [7 Marks]** **Model Answer:** **CVSS Overview (3 marks):** CVSS v3.1 is an open framework maintained by **FIRST.org** for communicating the characteristics and severity of software vulnerabilities. It produces a Base Score in the range **0.0 – 10.0**, classified as: - **Low:** 0.1 – 3.9 - **Medium:** 4.0 – 6.9 - **High:** 7.0 – 8.9 - **Critical:** 9.0 – 10.0 The Base Score is computed from three metric groups: **Exploitability**, **Impact**, and **Scope (S)**. **Metric Interpretation (1 mark):** - **AV:N** (Attack Vector: Network) → Score = 0.85 - **AC:L** (Attack Complexity: Low) → Score = 0.77 - **PR:N** (Privileges Required: None) → Score = 0.85 - **UI:N** (User Interaction: None) → Score = 0.85 - **S:U** (Scope: Unchanged) → No multiplier applied - **C:H** (Confidentiality: High) → 0.56 - **I:H** (Integrity: High) → 0.56 - **A:N** (Availability: None) → 0.00 **Calculation (3 marks):** > **Step 1: Compute Impact Sub-Score (ISC)** > $$\text{ISC} = 1 - [(1-0.56) \times (1-0.56) \times (1-0.00)]$$ > $$\text{ISC} = 1 - [0.44 \times 0.44 \times 1.00]$$ > $$\text{ISC} = 1 - 0.1936 = 0.8064$$ > **Step 2: Compute Impact (since Scope = U)** > $$\text{Impact} = 6.42 \times \text{ISC} = 6.42 \times 0.8064 = 5.177$$ > **Step 3: Compute Exploitability** > $$\text{Exploitability} = 8.22 \times AV \times AC \times PR \times UI$$ > $$\text{Exploitability} = 8.22 \times 0.85 \times 0.77 \times 0.85 \times 0.85$$ > $$\text{Exploitability} = 8.22 \times 0.4735 = 3.892$$ > **Step 4: Compute Base Score (Scope = U)** > $$\text{Base} = \text{Impact} + \text{Exploitability} = 5.177 + 3.892 = 9.069$$ > $$\text{Final Base Score} = \text{roundup}(9.069) = \mathbf{9.1}$$ **Severity:** **CRITICAL** (since 9.1 ≥ 9.0). **(7 marks complete)** --- ### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Choice 2) **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** **(a) What are the OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities? Explain any five with example attacks and mitigations. [7 Marks]** **Model Answer:** **Introduction (1 mark):** The **OWASP Top 10** is a standard awareness document published by the Open Web Application Security Project representing the most critical web application security risks. The current 2021 edition consolidates data from hundreds of organizations and tens of thousands of vulnerabilities. **Five Key Vulnerabilities (6 marks, 1.2 each):** **1. A01 — Broken Access Control** - *Issue:* Users can act outside their intended permissions. - *Example:* Changing `?id=100` to `?id=101` in the URL to view another user's private data. - *Mitigation:* Implement Role-Based Access Control (RBAC), deny access by default, log access failures. **2. A02 — Cryptographic Failures** - *Issue:* Weak, missing, or improperly applied encryption. - *Example:* Storing passwords using MD5 (collision-vulnerable) instead of bcrypt. - *Mitigation:* Use TLS 1.3 in transit, AES-256 at rest, bcrypt/Argon2 for passwords. **3. A03 — Injection (SQLi)** - *Issue:* Untrusted input is concatenated into a command/query. - *Example:* Input `' OR '1'='1' --` in a login field bypasses authentication. - *Mitigation:* Use parameterized queries, ORMs, and input validation (allowlists). **4. A05 — Security Misconfiguration** - *Issue:* Default credentials, exposed debug ports, unnecessary features enabled. - *Example:* Admin panel left with default admin/admin credentials. - *Mitigation:* Automated hardening scripts, CIS Benchmarks, periodic configuration audits. **5. A07 — Identification and Authentication Failures** - *Issue:* Weak password policies, missing MFA, credential stuffing. - *Example:* Login accepts 6-character passwords and no MFA. - *Mitigation:* Enforce NIST SP 800-63B password standards, mandate MFA, rate-limit login attempts. **(7 marks complete)** --- **(b) Discuss the security best practices for password management and multi-factor authentication. Compute the entropy of a 12-character password using uppercase, lowercase, digits, and symbols. [7 Marks]** **Model Answer:** **Password Management Best Practices (3 marks):** > 1. **Length over Complexity:** NIST SP 800-63B recommends a **minimum 8 characters** (preferably 12+), deprecating forced periodic rotation. > 2. **Use Passphrases:** Four random unrelated words (e.g., `correct-horse-battery-staple`) are stronger and memorable. > 3. **Password Managers:** Encourage use of tools like Bitwarden, 1Password, or KeePass to store unique passwords per site. > 4. **Hash Storage:** Never store plain text. Use **bcrypt**, **scrypt**, or **Argon2id** with appropriate salt and work factor. > 5. **No Default Credentials:** Force password change on first login. > 6. **Breach Monitoring:** Check passwords against known breach corpora (e.g., Have I Been Pwned). **Multi-Factor Authentication (2 marks):** MFA combines ≥2 of the following **independent** factors: - **Something you know** (password, PIN) - **Something you have** (hardware token, smartphone OTP) - **Something you are** (fingerprint, face, iris) - **Somewhere you are** (geo-location) - **Something you do** (behavioral biometrics) MFA blocks **99.9%** of automated account-takeover attacks per Microsoft research. Recommended: **TOTP apps (Google Authenticator)** or **FIDO2/WebAuthn hardware keys** (YubiKey). **Entropy Calculation (2 marks):** > **Given:** > - Length $L = 12$ > - Character set $C = 26 \text{ (lowercase)} + 26 \text{ (uppercase)} + 10 \text{ (digits)} + 32 \text{ (symbols)} = 94$ > **Step 1:** Apply entropy formula > $$H = L \times \log_2(C)$$ > **Step 2:** Substitute values > $$H = 12 \times \log_2(94)$$ > **Step 3:** Evaluate > $$H = 12 \times 6.5546 = \mathbf{78.66 \text{ bits}}$$ > **Step 4:** Interpret > 78.66 bits is **strong** — would require approximately $2^{78.66} \approx 4.4 \times 10^{23}$ brute-force attempts, taking centuries on modern hardware. **(7 marks complete)** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ WHERE STUDENTS LOSE MARKS — READ CAREFULLY** > > **1. Confusing CIA with AAA (1–2 mark loss):** CIA = data properties. AAA = access control properties. Examiners deduct for interchange. > > **2. Not Memorizing CVSS Thresholds (2 mark loss):** You must know the Low/Medium/High/Critical boundaries (0.1–3.9, 4.0–6.9, 7.0–8.9, 9.0–10.0). > > **3. OWASP Version Mismatch (1–2 mark loss):** The current list is **OWASP 2021**, NOT 2017. Listing the old "Injection" as #1 loses marks. > > **4. Skipping Diagrams in 14-mark answers (2–3 mark loss):** Defense in Depth, Vulnerability Lifecycle, and Zero Trust *must* be supported with a labeled diagram. A textual description alone is insufficient. > > **5. Not Showing Calculation Steps in ALE/CVSS (2 mark loss):** Examiners award partial marks for *each* correct step. Writing only the final answer yields 0. > > **6. Confusing CWE with CVE (1 mark loss):** CWE = weakness type (a category). CVE = specific instance (a real bug in a product). > > **7. Forgetting Units in SLE/ALE:** Always write "₹" or "Rupees" — naked numbers lose 0.5 mark for unit omission. > > **8. Writing "Confidentiality = 1" instead of "0.56"** in CVSS Impact calculation. Memorize the discrete CVSS values exactly. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **🔥 HIGH-YIELD RAPID REVISION — Read This the Night Before the Exam** - [ ] **CIA Triad** = Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability. EVERY flaw maps to ≥1. - [ ] **AAA** = Authentication, Authorization, Accounting. Focuses on *user actions*. - [ ] **OWASP Top 10 (2021):** Memorize A01–A05 minimum. A01 = Broken Access, A02 = Crypto, A03 = Injection, A04 = Insecure Design, A05 = Misconfig. - [ ] **SANS Top 25** groups errors into Insecure Interaction, Risky Resource Management, Porous Defenses. - [ ] **CVE** = unique ID for a *specific* vulnerability. **CWE** = category/weakness type. **NVD** = enriched CVE database with CVSS. - [ ] **CVSS v3.1 Score Range:** 0.0–10.0. Thresholds: Low < 4.0, Medium < 7.0, High < 9.0, Critical ≥ 9.0. - [ ] **CVSS Discrete Values:** Impact CIA ∈ {0.00, 0.22, 0.56}. Exploitability multipliers: AV ∈ {0.20, 0.55, 0.62, 0.85}, etc. - [ ] **ALE Formula:** $\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO} = (\text{AV} \times \text{EF}) \times \text{ARO}$. - [ ] **ROSI Formula:** $(\text{ALE}_{before} - \text{ALE}_{after} - \text{ACS}) / \text{ACS}$. Positive ROSI = justified investment. - [ ] **Password Entropy:** $H = L \times \log_2(C)$. NIST recommends ≥ 64 bits. - [ ] **10 Golden Principles:** Least Privilege, Defense in Depth, Fail Securely, Separation of Duties, Complete Mediation, Open Design (Kerckhoffs), Psychological Acceptability, Economy of Mechanism, Weakest Link, Zero Trust. - [ ] **Zero Trust Tenet:** "Never trust, always verify." NIST SP 800-207. - [ ] **Defense in Depth** = 7 layers: Physical, Perimeter, Network, Host, Application, Data, Policy. - [ ] **Vulnerability Management Lifecycle** = Identify → Analyze → Prioritize → Remediate → Verify → Report → (loop). - [ ] **Common Vulnerabilities Quick-Fire:** - SQL Injection → parameterized queries - XSS → output encoding + CSP - CSRF → anti-CSRF tokens - Buffer Overflow → ASLR, DEP, Rust/Go - DoS/DDoS → CDN, rate limiting - Hardcoded creds → Vault - SSRF → URL allowlist - [ ] **Patch Management** is a top-3 cause of breaches (Equifax 2017 = unpatched Apache Struts). - [ ] **Hardening Standards:** CIS Benchmarks, DISA STIGs, NIST SP 800-53. - [ ] **Encryption Standards:** TLS 1.3 in transit, AES-256 at rest, bcrypt/Argon2 for passwords. **Never** use MD5 or SHA-1 for passwords. - [ ] **MFA Factors:** Know, Have, Are, Where, Do. Two independent factors required. - [ ] **Frameworks to Know:** NIST RMF, ISO 27001, OWASP, CIS Critical Security Controls, PCI-DSS.

topics.title: Architectural Risk Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT418 - SECURITY ASSESSMENT AND RISK ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Architectural Risk Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Architectural Risk Analysis (ARA)** is a systematic, structured methodology used in information security engineering to identify, classify, and prioritize security risks that are fundamentally embedded within the **design, structure, and interconnections** of a software or system architecture — *before* the system is built or deployed at scale. Unlike code-level vulnerability scanning, ARA operates at the **blueprint level**, analyzing how components, data flows, trust boundaries, and dependencies interact to produce latent security weaknesses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Architectural Risk Analysis is a cornerstone of the *Design-Level Security Assessment* domain. It is the bridge between high-level threat modeling and concrete code-level penetration testing. The KTU Board Examiner expects students to clearly distinguish ARA from operational risk analysis (which deals with deployed systems and human/physical processes).

**Standard Industry Terminology (aligned with NIST SP 800-160 and OWASP SAMM):**

- **Asset:** Any resource of value (data, service, hardware) within the architecture.
- **Trust Boundary:** A logical or physical perimeter across which the level of trust changes.
- **Attack Surface:** The sum of all points (entry points, interfaces, protocols) where an unauthorized user can attempt to enter or extract data.
- **Threat Agent:** An entity (human, software, environmental) capable of initiating a threat.
- **Residual Risk:** The risk remaining *after* a mitigation control has been applied.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Building Inspector

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition: "Architectural Risk Analysis is the security equivalent of a structural engineer's blueprint review before a skyscraper is built."**

Imagine a civil engineer reviewing the architectural drawings of a 50-story high-rise *before* a single brick is laid. The engineer does not pound on walls to find cracks — those walls do not exist yet. Instead, the engineer traces load paths, studies wind resistance diagrams, identifies column-beam junctions that concentrate stress, and asks: *"If an earthquake hits, which joint fails first?"*

**Architectural Risk Analysis is exactly this, but for software systems:**

| Civil Blueprint Review | Architectural Risk Analysis |
|---|---|
| Examines structural diagrams | Examines DFDs, UML, deployment diagrams |
| Identifies load-bearing weak points | Identifies single points of failure / trust violations |
| Asks: "Where will the building crack?" | Asks: "Where will the attacker break in?" |
| Uses physics (stress, torque) | Uses threat models (STRIDE, PASTA) |
| Output: Revised design blueprint | Output: Mitigated architecture + residual risk register |

The key insight is that **fixing a flaw in a blueprint costs ₹1 lakh; fixing the same flaw after construction costs ₹1 crore.** This is the *economic justification* for ARA in the KTU syllabus.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in ARA

The following standard risk metrics are explicitly evaluated by KTU examiners:

- **Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE)** measured in monetary units (₹ or $)
- **Likelihood of Exploitation (LoE)** expressed as a normalized value in the range **[0, 1]**
- **Risk Score** dimensionless, on a scale of **0 to 10**
- **MITRE ATT&CK Tactic ID** for categorizing threat behavior

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Tip:** Whenever you quote a numeric risk value in a KTU answer, always state its **unit** explicitly. Writing "Risk = 6" loses marks; writing "Risk Score = 6 / 10" or "ALE = ₹4,50,000 / year" earns full credit.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six-Phase Architectural Risk Analysis Process

ARA is not a single activity — it is a **closed-loop engineering process** consisting of six distinct phases. KTU frequently asks for these phases in 7-mark and 14-mark questions.

**Phase 1 — Architectural Decomposition**

- Decompose the system into its constituent components, services, data stores, and external entities.
- Use **Data Flow Diagrams (DFD)**, **UML Component Diagrams**, or **deployment diagrams** as the canonical representation.
- Output: A documented architecture map with all entry points, exits, and inter-process calls.

**Phase 2 — Asset Identification & Valuation**

- Enumerate every asset the system protects: PII, financial data, intellectual property, availability, reputation.
- Assign each asset a **Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability (CIA) weight** based on business impact.
- This is the foundation for *impact* calculation downstream.

**Phase 3 — Threat Modeling & Classification**

- Identify realistic threat agents and apply a structured classification taxonomy:
  - **STRIDE** (Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, Denial of Service, Elevation of Privilege)
  - **PASTA** (Process for Attack Simulation and Threat Analysis)
  - **LINDDUN** (privacy-focused)
- Map each threat to a specific architectural element.

**Phase 4 — Vulnerability & Attack Surface Mapping**

- For each identified threat, locate the corresponding architectural weakness.
- Enumerate the **Attack Surface**:
  - Open network ports and APIs
  - Input validation points
  - Authentication/authorization enforcement points
  - Cryptographic boundaries
  - Third-party dependency interfaces

**Phase 5 — Risk Calculation & Prioritization**

- Compute a quantitative or qualitative risk score for each architectural weakness.
- Rank weaknesses in a **Risk Register** by descending risk.
- Apply **Pareto prioritization**: focus on the top 20% of risks that produce 80% of total exposure.

**Phase 6 — Mitigation, Documentation & Residual Risk Acceptance**

- Recommend architectural changes: defense-in-depth, zero-trust segmentation, encryption, MFA.
- Document the **mitigated architecture** and recalculate residual risk.
- Obtain formal **sign-off from stakeholders** for accepted residual risks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the Loop Closes:** Phase 6 feeds back into Phase 1, because architectural changes (e.g., adding a new microservice) introduce *new* assets, *new* trust boundaries, and *new* attack surfaces. ARA is therefore a **continuous, iterative discipline**, not a one-time audit.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table is the **canonical reference** for every numerical risk-calculation question you may encounter in the KTU 2024 End Semester Examination (ESE).

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Domain | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)** | $SLE = Asset\ Value \times Exposure\ Factor$ | ₹ (monetary) | Value of one successful attack |
| 2 | **Annualized Rate of Occurrence (ARO)** | $ARO = \dfrac{Expected\ Attacks}{Year}$ | events / year | Frequency of threat realization |
| 3 | **Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE)** | $ALE = SLE \times ARO$ | ₹ / year | Total expected annual loss |
| 4 | **Quantitative Risk Score** | $R_{quant} = Probability \times Impact \times Asset\ Value$ | ₹ / year | When all three values are numeric |
| 5 | **DREAD Score (qualitative)** | $R_{DREAD} = \dfrac{D + R + E + A + D}{5}$ | dimensionless, [0,10] | When subjective expert judgment is used |
| 6 | **Composite Risk (NIST-aligned)** | $R = Likelihood \times Impact$ | dimensionless, [0,25] | NIST SP 800-30 style 5×5 matrix |
| 7 | **Residual Risk** | $R_{residual} = R_{inherent} - R_{mitigated}$ | dimensionless | After applying controls |
| 8 | **Risk Reduction Percentage** | $RR\% = \dfrac{R_{inherent} - R_{residual}}{R_{inherent}} \times 100$ | percent (%) | Efficiency of a control |
| 9 | **Attack Surface Reduction Ratio** | $ASRR = 1 - \dfrac{AS_{after}}{AS_{before}}$ | dimensionless, [0,1] | Measuring architectural hardening |
| 10 | **Total Risk Exposure** | $TRE = \sum_{i=1}^{n} ALE_i$ | ₹ / year | Portfolio-level view of architectural risk |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Notation Convention:** The KTU Board Examiner uses the symbol $\mid x \mid$ to denote absolute value or cardinality. In your answer scripts, always write it as $\lvert x \vert$ to avoid the table-rendering bug present in most markdown engines.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Architectural Risk Analysis is not merely an academic exercise. It is the **bedrock of every certified secure software development lifecycle** in industry:

- **Automotive (ISO/SAE 21434):** ARA is mandatory for ADAS and autonomous driving ECUs before code is written.
- **Medical Devices (FDA Pre-market Cybersecurity Guidance):** ARA is required for FDA submission.
- **Banking (RBI Cyber Security Framework):** Critical for UPI, NEFT, and core banking system architecture approvals.
- **Aviation (DO-326A / ED-202A):** Airworthiness security certification begins with ARA.
- **Cloud-native (CIS Benchmarks, CSA STAR):** Microservices-based architectures require continuous ARA.

> [!TIP]
> **For 14-mark KTU answers, always anchor your final paragraph to a real industry framework (NIST, OWASP, ISO 27001). Examiners award 1–2 'application marks' for demonstrating industry awareness.**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Quantitative Architectural Risk Equation

The KTU 2024 Board Examiner frequently asks: *"Derive the relationship between SLE, ARO, and ALE, and apply it to a given scenario."* The following is the full, step-by-step derivation.

**Step 1 — Define the underlying observable quantity.**

The fundamental unit of loss in risk analysis is the **expected loss per single adverse event** against a single asset. We define this as the Single Loss Expectancy.

$$
SLE = AV \times EF
$$

Where:
- $AV$ = Asset Value (₹), the replacement or business-continuity cost of the asset
- $EF$ = Exposure Factor, the fraction of the asset destroyed by a single successful attack, $EF \in [0, 1]$

**Step 2 — Extend to the annual time horizon.**

If a threat agent attempts exploitation with an expected frequency of $ARO$ events per year, the *annual* loss expectation is the product of the per-event loss and the per-year event count.

$$
ALE = SLE \times ARO
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute to obtain the fully expanded form.**

Substituting Step 1 into Step 2:

$$
ALE = (AV \times EF) \times ARO
$$

This is the **canonical quantitative risk equation** taught in every ARA module globally.

**Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)**

**Problem:** A customer database is valued at ₹50,00,000. A SQL injection attack would compromise 60% of the records. The threat is expected to succeed 0.4 times per year. Compute SLE, ALE, and the post-mitigation residual ALE if a WAF reduces exposure factor to 15% and the attack frequency to 0.1 / year.

**Step 4a — Compute SLE (inherent):**

$$
SLE_{inherent} = 50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.60
$$

$$
SLE_{inherent} = 30{,}00{,}000 \ \text{₹}
$$

**Step 4b — Compute ALE (inherent):**

$$
ALE_{inherent} = 30{,}00{,}000 \times 0.4
$$

$$
ALE_{inherent} = 12{,}00{,}000 \ \text{₹ / year}
$$

**Step 4c — Compute SLE (post-mitigation):**

$$
SLE_{mitigated} = 50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.15
$$

$$
SLE_{mitigated} = 7{,}50{,}000 \ \text{₹}
$$

**Step 4d — Compute ALE (post-mitigation):**

$$
ALE_{mitigated} = 7{,}50{,}000 \times 0.1
$$

$$
ALE_{mitigated} = 75{,}000 \ \text{₹ / year}
$$

**Step 4e — Compute the Annualized Risk Reduction (in ₹):**

$$
\Delta ALE = ALE_{inherent} - ALE_{mitigated}
$$

$$
\Delta ALE = 12{,}00{,}000 - 75{,}000 = 11{,}25{,}000 \ \text{₹ / year}
$$

**Step 4f — Compute the Risk Reduction Percentage (RR%):**

$$
RR\% = \dfrac{11{,}25{,}000}{12{,}00{,}000} \times 100
$$

$$
RR\% = 93.75\%
$$

**Interpretation for the KTU answer:** The Web Application Firewall (WAF) eliminates ₹11.25 lakh of expected loss per year, representing a **93.75% reduction in architectural risk exposure**. This is a textbook *cost-justified* mitigation decision.

### 3.2 DREAD Scoring Derivation

When asset values are not directly monetizable (e.g., reputational damage, brand erosion), KTU examiners accept the qualitative **DREAD** model. Each letter scores 1–10:

- **D** — Damage potential
- **R** — Reproducibility
- **E** — Exploitability
- **A** — Affected users
- **D** — Discoverability

The composite score is the arithmetic mean:

$$
R_{DREAD} = \dfrac{D_{dmg} + D_{rep} + E_{xpl} + A_{ff}} + D_{isc}}{5}
$$

**Worked example:** A stored XSS in the admin dashboard scores D=8, R=9, E=7, A=9, D=8.

$$
R_{DREAD} = \dfrac{8 + 9 + 7 + 9 + 8}{5} = \dfrac{41}{5} = 8.2
$$

A score of **8.2 / 10** classifies this as a **Critical** architectural risk requiring immediate mitigation.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: ARA vs Other Risk Analysis Types

The following matrix is the most common **"compare and contrast"** question in KTU Module 3.

| Dimension | Architectural Risk Analysis | Operational Risk Analysis | Application-Level (Code) Risk Analysis |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Stage of System Lifecycle** | Design / Pre-build | Production / Post-deployment | Development / Testing |
| **Primary Input** | Architecture diagrams, DFDs | Logs, SIEM alerts, incident reports | Source code, SAST/DAST reports |
| **Granularity** | Component / subsystem | Process / system | Function / line of code |
| **Primary Toolset** | STRIDE, PASTA, attack trees | FAIR, NIST 800-30 operational tier | OWASP Top 10, CWE, SAST scanners |
| **Cost of Fix** | Lowest (design changes are cheap) | Highest (live system patching) | Medium (refactoring) |
| **Output Artifact** | Risk register + revised architecture | Risk treatment plan + BC/DR plan | Patched code + security unit tests |
| **Frequency** | At every major design change | Continuous (24×7 SOC monitoring) | Per build / per sprint |
| **KTU Mark Weightage** | **High (7–14 marks)** | Medium (3–7 marks) | Medium (3–7 marks) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-Examiner Insight:** When a KTU question asks you to "critically evaluate," the word *critically* demands that you state **advantages AND limitations**. ARA, for instance, has a limitation: it depends on the *accuracy* of the input architecture diagram — a flawed or outdated blueprint yields a flawed risk model. Always cite this in your conclusion.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Six-Phase ARA Process Flow

The following Mermaid block compiles a faithful block-level architecture flow of the ARA process. It is fully compatible with the KTU 2024 NEP 2020 outcome-based assessment framework.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Phase 1: Architectural Decomposition] --> A2[Phase 2: Asset Identification and Valuation]
    A2 --> A3[Phase 3: Threat Modeling using STRIDE or PASTA]
    A3 --> A4[Phase 4: Vulnerability and Attack Surface Mapping]
    A4 --> A5[Phase 5: Risk Calculation and Prioritization]
    A5 --> A6[Phase 6: Mitigation Design and Residual Risk Acceptance]
    A6 --> A7{New Architectural Changes Introduced?}
    A7 -- Yes --> A1
    A7 -- No --> A8[Final Signed Risk Register and Audit Report]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Each rectangle represents a distinct **analytical phase** with its own deliverable.
- The decision diamond `A7` enforces the *iterative, closed-loop* nature of ARA.
- The terminal node `A8` is the auditable artifact — the very document a KTU examiner expects to see referenced in a 14-mark answer.

### 4.2 Trust Boundary & Data Flow Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    U[Untrusted User Zone] -->|HTTPS Request| TB1{{Trust Boundary 1: Perimeter DMZ}}
    TB1 --> WAF[Web Application Firewall]
    WAF --> APP[Application Server Tier]
    APP -->|Encrypted Channel| TB2{{Trust Boundary 2: Internal Services}}
    TB2 --> DB[(Customer Database PII Store)]
    APP -.->|Telemetry| SIEM[(Security Information and Event Management)]
    SIEM --> SOC[Security Operations Center Analyst]
```

**Annotation for KTU answer script:**

- **Trust Boundary 1 (TB1)** marks the transition from *untrusted* to *partially trusted* — it must enforce authentication and WAF rules.
- **Trust Boundary 2 (TB2)** marks the transition from *application tier* to *data tier* — it must enforce least-privilege authorization and encryption-at-rest.
- The **dashed telemetry line** to SIEM represents a *monitoring* edge, not a data path. Architectural risk analysis evaluates both **functional flows** (solid) and **observability flows** (dashed) because an unmonitored trust boundary is itself a critical risk.

### 4.3 Risk Calculation Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN1[Asset Value AV] --> MUL1((Multiply))
    IN2[Exposure Factor EF] --> MUL1
    MUL1 --> SLE[SLE: Single Loss Expectancy]
    IN3[Annualized Rate of Occurrence ARO] --> MUL2((Multiply))
    SLE --> MUL2
    MUL2 --> ALE[ALE: Annualized Loss Expectancy]
    ALE --> SUB((Subtract))
    ALE2[ALE after Mitigation] --> SUB
    SUB --> DELTA[Delta ALE: Annualized Risk Reduction in INR per year]
    DELTA --> PCT((Divide and Multiply by 100))
    ALE --> PCT
    PCT --> RR[Risk Reduction Percentage RR percent]
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The top half computes the **inherent risk** (before any control).
- The bottom-right input supplies the **mitigated risk** value.
- The subtraction block yields the **financial benefit** of the mitigation — the single most defensible number in a 14-mark ARA answer.

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU Diagram Questions:** A well-labeled block diagram earns 2–3 marks *by itself*, even if your surrounding text is imperfect. Always include directional arrows, clearly mark inputs vs. outputs, and label every processing block with its *formula name*, not just its acronym.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]**

**Q1. [3 Marks]** *Define Architectural Risk Analysis. How does it differ from operational risk analysis?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Architectural Risk Analysis (ARA) is a structured methodology used to **identify, classify, and prioritize security risks embedded within the design and structure** of a software or system architecture before it is built or deployed at scale. It operates at the blueprint level using artifacts like Data Flow Diagrams and UML component diagrams.

It differs from operational risk analysis in three ways:
1. **Lifecycle stage:** ARA is performed during the *design phase*; operational risk analysis is performed on *live production systems*.
2. **Input data:** ARA consumes static design documents; operational analysis consumes live logs, SIEM alerts, and incident reports.
3. **Cost of remediation:** ARA-driven fixes are *cheap* (design changes); operational fixes are *expensive* (live patching, downtime).

> *(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the lifecycle distinction, 1 mark for the input/cost distinction. Award partial marks generously for any two of the three contrasts.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]**

**Q2. [3 Marks]** *List the components of the STRIDE threat classification model and give one architectural example for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Letter | Threat | Architectural Example |
|---|---|---|
| **S** | Spoofing | An attacker forging a JWT token to impersonate a legitimate user at the API gateway |
| **T** | Tampering | Unauthorized modification of price fields in a checkout microservice via a parameter pollution attack |
| **R** | Repudiation | A user denying a fund transfer because the transaction log lacks a digitally signed audit trail |
| **I** | Information Disclosure | A misconfigured S3 bucket exposing customer PII to the public internet |
| **D** | Denial of Service | A volumetric HTTP flood overwhelming the load balancer, taking down the entire e-commerce site |
| **E** | Elevation of Privilege | A buffer overflow in the admin panel allowing a guest user to gain root access on the application server |

> *(Examiner's note: Full 3 marks for correctly listing all six letters and providing an architectural — not network-level — example for each. A common mistake is citing generic examples like "phishing" for Spoofing, which is too broad and loses 1 mark.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Question — Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper, Module 3]**
> *Note: KTU 2024 scheme provides internal choice between two 14-mark questions per module. Answer ANY ONE of the following:*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the six phases of Architectural Risk Analysis with a neat block diagram. Apply STRIDE to a real-world cloud-based payment gateway architecture (components: mobile app, API gateway, authentication service, payment processor, database, audit log).

**(b) [7 Marks]** A banking application has a transaction database valued at ₹2 crore. An insider threat has an exposure factor of 0.8 and an ARO of 0.5 per year. After deploying multi-factor authentication and database encryption, the new exposure factor is 0.1 and ARO is 0.05 per year. Compute SLE, ALE, residual ALE, and the percentage risk reduction. Justify whether the mitigation is cost-effective if the MFA + encryption solution costs ₹15 lakh per year.

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]:**

1. **Six Phases** (3 marks for naming + explaining all six):
   - *Decomposition, Asset Valuation, Threat Modeling, Vulnerability Mapping, Risk Calculation, Mitigation & Residual Risk.*
2. **Block diagram** (2 marks): A clear flowchart showing the six phases with the iterative feedback loop.
3. **STRIDE application** (2 marks): A table mapping each STRIDE category to a specific component of the payment gateway. For example:
   - **S** → Spoofing at the API gateway via stolen JWT
   - **T** → Tampering of payment amount in transit
   - **R** → Repudiation if audit log is not write-once
   - **I** → Information disclosure from the transaction database
   - **D** → DoS at the load balancer in front of the payment processor
   - **E** → Privilege escalation in the mobile app bypassing the authentication service

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]:**

$$
SLE_{inherent} = 2{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.8 = 1{,}60{,}00{,}000 \ \text{₹}
$$

$$
ALE_{inherent} = 1{,}60{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5 = 80{,}00{,}000 \ \text{₹ / year}
$$

$$
SLE_{mitigated} = 2{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.1 = 20{,}00{,}000 \ \text{₹}
$$

$$
ALE_{mitigated} = 20{,}00{,}000 \times 0.05 = 1{,}00{,}000 \ \text{₹ / year}
$$

$$
\Delta ALE = 80{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 = 79{,}00{,}000 \ \text{₹ / year}
$$

$$
RR\% = \dfrac{79{,}00{,}000}{80{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 98.75\%
$$

**Cost-effectiveness verdict:** The mitigation costs ₹15 lakh/year and saves ₹79 lakh/year. The **Return on Security Investment (ROSI)** is:

$$
ROSI = \dfrac{\Delta ALE - Cost\ of\ Control}{Cost\ of\ Control} \times 100
$$

$$
ROSI = \dfrac{79{,}00{,}000 - 15{,}00{,}000}{15{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 426.67\%
$$

> A positive ROSI confirms the mitigation is **strongly cost-effective**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the DREAD risk-scoring methodology. Compute the DREAD score for a vulnerability with the following expert-assigned scores: Damage = 9, Reproducibility = 8, Exploitability = 6, Affected Users = 9, Discoverability = 7. Classify the resulting risk as Low, Medium, High, or Critical.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast Architectural Risk Analysis, Operational Risk Analysis, and Code-Level (Application) Risk Analysis across at least five dimensions. Which one offers the highest ROI for a startup building a brand-new SaaS product, and why?

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]:**

DREAD is a **Microsoft-developed qualitative risk-rating model** used in architectural risk analysis. It produces a composite score on a 0–10 scale by averaging five expert-assigned sub-scores.

**Calculation:**

$$
R_{DREAD} = \dfrac{9 + 8 + 6 + 9 + 7}{5} = \dfrac{39}{5} = 7.8
$$

**Risk classification (standard band):**

- 0.0 – 3.9 → **Low**
- 4.0 – 5.9 → **Medium**
- 6.0 – 7.9 → **High**
- 8.0 – 10.0 → **Critical**

A score of **7.8 / 10** classifies this vulnerability as **High Risk**, requiring scheduled mitigation within the current release cycle but not necessarily an emergency hot-fix.

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]:**

| Dimension | Architectural | Operational | Code-Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lifecycle stage | Design | Production | Development |
| Cost of fix | Lowest | Highest | Medium |
| Expertise required | Security architect | SOC analyst | Developer + AppSec |
| Tools | STRIDE/PASTA | SIEM/SOAR | SAST/DAST |
| Time-to-value | Months (long-term resilience) | Hours (immediate detection) | Days (per-sprint) |

**Highest ROI for a brand-new SaaS startup:** **Architectural Risk Analysis.** The reasoning:
1. The system is *not yet built* — every fix is a design change, not a refactor.
2. The startup has *no production telemetry* to feed operational analysis.
3. The code is *not yet mature* — SAST will generate noise on incomplete code.
4. A single ARA-driven decision (e.g., choosing a zero-trust API gateway) prevents *thousands* of downstream vulnerabilities.

> Therefore, for a *pre-launch* SaaS, ARA delivers the maximum risk reduction per rupee invested.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Module 3 ARA Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing ARA with Penetration Testing** — Pen-testing is *post-build* dynamic black-box testing. ARA is *pre-build* static structural analysis. Mixing them up costs 2–3 marks instantly.
> 2. **Quoting Risk without Units** — "Risk = 6" is incomplete. Always state the scale, e.g., "Risk Score = 6 / 10" or "ALE = ₹12,00,000 / year."
> 3. **Omitting the Iterative Loop** — Many students describe the six ARA phases as linear. The KTU 2024 outcome-based marking scheme *explicitly awards 1 mark* for stating that ARA is iterative and feeds back into Phase 1.
> 4. **Forgetting Residual Risk Acceptance** — Stating only the *mitigated* risk without acknowledging the *residual* risk (and the formal sign-off process) loses 1 mark in 14-mark questions.
> 5. **Not Using a Diagram** — A 14-mark ARA answer *without* a block diagram forfeits 2 marks reserved for visual communication under NEP 2020.
> 6. **Memorizing STRIDE without examples** — Listing the six STRIDE letters without a per-component architectural example fetches only 1 of the 2 marks allocated for that sub-question.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Architectural Risk Analysis**

- **Definition:** ARA = pre-build, blueprint-level, structural security risk identification and prioritization.
- **Six Phases:** Decomposition → Asset Valuation → Threat Modeling → Vulnerability Mapping → Risk Calculation → Mitigation & Residual Acceptance.
- **Three Core Inputs:** Architecture diagrams, Asset inventory, Threat intelligence.
- **Two Key Frameworks:** STRIDE (Microsoft) and PASTA (process-oriented).
- **Three Key Equations:** $SLE = AV \times EF$, $ALE = SLE \times ARO$, $RR\% = (\Delta ALE / ALE_{inherent}) \times 100$.
- **One Key Insight:** ARA is *iterative* — every architectural change triggers a new ARA cycle.
- **One Key Comparison:** ARA < Operational < Code-Level in terms of *cost of fix*.
- **One Industry Anchor:** NIST SP 800-160 (Systems Security Engineering) is the gold-standard reference.
- **One Common Mistake:** Treating ARA as a one-time audit rather than a continuous engineering discipline.
- **One Board Exam Heuristic:** Every 14-mark ARA answer must contain **at least one diagram**, **at least one numerical example**, and **at least one industry framework reference** to earn full marks under the 2024 NEP 2020 outcome-based marking scheme.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Security Testing & Reliability (Pen Testing)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT418 - SECURITY ASSESSMENT AND RISK ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Security Testing & Reliability (Pen Testing)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Penetration Testing (Pen Testing)** is an authorized, systematic, and methodical process of **proactively evaluating the security posture** of an information system, network, or application by simulating an attack from malicious outsiders (black-hat hackers), malicious insiders, or accidental misuse. It involves the **active exploitation of discovered vulnerabilities** to determine the real-world effectiveness of existing security controls and to quantify the potential business impact of a successful breach.

According to the **NIST SP 800-115** Technical Guide to Information Security Testing and Assessment, penetration testing is defined as a security testing methodology in which evaluators attempt to circumvent the security features of a system based on their understanding of the system design and implementation. The **PTES (Penetration Testing Execution Standard)** describes it as a structured assessment of the **Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability (CIA Triad)** of a target system through the controlled use of offensive techniques.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, the subject is treated as a measurable, repeatable engineering process that feeds **quantitative risk metrics** into the broader Risk Analysis framework.

## 1.2 Key Terminology Hierarchy

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Vulnerability** | A weakness in a system that could be exploited to compromise security. |
| **Threat** | Any circumstance or event with the potential to cause harm to a system. |
| **Exploit** | A piece of code, technique, or tool that takes advantage of a vulnerability. |
| **Payload** | The component of an exploit that performs the malicious action (e.g., reverse shell). |
| **Attack Vector** | The path or method used by an attacker to gain unauthorized access. |
| **Attack Surface** | The sum of all possible points (endpoints, inputs, interfaces) where an unauthorized user can attempt to enter or extract data. |
| **Post-Exploitation** | The phase after successful exploitation, used to determine the value of the compromised target. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Bank Vault Inspection**
> Imagine a bank hires a former locksmith (the *penetration tester*) to break into its own vault. The bank does not give the locksmith the keys (mimicking an **external black-box attack**), or it may give the blueprints (mimicking a **white-box insider attack**). The locksmith tries to pick the lock, tamper with the door hinges, or trick a guard. Whatever the locksmith does, the bank learns **exactly how a real criminal would attack**, and most importantly, **how to fix those weaknesses before an actual robbery occurs**.
>
> The key insight: Penetration testing is not about *confirming* that you are secure — it is about **measuring** how *insecure* you actually are and producing **actionable evidence** for remediation.

The reliability of a system, when viewed through this lens, is not just "does it work" but "**can it resist an intelligent, motivated adversary over time?**" This shifts security testing from a binary yes/no assessment to a **continuous, probabilistic measurement** of defensive strength.

## 1.4 Industry-Standard Metrics (Bold Highlights)

The following metrics are universally referenced in KTU cybersecurity syllabi and professional practice:

- **CVSS (Common Vulnerability Scoring System)** — a **0.0 to 10.0** scale quantifying severity.
- **DREAD** — a Microsoft-originated risk-rating model.
- **CVE (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures)** — a unique identifier (e.g., **CVE-2024-12345**).
- **OWASP Top 10** — a globally recognized list of the **ten most critical web-application security risks**.
- **PTES Phases** — 7 standardized phases of a professional penetration test.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Phases of Penetration Testing (PTES-Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Anchor:** "**R-E-I-R-P**" → **R**econ, **E**numeration, **I**nitial Access, **R**eport, **P**ost-Exploitation (with E and I often grouped as Information Gathering).

### Phase 1 — Planning & Reconnaissance (Information Gathering)
- Define the **scope**, **rules of engagement (RoE)**, and **authorization** in writing.
- **Passive Reconnaissance:** OSINT (Open Source Intelligence) — searching public records, DNS, social media, Shodan, Google Dorking. The target is never touched directly.
- **Active Reconnaissance:** Direct probing of the target using tools such as **Nmap**, **Masscan**, and **Netcraft** to discover live hosts, open ports, and service banners.

### Phase 2 — Scanning & Enumeration
- **Vulnerability Scanning:** Automated tools such as **Nessus**, **Qualys**, and **OpenVAS** identify known CVEs.
- **Port & Service Enumeration:** Identifying versions of running services (e.g., *Apache 2.4.49*, *OpenSSH 7.2*) to map them against known exploits.
- **Network Mapping:** Building a topology diagram of routers, switches, firewalls, and endpoints.

### Phase 3 — Gaining Access (Exploitation)
- The pen tester actively attempts to breach the system using exploits (e.g., **Metasploit Framework**, manual SQL Injection, buffer overflow).
- Common entry vectors: **phishing emails, exposed RDP, unpatched web servers, weak credentials**.

### Phase 4 — Maintaining Access & Post-Exploitation
- Once inside, the tester determines the **blast radius** — how far an attacker could pivot.
- Techniques: **privilege escalation** (vertical/horizontal), **lateral movement**, **persistence mechanisms** (cron jobs, registry keys, rootkits).
- **Goal:** Demonstrate the *worst-case business impact* — e.g., exfiltrating the customer database.

### Phase 5 — Analysis & Reporting
- The most **exam-relevant** phase.
- Deliverables: **Executive Summary**, **Technical Findings**, **Risk Rating**, **Remediation Roadmap**, **Re-test Plan**.
- Every finding must include: **Vulnerability description, CVSS score, proof-of-concept (PoC), impact, and remediation steps**.

## 2.2 The Three Testing Methodologies (Knowledge & Approach)

| Methodology | Tester Knowledge | Realism | Time/Cost | KTU Use |
|------------|------------------|---------|-----------|---------|
| **Black-Box** | Zero prior knowledge of the system; simulates an external hacker | High | High | Most common in exams |
| **White-Box / Crystal-Box** | Full access to source code, architecture diagrams, credentials | Low (artificial) | Low | Tests internal code quality |
| **Gray-Box** | Partial knowledge (e.g., logged-in user credentials) | Medium | Medium | Realistic insider threat simulation |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Risk Quantification)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently test the relationship between **Threat**, **Vulnerability**, **Asset Value**, and **Likelihood**. Memorize the master risk equation and its variants below.

### Master Risk Equation

$$
R = L \times I
$$

Where:
- $R$ = **Risk** (annualized, in monetary or qualitative units)
- $L$ = **Likelihood** of exploitation (probability per year, $0 \le L \le 1$)
- $I$ = **Impact** if exploitation occurs (monetary loss, or qualitative scale 1–5)

### Annualized Loss Expectation (ALE)

$$
ALE = SLE \times ARO
$$

Where:
- $SLE$ = **Single Loss Expectancy** = Asset Value $\times$ Exposure Factor
- $ARO$ = **Annualized Rate of Occurrence** (expected number of incidents per year)

### CVSS v3.1 Base Score Components

The Base Score ($B$) is derived from **Impact** ($I$) and **Exploitability** ($E$):

$$
B = \text{roundup}(\min(I + E, 10))
$$

The **Impact Sub-Score (ISC)** is:

$$
ISC = 1 - \left[(1 - C) \times (1 - I_c) \times (1 - A)\right]
$$

Where $C$, $I_c$, and $A$ are the **Confidentiality**, **Integrity**, and **Availability** impact metrics, each rated Low (0.22), High (0.56), or None (0.0).

### DREAD Risk Rating (Per-Finding Score)

$$
D_{\text{score}} = \frac{D + R + E + A + D_2}{5}
$$

Where each letter (Damage, Reproducibility, Exploitability, Affected Users, Discoverability) is rated 1–10.

### Master Summary Table

| Formula | Purpose | Boundary Conditions |
|---------|---------|---------------------|
| $R = L \times I$ | Qualitative risk score | $L \in [0,1]$, $I \in [0,1]$ |
| $SLE = V \times EF$ | Loss per incident | $0 \le EF \le 1$ |
| $ALE = SLE \times ARO$ | Annual expected loss | $ARO \in \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ |
| $ISC = 1 - \prod(1-m_i)$ | Multi-attribute impact | Each $m_i \in \{0, 0.22, 0.56\}$ |
| $B = \text{roundup}(\min(I+E, 10))$ | CVSS base severity | $0 \le B \le 10$ |
| $D_{\text{score}} = \overline{x}_{D,R,E,A,D_2}$ | Average risk rating | Each $\in [1, 10]$ |

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **CVSS** is the de-facto standard used by **NVD (National Vulnerability Database)**, the **US-CERT**, and **patch management tools**.
- **ALE/ARO** is mandated by **ISO 27005** and **NIST SP 800-30** for quantitative risk assessments in **Banking, Healthcare, and BFSI sectors** — all of which are heavily relevant to Kerala's IT and FinTech job market.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — CVSS Base Score Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** A web application has a vulnerability where an unauthenticated remote attacker can read any file on the server (e.g., a classic *Path Traversal* in an Apache 2.4.49 server, similar to **CVE-2021-41773**). Determine the CVSS v3.1 Base Score.

### Step 1 — Identify the Base Metrics

| Metric | Value | Code | Numeric Value |
|--------|-------|------|---------------|
| Attack Vector (AV) | Network | `N` | 0.85 |
| Attack Complexity (AC) | Low | `L` | 0.77 |
| Privileges Required (PR) | None | `N` | 0.85 |
| User Interaction (UI) | None | `N` | 0.85 |
| Confidentiality Impact (C) | High | `H` | 0.56 |
| Integrity Impact (I) | None | `N` | 0.00 |
| Availability Impact (A) | None | `N` | 0.00 |

### Step 2 — Compute the Impact Sub-Score (ISC)

$$
\begin{aligned}
ISC &= 1 - \left[(1 - C) \times (1 - I_c) \times (1 - A)\right] \\
    &= 1 - \left[(1 - 0.56) \times (1 - 0.00) \times (1 - 0.00)\right] \\
    &= 1 - \left[0.44 \times 1.00 \times 1.00\right] \\
    &= 1 - 0.44 \\
    &= 0.56
\end{aligned}
$$

The ISC value 0.56 is **greater than 0**, so the system is "impactful" (otherwise the vulnerability would be scored 0.0).

### Step 3 — Compute the Exploitability Sub-Score

The CVSS v3.1 formula for Exploitability is:

$$
E = 8.22 \times AV \times AC \times PR \times UI
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= 8.22 \times 0.85 \times 0.77 \times 0.85 \times 0.85 \\
  &= 8.22 \times (0.85)^3 \times 0.77 \\
  &= 8.22 \times 0.614125 \times 0.77 \\
  &= 8.22 \times 0.472876 \\
  &= 3.887 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Base Score

Because ISC > 0, the Base Score is:

$$
B = \text{roundup}\left(\min\left((ISC \times 0.6 + E \times 0.4) \times 1.5, 10\right)\right)
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{\text{inner}} &= (0.56 \times 0.6) + (3.887 \times 0.4) \\
                 &= 0.336 + 1.5548 \\
                 &= 1.8908 \\
B_{\text{raw}}   &= 1.8908 \times 1.5 \\
                 &= 2.8362 \\
B                &= \text{roundup}(2.8362) \\
                 &= 3.0
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result: CVSS Base Score = 3.0 → Severity = LOW.** This is a typical outcome when only Confidentiality is affected with no Integrity or Availability loss. Compare with a "complete system takeover" scenario that would yield a 9.8 (CRITICAL).

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — ALE Calculation for a Banking Server

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** A regional bank's customer database server is valued at **₹50,00,000 (50 Lakhs INR)**. Historical data shows that on average, **1 breach occurs every 2 years**, and each breach exposes **40%** of the database. Compute the SLE, ARO, and ALE.

### Step 1 — Compute the Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)

$$
\begin{aligned}
SLE &= V \times EF \\
    &= 50,00,000 \times 0.40 \\
    &= 20,00,000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $V = 50,00,000$ (Asset Value)
- $EF = 0.40$ (Exposure Factor — fraction of asset lost per incident)

### Step 2 — Compute the Annualized Rate of Occurrence (ARO)

$$
ARO = \frac{1}{\text{Period in years}} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5 \text{ incidents/year}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE)

$$
\begin{aligned}
ALE &= SLE \times ARO \\
    &= 20,00,000 \times 0.5 \\
    &= 10,00,000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Cost-Benefit Decision for a Firewall (₹2,00,000/year)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Recommended if } & ALE > \text{Annualized Control Cost} \\
                       & 10,00,000 > 2,00,000 \quad \checkmark \\
\text{Net Savings}      & = ALE - Control Cost \\
                       & = 10,00,000 - 2,00,000 \\
                       & = 8,00,000 \text{ INR per year} \quad \text{(Implement the firewall)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a classic exam question pattern — the student is expected to convert real-world scenarios into the $ALE = V \times EF \times ARO$ equation and justify a security investment.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Pen Test vs. Vulnerability Scan

| Dimension | Vulnerability Scan | Penetration Test |
|-----------|-------------------|------------------|
| **Depth** | Surface-level, automated | Deep, manual + automated |
| **Goal** | Find known flaws | Exploit flaws to measure real impact |
| **Output** | List of CVEs with CVSS | Realistic attack narrative + business risk |
| **Frequency** | Weekly/Monthly | Quarterly/Annually |
| **Tools** | Nessus, Qualys | Metasploit, Burp Suite, Cobalt Strike |
| **Skill Required** | Low-Medium | High (Offensive security expert) |
| **Cost** | Low | High |
| **KTU Exam Likelihood** | Low | **Very High** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student error:** Confusing a *vulnerability scan* with a *penetration test*. KTU questions often frame them as synonymous — they are NOT. A vulnerability scan **lists** weaknesses; a pen test **exploits** them to demonstrate damage.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Pen Testing Lifecycle (Mermaid Flow Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Engagement] --> B[Phase 1: Planning and Scoping]
    B --> C[Phase 2: Reconnaissance]
    C --> D[Phase 3: Scanning and Enumeration]
    D --> E[Phase 4: Vulnerability Analysis]
    E --> F[Phase 5: Exploitation]
    F --> G{Access Gained?}
    G -- No --> H[Document Defensive Strengths]
    G -- Yes --> I[Phase 6: Post-Exploitation]
    I --> J[Phase 7: Reporting and Remediation]
    H --> J
```
