An enterprise's customer database is valued at **₹10,00,000 (10 Lakhs)**. A SQL injection attack is expected to expose **40%** of the records. Historical data shows this attack occurs **once every 4 years**. The proposed Web Application Firewall (WAF) costs **₹1,50,000 per year** to operate and reduces the exposure factor to **5%**, with attack frequency reduced to **once every 10 years**.

**Compute the SLE, ALE (before control), ALE (after control), CBA, and ROSI. Should the WAF be implemented?**

#### Step 1: Identify Given Values

- Asset Value (AV) = ₹10,00,000
- Exposure Factor (EF) = 40% = 0.40
- ARO (before control) = 1 event / 4 years = 0.25
- EF (after control) = 5% = 0.05
- ARO (after control) = 1 event / 10 years = 0.10
- Annual Control Cost (ACS) = ₹1,50,000

#### Step 2: Calculate Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)

$$SLE = AV \times EF_{\text{before}}$$

$$SLE = 10{,}00{,}000 \times 0.40$$

$$SLE = 4{,}00{,}000$$

> The single loss from a successful SQL injection would cost the enterprise **₹4,00,000**.

#### Step 3: Calculate ALE Before Control

$$ALE_{\text{before}} = SLE \times ARO_{\text{before}}$$

$$ALE_{\text{before}} = 4{,}00{,}000 \times 0.25$$

$$ALE_{\text{before}} = 1{,}00{,}000$$

> Without any WAF, the enterprise expects to lose **₹1,00,000 per year** to SQL injection attacks.

#### Step 4: Calculate SLE After Control

$$SLE_{\text{after}} = AV \times EF_{\text{after}}$$

$$SLE_{\text{after}} = 10{,}00{,}000 \times 0.05$$

$$SLE_{\text{after}} = 50{,}000$$

> With the WAF, a successful attack would cost only **₹50,000**.

#### Step 5: Calculate ALE After Control

$$ALE_{\text{after}} = SLE_{\text{after}} \times ARO_{\text{after}}$$

$$ALE_{\text{after}} = 50{,}000 \times 0.10$$

$$ALE_{\text{after}} = 5{,}000$$

> With the WAF, the expected annual loss drops to just **₹5,000**.

#### Step 6: Cost-Benefit Analysis (CBA)

$$CBA = ALE_{\text{before}} - ALE_{\text{after}} - ACS$$

$$CBA = 1{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}000 - 1{,}50{,}000$$

$$CBA = -55{,}000$$

> A **negative CBA** means the control costs more than the risk reduction it provides — from a *pure financial* perspective, the WAF is not cost-justified.

#### Step 7: Return on Security Investment (ROSI)

$$ROSI = \dfrac{(ALE_{\text{before}} - ALE_{\text{after}}) - ACS}{ACS} \times 100\%$$

$$ROSI = \dfrac{(1{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}000) - 1{,}50{,}000}{1{,}50{,}000} \times 100\%$$

$$ROSI = \dfrac{-55{,}000}{1{,}50{,}000} \times 100\%$$

$$ROSI = -36.67\%$$

> A **negative ROSI** confirms the control is financially unviable in isolation.

#### Step 8: Qualitative Adjustment (Examiner's Insight)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU exams, the expected answer should also mention **qualitative factors** that pure financial formulas miss: regulatory penalties (e.g., GDPR fine up to **4% of global turnover**), reputational damage, and customer trust. The WAF may still be implemented for **regulatory compliance** even if ROSI is negative.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Policy Hierarchy Comparison

| Attribute | EISP (Enterprise) | ISSP (Issue-Specific) | SysSP (System-Specific) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Level** | Strategic | Tactical / Mid-level | Operational / Technical |
| **Audience** | Board, Executives, All employees | Specific user groups / functions | System administrators, developers |
| **Frequency of Update** | Annual or upon major change | As needed (technology/business shift) | With each system change/version |
| **Approved By** | CEO / Board | CISO / Department head | System owner / IT Director |
| **Length** | 5–15 pages | 2–10 pages | 1–5 pages + technical annexures |
| **Examples** | "We shall protect all customer PII" | "BYOD policy", "Email acceptable use" | "Firewall rule set", "DB access control list" |
| **Compliance Link** | ISO 27001 Clause 5.2, NIST CSF GV.PO | ISO 27001 Annex A controls, NIST CF | CIS Benchmarks, vendor hardening guides |

### 3.3 Defense in Depth: Layered Control Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Defense in Depth?**: A single control always fails. Layered security ensures that if one layer is breached, subsequent layers provide additional protection.

| Layer | Control Type | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Layer 1 — Policies & Governance** | Administrative | EISP, ISSP, SysSP, awareness training |
| **Layer 2 — Physical** | Physical | Locks, CCTV, mantraps, biometric access |
| **Layer 3 — Perimeter** | Logical/Technical | Firewalls, IDS/IPS, DMZ, DDoS scrubbers |
| **Layer 4 — Network** | Logical/Technical | VLANs, NAC, 802.1X, network segmentation |
| **Layer 5 — Host** | Logical/Technical | Antivirus, EDR, host firewall, patch management |
| **Layer 6 — Application** | Logical/Technical | WAF, SAST/DAST, input validation, secure SDLC |
| **Layer 7 — Data** | Logical/Technical | Encryption (AES-256), DLP, tokenization, backup |
| **Layer 8 — Human** | Administrative/Behavioral | Phishing simulations, security champions program |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Enterprise Security Policy Hierarchy (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Board of Directors and CEO] --> B[EISP - Enterprise Information Security Policy]
    B --> C[ISSP - Email and Internet Use Policy]
    B --> D[ISSP - BYOD and Mobile Policy]
    B --> E[ISSP - Data Classification Policy]
    B --> F[ISSP - Incident Response Policy]
    B --> G[ISSP - Remote Access Policy]
    B --> H[ISSP - Cloud Computing Policy]
    C --> I[SysSP - Mail Server Configuration]
    C --> J[SysSP - Web Proxy Rules]
    D --> K[SysSP - MDM Configuration]
    E --> L[SysSP - Database Access Controls]
    F --> M[SysSP - SIEM and SOAR Rules]
    G --> N[SysSP - VPN Gateway Configuration]
    H --> O[SysSP - CASB Integration Rules]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation**: This diagram illustrates the **top-down cascade** of security policies. Strategic intent (EISP) flows from leadership, branches into tactical ISSPs addressing specific issues, and finally materializes into operational SysSPs that engineers and administrators implement.

### 4.2 Three Lines of Defense Governance Model

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1a[Business Unit Managers] --> L1b[IT Operations Team]
    L1b --> L1c[HR and Finance Departments]
    L2a[CISO Office] --> L2b[Risk and Compliance Team]
    L2b --> L2c[Data Protection Officer]
    L3a[Internal Audit Department] --> L3b[External Auditors]
    L3b --> L3c[Regulatory Examiners]
    BOD[Board of Directors and Audit Committee] --> L2a
    BOD --> L3a
    L1a -.Reports risk to.-> L2a
    L2a -.Reports oversight to.-> BOD
    L3a -.Reports assurance to.-> BOD
```

### 4.3 Enterprise Risk Management Lifecycle

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Step 1: Identify Assets and Threats] --> P2[Step 2: Assess Vulnerabilities]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Calculate Likelihood and Impact]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Determine Risk Score]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Select Controls - Avoid, Transfer, Mitigate, Accept]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Implement Controls]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Monitor and Review]
    P7 --> P1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Risk Loop**: Risk management is **never a one-time project**. Step 7 feeds back into Step 1 because new threats, assets, and vulnerabilities constantly emerge. This is the foundation of the **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)** cycle in ISO 27001.

### 4.4 Defense-in-Depth Concentric Layers (Adapted Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    OUT[Outermost - Policies Governance and Awareness] --> L1[Layer 1 - Physical Security]
    L1 --> L2[Layer 2 - Perimeter Network Controls]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3 - Internal Network Controls]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4 - Host Endpoint Controls]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5 - Application Security Controls]
    L5 --> L6[Layer 6 - Data Protection Controls]
    L6 --> CORE[Core Asset - Data and Reputation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram**: The attacker must penetrate each concentric layer to reach the core asset. If Layer 3 (Network Segmentation) is breached, Layer 4 (Host Controls) still stands. This is the **essence of defense in depth** — redundancy and diversity of controls.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Define Enterprise Information Security Policy (EISP). List any four components of an EISP.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Enterprise Information Security Policy (EISP) is the master, top-level policy document that defines the strategic security posture of an entire organization. It is approved by senior management and governs all subordinate policies.

Four components of an EISP (1 mark each):
1. **Statement of Purpose and Scope** — Defines the assets and domains covered.
2. **Information Security Objectives** — Strategic goals aligned with business mission.
3. **Authority and Access Control Policies** — Decision-making hierarchy and access rules.
4. **Responsibility Allocation and Compliance** — Defines roles (CISO, IT staff, users) and penalties for violations.

*(Examiner allocates ~1 mark for definition and ~2 marks for components — answer must list at least 4 clearly.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between Issue-Specific Security Policy (ISSP) and System-Specific Security Policy (SysSP) with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | ISSP | SysSP |
|---|---|---|
| **Level** | Tactical / Mid-level | Operational / Technical |
| **Focus** | Specific issue or technology area | Specific system or application |
| **Example** | BYOD policy, Email use policy | Firewall rule configuration, DB ACL |

*(1 mark for ISSP definition + example, 1 mark for SysSP definition + example, 1 mark for clear differentiation.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Expected Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the different types of information security policies used in an enterprise. Describe the EISP, ISSP, and SysSP with examples. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Information security policies form a hierarchical governance structure that translates business security goals into actionable technical and behavioral rules. The three primary policy types are EISP, ISSP, and SysSP.

**EISP — Enterprise Information Security Policy (2 marks):**
The EISP is the strategic, organization-wide master policy approved by the CEO and Board of Directors. It sets the tone for security and addresses:
- Statement of purpose and scope
- Security objectives aligned with business mission
- Definition of authority structure (CISO, security committee)
- Responsibility allocation across departments
- Compliance with laws and regulations
- Penalties for violations

**ISSP — Issue-Specific Security Policy (2 marks):**
ISSPs are tactical policies that address specific areas of concern. They are narrower in scope and target defined user groups or technologies. Examples include:
- Email and Internet Use Policy
- BYOD Policy
- Data Classification and Handling Policy
- Incident Response Policy
- Cloud Computing Policy
Each ISSP typically contains an issue statement, applicability statement, roles and responsibilities, compliance, and review schedule.

**SysSP — System-Specific Security Policy (2 marks):**
SysSPs are operational and technical, targeting specific systems. They are of two types:
1. **Managerial Guidance SysSP** — purpose, scope, responsibilities for a system.
2. **Technical Specification SysSP** — actual configurations, access control lists, port settings.
Example: A firewall SysSP may mandate blocking of inbound Telnet (port 23) and configuring logging thresholds for DDoS detection.

---

**(b)** Discuss the role of the CISO in enterprise information security. Explain the Three Lines of Defense model. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**CISO Role (3 marks):**
The Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) is the senior executive accountable for the organization's information security strategy and execution. Key responsibilities include:
- Developing and maintaining the EISP
- Leading the security operations center (SOC)
- Advising the Board on cyber risk and threat landscape
- Ensuring regulatory compliance (GDPR, HIPAA, RBI guidelines)
- Managing incident response and business continuity
- Driving security awareness training across the workforce
- Liaising with law enforcement and external auditors during breaches

**Three Lines of Defense Model (4 marks):**
This governance model, popularized by IIA (Institute of Internal Auditors), structures accountability:
- **Line 1 — Operational Management:** Business units and IT operations *own* day-to-day risks and implement controls. They are the first line of defense.
- **Line 2 — Risk Management and Compliance:** The CISO office, risk team, and compliance functions *oversee* Line 1, set policies, and challenge risk decisions.
- **Line 3 — Internal Audit:** Provides *independent assurance* to the Board and Audit Committee on the effectiveness of Lines 1 and 2.
The model ensures separation of duties, prevents conflict of interest, and creates a clear escalation path for risk issues from operational teams up to the Board.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Expected Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the Defense-in-Depth strategy. List any six layers of defense in depth. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Defense in Depth is a layered security strategy that employs multiple, overlapping controls so that the failure of one control does not result in the compromise of the entire system.

**Justification (1 mark):**
No single control is 100% effective. Layered controls provide redundancy, diversity, and time — forcing attackers to defeat multiple independent defenses.

**Six Layers (3 marks):**

| Layer | Examples |
|---|---|
| 1. Policies and Governance | EISP, ISSP, SysSP, security awareness training |
| 2. Physical Security | Locks, CCTV, mantraps, biometric access, guards |
| 3. Perimeter Network | Firewalls, IDS/IPS, DMZ, anti-DDoS appliances |
| 4. Internal Network | VLANs, NAC, 802.1X, network segmentation |
| 5. Host Endpoint | Antivirus, EDR, host firewall, OS hardening, patching |
| 6. Application and Data | WAF, input validation, encryption, DLP, backup |

**Diagram (2 marks):** See Mermaid Diagram 4.4 above — concentric layers from outermost (policies) to innermost (data).

---

**(b)** An enterprise's e-commerce server is valued at ₹20,00,000. A DDoS attack is expected to make 60% of the server unavailable for 8 hours per event. Such attacks occur 3 times per year. The proposed mitigation (Cloud DDoS Protection Service) costs ₹4,00,000 per year and reduces the exposure factor to 10%, with attack frequency reduced to 0.5 times per year. Calculate the SLE, ALE (before and after), and ROSI. Recommend whether the control should be implemented. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- AV = ₹20,00,000
- EF (before) = 60% = 0.60
- ARO (before) = 3 events/year
- EF (after) = 10% = 0.10
- ARO (after) = 0.5 events/year
- ACS = ₹4,00,000

**Step 1: SLE (before) (1 mark)**

$$SLE_{\text{before}} = AV \times EF_{\text{before}} = 20{,}00{,}000 \times 0.60 = 12{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 2: ALE (before) (1 mark)**

$$ALE_{\text{before}} = SLE_{\text{before}} \times ARO_{\text{before}} = 12{,}00{,}000 \times 3 = 36{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 3: SLE (after) (1 mark)**

$$SLE_{\text{after}} = AV \times EF_{\text{after}} = 20{,}00{,}000 \times 0.10 = 2{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 4: ALE (after) (1 mark)**

$$ALE_{\text{after}} = SLE_{\text{after}} \times ARO_{\text{after}} = 2{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5 = 1{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 5: ROSI (2 marks)**

$$ROSI = \dfrac{(ALE_{\text{before}} - ALE_{\text{after}}) - ACS}{ACS} \times 100\%$$

$$ROSI = \dfrac{(36{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000) - 4{,}00{,}000}{4{,}00{,}000} \times 100\%$$

$$ROSI = \dfrac{31{,}00{,}000}{4{,}00{,}000} \times 100\% = 775\%$$

**Recommendation (1 mark):**
ROSI of **775%** is exceptionally high. The Cloud DDoS Protection Service should be **implemented immediately** as it delivers significant financial return and also reduces reputational risk, customer churn, and regulatory exposure.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing the policy hierarchy**: Students frequently mix up EISP, ISSP, and SysSP. Remember the cascade: **Strategic (EISP) → Tactical (ISSP) → Operational (SysSP)**.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract ACS in CBA**: The formula is $CBA = ALE_{\text{before}} - ALE_{\text{after}} - ACS$. Missing the ACS term costs 1 full mark.
> 3. **Ignoring qualitative factors in risk decisions**: Examiners expect a *one-line justification* of qualitative factors (reputation, regulation) even in numerical questions.
> 4. **Incorrect ARO units**: ARO must be expressed as **events per year**, not "events every X years". Convert: 1 event every 4 years = 0.25 ARO.
> 5. **Vague answers on CISO role**: Listing "responsible for security" is too generic. Always name **specific functions**: policy development, SOC oversight, Board advisory, regulatory compliance, incident response.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Enterprise Information Security**

- **EISP** is the **master policy** approved by the **Board/CEO**; sets the strategic tone.
- **ISSP** addresses **specific issues** (BYOD, Email, Cloud, Remote Access) for specific user groups.
- **SysSP** has two flavors: **Managerial** (purpose/scope) and **Technical** (configurations/ACLs).
- The **CISO** reports to CEO/CIO/Board and owns the security strategy end-to-end.
- The **Three Lines of Defense**: Line 1 = Operations, Line 2 = Risk/Compliance, Line 3 = Internal Audit.
- **Defense in Depth** uses at least **6–8 layers**: Policy → Physical → Perimeter → Network → Host → Application → Data → Human.
- **SLE = AV × EF**, **ALE = SLE × ARO**, **ROSI** evaluates whether a control is worth the cost.
- **CBA is negative** if ACS exceeds ALE reduction; **qualitative factors** may still justify the control.
- Security governance follows the **PDCA cycle** (Plan-Do-Check-Act) per **ISO 27001**.
- **NIST CSF** functions (Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover, Govern) align with enterprise security.
- Always state **units and assumptions** clearly in numerical answers.
- **Residual Risk** = Inherent Risk − Control Effectiveness; some risk is always accepted.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Symmetric and Asymmetric Cryptography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT418 - SECURITY ASSESSMENT AND RISK ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Symmetric and Asymmetric Cryptography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Cryptography: The Science of Secure Communication

**Cryptography** is the mathematical science of transforming plaintext (readable data) into ciphertext (unreadable, scrambled data) and back again, using mathematical algorithms and keys, to ensure confidentiality, integrity, authentication, and non-repudiation of information.

In the context of KTU's Security Assessment and Risk Analysis (PEAIT418) syllabus, cryptography forms the foundational pillar of all defensive security controls. Without cryptography, digital banking, e-commerce, VPN tunnels, and even simple WhatsApp messages would be trivially interceptable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> Cryptography is the practice and study of techniques for secure communication in the presence of adversarial behavior. It involves constructing and analyzing protocols that prevent third parties or the public from reading private messages.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Locked Treasure Box

Imagine you want to send a secret diary entry to your friend living in another city. You place the diary inside a steel box.

- **Scenario A (Symmetric Key):** You lock the box with a physical key and send the locked box via courier. To open it, your friend needs an **identical copy** of the same physical key. The problem: how do you safely deliver that key? If the courier steals it, the secret is gone.

- **Scenario B (Asymmetric Key):** Your friend gives you an **open padlock** (public key) for which only she holds the key (private key). You put the diary in the box, snap the padlock shut, and send it. Even if the courier sees the box, he cannot open it — only your friend can. The beauty: the unlocking key never travels.

This single analogy captures the entire essence of symmetric vs. asymmetric cryptography.

---

## 1.2 Symmetric Key Cryptography (Secret-Key Cryptography)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> Symmetric key cryptography is a cryptographic system where the **same secret key** is used for both encryption and decryption operations by the sender and the receiver. It is also known as **conventional cryptography**, **shared-secret cryptography**, or **private-key cryptography**.

**Mathematical Representation:**

$$ C = E_K(M) \quad \text{and} \quad M = D_K(C) $$

Where:
- $M$ = Plaintext message
- $C$ = Ciphertext
- $E_K$ = Encryption algorithm using key $K$
- $D_K$ = Decryption algorithm using key $K$
- $K$ = The same secret key for both operations

### Key Characteristics
- **Key Length:** Typically **128, 192, or 256 bits** (in modern AES)
- **Speed:** **Extremely fast** — 100x to 1000x faster than asymmetric
- **Key Distribution Problem:** Major weakness — secure channel needed for key sharing
- **Scalability Issue:** $n$ users require $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ unique keys (in pure symmetric systems)

### Two Sub-Categories of Symmetric Cryptography

**A) Block Ciphers** — Operate on fixed-size blocks of plaintext (typically 64 or 128 bits)
- Examples: **DES, 3DES (Triple DES), AES (Advanced Encryption Standard), Blowfish, Twofish**

**B) Stream Ciphers** — Operate on one bit or one byte at a time
- Examples: **RC4, Salsa20, ChaCha20, A5/1 (GSM)**

---

## 1.3 Asymmetric Key Cryptography (Public-Key Cryptography)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> Asymmetric key cryptography is a cryptographic system that uses a **mathematically related pair of keys** — a public key (freely distributed) for encryption, and a private key (kept secret) for decryption. The public key cannot feasibly derive the private key, ensuring security even over open channels.

**Mathematical Representation:**

$$ C = E_{K_{pub}}(M) \quad \text{and} \quad M = D_{K_{priv}}(C) $$

Where:
- $K_{pub}$ = Public key (advertised openly, e.g., in X.509 certificates)
- $K_{priv}$ = Private key (held only by the owner)
- Mathematical relation: $K_{priv} \neq K_{pub}$ but $f(K_{pub}) \leftrightarrow K_{priv}$

### Key Characteristics
- **Key Length:** Typically **2048, 3072, or 4096 bits** (RSA) or **256 bits** (ECC)
- **Speed:** **Computationally slow** — 1000x slower than symmetric
- **Solves Key Distribution:** No need for pre-shared secret
- **Enables Digital Signatures:** Provides authentication + non-repudiation
- **Scalability:** $n$ users require only $2n$ keys total

### Foundational Asymmetric Algorithms
- **RSA** (Rivest–Shamir–Adleman, 1977) — based on integer factorization
- **Diffie–Hellman** (1976) — based on discrete logarithm problem
- **ECC** (Elliptic Curve Cryptography) — based on elliptic curve discrete logarithm
- **DSA, ElGamal, Rabin** — other notable systems

---

## 1.4 Why Both Exist? The Need for Hybrid Cryptography

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:**
> Pure asymmetric systems are too slow for bulk data. Pure symmetric systems have an unresolvable key distribution problem. Real-world systems (TLS/HTTPS, PGP, SSH) use **hybrid cryptography** — asymmetric crypto safely exchanges a symmetric key, and symmetric crypto encrypts the actual data.

**Hybrid Encryption Workflow (TLS 1.3 Simplified):**
1. Client generates a random **symmetric session key** $K_s$
2. Client encrypts $K_s$ using server's **public key** (asymmetric step)
3. Server decrypts using its **private key** to recover $K_s$
4. Both parties use $K_s$ for fast symmetric encryption (AES-GCM)

---

## 1.5 Key Cryptographic Security Metrics (Bold Constants)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry-Standard Security Strengths (NIST SP 800-57):**
> - **AES-128** = **128-bit** security level
> - **AES-256** = **256-bit** security level
> - **RSA-2048** = **112-bit** equivalent security
> - **RSA-3072** = **128-bit** equivalent security
> - **ECC-256** = **128-bit** equivalent security
> - **DES** = **56-bit** (broken; deprecated since 2005)
> - **3DES** = **112-bit** (deprecated by NIST in 2024)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanics of Symmetric Cryptography

### 2.1.1 The Feistel Cipher Structure (Foundation of DES)

Most block ciphers (DES, Blowfish, CAST-128) are built on the **Feistel network**, proposed by Horst Feistel at IBM in 1973. The plaintext is split into two halves ($L_0$ and $R_0$), and multiple rounds of substitution and permutation are applied.

**Feistel Round Operation:**

$$ L_{i+1} = R_i $$
$$ R_{i+1} = L_i \oplus F(R_i, K_i) $$

Where:
- $L_i, R_i$ = Left and right halves in round $i$
- $F$ = Round function (the only non-linear part)
- $K_i$ = Subkey for round $i$, derived from main key $K$
- $\oplus$ = Bitwise XOR operation

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Feistel Structure?**
> Decryption uses the **same algorithm** as encryption, but with subkeys applied in **reverse order**. This was historically crucial because it meant only one algorithm needed to be implemented in hardware.

### 2.1.2 Data Encryption Standard (DES) — Historical Context

- **Block size:** **64 bits**
- **Key size:** **56 bits** (8 parity bits stripped from 64-bit key)
- **Rounds:** **16 Feistel rounds**
- **Status:** **DEPRECATED** — broken in 1998 by EFF's "Deep Crack" in 56 hours
- **Modern replacement:** AES

### 2.1.3 Triple DES (3DES / TDEA)

Applies DES three times with two or three different keys:

$$ C = E_{K_1}(D_{K_2}(E_{K_3}(M))) $$

- **Effective key size:** **112 bits** (with 2 keys) or **168 bits** (with 3 keys)
- **Status:** **Deprecated by NIST in 2024** for all new applications
- **Use case:** Legacy banking systems (still found in ATMs)

### 2.1.4 Advanced Encryption Standard (AES) — Current Gold Standard

Selected by NIST in 2001 (Rijndael cipher by Vincent Rijmen & Joan Daemen).

| AES Variant | Key Size | Block Size | Rounds |
|-------------|----------|------------|--------|
| AES-128     | 128 bits | 128 bits   | 10     |
| AES-192     | 192 bits | 128 bits   | 12     |
| AES-256     | 256 bits | 128 bits   | 14     |

**AES Internal Operations (per round):**
1. **SubBytes** — Non-linear byte substitution using S-box
2. **ShiftRows** — Cyclic shift of row bytes
3. **MixColumns** — Matrix multiplication in GF($2^8$)
4. **AddRoundKey** — XOR with round subkey

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AES operates on a 4×4 byte matrix called the "State"**. Unlike Feistel, AES uses a **substitution-permutation network (SPN)**, requiring a separate decryption algorithm (inverse cipher).

### 2.1.5 Stream Ciphers & XOR Operation

Stream ciphers generate a **keystream** $K$ and combine it with plaintext via XOR:

$$ C_i = M_i \oplus K_i \quad \text{and} \quad M_i = C_i \oplus K_i $$

**Critical property:** $\oplus$ is its own inverse. The keystream must be **unpredictable** and **never reused** (the "two-time pad" attack breaks reuse).

---

## 2.2 Operational Mechanics of Asymmetric Cryptography

### 2.2.1 RSA Algorithm — The Workhorse of Public-Key Crypto

**Key Generation Steps:**
1. Choose two large primes: $p$ and $q$ (each $\geq$ 1024 bits in modern RSA)
2. Compute modulus: $n = p \times q$
3. Compute Euler's totient: $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$
4. Choose public exponent: $e$ such that $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$ (commonly $e = 65537$)
5. Compute private exponent: $d \equiv e^{-1} \pmod{\phi(n)}$, i.e., $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$

**Public key:** $(e, n)$  
**Private key:** $(d, n)$

**Encryption (with public key):**

$$ C = M^e \mod n $$

**Decryption (with private key):**

$$ M = C^d \mod n $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mathematical Foundation:**
> RSA's security relies on the **Integer Factorization Problem** — given $n$, finding $p$ and $q$ is computationally infeasible for large primes. This is the **one-way function** underpinning RSA.

### 2.2.2 Diffie–Hellman Key Exchange (DHKE)

Allows two parties to establish a **shared secret** over an insecure channel — does NOT encrypt data directly.

**Protocol Steps:**
1. Public parameters: prime $p$, generator $g$ of $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$
2. Alice picks private $a$, computes public $A = g^a \mod p$
3. Bob picks private $b$, computes public $B = g^b \mod p$
4. Exchange $A$ and $B$
5. Alice computes: $S = B^a \mod p$
6. Bob computes: $S = A^b \mod p$
7. Both arrive at: $S = g^{ab} \mod p$

**Shared Secret:**

$$ S = g^{ab} \mod p $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diffie–Hellman is vulnerable to Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) attacks** without authentication. Modern TLS uses **ECDHE** (Elliptic Curve DH Ephemeral) for forward secrecy, authenticated via digital signatures.

### 2.2.3 Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC)

Operates on elliptic curves of the form:

$$ y^2 = x^3 + ax + b \pmod{p} $$

Over a finite field $\mathbb{F}_p$. The "hard problem" is the **Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP)**.

**ECC Advantage Table:**

| Security Level | RSA Key Size | ECC Key Size |
|----------------|--------------|--------------|
| 80-bit         | 1024 bits    | 160 bits     |
| 128-bit        | 3072 bits    | 256 bits     |
| 192-bit        | 7680 bits    | 384 bits     |
| 256-bit        | 15360 bits   | 512 bits     |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world ECC usage:** Bitcoin and Ethereum use **secp256k1** curve. TLS 1.3 mandates **X25519** (Curve25519) for key exchange.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Quick-Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table summarizes ALL high-yield formulas for exam preparation. Memorize these.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Used In |
|---|-------------------|-------------|---------|
| 1 | $C = E_K(M)$, $M = D_K(C)$ | Symmetric encryption/decryption | DES, AES |
| 2 | $C = M^e \mod n$, $M = C^d \mod n$ | RSA encryption/decryption | RSA |
| 3 | $n = p \times q$ | RSA modulus | RSA |
| 4 | $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ | Euler's totient for RSA | RSA |
| 5 | $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$ | RSA key relation | RSA |
| 6 | $S = g^{ab} \mod p$ | Diffie–Hellman shared secret | DHKE |
| 7 | $L_{i+1} = R_i$, $R_{i+1} = L_i \oplus F(R_i, K_i)$ | Feistel round | DES |
| 8 | $C_i = M_i \oplus K_i$ | Stream cipher (XOR) | RC4, OTP |
| 9 | $C = E_{K_{pub}}(K_s)$ then AES-$K_s$(data) | Hybrid encryption | TLS, PGP |
| 10 | $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ symmetric keys needed | Pure symmetric scalability | Key management |

> [!WARNING]
> **No vertical pipes `|` inside table cells.** This rule prevents markdown rendering bugs. Always use `\vert` for "divides" symbol in exam answers.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Domain | Symmetric Use | Asymmetric Use |
|--------|---------------|----------------|
| **HTTPS / TLS** | AES-GCM bulk data encryption | RSA/ECDHE key exchange + digital signatures |
| **WiFi (WPA3)** | AES-CCMP / GCMP for data | ECDH for 4-way handshake |
| **Banking (ATMs)** | 3DES PIN block encryption | RSA for secure key loading to HSMs |
| **Blockchain** | AES-256 wallet encryption | secp256k1 ECDSA for transactions |
| **Disk Encryption (BitLocker)** | AES-XTS full disk | RSA for TPM key unwrapping |
| **SSH** | ChaCha20/AES session | RSA/Ed25519 host authentication |
| **PGP / S/MIME Email** | AES for message body | RSA for key encryption + signing |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 RSA Worked Example (Exhaustive Derivation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the single most important numerical problem in the KTU 2024 scheme. Practice the full derivation.**

### Problem Statement
Generate an RSA key pair with $p = 61$, $q = 53$, and public exponent $e = 17$. Then encrypt the message $M = 42$ and decrypt to verify.

### Step 1: Compute the Modulus $n$

$$ n = p \times q $$

$$ n = 61 \times 53 $$

We perform long multiplication explicitly:
$$ 61 \times 53 = 61 \times 50 + 61 \times 3 $$
$$ = 3050 + 183 $$
$$ = 3233 $$

**Therefore:** $n = 3233$

### Step 2: Compute Euler's Totient $\phi(n)$

For RSA where $n = p \times q$ (product of two distinct primes), Euler's totient is:

$$ \phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1) $$

Substituting:
$$ \phi(n) = (61-1)(53-1) $$
$$ = 60 \times 52 $$

We compute $60 \times 52$:
$$ 60 \times 52 = 60 \times 50 + 60 \times 2 $$
$$ = 3000 + 120 $$
$$ = 3120 $$

**Therefore:** $\phi(n) = 3120$

### Step 3: Verify Public Exponent Validity

We require $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$. Given $e = 17$ and $\phi(n) = 3120$:

$$ 3120 = 183 \times 17 + 9 $$
$$ 17 = 1 \times 9 + 8 $$
$$ 9 = 1 \times 8 + 1 $$
$$ 8 = 8 \times 1 + 0 $$

The Euclidean algorithm gives $\gcd(17, 3120) = 1$. ✓ **Valid.**

### Step 4: Compute Private Exponent $d$

We need $d$ such that:
$$ e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)} $$
$$ 17 \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{3120} $$

Use the Extended Euclidean Algorithm. We back-substitute:

From the steps above:
$$ 1 = 9 - 1 \times 8 $$
$$ 1 = 9 - 1 \times (17 - 1 \times 9) = 2 \times 9 - 1 \times 17 $$
$$ 1 = 2 \times (3120 - 183 \times 17) - 1 \times 17 $$
$$ 1 = 2 \times 3120 - 366 \times 17 - 1 \times 17 $$
$$ 1 = 2 \times 3120 - 367 \times 17 $$

Therefore:
$$ -367 \times 17 \equiv 1 \pmod{3120} $$

To get a positive $d$:
$$ d = -367 \pmod{3120} $$
$$ d = 3120 - 367 $$
$$ d = 2753 $$

**Verification:** $17 \times 2753 = 46801$. Now $46801 \mod 3120$:
$$ 3120 \times 15 = 46800 $$
$$ 46801 - 46800 = 1 $$ ✓

**Therefore:** $d = 2753$

### Step 5: Encrypt Message $M = 42$

Encryption formula:
$$ C = M^e \mod n $$

$$ C = 42^{17} \mod 3233 $$

We use repeated squaring to avoid huge numbers. Compute $42^1, 42^2, 42^4, 42^8, 42^{16} \pmod{3233}$:

$42^1 = 42$

$42^2 = 1764$

$42^4 = 1764^2 = 3,111,696$. Now $3,111,696 \mod 3233$:
$$ 3233 \times 962 = 3,110,146 $$
$$ 3,111,696 - 3,110,146 = 1550 $$
So $42^4 \equiv 1550 \pmod{3233}$

$42^8 = 1550^2 = 2,402,500$. Now $2,402,500 \mod 3233$:
$$ 3233 \times 743 = 2,402,119 $$
$$ 2,402,500 - 2,402,119 = 381 $$
So $42^8 \equiv 381 \pmod{3233}$

$42^{16} = 381^2 = 145,161$. Now $145,161 \mod 3233$:
$$ 3233 \times 44 = 142,252 $$
$$ 145,161 - 142,252 = 2909 $$
So $42^{16} \equiv 2909 \pmod{3233}$

Now $17 = 16 + 1$, so:
$$ 42^{17} = 42^{16} \times 42^1 \equiv 2909 \times 42 \pmod{3233} $$
$$ = 122,178 $$

Reduce modulo 3233:
$$ 3233 \times 37 = 119,621 $$
$$ 122,178 - 119,621 = 2557 $$

**Therefore:** $C = 2557$

### Step 6: Decrypt Ciphertext $C = 2557$

Decryption formula:
$$ M = C^d \mod n $$
$$ M = 2557^{2753} \mod 3233 $$

For brevity (this exponentiation is enormous), we use Euler's theorem which guarantees correctness. The mathematical result is:

$$ M = 42 \pmod{3233} $$

This matches the original plaintext, confirming the RSA round-trip integrity. ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Real RSA-2048 uses random 1024-bit primes, making $n$ 2048 bits. Manual computation is impossible — the same modular exponentiation algorithm is used, just at vastly larger scales.**

---

## 3.2 Diffie–Hellman Worked Example

**Given:** $p = 23$, $g = 5$, Alice's private $a = 6$, Bob's private $b = 15$.

### Step 1: Alice Computes Public Value

$$ A = g^a \mod p $$
$$ A = 5^6 \mod 23 $$

Compute $5^2 = 25 \equiv 2 \pmod{23}$
Compute $5^4 = 2^2 = 4 \pmod{23}$
Compute $5^6 = 5^4 \times 5^2 = 4 \times 2 = 8 \pmod{23}$

**Therefore:** $A = 8$

### Step 2: Bob Computes Public Value

$$ B = g^b \mod p $$
$$ B = 5^{15} \mod 23 $$

We already have $5^4 = 4$, $5^8 = 4^2 = 16 \pmod{23}$

$5^{15} = 5^8 \times 5^4 \times 5^2 \times 5^1$
$$ = 16 \times 4 \times 2 \times 5 \pmod{23} $$

Compute stepwise:
$16 \times 4 = 64 \equiv 64 - 2(23) = 64 - 46 = 18 \pmod{23}$
$18 \times 2 = 36 \equiv 36 - 23 = 13 \pmod{23}$
$13 \times 5 = 65 \equiv 65 - 2(23) = 65 - 46 = 19 \pmod{23}$

**Therefore:** $B = 19$

### Step 3: Alice Computes Shared Secret

$$ S = B^a \mod p $$
$$ S = 19^6 \mod 23 $$

Compute powers of 19 mod 23:
$19^2 = 361$. $361 / 23 = 15.69...$, so $23 \times 15 = 345$, $361 - 345 = 16$. Thus $19^2 \equiv 16 \pmod{23}$.
$19^4 = 16^2 = 256 \pmod{23}$. $256 / 23 = 11.13...$, $23 \times 11 = 253$, $256 - 253 = 3$. So $19^4 \equiv 3 \pmod{23}$.
$19^6 = 19^4 \times 19^2 = 3 \times 16 = 48 \pmod{23}$. $48 - 23 = 25$, $25 - 23 = 2$. So $19^6 \equiv 2 \pmod{23}$.

**Therefore:** $S_{Alice} = 2$

### Step 4: Bob Computes Shared Secret (Verification)

$$ S = A^b \mod p $$
$$ S = 8^{15} \mod 23 $$

$8^2 = 64 \equiv 64 - 2(23) = 18 \pmod{23}$
$8^4 = 18^2 = 324 \pmod{23}$. $23 \times 14 = 322$, $324 - 322 = 2$. So $8^4 \equiv 2 \pmod{23}$.
$8^8 = 2^2 = 4 \pmod{23}$

$8^{15} = 8^8 \times 8^4 \times 8^2 \times 8^1 = 4 \times 2 \times 18 \times 8 \pmod{23}$

Compute stepwise:
$4 \times 2 = 8$
$8 \times 18 = 144 \pmod{23}$. $23 \times 6 = 138$, $144 - 138 = 6$.
$6 \times 8 = 48 \equiv 2 \pmod{23}$

**Therefore:** $S_{Bob} = 2$ ✓

**Both parties share the same secret $S = 2$.**

---

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Property | Symmetric (AES) | Asymmetric (RSA) |
|----------|-----------------|-------------------|
| **Key Count (n users)** | $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | $2n$ |
| **Encryption Speed** | ~1 GB/sec (hardware) | ~1000 ops/sec |
| **Key Length (equivalent 128-bit security)** | 128 bits | 3072 bits |
| **Confidentiality** | ✓ | ✓ |
| **Authentication** | Limited (needs MAC) | ✓ (digital signatures) |
| **Non-repudiation** | ✗ | ✓ |
| **Key Distribution** | Hard problem | Easy (public key) |
| **Math Basis** | Substitution, permutation | Number theory, factoring |
| **Algorithm Examples** | AES, 3DES, ChaCha20 | RSA, ECC, DSA |
| **Used For** | Bulk data encryption | Key exchange, signatures |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Symmetric Cryptography — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Plaintext M] --> B[Encryption Algorithm E]
    K1[Secret Key K] --> B
    B --> C[Ciphertext C]
    C --> D[Insecure Channel]
    D --> E[Decryption Algorithm D]
    K1 -.->|Same K| E
    E --> F[Plaintext M]
    
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style F fill:#e1f5ff
    style C fill:#ffe1e1
    style K1 fill:#fff4e1
    style B fill:#d4f4dd
    style E fill:#d4f4dd
    style D fill:#f0f0f0
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The single secret key $K$ is the critical vulnerability point. If compromised, both encryption and decryption are broken.

---

## 4.2 Asymmetric Cryptography — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Plaintext M] --> B[Encryption with Public Key]
    PK[Public Key of Bob] --> B
    B --> C[Ciphertext C]
    C --> D[Insecure Channel]
    D --> E[Decryption with Private Key]
    SK[Private Key of Bob] --> E
    E --> F[Plaintext M]
    
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style F fill:#e1f5ff
    style C fill:#ffe1e1
    style PK fill:#d4f4dd
    style SK fill:#fff4e1
    style B fill:#d4f4dd
    style E fill:#d4f4dd
    style D fill:#f0f0f0
```

---

## 4.3 Hybrid Cryptography — TLS-Style Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Session Begins]) --> A1[Client Generates Random Symmetric Key Ks]
    A1 --> A2[Retrieve Server Public Key from Certificate]
    A2 --> A3[Encrypt Ks using Server Public Key]
    A3 --> A4[Send Encrypted Ks to Server]
    A4 --> B1[Server Decrypts using Private Key]
    B1 --> B2[Both Parties now share Ks]
    B2 --> C1[Bulk Data Exchange using AES with Ks]
    C1 --> End([Session Ends, Ks Discarded])
    
    style A1 fill:#d4f4dd
    style B1 fill:#d4f4dd
    style C1 fill:#fff4e1
    style A3 fill:#ffe1e1
    style B2 fill:#e1f5ff
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Note:** This is exactly how HTTPS, SSH, and PGP operate. The asymmetric crypto is used **only once** to bootstrap a symmetric session, then AES-256-GCM encrypts the entire session at gigabit speeds.

---

## 4.4 RSA Key Generation & Operation Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Select Large Primes p and q] --> P2[Compute n = p x q]
    P2 --> P3[Compute phi n = p-1 x q-1]
    P3 --> P4[Choose e with gcd e, phi n = 1]
    P4 --> P5[Compute d such that e x d mod phi n = 1]
    P5 --> P6[Public Key: e, n]
    P5 --> P7[Private Key: d, n]
    P6 --> OP1[Encryption: C = M to the e mod n]
    P7 --> OP2[Decryption: M = C to the d mod n]
    OP1 --> OP3[Digital Signature: S = M to the d mod n]
    OP2 --> OP4[Verification: M = S to the e mod n]
    
    style P1 fill:#e1f5ff
    style P6 fill:#d4f4dd
    style P7 fill:#fff4e1
    style OP1 fill:#ffe1e1
    style OP2 fill:#d4f4dd
```

---

## 4.5 Symmetric vs Asymmetric — Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q{Need to encrypt data?} --> Q1{Is it bulk data?}
    Q1 -->|Yes, large volumes| Rec1[Use SYMMETRIC: AES-256-GCM]
    Q1 -->|No, small key| Q2{Need authentication or signatures?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Rec2[Use ASYMMETRIC: RSA-2048 or ECC-256]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Communicating with unknown party?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| Rec3[Use HYBRID: Asymmetric key exchange + Symmetric data]
    Q3 -->|No, pre-shared secret exists| Rec1
    
    style Rec1 fill:#d4f4dd
    style Rec2 fill:#fff4e1
    style Rec3 fill:#e1f5ff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked "which cryptography to use," always justify with three criteria — speed, key distribution, and required security services (confidentiality, authentication, non-repudiation).
