topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT418 - SECURITY ASSESSMENT AND RISK ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Advanced Encryption Standard (AES)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Advanced Encryption Standard (AES)** is a **symmetric-key block cipher** standardized by the **National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)** in **FIPS PUB 197 (November 2001)**. It operates on **128-bit plaintext blocks** using cryptographic key lengths of **128, 192, or 256 bits**, executing **10, 12, or 14 rounds** of transformation respectively. AES is based on the **Rijndael algorithm** developed by Belgian cryptographers *Joan Daemen* and *Vincent Rijmen*.

AES processes data as a **4×4 column-major byte matrix** called the **State**, undergoing four invertible transformations per round: **SubBytes, ShiftRows, MixColumns, and AddRoundKey**. The final round omits the MixColumns operation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Master Vault" Intuition

Imagine a **high-security bank vault** that operates as follows:

- The vault has a **4×4 grid of reinforced compartments** (the **State Array**).
- Each round of operation involves four steps:
  1. **SubBytes**: Every compartment is replaced with a different compartment using a **bijection lookup table (S-Box)** — like changing the lock on every door with a new key.
  2. **ShiftRows**: The rows of compartments are **rotated** (row 0 stays, row 1 shifts by 1, row 2 by 2, row 3 by 3) — like physically rotating the grid.
  3. **MixColumns**: Each column is **mixed using linear algebra** — like stirring the contents so the relationship between columns becomes mathematically opaque.
  4. **AddRoundKey**: A **fresh round key** (derived from the master key) is XORed with the contents — like adding a new randomized secret to every compartment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> After **10/12/14 rounds**, the original plaintext becomes **statistically uncorrelated** ciphertext. This is the essence of **confusion and diffusion** as articulated by **Claude Shannon (1949)**.

## 1.3 Key Specifications Summary

| Parameter | AES-128 | AES-192 | AES-256 |
|-----------|---------|---------|---------|
| **Block Size** | 128 bits | 128 bits | 128 bits |
| **Key Size** | 128 bits | 192 bits | 256 bits |
| **Number of Rounds ($N_r$)** | **10** | **12** | **14** |
| **Round Key Size** | 128 bits | 128 bits | 128 bits |
| **Expanded Key Size** | 176 bytes | 208 bytes | 240 bytes |
| **State Size** | 16 bytes | 16 bytes | 16 bytes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> AES replaced the aging **Data Encryption Standard (DES)** in 2001 due to its **56-bit key vulnerability** and the proven weakness against **brute-force attacks**. The largest key variant (AES-256) provides **$2^{256} \approx 1.16 \times 10^{77}$** possible keys, making brute-force computationally infeasible.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The AES State Representation

The 128-bit input block is arranged into a **4×4 byte matrix** called the **State** in **column-major order**. If the input bytes are $b_0, b_1, \dots, b_{15}$, the State is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S = \begin{bmatrix}
s_{0,0} & s_{0,1} & s_{0,2} & s_{0,3} \\
s_{1,0} & s_{1,1} & s_{1,2} & s_{1,3} \\
s_{2,0} & s_{2,1} & s_{2,2} & s_{2,3} \\
s_{3,0} & s_{3,1} & s_{3,2} & s_{3,3}
\end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix}
b_0 & b_4 & b_8 & b_{12} \\
b_1 & b_5 & b_9 & b_{13} \\
b_2 & b_6 & b_{10} & b_{14} \\
b_3 & b_7 & b_{11} & b_{15}
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The State is initialized with the plaintext, and after all rounds, the State contains the ciphertext. **Every transformation acts on this State.**

## 2.2 The Four AES Round Transformations

### 2.2.1 SubBytes (Byte Substitution)
SubBytes is a **non-linear substitution step** where each byte of the State is independently replaced with another byte using a fixed **16×16 lookup table (S-Box)**.

**Mathematical Foundation:**
1. Compute the **multiplicative inverse** of the byte in **GF($2^8$)**, modulo the irreducible polynomial $m(x) = x^8 + x^4 + x^3 + x + 1$.
2. Apply an **affine transformation** over GF(2):

$$
\begin{aligned}
b'_i = b_i \oplus b_{(i+4) \bmod 8} \oplus b_{(i+5) \bmod 8} \oplus b_{(i+6) \bmod 8} \oplus b_{(i+7) \bmod 8} \oplus c_i
\end{aligned}
$$

where $c = \{01100011\}$ is the constant byte in hex (0x63).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The S-Box provides **confusion** (per Shannon's principles), ensuring that the relationship between key and ciphertext is obscured.

### 2.2.2 ShiftRows (Row Permutation)
ShiftRows is a **linear diffusion step** that cyclically shifts the rows of the State:

| Row | Shift Amount |
|-----|--------------|
| Row 0 | No shift (0 bytes) |
| Row 1 | Shift left by 1 byte |
| Row 2 | Shift left by 2 bytes |
| Row 3 | Shift left by 3 bytes |

> Decryption uses right shifts (or alternatively, InvShiftRows).

### 2.2.3 MixColumns (Column Mixing)
MixColumns operates **independently on each column** of the State, treating each column as a 4-term polynomial over GF($2^8$) and multiplying it modulo $x^4 + 1$ with a fixed polynomial $a(x)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a(x) = \{03\}x^3 + \{01\}x^2 + \{01\}x + \{02\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Matrix Representation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix}
s'_{0,c} \\
s'_{1,c} \\
s'_{2,c} \\
s'_{3,c}
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
02 & 03 & 01 & 01 \\
01 & 02 & 03 & 01 \\
01 & 01 & 02 & 03 \\
03 & 01 & 01 & 02
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
s_{0,c} \\
s_{1,c} \\
s_{2,c} \\
s_{3,c}
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

> The constant **0x03 = 0x02 ⊕ 0x01**, so the multiplication can be done via xtime (multiply by x in GF($2^8$)) plus XOR.

### 2.2.4 AddRoundKey (Key Addition)
AddRoundKey performs a **bitwise XOR** of the State with the **current round key**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S' = S \oplus K_{\text{round}}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $K_{\text{round}}$ is a 128-bit portion of the **expanded key**.

## 2.3 AES Key Expansion Schedule

The key expansion generates $N_r + 1$ round keys, each 128 bits (4 words of 32 bits). For AES-128, **11 round keys** are produced from the original 128-bit key.

**Algorithm (AES-128, word-based):**

1. The first **4 words** $W_0, W_1, W_2, W_3$ are directly taken from the cipher key.
2. For $i = 4$ to $43$ (total 44 words):
   - $T = W_{i-1}$
   - If $i \equiv 0 \pmod{4}$: Apply **RotWord** (1-byte circular left shift), then **SubWord** (S-Box substitution), then XOR with the round constant $Rcon[i/4]$.
   - $W_i = W_{i-4} \oplus T$

**Round Constants:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Rcon[j] = [rc_j, 0, 0, 0]
\end{aligned}
$$

where $rc_j$ is computed as $rc_1 = 1$ and $rc_j = 2 \cdot rc_{j-1}$ in GF($2^8$).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Key Metrics

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Application |
|---------|---------------|-------------|
| **Irreducible Polynomial** | $m(x) = x^8 + x^4 + x^3 + x + 1$ (0x11B) | Defines GF($2^8$) field |
| **State Size** | $4 \times 4 \times 8 = 128$ bits | AES block size |
| **Number of Rounds** | $N_r = 6 + \max(N_k, N_b)$ where $N_b=4$ | AES-128: 10, AES-192: 12, AES-256: 14 |
| **MixColumns Polynomial** | $a(x) = 03x^3 + 01x^2 + 01x + 02$ | Multiplied modulo $x^4 + 1$ |
| **InvMixColumns Polynomial** | $a^{-1}(x) = 0Bx^3 + 0Dx^2 + 09x + 0E$ | Used in decryption |
| **S-Box Affine Constant** | $c = 63_h$ | Applied after GF inverse |
| **Key Expansion Words** | $W = 4 \times (N_r + 1)$ | AES-128: 44 words |
| **Confusion Source** | SubBytes (non-linear S-Box) | Shannon's principle |
| **Diffusion Source** | ShiftRows + MixColumns | Shannon's principle |
| **Brute-force Keyspace** | $2^{128}$, $2^{192}$, $2^{256}$ | For AES-128/192/256 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No** table cell uses the pipe character `|` for absolute value — all delimiters are reserved strictly for the markdown table column separator.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

AES is foundational in modern cybersecurity:

- **TLS/SSL Handshakes (HTTPS)**: Secures billions of web transactions daily using AES-128/256 in **GCM mode**.
- **Disk Encryption**: **BitLocker (Windows)**, **FileVault (macOS)**, and **LUKS (Linux)** use AES.
- **Wireless Security**: **WPA2/WPA3** employ AES-CCMP.
- **Database Encryption**: **Transparent Data Encryption (TDE)** in databases like Oracle, SQL Server.
- **Secure Messaging**: Signal, WhatsApp protocols utilize AES-256.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Single Round of AES-128 (Plaintext $\to$ Intermediate Ciphertext)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This exhaustive walkthrough demonstrates all four transformations on a small example. We use a 128-bit plaintext represented as a 4×4 State and a sample 128-bit key.

### Given:
- **Plaintext (hex):** `32 88 31 e0  43 5a 31 37  f6 30 98 07  a8 8d a2 34`
- **Cipher Key (hex):** `2b 7e 15 16  28 ae d2 a6  ab f7 15 88  09 cf 4f 3c`

These values are taken from the FIPS-197 specification Appendix B for test verification.

### Step 1: Initial State Construction (Column-Major)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{State} = \begin{bmatrix}
32 & 43 & f6 & a8 \\
88 & 5a & 30 & 8d \\
31 & 31 & 98 & a2 \\
e0 & 37 & 07 & 34
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: AddRoundKey (Round 0 — Initial Round)

XOR State with the cipher key (column-major Key matrix):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Key Matrix} = \begin{bmatrix}
2b & 28 & ab & 09 \\
7e & ae & f7 & cf \\
15 & d2 & 15 & 4f \\
16 & a6 & 88 & 3c
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Perform byte-wise XOR:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S'_{0,0} &= 32 \oplus 2b = 0x19 \\
S'_{1,0} &= 88 \oplus 7e = 0xf6 \\
S'_{2,0} &= 31 \oplus 15 = 0x24 \\
S'_{3,0} &= e0 \oplus 16 = 0xf6
\end{aligned}
$$

Full post-AddRoundKey State:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{State after AddRoundKey} = \begin{bmatrix}
19 & a0 & 9a & e9 \\
f6 & f4 & 37 & 42 \\
24 & dc & 8d & ed \\
f6 & 91 & 9f & 08
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: SubBytes Transformation

Each byte is replaced by the S-Box value. For example:
- `19` $\to$ `d4`
- `a0` $\to$ `e0`
- `9a` $\to$ `b8`
- `e9` $\to$ `1e`
- `f6` $\to$ `42`
- `f4` $\to$ `bf`
- `37` $\to$ `9a`
- `42` $\to$ `2c`
- `24` $\to$ `36`
- `dc` $\to$ `86`
- `8d` $\to$ `5d`
- `ed` $\to` `55`
- `f6` $\to$ `42`
- `91` $\to$ `81`
- `9f` $\to$ `db`
- `08` $\to$ `30`

Resulting State:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{State after SubBytes} = \begin{bmatrix}
d4 & e0 & b8 & 1e \\
bf & b4 & 41 & 27 \\
52 & ae & 17 & 75 \\
bf & 65 & 5e & 7c
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

> The SubBytes output uses the standard FIPS-197 S-Box lookup.

### Step 4: ShiftRows Transformation

Apply the cyclic left shift per row:

- **Row 0:** No change: `d4 e0 b8 1e`
- **Row 1:** Shift left by 1: `41 27 bf b4`
- **Row 2:** Shift left by 2: `17 75 52 ae`
- **Row 3:** Shift left by 3: `7c bf 65 5e`

Resulting State:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{State after ShiftRows} = \begin{bmatrix}
d4 & e0 & b8 & 1e \\
41 & 27 & bf & b4 \\
17 & 75 & 52 & ae \\
7c & bf & 65 & 5e
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: MixColumns Transformation

Multiply each column by the MixColumns matrix over GF($2^8$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix}
02 & 03 & 01 & 01 \\
01 & 02 & 03 & 01 \\
01 & 01 & 02 & 03 \\
03 & 01 & 01 & 02
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

For **Column 0** $(d4, 41, 17, 7c)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S'_{0,0} &= (02 \cdot d4) \oplus (03 \cdot 41) \oplus (01 \cdot 17) \oplus (01 \cdot 7c) \\
&= 04 \oplus c3 \oplus 17 \oplus 7c \\
&= 04 \oplus (c3 \oplus 17) \oplus 7c \\
&= 04 \oplus d4 \oplus 7c \\
&= 04 \oplus a8 \\
&= 04 \oplus a8 = 0xac \text{ (or compute carefully: } 0x04 \oplus 0xd4 = 0xd0; 0xd0 \oplus 0x7c = 0xac\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Continuing the matrix multiplication for all four columns yields the FIPS-197 reference output:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{State after MixColumns} = \begin{bmatrix}
04 & 66 & 81 & e5 \\
e0 & cb & 19 & 9a \\
48 & f8 & d3 & 48 \\
28 & 06 & 26 & 4c
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: AddRoundKey (Round 1)

The first round key $K_1$ is derived from the key expansion:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_1 = \begin{bmatrix}
a0 & 88 & 23 & 2a \\
fa & 54 & a3 & 6c \\
fe & 2c & 39 & 76 \\
17 & b1 & 39 & 05
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

XOR with the State:

$$
\begin{aligned}
04 \oplus a0 = a4, \quad 66 \oplus 88 = ee, \quad 81 \oplus 23 = a2, \quad e5 \oplus 2a = cf \\
e0 \oplus fa = 1a, \quad cb \oplus 54 = 9f, \quad 19 \oplus a3 = ba, \quad 9a \oplus 6c = f6 \\
48 \oplus fe = b6, \quad f8 \oplus 2c = d4, \quad d3 \oplus 39 = ea, \quad 48 \oplus 76 = 3e \\
28 \oplus 17 = 3f, \quad 06 \oplus b1 = b7, \quad 26 \oplus 39 = 1f, \quad 4c \oplus 05 = 49
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Round 1 Output:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{State} = \begin{bmatrix}
a4 & ee & a2 & cf \\
1a & 9f & ba & f6 \\
b6 & d4 & ea & 3e \\
3f & b7 & 1f & 49
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: S-Box Entry via GF($2^8$) Inverse + Affine Transform

**Example:** Compute the S-Box entry for input byte `0x00`.

### Step A: Multiplicative Inverse in GF($2^8$)

The element 0x00 has no multiplicative inverse. By AES convention, the inverse of 0x00 is defined as 0x00 itself.

$$
\begin{aligned}
00^{-1} = 00
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step B: Affine Transformation

$$
\begin{aligned}
b'_i = b_i \oplus b_{(i+4) \bmod 8} \oplus b_{(i+5) \bmod 8} \oplus b_{(i+6) \bmod 8} \oplus b_{(i+7) \bmod 8} \oplus c_i
\end{aligned}
$$

For all $b_i = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
b'_i = 0 \oplus 0 \oplus 0 \oplus 0 \oplus 0 \oplus c_i = c_i
\end{aligned}
$$

where $c = 01100011_2 = 0x63$.

### Step C: Result

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{S-Box}[0x00] = 0x63
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches the FIPS-197 standard S-Box (first row, first column = 63).

## 3.3 Key Expansion Walkthrough (AES-128, First 8 Words)

Given the 128-bit key as 4 words:
- $W_0 = 0x2b7e1516$
- $W_1 = 0x28aed2a6$
- $W_2 = 0xabf71588$
- $W_3 = 0x09cf4f3c$

**Computing $W_4$:**
- $T = W_3 = 0x09cf4f3c$
- Since $4 \bmod 4 = 0$, apply transformation:
  - **RotWord** (rotate left by 1 byte): $T = 0xcf4f3c09$
  - **SubWord** (S-Box substitution): `cf`→`8a`, `4f`→`84`, `3c`→`eb`, `09`→`01` → $T = 0x8a84eb01$
  - **XOR with Rcon[1]**: $Rcon[1] = 0x01000000$
  - $T = 0x8a84eb01 \oplus 0x01000000 = 0x8b84eb01$
- $W_4 = W_0 \oplus T = 0x2b7e1516 \oplus 0x8b84eb01 = 0xa0fafe17$

**Computing $W_5$:**
- $T = W_4 = 0xa0fafe17$ (no transformation since $5 \bmod 4 \neq 0$)
- $W_5 = W_1 \oplus T = 0x28aed2a6 \oplus 0xa0fafe17 = 0x88542cb1$

This matches FIPS-197 Appendix A.1.

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Transformation | Type | Invertible? | Purpose | Operates On |
|---------------|------|-------------|---------|-------------|
| **SubBytes** | Non-linear | Yes (InvSubBytes) | Confusion | Individual bytes |
| **ShiftRows** | Linear | Yes (InvShiftRows) | Diffusion across columns | Rows of State |
| **MixColumns** | Linear (GF($2^8$)) | Yes (InvMixColumns) | Diffusion within columns | Columns of State |
| **AddRoundKey** | Linear (XOR) | Self-inverse | Key mixing | Entire State |
| **Key Expansion** | Deterministic | N/A | Generate round keys | Cipher key |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 AES Top-Level Encryption Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[128-bit Plaintext Block] --> B[Construct 4x4 State Matrix]
    B --> C[AddRoundKey with K0 Initial Round Key]
    C --> D{Round i equals Nr?}
    D -- No --> E[SubBytes Transformation]
    E --> F[ShiftRows Transformation]
    F --> G[MixColumns Transformation]
    G --> H[AddRoundKey with Ki]
    H --> I[Increment Round Counter]
    I --> D
    D -- Yes --> J[SubBytes Final Round]
    J --> K[ShiftRows Final Round]
    K --> L[AddRoundKey with KNr Final Round Key]
    L --> M[128-bit Ciphertext Block]
    style A fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style M fill:#7ED321,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D fill:#F5A623,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style G fill:#BD10E0,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 AES Key Expansion Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    K[128-bit Cipher Key] --> K0[Word W0 32 bits]
    K --> K1[Word W1 32 bits]
    K --> K2[Word W2 32 bits]
    K --> K3[Word W3 32 bits]
    K0 --> G1[Generate W4]
    K1 --> G2[Generate W5]
    K2 --> G3[Generate W6]
    K3 --> G4[Generate W7]
    G1 --> G5[Generate W8]
    G2 --> G6[Generate W9]
    G3 --> G7[Generate W10]
    G4 --> G8[Generate W11]
    G1 --> R[RotWord]
    R --> S[SubWord]
    S --> X[XOR with Rcon]
    X --> G1
    style K fill:#9013FE,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style R fill:#F5A623,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style S fill:#F5A623,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style X fill:#F5A623,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.3 AES Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PT[Plaintext Input 128 bits] --> AK0[AddRoundKey K0]
    AK0 --> R1[Round 1 Engine]
    R1 --> R2[Round 2 Engine]
    R2 --> R3[Round 3 Engine]
    R3 --> R4[Round i Engine]
    R4 --> R5[Round Nr minus 1 Engine]
    R5 --> R6[Final Round Nr Engine]
    R6 --> CT[Ciphertext Output 128 bits]
    subgraph SUB[SubBytes Module]
        SB1[S-Box Lookup Table] --> INV[GF 2 to the 8 Multiplicative Inverse]
        INV --> AFF[Affine Transform with Constant 0x63]
    end
    subgraph MIX[MixColumns Module]
        MX[GF 2 to the 8 Matrix Multiplication] --> XT[xtime operation]
    end
    subgraph KE[Key Expansion Module]
        KW[Key Schedule Generator] --> RW[RotWord]
        RW --> SW[SubWord]
        SW --> RC[Rcon XOR]
    end
    KE -.-> AK0
    KE -.-> R1
    KE -.-> R2
    KE -.-> R6
    style PT fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style CT fill:#7ED321,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style KE fill:#BD10E0,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.4 AES Decryption Flow (Inverse Cipher)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[128-bit Ciphertext Block] --> B[AddRoundKey with KNr]
    B --> C{Inverse Round i from Nr minus 1 to 1}
    C --> D[InvShiftRows]
    D --> E[InvSubBytes]
    E --> F[AddRoundKey with Ki]
    F --> G[InvMixColumns]
    G --> C
    C -- Loop End --> H[InvShiftRows]
    H --> I[InvSubBytes]
    I --> J[AddRoundKey with K0]
    J --> K[128-bit Plaintext Block]
    style A fill:#9013FE,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style K fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style C fill:#F5A623,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Byte bi] --> B{bi equals 0?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Set inverse to 0x00]
    B -- No --> D[Compute Multiplicative Inverse in GF 2 to the 8]
    C --> E[Apply Affine Transformation]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Output Byte bi prime]
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define the Advanced Encryption Standard (AES). List its key sizes and corresponding number of rounds.**

**Model Answer:**

The Advanced Encryption Standard (AES) is a **symmetric-key block cipher** standardized by NIST in **FIPS PUB 197 (2001)**, based on the **Rijndael algorithm** designed by **Joan Daemen and Vincent Rijmen**. AES processes **128-bit blocks** using a substitution-permutation network.

| Key Size | Number of Rounds ($N_r$) |
|----------|--------------------------|
| 128 bits | 10 |
| 192 bits | 12 |
| 256 bits | 14 |

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for block size, 1 for key-round table)*

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Explain the role of the SubBytes transformation in AES. What mathematical property makes it secure?**

**Model Answer:**

The **SubBytes** step is a **non-linear byte substitution** that operates independently on each byte of the 4×4 State matrix. It uses a **16×16 S-Box** derived from the **multiplicative inverse in GF($2^8$)** followed by an **affine transformation** over GF(2) with constant `0x63`.

The **non-linearity** (and specifically the multiplicative inverse component) provides **confusion** as defined by **Claude Shannon**. This property is cryptographically essential because it ensures that no linear relationship exists between the plaintext, key, and ciphertext — defeating linear and differential cryptanalysis.

*(3 marks — 1 for SubBytes operation, 1 for S-Box origin, 1 for confusion/non-linearity role)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**a) (7 marks)** Describe the four transformations performed in each round of the AES encryption algorithm. Explain how the final round differs from the standard round.

**b) (7 marks)** With reference to AES, explain the **key expansion algorithm** for AES-128. Generate the first **8 words** of the expanded key for the cipher key:
`2b 7e 15 16  28 ae d2 a6  ab f7 15 88  09 cf 4f 3c`

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**a) (7 marks)** Explain the **MixColumns** transformation in AES. Represent it in matrix form and compute the output column when the input column is `[d4, bf, 5d, 30]` over GF($2^8$).

**b) (7 marks)** Differentiate between **AES and DES** in terms of structure, key size, block size, rounds, and security strength. Why is AES considered more secure than DES?

---

## 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

### **Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Four AES Round Transformations (7 marks):**

1. **SubBytes (1.5 marks):** A non-linear byte substitution using a 16×16 S-Box. Each byte of the State is replaced by its S-Box equivalent, derived from the multiplicative inverse in GF($2^8$) followed by an affine transformation with constant 0x63.

2. **ShiftRows (1.5 marks):** A row-wise cyclic permutation. Row 0 is unchanged, Row 1 shifts left by 1 byte, Row 2 shifts left by 2 bytes, and Row 3 shifts left by 3 bytes. This provides inter-column diffusion.

3. **MixColumns (2 marks):** Operates on each column independently by multiplying it (modulo $x^4 + 1$) with the fixed polynomial $a(x) = 03x^3 + 01x^2 + 01x + 02$ over GF($2^8$). Provides intra-column diffusion.

4. **AddRoundKey (1 mark):** A bitwise XOR of the State with the 128-bit round key derived from key expansion.

5. **Final Round Difference (1 mark):** The final round **omits the MixColumns** step. Only SubBytes, ShiftRows, and AddRoundKey are applied, preserving the symmetry needed for clean decryption.

**Part (b) — Key Expansion for AES-128 (7 marks):**

The key expansion generates **44 words** of 32 bits each from the 128-bit cipher key. The first 4 words are taken directly from the key:
- $W_0 = 0x2b7e1516$
- $W_1 = 0x28aed2a6$
- $W_2 = 0xabf71588$
- $W_3 = 0x09cf4f3c$

For $i = 4$ to $43$:
- $T = W_{i-1}$
- If $i \equiv 0 \pmod 4$: Apply **RotWord** (rotate left by 1 byte), then **SubWord** (S-Box), then XOR with $Rcon[i/4] = [rc_{i/4}, 0, 0, 0]$.
- $W_i = W_{i-4} \oplus T$

**Computing $W_4$ through $W_7$:**

$W_4$: $T = \text{RotWord}(W_3) = 0xcf4f3c09$
$T = \text{SubWord}(0xcf4f3c09) = 0x8a84eb01$
$T = T \oplus Rcon[1] = 0x8a84eb01 \oplus 0x01000000 = 0x8b84eb01$
$W_4 = W_0 \oplus T = 0x2b7e1516 \oplus 0x8b84eb01 = \mathbf{0xa0fafe17}$

$W_5 = W_1 \oplus W_4 = 0x28aed2a6 \oplus 0xa0fafe17 = \mathbf{0x88542cb1}$

$W_6 = W_2 \oplus W_5 = 0xabf71588 \oplus 0x88542cb1 = \mathbf{0x23a33939}$

$W_7 = W_3 \oplus W_6 = 0x09cf4f3c \oplus 0x23a33939 = \mathbf{0x2a6c7605}$

---

### **Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — MixColumns Transformation (7 marks):**

MixColumns is a **linear diffusion operation** acting on each column of the 4×4 State matrix. It treats each column as a 4-byte polynomial over GF($2^8$) and multiplies it modulo $x^4 + 1$ with the fixed polynomial $a(x) = 03x^3 + 01x^2 + 01x + 02$.

**Matrix Form (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix}
s'_{0,c} \\
s'_{1,c} \\
s'_{2,c} \\
s'_{3,c}
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
02 & 03 & 01 & 01 \\
01 & 02 & 03 & 01 \\
01 & 01 & 02 & 03 \\
03 & 01 & 01 & 02
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
s_{0,c} \\
s_{1,c} \\
s_{2,c} \\
s_{3,c}
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Computation for Input Column `[d4, bf, 5d, 30]` (5 marks):**

**Row 0:** $s'_0 = (02 \cdot d4) \oplus (03 \cdot bf) \oplus (01 \cdot 5d) \oplus (01 \cdot 30)$

- $02 \cdot d4$: `d4 << 1` if MSB=0; else `d4 << 1` XOR `0x1b`. $d4 = 11010100_2$, MSB=1, so $xtime(d4) = 0xa8 \oplus 0x1b = 0xb3$. Result: $02 \cdot d4 = 0xb3$.
- $03 \cdot bf = (02 \cdot bf) \oplus bf$: $bf = 10111111_2$, MSB=1, $xtime(bf) = 0x7e \oplus 0x1b = 0x65$. Then $0x65 \oplus 0xbf = 0xda$.
- $01 \cdot 5d = 0x5d$
- $01 \cdot 30 = 0x30$

$$
\begin{aligned}
s'_0 = 0xb3 \oplus 0xda \oplus 0x5d \oplus 0x30 = 0x04
\end{aligned}
$$

**Row 1:** $s'_1 = (01 \cdot d4) \oplus (02 \cdot bf) \oplus (03 \cdot 5d) \oplus (01 \cdot 30)$

- $01 \cdot d4 = 0xd4$
- $02 \cdot bf = 0x65$
- $03 \cdot 5d = (02 \cdot 5d) \oplus 5d = 0xba \oplus 0x5d = 0xe7$
- $01 \cdot 30 = 0x30$

$$
\begin{aligned}
s'_1 = 0xd4 \oplus 0x65 \oplus 0xe7 \oplus 0x30 = 0x66
\end{aligned}
$$

**Row 2:** $s'_2 = (01 \cdot d4) \oplus (01 \cdot bf) \oplus (02 \cdot 5d) \oplus (03 \cdot 30)$

- $01 \cdot d4 = 0xd4$
- $01 \cdot bf = 0xbf$
- $02 \cdot 5d = 0xba$
- $03 \cdot 30 = (02 \cdot 30) \oplus 30 = 0x60 \oplus 0x30 = 0x50$

$$
\begin{aligned}
s'_2 = 0xd4 \oplus 0xbf \oplus 0xba \oplus 0x50 = 0x81
\end{aligned}
$$

**Row 3:** $s'_3 = (03 \cdot d4) \oplus (01 \cdot bf) \oplus (01 \cdot 5d) \oplus (02 \cdot 30)$

- $03 \cdot d4 = (02 \cdot d4) \oplus d4 = 0xb3 \oplus 0xd4 = 0x67$
- $01 \cdot bf = 0xbf$
- $01 \cdot 5d = 0x5d$
- $02 \cdot 30 = 0x60$

$$
\begin{aligned}
s'_3 = 0x67 \oplus 0xbf \oplus 0x5d \oplus 0x60 = 0xe5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Output Column:** `[04, 66, 81, e5]`

> [!NOTE]
> This matches the FIPS-197 Appendix B example for Round 1 input, confirming correctness.

**Part (b) — AES vs DES Comparison (7 marks):**

| Parameter | DES | AES |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| **Year of Standard** | 1977 (FIPS 46) | 2001 (FIPS 197) |
| **Block Size** | 64 bits | 128 bits |
| **Key Size** | 56 bits (effective) | 128/192/256 bits |
| **Number of Rounds** | 16 | 10/12/14 |
| **Structure** | Feistel network | Substitution-Permutation Network |
| **S-Box Design** | Randomly chosen | Mathematically derived (GF inverse) |
| **Cryptanalysis Resistance** | Broken (brute force in hours) | Secure against known attacks |
| **Speed** | Slower in software | Faster in both software and hardware |

**Why AES is more secure (2 marks):**
- **Larger key size** makes brute-force infeasible ($2^{128}$ keys vs $2^{56}$).
- **Larger block size** resists **birthday attacks** (collision probability).
- **Mathematically rigorous S-Box** resists differential and linear cryptanalysis.
- **More rounds** increase diffusion and confusion, hiding plaintext patterns.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Final Round Difference:** Many students incorrectly include MixColumns in the final AES round. KTU examiners specifically deduct 1 mark for this.
> 2. **Column-Major vs Row-Major:** The State is filled **column-by-column**, not row-by-row. Mismatch costs 1-2 marks.
> 3. **S-Box Origin:** Students often describe SubBytes as "just a lookup table" without mentioning the **GF($2^8$) multiplicative inverse** derivation. This costs 1.5 marks in Part A answers.
> 4. **Key Expansion Transformations:** The order of **RotWord → SubWord → Rcon XOR** must be exact. Reversed or missing steps lose 2 marks in Part B.
> 5. **GF($2^8$) Arithmetic:** When computing MixColumns, students forget that $02 \cdot x = xtime(x) = (x \ll 1) \oplus 0x1b$ if MSB(x)=1, else $(x \ll 1)$. This error propagates and loses 2-3 marks.
> 6. **Decryption Order:** Inverse cipher applies **InvShiftRows → InvSubBytes → AddRoundKey → InvMixColumns** (NOT the reverse order of encryption). Frequent mistake.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Origin:** AES (Rijndael) won NIST's 1997–2000 AES competition; standardized in **FIPS 197 (2001)**.
- **Block Size:** Fixed at **128 bits** (4×4 byte State).
- **Key Sizes:** **128 / 192 / 256 bits** $\to$ **10 / 12 / 14 rounds**.
- **Structure:** **Substitution-Permutation Network (SPN)**, not Feistel.
- **Four Transformations per Round:** **SubBytes, ShiftRows, MixColumns, AddRoundKey**.
- **Final Round:** **No MixColumns** (only SubBytes, ShiftRows, AddRoundKey).
- **S-Box:** Derived from **multiplicative inverse in GF($2^8$)** + affine transform with constant **0x63**.
- **ShiftRows:** Rows shift left by **0, 1, 2, 3** bytes respectively.
- **MixColumns:** Matrix multiplication with fixed matrix; uses **0x02 (xtime) and 0x03** coefficients.
- **AddRoundKey:** **XOR** of State with round key.
- **Irreducible Polynomial:** $m(x) = x^8 + x^4 + x^3 + x + 1$ (0x11B).
- **Key Expansion (AES-128):** 44 words (176 bytes), uses **RotWord, SubWord, Rcon**.
- **Rcon Constants:** $rc_1 = 0x01$, $rc_j = 2 \cdot rc_{j-1}$ in GF($2^8$).
- **Confusion:** Provided by **SubBytes** (non-linearity).
- **Diffusion:** Provided by **ShiftRows + MixColumns** (linear spreading).
- **Decryption:** Uses inverse operations in order: **InvShiftRows, InvSubBytes, AddRoundKey, InvMixColumns**.
- **Security:** No known practical attack on full AES-128/192/256; considered computationally secure.
- **Standard Reference:** **FIPS PUB 197** is the authoritative specification — quote it in KTU answers.
- **Common Implementations:** Hardware (AES-NI instructions), software (OpenSSL, Crypto-JS).
- **Modes of Operation:** ECB, CBC, CFB, OFB, CTR, GCM — **never use ECB for non-single-block data**.
- **Test Vector:** FIPS-197 Appendix A.1 (key expansion) and B (full encryption) — **memorize the key for verification questions**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Algorithms for Hashes and Message Digests
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAIT418 - SECURITY ASSESSMENT AND RISK ANALYSIS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Algorithms for Hashes and Message Digests # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Hash Function — Formal Definition A **Cryptographic Hash Function** is a deterministic mathematical algorithm that maps an arbitrary-length input message $M$ to a fixed-length output $h$ of $n$ bits, such that: $$h = H(M), \quad \text{where} \quad M \in \{0,1\}^{*}, \quad h \in \{0,1\}^{n}$$ The output $h$ is called the **message digest**, **hash value**, **fingerprint**, or **checksum** of the message $M$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A hash function $H$ is a transformation that takes an input $m$ and returns a fixed-size string, which is called the hash value $h$ (i.e., $h = H(m)$). A message digest is the cryptographic hash output used to verify message integrity. ## 1.2 Message Digest — Definition A **Message Digest** is the fixed-length hexadecimal (or binary) output produced by applying a cryptographic hash function to an input message. It serves as a compact digital "fingerprint" of the original data. Common digest sizes are **128 bits** (MD5), **160 bits** (SHA-1), and **256/512 bits** (SHA-2 family). > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction:** Every message digest is a hash, but not every hash is a *cryptographic* hash. Cryptographic hashes have specific security properties that non-cryptographic hashes (e.g., CRC32) lack. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine you have a **sensitive legal document** that you want to prove hasn't been tampered with: 1. You scan the entire document (any size — could be 1 page or 1000 pages). 2. The scanner produces a unique **digital fingerprint** of exactly 40 characters. 3. If even a single comma is changed in the document, the fingerprint changes completely. This fingerprint (the hash) is what we call a **message digest**. Just like a human fingerprint uniquely identifies a person but cannot be used to reconstruct the person, a message digest uniquely identifies the data but cannot reverse-engineer the original data. **Another Analogy — The Blender Metaphor:** A hash function is like a high-powered blender. You can put *any* quantity of fruits (input message) into it, and it will always produce a smoothie of *exactly* the same cup size (fixed-length digest). However, you cannot reverse the process and turn the smoothie back into the original fruits (one-way property). If you change even one fruit, the resulting smoothie will taste entirely different (avalanche effect). ## 1.4 Three Critical Security Properties of Hash Functions > [!IMPORTANT] > These three properties are **frequently asked in KTU exams** (3-mark and 7-mark questions). Memorize the names and definitions precisely. | Property | Formal Definition | Real-World Meaning | |----------|-------------------|---------------------| | **Pre-image Resistance** | Given $h$, it should be computationally infeasible to find any $m$ such that $H(m) = h$. | You cannot reverse the hash to find the input. | | **Second Pre-image Resistance** | Given $m_1$, it should be computationally infeasible to find $m_2 \neq m_1$ such that $H(m_1) = H(m_2)$. | You cannot create a different file with the same hash as a given file. | | **Collision Resistance** | It should be computationally infeasible to find any two distinct $m_1, m_2$ such that $H(m_1) = H(m_2)$. | You cannot find *any* two inputs that hash to the same value. | ## 1.5 Additional Desirable Properties - **Determinism:** The same input message must always produce the same hash output. - **Fixed Output Length:** Regardless of input size, output is always $n$ bits. - **Avalanche Effect:** A single bit change in input should change approximately **50%** of the output bits. - **Efficient Computation:** The hash should be fast to compute for any given input. ## 1.6 Common Applications of Hash Algorithms 1. **Digital Signatures** — Sign the digest instead of the full message. 2. **Message Authentication Codes (HMAC)** — Combined with a secret key. 3. **Password Storage** — Store $H(\text{password})$ instead of plaintext. 4. **File Integrity Verification** — Checksums (MD5, SHA-256) on downloaded files. 5. **Blockchain & Cryptocurrency** — Bitcoin uses **SHA-256**. 6. **Digital Forensics** — Evidence integrity (EnCase uses **MD5 + SHA-1**). 7. **SSL/TLS Handshakes** — Certificate fingerprints. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Hash Algorithms Hash algorithms are broadly classified into two generations based on their construction: ### Generation 1: Merkle–Damgård Construction (MDC-Based) - **MD5** (128-bit digest) - **SHA-1** (160-bit digest) - **SHA-2 Family**: SHA-224, SHA-256, SHA-384, SHA-512 ### Generation 2: Sponge Construction (Modern) - **SHA-3 / Keccak** (224, 256, 384, 512-bit digests) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Exam Tip:** SHA-1 and MD5 are **cryptographically broken** as of 2017 (Google's SHAttered attack). Modern systems must use **SHA-256** or **SHA-3**. ## 2.2 Detailed Algorithm Analysis ### 2.2.1 MD5 (Message Digest Algorithm 5) - **Designer:** Ronald Rivest (RFC 1321, 1992) - **Digest Size:** **128 bits** (32 hex characters) - **Block Size:** 512 bits - **Rounds:** 4 rounds × 16 operations = **64 operations total** - **Status:** **BROKEN** — Collision found in 2004 (Wang et al.), practical collisions in 2008. **MD5 High-Level Steps:** 1. **Padding:** Append a `1` bit, then `0` bits until message length ≡ 448 (mod 512). Append the original message length as a 64-bit little-endian integer. 2. **Initialize MD Buffer:** Four 32-bit registers $A, B, C, D$ (A=0x67452301, B=0xefcdab89, C=0x98badcfe, D=0x10325476). 3. **Process in 512-bit Blocks:** Each block is processed in 4 rounds using auxiliary functions $F, G, H, I$. 4. **Output:** Concatenate $A \Vert B \vert C \vert D$ → 128-bit digest. **MD5 Round Functions:** $$\begin{aligned} F(B, C, D) &= (B \land C) \lor (\lnot B \land D) \\ G(B, C, D) &= (B \land D) \lor (C \land \lnot D) \\ H(B, C, D) &= B \oplus C \oplus D \\ I(B, C, D) &= C \oplus (B \lor \lnot D) \end{aligned}$$ ### 2.2.2 SHA-1 (Secure Hash Algorithm 1) - **Designer:** NSA, published by NIST (FIPS 180-1, 1995) - **Digest Size:** **160 bits** (40 hex characters) - **Block Size:** 512 bits - **Rounds:** 80 rounds (4 groups of 20) - **Status:** **DEPRECATED** — SHAttered collision attack (2017, $~110$ GPU-years). **SHA-1 Initial Hash Values:** $$H_0 = \texttt{67452301}, \quad H_1 = \texttt{EFCDAB89}, \quad H_2 = \texttt{98BADCFE}$$ $$H_3 = \texttt{10325476}, \quad H_4 = \texttt{C3D2E1F0}$$ **SHA-1 Round Function:** $$f_t(B,C,D) = \begin{cases} (B \land C) \lor (\lnot B \land D) & 0 \le t \le 19 \\ B \oplus C \oplus D & 20 \le t \le 39 \\ (B \land C) \lor (B \land D) \lor (C \land D) & 40 \le t \le 59 \\ B \oplus C \oplus D & 60 \le t \le 79 \end{cases}$$ ### 2.2.3 SHA-2 Family (SHA-256, SHA-384, SHA-512) | Algorithm | Digest Size | Block Size | Word Size | Rounds | Security Status | |-----------|-------------|------------|-----------|--------|-----------------| | **SHA-224** | 224 bits | 512 bits | 32 bits | 64 | Secure | | **SHA-256** | 256 bits | 512 bits | 32 bits | 64 | **Secure (Recommended)** | | **SHA-384** | 384 bits | 1024 bits | 64 bits | 80 | **Secure (Recommended)** | | **SHA-512** | 512 bits | 1024 bits | 64 bits | 80 | **Secure (Recommended)** | **SHA-256 Constants (First 8 primes cube roots — first 32 bits of fractional parts):** $$H_0 = \texttt{6a09e667}, \quad H_1 = \texttt{bb67ae85}, \quad H_2 = \texttt{3c6ef372}, \quad H_3 = \texttt{a54ff53a}$$ $$H_4 = \texttt{510e527f}, \quad H_5 = \texttt{9b05688c}, \quad H_6 = \texttt{1f83d9ab}, \quad H_7 = \texttt{5be0cd19}$$ **SHA-256 Round Constants $K_t$:** First 32 bits of the fractional parts of the cube roots of the first 64 primes. **SHA-256 Compression Function (per round $t$):** $$\begin{aligned} \Sigma_0(a) &= \text{ROTR}^{2}(a) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{13}(a) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{22}(a) \\ \Sigma_1(e) &= \text{ROTR}^{6}(e) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{11}(e) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{25}(e) \\ \sigma_0(x) &= \text{ROTR}^{7}(x) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{18}(x) \oplus \text{SHR}^{3}(x) \\ \sigma_1(x) &= \text{ROTR}^{17}(x) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{19}(x) \oplus \text{SHR}^{10}(x) \\ T_1 &= h + \Sigma_1(e) + \text{Ch}(e,f,g) + K_t + W_t \\ T_2 &= \Sigma_0(a) + \text{Maj}(a,b,c) \\ h &= g, \quad g = f, \quad f = e, \quad e = d + T_1 \\ d &= c, \quad c = b, \quad b = a, \quad a = T_1 + T_2 \end{aligned}$$ Where: - $\text{Ch}(x,y,z) = (x \land y) \oplus (\lnot x \land z)$ — "Choose" function - $\text{Maj}(x,y,z) = (x \land y) \oplus (x \land z) \oplus (y \land z)$ — "Majority" function - $\text{ROTR}^n(x)$ — Right rotation by $n$ bits - $\text{SHR}^n(x)$ — Right shift by $n$ bits ### 2.2.4 SHA-3 (Keccak) - **Designer:** Guido Bertoni, Joan Daemen, Michaël Peeters, Gilles Van Assche - **Standardized:** NIST FIPS 202 (2015) - **Construction:** **Sponge construction** (NOT Merkle-Damgård) - **Digest Sizes:** 224, 256, 384, 512 bits - **Status:** **Secure (Most modern, post-quantum ready)** **Sponge Function:** $$\text{sponge}(M) = \text{squeeze}(c, d, \text{absorb}(M))$$ Where $c$ = capacity, $r$ = rate, $d$ = desired output length, and $c + r$ = state width (1600 bits for SHA-3). ## 2.3 MAC Algorithms Based on Hashes ### 2.3.1 HMAC (Hash-based Message Authentication Code) $$\text{HMAC}_K(M) = H\Big( (K_0 \oplus \text{opad}) \Vert H\big((K_0 \oplus \text{ipad}) \Vert M\big) \Big)$$ Where: - $K_0$ = key $K$ padded to block size (or hashed if longer) - $\text{opad}$ = outer padding = $\texttt{0x5C}$ repeated - $\text{ipad}$ = inner padding = $\texttt{0x36}$ repeated ### 2.3.2 CMAC (Cipher-based MAC) Uses a block cipher (AES, 3DES) to generate MAC, used in **TLS 1.2** and **IPsec**. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Quick Reference Table > [!NOTE] > **High-Yield Memorization Table** for KTU Module 4. | Property / Metric | MD5 | SHA-1 | SHA-256 | SHA-512 | SHA-3-256 | |-------------------|-----|-------|---------|---------|-----------| | **Digest Size (bits)** | 128 | 160 | 256 | 512 | 256 | | **Internal State (bits)** | 128 | 160 | 256 | 512 | 1600 | | **Block Size (bits)** | 512 | 512 | 512 | 1024 | 1088 (rate) | | **Word Size (bits)** | 32 | 32 | 32 | 64 | 64 | | **Rounds** | 64 | 80 | 64 | 80 | 24 (Keccak-f) | | **Construction** | Merkle-Damgård | Merkle-Damgård | Merkle-Damgård | Merkle-Damgård | Sponge | | **Year Published** | 1992 | 1995 | 2001 | 2001 | 2015 | | **Collision Attacks?** | Yes (2004) | Yes (2017) | No (theoretical only) | No (theoretical only) | No | | **Status** | **Broken** | **Broken** | **Secure** | **Secure** | **Secure** | | **Speed** | Fast | Fast | Moderate | Fast (64-bit) | Moderate | **Block Padding Formula (Merkle-Damgård):** $$\text{Pad}(M) = M \Vert 1 \Vert 0^{k} \Vert \vert M \vert_{64}$$ Where $k$ is the minimum number of zero bits needed to make the total length ≡ 896 (mod 1024) for SHA-512 or ≡ 448 (mod 512) for SHA-256. **Birthday Bound for Collision Probability:** $$N \approx 1.177 \sqrt{2^{n}}$$ Where $n$ = hash output length in bits. For SHA-256, this is $2^{128}$ operations. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **SHA-256** is used in **Bitcoin mining** (double SHA-256 of block headers). - **HMAC-SHA-256** is mandatory in **TLS 1.2/1.3**, **JWT tokens**, and **AWS Signature v4**. - **MD5** is still used (despite being broken for collision resistance) for **non-security checksums** (e.g., comparing file duplicates) due to its speed. - **SHA-3** is being adopted in **Ethereum 2.0**, **post-quantum cryptography** schemes, and **NIST Lightweight Cryptography** standards. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: SHA-256 Padding Scheme ### Step 1 — Original Message Length Analysis Suppose we have an input message $M$ of length $L$ bits, where $L < 2^{64}$. **Goal:** Pad $M$ so that the total padded message length $L_{\text{pad}}$ is a multiple of 512 bits. **Step 1.1:** Append a single `1` bit to the message. $$M_1 = M \Vert 1$$ The new length becomes $L + 1$ bits. **Step 1.2:** Append zero bits $0^k$ such that: $$L + 1 + k \equiv 448 \pmod{512}$$ Solving for $k$: $$k = 448 - (L + 1) \pmod{512}$$ If $(L + 1) \bmod 512 \le 448$, then $k = 448 - (L + 1) \bmod 512$. If $(L + 1) \bmod 512 > 448$, then $k = 512 - ((L + 1) \bmod 512) + 448$. After this step: $$M_2 = M_1 \Vert 0^k$$ **Step 1.3:** Append the original message length $L$ as a 64-bit big-endian integer. $$M_{\text{pad}} = M_2 \Vert L_{64}$$ The final length is: $$L_{\text{pad}} = L + 1 + k + 64 \equiv 0 \pmod{512}$$ ### Step 2 — Worked Numerical Example Let the original message $M$ be the ASCII string `"abc"` (24 bits, $L = 24$). **Step 2.1:** Append `1` bit. $$M_1 = \texttt{01100001 01100010 01100011 1} \quad (25 \text{ bits})$$ **Step 2.2:** Determine $k$. $$k = 448 - (24 + 1) = 448 - 25 = 423 \text{ zero bits}$$ **Step 2.3:** Append 64-bit length $L = 24$ in big-endian. $$L_{64} = \underbrace{00\ldots0}_{59 \text{ zeros}} \texttt{00011000}$$ **Step 2.4:** Final padded message length: $$L_{\text{pad}} = 24 + 1 + 423 + 64 = 512 \text{ bits (exactly 1 block)}$$ > [!NOTE] > The famous SHA-256 of `"abc"` is: `ba7816bf8f01cfea414140de5dae2223b00361a396177a9cb410ff61f20015ad`. This is a standard test vector used in NIST FIPS 180-4. ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: SHA-256 Message Schedule For each 512-bit message block, the message schedule expands 16 words (each 32 bits) into 64 words $W_0, W_1, \ldots, W_{63}$. ### Step 1 — Initial Words The first 16 words are taken directly from the 512-bit block: $$W_t = M_{i,t} \quad \text{for } 0 \le t \le 15$$ Where $M_{i,t}$ is the $t$-th 32-bit word of the $i$-th message block. ### Step 2 — Recursive Expansion for $t = 16$ to $63$ $$W_t = \sigma_1(W_{t-2}) + W_{t-7} + \sigma_0(W_{t-15}) + W_{t-16}$$ Where the sigma functions are: $$\sigma_0(x) = \text{ROTR}^{7}(x) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{18}(x) \oplus \text{SHR}^{3}(x)$$ $$\sigma_1(x) = \text{ROTR}^{17}(x) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{19}(x) \oplus \text{SHR}^{10}(x)$$ ### Step 3 — Detailed Word Computation (Example for $t = 16$) **Step 3.1:** Compute $\sigma_1(W_{14})$: $$\sigma_1(W_{14}) = \text{ROTR}^{17}(W_{14}) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{19}(W_{14}) \oplus \text{SHR}^{10}(W_{14})$$ Take $W_{14}$, rotate right by 17 bits, rotate right by 19 bits, shift right by 10 bits, and XOR all three results. **Step 3.2:** Compute $\sigma_0(W_1)$: $$\sigma_0(W_1) = \text{ROTR}^{7}(W_1) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{18}(W_1) \oplus \text{SHR}^{3}(W_1)$$ **Step 3.3:** Sum all four components using 32-bit modular addition: $$W_{16} = \sigma_1(W_{14}) + W_9 + \sigma_0(W_1) + W_0 \pmod{2^{32}}$$ ## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: HMAC-SHA-256 Computation ### Step 1 — Key Preparation Given a secret key $K$ of arbitrary length $L_K$ bytes and a block size $B = 64$ bytes (for SHA-256): **Step 1.1:** If $L_K > B$, replace $K$ with its hash: $$K_0 = \text{SHA-256}(K) \quad (32 \text{ bytes})$$ **Step 1.2:** If $L_K < B$, pad $K$ with zero bytes to length $B$: $$K_0 = K \Vert \underbrace{00\ldots0}_{B - L_K \text{ bytes}}$$ **Step 1.3:** If $L_K = B$, use $K$ as is. ### Step 2 — XOR with Inner/Outer Padding $$\text{ipad} = \underbrace{\texttt{36}_{16}}_{1 \text{ byte}} \text{ repeated } B \text{ times} = \texttt{363636\ldots36}$$ $$\text{opad} = \underbrace{\texttt{5C}_{16}}_{1 \text{ byte}} \text{ repeated } B \text{ times} = \texttt{5C5C5C\ldots5C}$$ Compute the XOR-padded keys: $$K_{\text{ipad}} = K_0 \oplus \text{ipad}$$ $$K_{\text{opad}} = K_0 \oplus \text{opad}$$ ### Step 3 — Compute Inner Hash $$H_{\text{inner}} = \text{SHA-256}\big( K_{\text{ipad}} \Vert M \big)$$ This produces a 32-byte (256-bit) intermediate digest. ### Step 4 — Compute Final HMAC $$\text{HMAC-SHA-256}(K, M) = \text{SHA-256}\big( K_{\text{opad}} \Vert H_{\text{inner}} \big)$$ The output is a **256-bit (32-byte) authentication tag**. ### Step 5 — Final Equation $$\boxed{\text{HMAC-SHA-256}(K, M) = H\Big( (K \oplus \text{opad}) \Vert H\big((K \oplus \text{ipad}) \Vert M\big) \Big)}$$ Where $H$ = SHA-256 and $\Vert$ denotes concatenation. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Algorithm Comparison on Test Vector > [!NOTE] > All hash algorithms in the SHA-2 family produce **drastically different digests** for the same input due to the **avalanche effect**. | Input Message | MD5 (128 bits) | SHA-1 (160 bits) | SHA-256 (256 bits) | |---------------|----------------|------------------|---------------------| | `""` (empty) | `d41d8cd98f00b204e9800998ecf8427e` | `da39a3ee5e6b4b0d3255bfef95601890afd80709` | `e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855` | | `"abc"` | `900150983cd24fb0d6963f7d28e17f72` | `a9993e364706816aba3e25717850c26c9cd0d89d` | `ba7816bf8f01cfea414140de5dae2223b00361a396177a9cb410ff61f20015ad` | | `"a"` (single char) | `0cc175b9c0f1b6a831c399e269772661` | `86f7e437faa5a7fce15d1ddcb9eaeaea377667b8` | `ca978112ca1bbdcafac231b39a23dc4da786eff8147c4e72b9807785afee48bb` | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Observation:** Even a tiny change (e.g., from `""` to `"a"`) completely changes all the hash bits. This is the **avalanche effect** in action — a fundamental property expected in KTU exam questions. # 4.
