KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **Define a cryptographic hash function. List any three of its essential security properties.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A cryptographic hash function $H$ is a mathematical algorithm that takes an input message $M$ of arbitrary length and produces a fixed-length output $h$ of $n$ bits, where $h = H(M)$. The three essential security properties are: 1. **Pre-image Resistance:** Given a hash value $h$, it is computationally infeasible to find any input $M$ such that $H(M) = h$. 2. **Second Pre-image Resistance:** Given an input $M_1$, it is computationally infeasible to find a different input $M_2 \neq M_1$ such that $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$. 3. **Collision Resistance:** It is computationally infeasible to find any two distinct inputs $M_1$ and $M_2$ such that $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$. --- ### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **What is a message digest? Differentiate between MD5 and SHA-256 with respect to digest size, block size, and security status.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **message digest** is the fixed-length cryptographic hash output produced by a hash function when applied to a message. It serves as a digital fingerprint of the input data. | Parameter | MD5 | SHA-256 | |-----------|-----|---------| | **Digest Size** | 128 bits | 256 bits | | **Block Size** | 512 bits | 512 bits | | **Rounds** | 64 (4 × 16) | 64 | | **Security Status** | Broken (collisions found in 2004) | Secure (recommended) | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** #### **Part (a) [7 marks]: Explain the SHA-256 algorithm in detail with its compression function and message schedule.** **Model Solution:** **1. Overview of SHA-256 (2 marks):** SHA-256 is a member of the SHA-2 family standardized by NIST in FIPS 180-2 (2001). It produces a **256-bit (32-byte) message digest** from input messages of arbitrary length up to $2^{64} - 1$ bits. SHA-256 processes input in **512-bit blocks** using **64 rounds** of compression with 8 working variables ($a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h$) of 32 bits each. **2. Initial Hash Values (1 mark):** The eight 32-bit initial hash values $H_0^{(0)} \ldots H_7^{(0)}$ are derived from the fractional parts of the square roots of the first 8 primes (2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13, 17, 19): $$H_0 = \texttt{6a09e667}, \quad H_1 = \texttt{bb67ae85}, \quad H_2 = \texttt{3c6ef372}, \quad H_3 = \texttt{a54ff53a}$$ $$H_4 = \texttt{510e527f}, \quad H_5 = \texttt{9b05688c}, \quad H_6 = \texttt{1f83d9ab}, \quad H_7 = \texttt{5be0cd19}$$ **3. Message Padding (1 mark):** - Append bit `1` to the message. - Append $k$ zero bits so that message length $\equiv 448 \pmod{512}$. - Append original message length as a 64-bit big-endian integer. **4. Message Schedule (1 mark):** For each 512-bit block, expand 16 words ($W_0 \ldots W_{15}$) into 64 words using: $$W_t = \begin{cases} M_t^{(i)} & 0 \le t \le 15 \\ \sigma_1(W_{t-2}) + W_{t-7} + \sigma_0(W_{t-15}) + W_{t-16} & 16 \le t \le 63 \end{cases}$$ Where: - $\sigma_0(x) = \text{ROTR}^{7}(x) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{18}(x) \oplus \text{SHR}^{3}(x)$ - $\sigma_1(x) = \text{ROTR}^{17}(x) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{19}(x) \oplus \text{SHR}^{10}(x)$ **5. Compression Function — Round Operations (2 marks):** For each of the 64 rounds, compute: $$\begin{aligned} T_1 &= h + \Sigma_1(e) + \text{Ch}(e,f,g) + K_t + W_t \\ T_2 &= \Sigma_0(a) + \text{Maj}(a,b,c) \\ h &= g, \quad g = f, \quad f = e, \quad e = d + T_1 \\ d &= c, \quad c = b, \quad b = a, \quad a = T_1 + T_2 \end{aligned}$$ Where the auxiliary functions are: - $\text{Ch}(x,y,z) = (x \land y) \oplus (\lnot x \land z)$ - $\text{Maj}(x,y,z) = (x \land y) \oplus (x \land z) \oplus (y \land z)$ - $\Sigma_0(x) = \text{ROTR}^{2}(x) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{13}(x) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{22}(x)$ - $\Sigma_1(x) = \text{ROTR}^{6}(x) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{11}(x) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{25}(x)$ **6. Final Hash Computation (1 mark):** After processing all blocks, the final hash is: $$H_0^{(N)} = H_0^{(N-1)} + a, \quad H_1^{(N)} = H_1^{(N-1)} + b, \ldots, H_7^{(N)} = H_7^{(N-1)} + h$$ The message digest is the concatenation $H_0^{(N)} \Vert H_1^{(N)} \Vert \ldots \Vert H_7^{(N)}$. --- #### **Part (b) [7 marks]: Explain the concept of HMAC. How does HMAC differ from a plain message digest? Provide the HMAC algorithm.** **Model Solution:** **1. Definition of HMAC (2 marks):** **HMAC (Hash-based Message Authentication Code)** is a specific construction for computing a message authentication code (MAC) involving a cryptographic hash function in combination with a secret cryptographic key. It provides both **data integrity** and **authentication**. The strength of HMAC depends on the underlying hash function's strength, the key's length and secrecy, and the hash output length. **2. HMAC vs Plain Message Digest (2 marks):** | Property | Plain Message Digest | HMAC | |----------|---------------------|------| | **Key Used** | No (keyless) | Yes (secret key $K$) | | **Integrity** | Yes | Yes | | **Authentication** | **No** — anyone can compute the digest | **Yes** — only key holder can generate/verify | | **Use Case** | File integrity checks, digital signatures | API authentication, TLS, JWT | | **Vulnerability** | Susceptible to length-extension attacks | Resistant due to nested hashing | **3. HMAC Algorithm (3 marks):** $$\text{HMAC}_K(M) = H\Big( (K_0 \oplus \text{opad}) \Vert H\big((K_0 \oplus \text{ipad}) \Vert M\big) \Big)$$ **Step-by-Step Procedure:** **Step 1:** Choose a hash function $H$ (e.g., SHA-256) with block size $B$ bytes (64 for SHA-256) and output length $L$ bytes (32 for SHA-256). **Step 2:** Prepare the key $K_0$: - If length of $K$ > $B$, then $K_0 = H(K)$ (32 bytes). - If length of $K$ < $B$, then $K_0 = K \Vert 0^{B - \text{len}(K)}$. - If length of $K$ = $B$, then $K_0 = K$. **Step 3:** Define padding constants: - $\text{ipad} = \texttt{0x36}$ repeated $B$ times. - $\text{opad} = \texttt{0x5C}$ repeated $B$ times. **Step 4:** Compute inner hash: $$H_{\text{inner}} = H\big( (K_0 \oplus \text{ipad}) \Vert M \big)$$ **Step 5:** Compute outer hash (final HMAC): $$\text{HMAC}_K(M) = H\big( (K_0 \oplus \text{opad}) \Vert H_{\text{inner}} \big)$$ **4. Why Two Hashes? (1 mark):** The nested structure (applying $H$ twice) defends against **length-extension attacks**, where an attacker who knows $H(M)$ and the length of $M$ can compute $H(M \Vert \text{ext})$ without knowing $M$. The outer hash $H(K_0 \oplus \text{opad} \Vert \cdot)$ makes the output unpredictable to attackers who don't know the key. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2023]** #### **Part (a) [7 marks]: Compare MD5 and SHA-1 algorithms. Why has MD5 been considered broken? Explain with reference to collision attacks.** **Model Solution:** **1. Comparative Table of MD5 vs SHA-1 (3 marks):** | Parameter | MD5 | SHA-1 | |-----------|-----|-------| | **Designer** | Ronald Rivest (1992) | NIST/NSA (1995) | | **Digest Size** | 128 bits | 160 bits | | **Block Size** | 512 bits | 512 bits | | **Number of Rounds** | 64 (4 × 16) | 80 (4 × 20) | | **Word Size** | 32 bits | 32 bits | | **Initial Hash Values** | 4 (A, B, C, D) | 5 ($H_0 \ldots H_4$) | | **Round Functions** | F, G, H, I | $f_t$ (changes per round group) | | **Status** | **Broken (2004)** | **Broken (2017)** | | **Standard** | RFC 1321 | FIPS 180-1 | **2. Why MD5 Is Considered Broken (3 marks):** MD5 is considered **cryptographically broken** because practical **collision attacks** have been demonstrated: **a) 2004 — Wang et al. Collision Attack:** Xiaoyun Wang and Hongbo Yu demonstrated a method to find two distinct messages $M_1 \neq M_2$ such that $\text{MD5}(M_1) = \text{MD5}(M_2)$ with computational complexity of approximately $2^{39}$ operations (much less than the birthday bound of $2^{64}$). **b) 2008 — Practical Collisions (Marc Stevens):** Researchers created **rogue CA certificates** with identical MD5 hashes but different content, allowing them to impersonate any SSL certificate authority. This was a real-world security disaster. **c) 2012 — Flame Malware:** The **Flame** cyber-espionage malware exploited MD5 collisions to forge Microsoft code-signing certificates, allowing the malware to appear as legitimate Microsoft software on Windows Update. **3. Implications of MD5 Weaknesses (1 mark):** - **Digital signatures** using MD5 are forgeable. - **SSL/TLS certificates** using MD5 are vulnerable to impersonation. - **Software distribution** using MD5 checksums is vulnerable to malicious substitution. - MD5 should **never be used** for new cryptographic applications — only for non-security checksums (e.g., detecting duplicate files). --- #### **Part (b) [7 marks]: Describe the SHA-3 (Keccak) algorithm and explain how it differs from the SHA-2 family. What is the sponge construction?** **Model Solution:** **1. SHA-3 Background (1 mark):** SHA-3 is the latest member of the Secure Hash Algorithm family, standardized by NIST in **FIPS 202 (August 2015)**. It was the winner of the NIST hash competition (2007-2012), designed by **Guido Bertoni, Joan Daemen, Michaël Peeters, and Gilles Van Assche**. SHA-3 uses a fundamentally different construction called the **sponge construction**, unlike the Merkle-Damgård construction used in SHA-1 and SHA-2. **2. SHA-3 Variants (1 mark):** | Variant | Output Size | Rate $r$ | Capacity $c$ | |---------|-------------|----------|--------------| | SHA3-224 | 224 bits | 1152 bits | 448 bits | | SHA3-256 | 256 bits | 1088 bits | 512 bits | | SHA3-384 | 384 bits | 832 bits | 768 bits | | SHA3-512 | 512 bits | 576 bits | 1024 bits | **3. Sponge Construction (3 marks):** The **sponge construction** is a framework for building hash functions from a fixed-length permutation (or transformation) $f$ operating on a state of width $b = r + c$ bits, where: - $r$ = **rate** (the portion of state that interacts with input/output) - $c$ = **capacity** (the hidden portion that provides security) **Sponge Phases:** **Phase 1 — Initialization:** The state is initialized to all zeros: $S_0 = 0^{b}$. **Phase 2 — Absorbing Phase:** The padded input message is absorbed in $r$-bit blocks. For each block $M_i$: $$S_{i+1} = f(S_i \oplus (M_i \Vert 0^{c}))$$ The $f$ function is the **Keccak-f[1600]** permutation with 24 rounds. **Phase 3 — Squeezing Phase:** Output is produced by repeatedly applying $f$ and extracting the first $r$ bits: $$Z_i = \text{truncate}(S_i, r), \quad S_{i+1} = f(S_i)$$ Continue until $d$ bits of output are produced. **4. SHA-3 vs SHA-2 Differences (2 marks):** | Property | SHA-2 | SHA-3 | |----------|-------|-------| | **Construction** | Merkle-Damgård (iterated compression) | Sponge (permutation-based) | | **Base Operation** | Compression function $f$ | Permutation $f$ (Keccak-f[1600]) | | **State Size** | Equal to digest size (256/512 bits) | 1600 bits (5 × 5 × 64 lanes) | | **Security Against Length Extension** | **Vulnerable** (requires HMAC) | **Inherently resistant** (no length extension) | | **Hardware Performance** | Good | Excellent (no ARX dependencies) | | **Use Cases** | TLS 1.2, Bitcoin, JWT | Ethereum 2.0, post-quantum crypto | | **Algebraic Structure** | ARX (Add-Rotate-XOR) | Substitution-Permutation Network | **5. Why SHA-3 Matters (1 mark):** - **Post-quantum security:** SHA-3's algebraic structure (no ARX) is believed to be more resistant to quantum attacks. - **Length-extension resistance:** The sponge construction inherently prevents length-extension attacks without requiring HMAC nesting. - **Hardware efficiency:** Keccak-f permutation maps well to FPGA and ASIC implementations. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **Confusing Digest Size with Block Size:** MD5 has a **128-bit digest** but a **512-bit block size**. Students frequently mix these up. *(−1 mark penalty)* > > 2. **Writing "SHA" without the number:** Always specify **SHA-1, SHA-256, SHA-512** etc. Writing "SHA" alone is incomplete and may lose marks. > > 3. **Forgetting the Three Security Properties:** KTU examiners **expect** all three properties (pre-image, second pre-image, collision resistance). Forgetting even one results in a **−1 to −2 mark** deduction. > > 4. **Wrong Constants:** SHA-256 initial hash value $H_0 = \texttt{6a09e667}$ is **memorized frequently**. Writing wrong constants will be marked down. > > 5. **Confusing HMAC with Plain Hash:** Many students write the HMAC equation incorrectly. The **correct order** is: $(K_0 \oplus \text{opad})$ is hashed with $H(K_0 \oplus \text{ipad} \Vert M)$ — **outer pad is hashed WITH the inner hash**, not the message. > > 6. **Not Mentioning Avalanche Effect:** A complete answer to hash function properties must include the **avalanche effect** as a desirable property. Skipping it costs marks. > > 7. **Merkle-Damgård vs Sponge Confusion:** SHA-1 and SHA-2 use **Merkle-Damgård**. SHA-3 uses **Sponge**. Do NOT claim SHA-2 uses sponge construction. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Hash Algorithms** - ✅ **Hash Function** maps arbitrary-length input → fixed-length output ($n$ bits). - ✅ **Message Digest** = the hash output; serves as a digital fingerprint. - ✅ **Three security properties (must memorize):** Pre-image resistance, Second pre-image resistance, Collision resistance. - ✅ **Avalanche effect:** 1-bit change → ~50% output bits change. - ✅ **MD5:** 128-bit digest, 512-bit block, 64 rounds, **BROKEN** (collisions since 2004). - ✅ **SHA-1:** 160-bit digest, 512-bit block, 80 rounds, **BROKEN** (SHAttered attack 2017). - ✅ **SHA-256:** 256-bit digest, 512-bit block, 64 rounds, 8 working variables (a-h), **SECURE & RECOMMENDED**. - ✅ **SHA-512:** 512-bit digest, 1024-bit block, 80 rounds, **SECURE**. - ✅ **SHA-3 (Keccak):** Sponge construction, 1600-bit state, 24 Keccak-f rounds, **MODERN & SECURE**. - ✅ **Merkle-Damgård Construction:** Padding → Split into blocks → Iteratively apply compression function $f$ with current hash → Final hash. - ✅ **Padding formula:** $M \Vert 1 \Vert 0^k \Vert L_{64}$ where final length ≡ 0 (mod 512). - ✅ **HMAC equation:** $\text{HMAC}_K(M) = H((K_0 \oplus \text{opad}) \Vert H((K_0 \oplus \text{ipad}) \Vert M))$. - ✅ **ipad = 0x36**, **opad = 0x5C** (both repeated to block size). - ✅ **HMAC provides both integrity AND authentication** (unlike plain digest which only provides integrity). - ✅ **SHA-256 initial hash value $H_0$** = `6a09e667` (first 32 bits of fractional part of $\sqrt{2}$). - ✅ **Ch function:** $(x \land y) \oplus (\lnot x \land z)$ — used in SHA-256 rounds. - ✅ **Maj function:** $(x \land y) \oplus (x \land z) \oplus (y \land z)$ — used in SHA-256 rounds. - ✅ **Birthday bound for collisions:** $\approx 1.177 \times 2^{n/2}$ operations needed to find a collision. - ✅ **For SHA-256:** collision requires $\approx 2^{128}$ operations — computationally infeasible. - ✅ **Bitcoin uses double SHA-256** (SHA-256(SHA-256(block_header))). - ✅ **TLS 1.2/1.3 uses HMAC-SHA-256** for message authentication. - ✅ **MD5 is still acceptable for non-security checksums** (e.g., file deduplication), but **NOT for digital signatures or certificates**. - ✅ **SHA-3 is inherently resistant to length-extension attacks** due to sponge construction.

topics.title: Authentication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAIT418 - SECURITY ASSESSMENT AND RISK ANALYSIS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Authentication # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Authentication** is the security mechanism that provides corroboration of the identity claim of a principal (user, process, or system) attempting to access a computing resource. It is the *first line of defense* in the **AAA (Authentication, Authorization, and Accounting)** security triad and forms a foundational control within the **NIST SP 800-53 Access Control family** and the **ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Annex A.8.2**. Formally, authentication validates one or more of the following binding proofs: $$ \text{Identity Claim} \xrightarrow{\text{Validates}} \text{Authenticator Factor} \rightarrow \text{Access Decision} $$ Where the **Authenticator Factor** must be drawn from at least one of the five recognized factor classes. ### The Five Authentication Factor Classes | Factor Class | Definition | Typical Examples | Security Strength | |--------------|------------|------------------|------------------| | **Type 1 — Something You Know** (Knowledge Factor) | Secret information possessed cognitively by the user | Password, PIN, Passphrase, Security Question | Low–Medium (vulnerable to shoulder surfing, brute force) | | **Type 2 — Something You Have** (Possession Factor) | Physical or logical token the user possesses | Smart card, Hardware token (RSA SecurID), Mobile OTP, USB key (YubiKey) | Medium–High | | **Type 3 — Something You Are** (Biometric / Inherence Factor) | Physiological or behavioral characteristic | Fingerprint, Iris scan, Facial geometry, Voiceprint, Keystroke dynamics | High | | **Type 4 — Something You Do** (Behavioral Factor) | Pattern of behavior measurable over time | Gait analysis, Mouse dynamics, Typing rhythm | Medium (emerging) | | **Type 5 — Somewhere You Are** (Location Factor) | Physical or logical geographic position | GPS coordinates, IP geolocation, Network SSID | Contextual (used in risk-based authentication) | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Airport Security Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Intuition:** Think of authentication like the multi-stage screening at an international airport. > > 1. **Show your boarding pass + Photo ID** (Username + Password) — this is *Type 1* (knowledge) verifying a *Type 3* (biometric) match between the photo on the ID and your face. > 2. **Walk through the body scanner** — this is *Type 3* (biometric verification), confirming you *are* who the boarding pass claims. > 3. **Your bag is screened separately** — this is authorization (what you can carry), which only happens *after* authentication succeeds. > > The boarding pass alone isn't enough (password alone), the photo ID alone isn't enough (biometric alone), but **combining them (MFA)** makes impersonation nearly impossible for an attacker. ## 1.3 Authentication vs. Authorization — A Critical Distinction > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Alert:** KTU frequently tests this distinction. Confusing these two costs students **2–3 marks** outright. $$ \text{Authentication} \neq \text{Authorization} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{AuthN (Who are you?)} \quad \longleftrightarrow \quad \text{AuthZ (What are you allowed to do?)}} $$ | Dimension | Authentication (AuthN) | Authorization (AuthZ) | |-----------|------------------------|----------------------| | **Question Answered** | "Who is this entity?" | "What can this entity do?" | | **Logical Position** | Pre-access (gatekeeper) | Post-access (policy enforcement) | | **Mechanism** | Credentials, tokens, biometrics | ACLs, RBAC, ABAC, Policy engines | | **Failure Outcome** | Login denied | Action denied (403 Forbidden) | | **Standard Protocol** | Kerberos, SAML, OAuth 2.0 | XACML, OAuth scopes, RBAC roles | | **NIST Mapping** | SP 800-63B (Digital Identity) | SP 800-53 AC-3, AC-6 | ## 1.4 The Three Authentication Assurance Levels (NIST SP 800-63B) > [!NOTE] > NIST defines **Authenticator Assurance Level (AAL)** as a measure of the strength of the authentication process: $$ \text{AAL1} < \text{AAL2} < \text{AAL3} $$ - **AAL1** — Single-factor (e.g., password); some assurance that the claimant controls the credential. - **AAL2** — Multi-factor (2FA); cryptographic login; replay-resistant. - **AAL3** — Hardware cryptographic authenticator + multi-factor + verifier impersonation resistance (e.g., FIDO2/WebAuthn). # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Pillars of an Authentication System An authentication mechanism must satisfy four operational pillars, often abbreviated as the **"IAAA-I" model** (Identification, Authentication, Authorization, Accounting, plus Integrity): ### Pillar 1 — Identification The user asserts an identity via a **username, email, or account ID**. This is the *claim* phase. ### Pillar 2 — Authentication (Verification) The user **proves** the identity claim using a factor. Verification logic: $$ \text{Verifier} \rightarrow \text{Compare}(\text{Supplied Factor}, \text{Stored Reference}) $$ Match → **Success**; Mismatch → **Failure** (often with backoff timer). ### Pillar 3 — Authorization (Out of scope, but linked) Based on authenticated identity, the **Access Control System (ACS)** issues a session token carrying privileges. ### Pillar 4 — Auditing / Accounting All authentication events are logged per **ISO 27001 A.8.15 (Logging)**, including timestamp, source IP, success/failure, and factor used. ## 2.2 Password-Based Authentication — Mathematical Foundations ### 2.2.1 Password Entropy (Shannon Information Theory) The strength of a password is measured in **bits of entropy**: $$ H = L \cdot \log_2(R) $$ Where: - $H$ = **Entropy in bits** (higher is stronger) - $L$ = **Length** of the password - $R$ = **Size of the character pool** (charset cardinality) - $\log_2$ = base-2 logarithm **Character pool sizes for standard charsets:** | Charset Type | Characters Available | Pool Size $R$ | |--------------|----------------------|----------------| | Digits only (0–9) | 10 | $R = 10$ | | Lowercase letters (a–z) | 26 | $R = 26$ | | Uppercase + lowercase | 52 | $R = 52$ | | Alphanumeric (a-z, A-Z, 0-9) | 62 | $R = 62$ | | Full ASCII printable | 95 | $R = 95$ | | Extended ASCII / Unicode | 256+ | $R \geq 256$ | ### 2.2.2 Password Cracking Time (Brute Force) The average time to crack a password via brute force is: $$ T_{crack} = \frac{R^L}{2 \cdot N_{hash/s}} $$ Where $N_{hash/s}$ is the attacker's hash computation rate (e.g., a modern GPU cluster = $\approx 10^{11}$ SHA-256 hashes/second). ### 2.2.3 Password Storage — Cryptographic Hashing Passwords must **NEVER** be stored in plaintext. They are stored as one-way hashes with a salt: $$ \text{Stored} = \text{Salt} \parallel \text{Hash}(\text{Password} \parallel \text{Salt}) $$ Where $\parallel$ denotes concatenation and $\text{Salt}$ is a cryptographically random nonce (typically **128 bits**). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Modern Password Hashing Standards (KTU high-yield):** > - **bcrypt** — Adaptive, includes salt, cost factor $2^{10}$–$2^{14}$. > - **scrypt** — Memory-hard; resists GPU/ASIC attacks. > - **Argon2id** — Winner of Password Hashing Competition (2015); recommended for new systems. > - **PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA256** — NIST SP 800-132 approved, ≥ 100,000 iterations. ## 2.3 Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) — Probability of Compromise The probability that an attacker bypasses MFA is governed by the **independent failure model**: $$ P_{\text{compromise}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P_i $$ Where $P_i$ is the probability of defeating factor $i$, assuming **statistical independence** between factors. **Example:** If factor 1 (password) is compromised with $P_1 = 10^{-6}$ and factor 2 (fingerprint) with $P_2 = 10^{-3}$: $$ P_{\text{MFA compromise}} = 10^{-6} \times 10^{-3} = 10^{-9} $$ This is **one in a billion** — the multiplicative (not additive) nature of MFA is its core security strength. ## 2.4 Biometric Authentication — Performance Metrics Biometric systems are evaluated using **False Accept Rate (FAR)** and **False Reject Rate (FRR)**: | Metric | Symbol | Definition | Security Implication | |--------|--------|------------|----------------------| | **False Accept Rate** | $FAR$ | Impostor accepted as genuine | Security failure (Type II error) | | **False Reject Rate** | $FRR$ | Genuine user rejected | Usability failure (Type I error) | | **Equal Error Rate** | $EER$ | Point where $FAR = FRR$ | System tuning benchmark | | **Failure to Enroll** | $FTE$ | User cannot enroll biometric | Accessibility concern | | **Failure to Capture** | $FTC$ | System cannot read biometric | Operational reliability | The **Crossover Error Rate (CER)** or **EER** is the industry's gold-standard comparison metric — **lower EER = better biometric system**. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Units / Notes | |---|-------------------|----------|---------------| | 1 | Password Entropy | $H = L \cdot \log_2(R)$ | bits | | 2 | Brute-Force Cracking Time | $T_{crack} = \dfrac{R^L}{2 \cdot N_{hash/s}}$ | seconds | | 3 | MFA Compromise Probability | $P_{comp} = \prod P_i$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) | | 4 | Salting Format | $\text{Stored} = \text{Salt} \parallel \text{Hash}(P \parallel \text{Salt})$ | bits | | 5 | Crossover Error Rate | $EER: FAR = FRR$ | percentage | | 6 | Kerberos Ticket Lifetime | $T_{ticket} \leq 8$ hours (typical) | minutes/hours | | 7 | OTP Validity Window | $T_{OTP} \in [30, 60]$ seconds | seconds | | 8 | Hash Iteration Count (PBKDF2) | $N_{iter} \geq 100{,}000$ | iterations | | 9 | Salt Length (NIST) | $\vert S \vert \geq 128$ bits | bits | | 10 | Biometric Threshold Tuning | $\tau \uparrow \Rightarrow FAR \downarrow, FRR \uparrow$ | operating point | ## 2.6 Engineering Utility of Authentication Theory In real-world security engineering, these formulas power: - **NIST AAL selection** for federal identity assurance. - **Password policy engines** (e.g., NIST SP 800-63B banned-list + minimum entropy enforcement). - **Risk-Based Authentication (RBA)** scoring, where $P_{comp}$ is dynamically computed per session using geolocation, device fingerprint, and behavioral analytics. - **Penetration Testing** — the $T_{crack}$ equation is used in tools like **Hashcat** and **John the Ripper** to estimate cracking time against stolen hash dumps. - **Zero-Trust Architecture (ZTA)** — Per NIST SP 800-207, *every* access request is authenticated and authorized, eliminating implicit trust. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Password Entropy from Information Theory ### Problem Statement Calculate the entropy of a password `P@ssw0rd!2024` (length 14) drawn from the full ASCII printable set ($R = 95$). ### Step 1 — Identify the Length and Pool Size - Length: $L = 14$ characters - Pool size: $R = 95$ (full ASCII printable: a-z, A-Z, 0-9, 32 punctuation symbols) ### Step 2 — Apply the Entropy Formula $$ H = L \cdot \log_2(R) $$ $$ H = 14 \cdot \log_2(95) $$ ### Step 3 — Compute $\log_2(95)$ $$ \log_2(95) = \frac{\ln(95)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{4.5539}{0.6931} \approx 6.5699 \text{ bits/char} $$ ### Step 4 — Final Calculation $$ H = 14 \times 6.5699 \approx 91.98 \text{ bits} $$ ### Step 5 — Interpretation According to **NIST SP 800-63B**, a password with $H \geq 80$ bits is considered **cryptographically strong** against offline brute-force attacks even at modern GPU speeds. So this password is **strong**, but the use of common dictionary words (`P@ssw0rd`) makes it vulnerable to **dictionary + rule-based attacks** despite the entropy. > [!NOTE] > **Key insight for students:** Entropy is a *theoretical maximum*; *actual* entropy is often lower due to human predictability. Always use **NIST password guidelines**: minimum length 8, no mandatory complexity, check against breached-password dictionaries (HIBP). --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Brute-Force Cracking Time ### Problem Statement Estimate the average time to crack an 8-character lowercase password ($R = 26$, $L = 8$) using a modern GPU capable of $N_{hash/s} = 10^{11}$ MD5 hashes per second. ### Step 1 — Total Keyspace $$ \text{Keyspace} = R^L = 26^8 $$ $$ 26^8 = (26^4)^2 = (456{,}976)^2 \approx 2.088 \times 10^{11} $$ ### Step 2 — Average Time Formula $$ T_{crack} = \frac{R^L}{2 \cdot N_{hash/s}} $$ ### Step 3 — Substitute Values $$ T_{crack} = \frac{2.088 \times 10^{11}}{2 \times 10^{11}} $$ $$ T_{crack} = \frac{2.088}{2} = 1.044 \text{ seconds} $$ ### Step 4 — Conclusion An 8-character lowercase password can be **cracked in roughly 1 second** by a modern GPU. This is why **password length + character diversity + salting + hashing algorithm** all matter collectively. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Exam Tip:** A common exam trap is to *omit the divide-by-2* (which accounts for *average* time, where the password is found halfway through the keyspace on average). Always write the full formula explicitly. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: MFA Security Multiplier ### Problem Statement A banking system uses: - A 6-digit PIN (Type 1 factor) — guessable in $10^6$ attempts. - A 60-second OTP (Type 2 factor) — guessable in $10^6$ attempts (per window). - Fingerprint biometric (Type 3 factor) — FAR = $10^{-5}$. Compute the joint compromise probability if all three are independent. ### Step 1 — Individual Factor Probabilities $$ P_1 = \frac{1}{10^6} = 10^{-6} $$ $$ P_2 = \frac{1}{10^6} = 10^{-6} $$ $$ P_3 = FAR = 10^{-5} $$ ### Step 2 — Apply Joint Independence $$ P_{comp} = P_1 \times P_2 \times P_3 $$ $$ P_{comp} = 10^{-6} \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{-5} $$ ### Step 3 — Combine Exponents $$ P_{comp} = 10^{-6 + (-6) + (-5)} = 10^{-17} $$ ### Step 4 — Interpretation The probability of bypassing all three factors is **1 in 100 quadrillion** — effectively zero. This is why **multi-factor authentication** is the single most effective control against credential theft. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Authentication Protocol Comparison | Protocol | Layer | Mutual Auth | Token Type | Primary Use Case | KTU Exam Relevance | |----------|-------|-------------|------------|------------------|---------------------| | **Kerberos v5** | Network (OSI 7) | ✅ Yes (TGT + Service Ticket) | TGT, Service Ticket, Ticket-Granting Ticket | Internal Windows/Active Directory domains | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **RADIUS** | Application | ✅ Yes | Access-Accept, Access-Reject | Network access (Wi-Fi, VPN, ISP) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **TACACS+** | Application | ✅ Yes (encrypts full payload) | REPLY/REQUEST packets | Cisco device admin (AAA) | ⭐⭐⭐ | | **OAuth 2.0** | Application | ❌ Authorization only | Bearer Access Token | API delegated authorization | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **OpenID Connect** | Application | ✅ Yes (on top of OAuth 2.0) | ID Token (JWT) | Federated SSO (Google, Facebook login) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **SAML 2.0** | Application | ✅ Yes | SAML Assertion (XML) | Enterprise SSO (cross-org) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **FIDO2 / WebAuthn** | Web/Application | ✅ Yes (cryptographic) | Public-key credential | Passwordless authentication | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **LDAP** | Application | ❌ No native encryption (LDAPS does) | Bind operation | Directory-based auth | ⭐⭐⭐ | --- ## 3.5 Kerberos Authentication — Step-by-Step Walkthrough The **Kerberos v5** protocol (RFC 4120) is KTU's most-tested authentication protocol. It uses a **Key Distribution Center (KDC)** = **AS (Authentication Server) + TGS (Ticket Granting Server)**. ### The 6-Step Kerberos Flow (Simplified) **Step 1 — AS-REQ (Client → AS)** $$ \text{Client} \rightarrow \text{AS}: \{ ID_c, ID_{TGS}, N_1 \} $$ User requests a **Ticket-Granting Ticket (TGT)**. **Step 2 — AS-REP (AS → Client)** $$ \text{AS} \rightarrow \text{Client}: \{ TGT \}_{K_{TGS}} \parallel \{ K_{c,TGS} \}_{K_c} $$ AS verifies the client's password-derived key $K_c$ and issues an encrypted TGT. **Step 3 — TGS-REQ (Client → TGS)** $$ \text{Client} \rightarrow \text{TGS}: \{ TGT \}_{K_{TGS}} \parallel \{ Authenticator \}_{K_{c,TGS}} \parallel ID_s $$ Client requests a **Service Ticket** for server S. **Step 4 — TGS-REP (TGS → Client)** $$ \text{TGS} \rightarrow \text{Client}: \{ Service\ Ticket \}_{K_s} \parallel \{ K_{c,s} \}_{K_{c,TGS}} $$ TGS issues a session key $K_{c,s}$ shared between client and service. **Step 5 — AP-REQ (Client → Service)** $$ \text{Client} \rightarrow \text{S}: \{ Service\ Ticket \}_{K_s} \parallel \{ Authenticator \}_{K_{c,s}} $$ Client presents the service ticket to the target server. **Step 6 — AP-REP (Service → Client) [Mutual Auth Confirmation]** $$ \text{S} \rightarrow \text{Client}: \{ Timestamp \}_{K_{c,s}} $$ Server proves its identity to the client, completing **mutual authentication**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Properties Achieved:** > - **Mutual Authentication** — both client and server prove identity. > - **Replay Protection** — nonces $N_i$ and timestamps prevent replay. > - **Single Sign-On (SSO)** — TGT enables multiple service tickets without re-entering credentials. > - **No plaintext password transmission** — credentials never go to the service. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — AAA Security Triad Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB A[User / Principal] --> B[Identification
username, email, ID] B --> C{Authentication
AuthN Factor} C -->|Success| D[Session Token Issued] C -->|Failure| E[Access Denied
Logged to SIEM] D --> F{Authorization
Policy Engine} F -->|Permitted| G[Resource Access Granted] F -->|Denied| H[403 Forbidden Response] D --> I[Accounting
Audit Logs] I --> J[SIEM / SOC Dashboard] classDef authn fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,color:#000 classDef authz fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000 classDef audit fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000 classDef fail fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000 class B,C,D authn class F,G authz class I,J audit class E,H fail ``` --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Kerberos 6-Step Authentication Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant C as Client
User Workstation participant AS as Authentication
Server AS participant TGS as Ticket Granting
Server TGS participant S as Target
Service Server C->>AS: STEP 1 AS REQ
User ID + TGS ID + Nonce AS-->>C: STEP 2 AS REP
TGT encrypted + Session Key Note over C: Decrypts using
password derived key C->>TGS: STEP 3 TGS REQ
TGT + Authenticator + Service ID TGS-->>C: STEP 4 TGS REP
Service Ticket + Session Key Note over C: Now has access to
target service C->>S: STEP 5 AP REQ
Service Ticket + Authenticator S-->>C: STEP 6 AP REP
Timestamp encrypted Note over C,S: Mutual Authentication
Complete ``` --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Multi-Factor Authentication Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Login Attempt Initiated] --> B[Capture Username
and Password] B --> C{Password
Valid?} C -->|No| D[Reject and Log
Failed Attempt] C -->|Yes| E[Trigger Second Factor
Challenge] E --> F{User Selected
Method} F -->|OTP SMS| G[Send 6 digit OTP
TTL 60 seconds] F -->|Authenticator App| H[Generate TOTP
RFC 6238] F -->|Hardware Token| I[Request Cryptographic
Response] F -->|Biometric| J[Capture Fingerprint
or Face] G --> K{OTP Correct
and Not Expired?} H --> K I --> K J --> K K -->|Yes| L[Grant Access
Issue Session Token] K -->|No| M[Reject Attempt
Increment Counter] M --> N{Lockout
Threshold Hit?} N -->|Yes| O[Account Locked
Alert SOC] N -->|No| D classDef success fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000 classDef fail fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000 classDef mfa fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,color:#000 classDef warn fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000 class L success class D,M,O fail class E,F,G,H,I,J,K mfa class N warn ``` --- ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Authentication Factor Strength Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Type 1
Knowledge
Password PIN] --> A2[Type 2
Possession
Token Smart Card OTP] A2 --> A3[Type 3
Inherence
Biometric Iris Face] A3 --> A4[Type 4
Behavior
Keystroke Gait] A4 --> A5[Type 5
Context
Location Device Time] B1[Low Security
Single Factor] --> B2[Medium Security
Two Factor 2FA] B2 --> B3[High Security
Multi Factor MFA] B3 --> B4[Very High Security
Adaptive Risk Based] A1 -.-> B1 A2 -.-> B2 A3 -.-> B3 A4 -.-> B3 A5 -.-> B4 classDef factor1 fill:#ffeaa7,stroke:#d63031,color:#000 classDef factor2 fill:#74b9ff,stroke:#0984e3,color:#000 classDef factor3 fill:#55efc4,stroke:#00b894,color:#000 classDef factor4 fill:#a29bfe,stroke:#6c5ce7,color:#000 classDef factor5 fill:#fd79a8,stroke:#e84393,color:#000 class A1 factor1 class A2 factor2 class A3 factor3 class A4 factor4 class A5 factor5 ``` --- ## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Single Sign-On (SSO) Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB U[End User] --> IDP[Identity Provider IdP
e.g. Okta Azure AD] IDP -->|SAML Assertion| SP1[Service Provider 1
e.g. Salesforce] IDP -->|SAML Assertion| SP2[Service Provider 2
e.g. Slack] IDP -->|SAML Assertion| SP3[Service Provider 3
e.g. GitHub] SP1 --> APP1[Salesforce Dashboard] SP2 --> APP2[Slack Workspace] SP3 --> APP3[GitHub Repositories] IDP --> AD[Active Directory
User Directory] IDP --> MFA[MFA Engine
DUO RSA] classDef idp fill:#a29bfe,stroke:#6c5ce7,color:#000 classDef sp fill:#74b9ff,stroke:#0984e3,color:#000 classDef app fill:#55efc4,stroke:#00b894,color:#000 classDef backend fill:#ffeaa7,stroke:#d63031,color:#000 class IDP,AD,MFA idp class SP1,SP2,SP3 sp class APP1,APP2,APP3 app class U backend ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. Differentiate between Authentication and Authorization with suitable examples.** *(3 Marks)* `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** Authentication is the process of **verifying the identity** of a user or system, whereas Authorization is the process of **determining access rights** for an already-authenticated entity. | Aspect | Authentication (AuthN) | Authorization (AuthZ) | |--------|------------------------|----------------------| | **Question** | "Who are you?" | "What can you do?" | | **Example** | Entering username + password to log in | An HR manager accessing salary data based on role | | **Mechanism** | Credentials, biometrics, tokens | RBAC, ACLs, ABAC policies | **Example:** When a user logs into Gmail with their credentials, **authentication** occurs. Once logged in, the user can only send emails but cannot access Google's internal admin console — this is **authorization**. Authentication always precedes authorization in the access control workflow. --- ### **Q2. List and explain any three authentication factors with examples.** *(3 Marks)* `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** The three primary authentication factors are: 1. **Something You Know (Knowledge Factor):** Information known only to the user, such as a password, PIN, or the answer to a security question. *Example:* A 6-digit ATM PIN. 2. **Something You Have (Possession Factor):** A physical or virtual token in the user's possession. *Example:* A smartphone receiving an OTP via SMS, or a hardware token like YubiKey. 3. **Something You Are (Biometric Factor):** A unique physiological or behavioral characteristic. *Example:* Fingerprint scan used to unlock a smartphone, or facial recognition at airport immigration. For stronger security, **multi-factor authentication (MFA)** combines two or more of these factors. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) ### **Question A — Option 1** *(14 Marks)* `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` #### **Q.A.(a)** Explain the Kerberos authentication protocol with a neat diagram. List its merits and demerits. *(7 Marks)* **Model Answer:** **Kerberos** is a **ticket-based symmetric key authentication protocol** developed at MIT (RFC 4120) and widely used in Microsoft Active Directory environments. It uses a centralized **Key Distribution Center (KDC)** that comprises an **Authentication Server (AS)** and a **Ticket-Granting Server (TGS)**. **Working — 6 Steps:** 1. **AS-REQ:** Client sends username and TGS identifier (in plaintext) to the AS. 2. **AS-REP:** AS verifies the user's password-derived key and returns a **TGT** (Ticket-Granting Ticket) encrypted with the TGS secret key, plus a client-TGS session key. 3. **TGS-REQ:** Client requests a service ticket for a specific server by sending the TGT plus an authenticator. 4. **TGS-REP:** TGS verifies the TGT, checks the PAC (Privilege Attribute Certificate), and issues a **service ticket** encrypted with the server's key, plus a client-server session key. 5. **AP-REQ:** Client presents the service ticket to the target server. 6. **AP-REP:** Server returns an encrypted timestamp to the client, completing **mutual authentication**. **Merits:** - ✅ **Mutual authentication** — both client and server prove identity. - ✅ **Single Sign-On (SSO)** — TGT enables access to multiple services. - ✅ **No plaintext password transmission** on the network. - ✅ **Replay-resistant** via timestamps and nonces. - ✅ **Scalable** via hierarchical realms with cross-realm trust. **Demerits:** - ❌ **Single point of failure** — KDC compromise breaks the entire domain. - ❌ **Requires synchronized clocks** (within 5 minutes per RFC 4120). - ❌ **Vulnerable to password guessing** on the AS-REQ (offline dictionary attack). - ❌ **Kerberoasting attacks** — attackers request service tickets and crack them offline. - ❌ **Complex setup and management** in multi-realm environments. #### **Q.A.(b)** With a suitable diagram, explain the working of Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA). Why is MFA considered superior to single-factor authentication? *(7 Marks)* **Model Answer:** **Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)** is a security mechanism that requires users to provide **two or more independent authentication factors** from different factor categories before access is granted. **Working (Refer Section 4.3 Diagram):** 1. User enters **username and password** (Type 1 — Knowledge factor). 2. If valid, the system triggers a **second-factor challenge**. 3. The user completes the challenge via one of: - **OTP** (SMS / email / authenticator app) — Type 2 (Possession) - **Biometric scan** (fingerprint / face) — Type 3 (Inherence) - **Hardware token response** — Type 2 (Possession) 4. All factors are verified independently. 5. Only if **all factors succeed**, a session token is issued. **Why MFA is Superior — Mathematical Justification:** The probability of bypassing MFA follows the **independent failure model**: $$ P_{MFA} = P_1 \times P_2 \times P_3 \cdots P_n $$ This is **multiplicative**, not additive. For example: - Password alone: $P = 10^{-6}$ - Password + OTP: $P = 10^{-6} \times 10^{-6} = 10^{-12}$ - Password + OTP + Biometric: $P = 10^{-6} \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{-5} = 10^{-17}$ **Key advantages:** - ✅ Mitigates **credential stuffing** and **phishing** attacks. - ✅ **Defense in depth** — even if one factor is compromised, the attacker still needs to defeat the others. - ✅ Aligns with **NIST SP 800-63B AAL2/AAL3** standards. - ✅ Reduces **account takeover (ATO) risk by 99.9%** (per Microsoft 2019 study). --- ### **Question B — Option 2** *(14 Marks)* `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` #### **Q.B.(a)** Explain the concept of biometric authentication. Discuss FAR, FRR, and CER with their significance. *(7 Marks)* **Model Answer:** **Biometric authentication** verifies identity based on the user's **unique physiological or behavioral characteristics**. It falls under the **Type 3 (Inherence) authentication factor**. **Common biometric modalities:** - **Physiological:** Fingerprint, iris, retina, facial geometry, hand geometry, DNA. - **Behavioral:** Voiceprint, keystroke dynamics, gait, signature. **Authentication Process:** 1. **Enrollment** — User's biometric sample is captured, processed via feature extraction, and stored as a **template**. 2. **Verification** — During login, a live sample is captured, features are extracted, and compared to the stored template. 3. **Decision** — Based on a similarity threshold, the system accepts or rejects. **Key Performance Metrics:** - **False Accept Rate (FAR):** Probability that an **impostor is accepted** as genuine. $$FAR = \frac{\text{Number of False Accepts}}{\text{Total Impostor Attempts}}$$ Represents a **security failure** (Type II error). Lower FAR = better security. - **False Reject Rate (FRR):** Probability that a **genuine user is rejected**. $$FRR = \frac{\text{Number of False Rejects}}{\text{Total Genuine Attempts}}$$ Represents a **usability failure** (Type I error). Lower FRR = better usability. - **Crossover Error Rate (CER) / Equal Error Rate (EER):** The operating point where $FAR = FRR$. $$CER: FAR(\tau) = FRR(\tau)$$ This is the **industry-standard benchmark** — **lower CER indicates a more accurate biometric system**. **Significance:** A well-designed biometric system balances FAR and FRR based on use-case criticality. For high-security facilities (e.g., nuclear plants), FAR is minimized even at the cost of higher FRR. For consumer devices (e.g., smartphones), FRR is minimized for usability. **Challenges:** Privacy concerns, template theft, liveness detection (anti-spoofing), and the **irrevocability** of compromised biometric data (unlike passwords, you cannot change your fingerprint). #### **Q.B.(b)** Compare and contrast OAuth 2.0, OpenID Connect, and SAML 2.0. When would you choose each? *(7 Marks)* **Model Answer:** | Feature | OAuth 2.0 | OpenID Connect (OIDC) | SAML 2.0 | |---------|-----------|------------------------|----------| | **Primary Purpose** | Authorization (delegated access) | Authentication + Identity layer on OAuth 2.0 | Authentication + Authorization (XML-based) | | **Token Format** | Opaque or JWT Bearer Access Token | **ID Token** (JWT) + Access Token | **SAML Assertion** (XML) | | **Year Introduced** | 2012 (RFC 6749) | 2014 (built on OAuth 2.0) | 2005 (OASIS Standard) | | **Transport** | HTTP/JSON | HTTP/JSON (REST-friendly) | HTTP/SOAP/XML | | **Typical Use Case** | API access, mobile apps, third-party integrations | "Login with Google/Facebook", modern SSO | Enterprise B2B SSO, legacy systems | | **Identity Info Returned** | No user identity (just scope/permissions) | User profile claims via UserInfo endpoint | Rich attribute statements in the assertion | | **Token Validation** | Introspection endpoint or JWT signature | JWT signature verification (JWKs endpoint) | XML signature verification | | **Browser Flow** | Authorization Code + PKCE | Authorization Code + PKCE + ID Token | HTTP Redirect + POST binding (SAMLRequest/SAMLResponse) | | **Mobile-Friendly** | ✅ Yes (native flow) | ✅ Yes | ❌ Browser-based only | **When to Choose:** - **Choose OAuth 2.0** when your application needs to **access APIs on behalf of a user** without handling credentials (e.g., a third-party app accessing a user's Google Drive). *Example: A photo-printing app accessing Google Photos.* - **Choose OpenID Connect (OIDC)** when you need **federated authentication + modern REST APIs** (e.g., "Sign in with Google"). It is the de-facto standard for **modern web/mobile SSO**. - **Choose SAML 2.0** when integrating with **legacy enterprise systems**, **cross-organization federations**, or government/regulated environments (e.g., banking, healthcare) that mandate XML-based assertions. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mistakes Students Make:** > > 1. **Conflating OAuth 2.0 with OpenID Connect:** OAuth 2.0 is **NOT** an authentication protocol — it is an **authorization framework**. OIDC adds authentication on top of it. Writing "OAuth is used for login" loses 2–3 marks. > > 2. **Skipping the mathematical justification in MFA questions:** Always state the **multiplicative probability formula** $P = P_1 \times P_2 \times \ldots \times P_n$ with at least one numerical example. > > 3. **Omitting the difference between FAR and FRR:** Many students write "CER is when FAR equals FRR" but forget to explain its **significance** as a system-tuning benchmark. > > 4. **Not drawing the Kerberos diagram:** Kerberos is a 6-step process — a textual description without the sequence diagram typically loses 2–3 marks. > > 5. **Confusing hashing with encryption in password storage:** Hashing is **one-way** (Argon2, bcrypt), encryption is **two-way** (AES). Passwords must always be **hashed, never encrypted**. > > 6. **Forgetting to mention salting and peppering** in password storage answers — this is a high-frequency exam point. > > 7. **Missing the mutual authentication aspect of Kerberos** — Step 6 (AP-REP) is often skipped by students, costing 1 mark. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Authentication** ### 🔑 Core Concepts - **Authentication (AuthN)** = "Who are you?" → precedes **Authorization (AuthZ)** = "What can you do?" - The **AAA triad**: Authentication, Authorization, Accounting (auditing). - **Five factor types**: Knowledge (Type 1), Possession (Type 2), Inherence/Biometric (Type 3), Behavioral (Type 4), Contextual/Location (Type 5). ### 🔐 Password Security - Entropy: $H = L \cdot \log_2(R)$ in bits. - Brute-force time: $T = \frac{R^L}{2 \cdot N_{hash/s}}$. - Always store as **salted hash** (Argon2id / bcrypt / scrypt / PBKDF2). - NIST SP 800-63B: min length **8**, no mandatory complexity, screen against **breached password lists (HIBP)**. ### 🛡️ Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) - Combines **≥ 2 factors from different categories**. - Compromise probability: **multiplicative**, not additive. - **Microsoft 2019 study:** MFA blocks **99.9%** of automated account takeover attacks. ### 🧬 Biometrics - Performance: **FAR** (security), **FRR** (usability), **CER/EER** (benchmark — lower is better). - Modalities: fingerprint, iris, face, voice, keystroke dynamics. - Concerns: **irrevocability**, template theft, liveness/spoofing detection. ### 🌐 Authentication Protocols - **Kerberos v5** — ticket-based, mutual auth, SSO via TGT; uses KDC (AS + TGS); 6-step flow. - **RADIUS** — UDP-based, network access (Wi-Fi/VPN), combines AuthN + AuthZ. - **TACACS+** — TCP-based, full-payload encryption, Cisco device admin AAA. - **OAuth 2.0** — authorization framework, **NOT** authentication. - **OpenID Connect (OIDC)** — authentication layer on top of OAuth 2.0; ID Token (JWT). - **SAML 2.0** — XML-based, enterprise SSO, B2B federation. - **FIDO2 / WebAuthn** — passwordless, public-key cryptography, phishing-resistant. ### 📊 Standards & Frameworks - **NIST SP 800-63B** — Digital Identity Guidelines (AAL1/AAL2/AAL3). - **NIST SP 800-53** — Access Control family (AC-2, AC-3, AC-6). - **NIST SP 800-132** — Password-based key derivation. - **NIST SP 800-207** — Zero Trust Architecture. - **ISO/IEC 27001:2022** — Annex A.8.2 (Privileged access rights), A.8.5 (Secure authentication). - **OWASP ASVS** — Application Security Verification Standard (V2 Authentication). ### ⚠️ Top Attack Vectors (Risk Analysis Perspective) - **Credential stuffing** — using leaked credentials from breaches. - **Phishing** — fake login pages stealing credentials. - **Brute force / dictionary attacks** — automated password guessing. - **Man-in-the-Middle (MitM)** — intercepting authentication tokens. - **Kerberoasting** — cracking service tickets in AD environments. - **Biometric spoofing** — fake fingerprints/faces bypassing scanners. - **Replay attacks** — capturing and re-sending valid tokens (mitigated by nonces/timestamps). ### 🎯 Best Practices Summary 1. **Enforce MFA** on all critical accounts (AAL2 minimum). 2. **Use passwordless** authentication (FIDO2/WebAuthn) where possible. 3. **Hash + salt** passwords with Argon2id; never store plaintext. 4. **Implement SSO** (SAML/OIDC) to reduce password fatigue. 5. **Apply risk-based authentication** with adaptive challenges. 6. **Audit and log** all authentication events to SIEM. 7. **Lock accounts** after 5–10 failed attempts (with exponential backoff). 8. **Adopt Zero Trust** — never trust, always verify.

topics.title: Authentication Schemes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT418 - SECURITY ASSESSMENT AND RISK ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Authentication Schemes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Authentication Scheme (ISO/IEC 24760 / NIST SP 800-63 Compliant Definition):**
> An **Authentication Scheme** is a formally specified, cryptographically verifiable mechanism used by an information system to confirm the **claimed identity** of a principal (user, device, or process) through the presentation and validation of one or more **authentication factors** drawn from the categories of *Knowledge*, *Possession*, and *Inherence*, evaluated against a previously enrolled identity template under a defined security policy.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context of **Security Assessment and Risk Analysis**, authentication schemes are evaluated not merely as controls, but as **risk-mitigation instruments** that reduce the probability of an unauthorized principal gaining access to a CIA-protected asset (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability). The strength of any authentication scheme is quantified by its **resistance to identity spoofing**, **false acceptance**, and **session hijacking** under defined threat models.

## 1.2 The Three Canonical Authentication Factors

Every authentication scheme, regardless of complexity, ultimately depends on one or more of these three universally accepted factors (per **NIST SP 800-63B**):

| Factor Class | What is Verified | Typical Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Knowledge (Something you Know)** | Information memorized by the user | Passwords, PINs, Security Questions, Passphrases |
| **Possession (Something you Have)** | A physical/digital artifact under user control | Smart Cards, Hardware Tokens, OTPs, Mobile Devices |
| **Inherence (Something you Are)** | Biometric traits unique to the user | Fingerprint, Iris, Face Geometry, Voice, Keystroke Dynamics |

> [!NOTE]
> **Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)** is achieved only when authentication relies on **two or more factors from DIFFERENT classes**. Using two passwords is *not* MFA — it is merely single-factor repetition.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Airport Security Checkpoint"

Imagine you are boarding a flight from Kochi to Dubai. The airport deploys a **layered authentication scheme**:

1. **Ticket Check (Knowledge Factor):** You must present your **PNR number** and **ID proof** — something only you (the legitimate passenger) should know.
2. **Boarding Pass Scan (Possession Factor):** You scan a **barcoded boarding pass** tied to your booking — a physical artifact issued only after identity verification.
3. **Biometric e-Gate (Inherence Factor):** Your **fingerprint or iris** is matched against the immigration database — a biological trait that cannot be lost, lent, or stolen easily.

Each layer **raises the attacker's cost and time** to impersonate you. If one factor is compromised (say, a screenshot of your boarding pass leaks), the biometric gate still protects you. This is precisely the philosophy of **defense-in-depth authentication** that KTU examiners expect students to articulate.

## 1.4 Authentication vs. Authorization — A Critical Distinction

> [!WARNING]
> **Examination Pitfall:** Students frequently conflate these two terms. The KTU board examiner will deduct marks if you use them interchangeably.

$$ \text{Authentication} \rightarrow \text{"Who are you?"} \quad (\text{Identity Verification}) $$

$$ \text{Authorization} \rightarrow \text{"What are you allowed to do?"} \quad (\text{Privilege Enforcement}) $$

Authentication happens *first*; authorization depends on a successful authenticated session.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Authentication Schemes — The Engineering View

Authentication schemes can be classified along four orthogonal axes that the KTU syllabus emphasizes:

### Axis 1: By Number of Factors
- **Single-Factor Authentication (SFA):** Relies on one factor class (e.g., password-only login)
- **Two-Factor Authentication (2FA):** Combines two factor classes
- **Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA):** Three or more factors or layered 2FA with risk signals

### Axis 2: By Token Type
- **Static Token Schemes:** Passwords, PINs (replayable)
- **One-Time Password (OTP) Schemes:** Time-based (TOTP, RFC 6238) or HMAC-based (HOTP, RFC 4226)
- **Challenge-Response Schemes:** Server issues a nonce, client computes response using shared secret

### Axis 3: By Cryptographic Mechanism
- **Symmetric-Key Schemes:** Kerberos (uses KDC with shared keys)
- **Asymmetric-Key Schemes:** X.509 certificate-based authentication (PKI)
- **Zero-Knowledge Proof Schemes:** User proves knowledge of secret without revealing it (e.g., Schnorr, ZK-SNARKs)

### Axis 4: By Architectural Distribution
- **Centralized Schemes:** Single IdP (Identity Provider) — e.g., LDAP, Active Directory
- **Federated Schemes:** Cross-domain trust — e.g., SAML 2.0, OpenID Connect, OAuth 2.0
- **Decentralized Schemes:** Blockchain-based DID (Decentralized Identifiers)

## 2.2 Operational Logic of a Generic Authentication Scheme

The scheme executes in a deterministic five-step protocol:

1. **Enrollment Phase:** The principal submits identity attributes and chosen factors; the system creates a **template** (hashed password, biometric template, public key certificate).
2. **Claim Phase:** The principal asserts identity via a username, certificate, or token identifier.
3. **Challenge Phase:** The verifier issues a challenge (nonce, CAPTCHA, biometric prompt).
4. **Response Phase:** The principal presents the requested factor(s).
5. **Verification Phase:** The verifier compares the response to the stored template within a defined **tolerance threshold** and issues an authenticated session token upon success.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master every equation in this table — these appear frequently in Part A, Part B, and competitive KTU assessments.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation / Definition | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Password Entropy (Shannon)** | $H = L \cdot \log_2(N)$ | Measures unpredictability in **bits**; $L$ = length, $N$ = charset size |
| 2 | **Keyspace Size** | $K = N^L$ | Total possible passwords — sets brute-force search space |
| 3 | **False Acceptance Rate (FAR)** | $FAR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Probability impostor is accepted — security metric |
| 4 | **False Rejection Rate (FRR)** | $FRR = \dfrac{FN}{FN + TP}$ | Probability genuine user is rejected — usability metric |
| 5 | **Equal Error Rate (EER)** | $EER = FAR = FRR$ | Threshold tuning target — lower is better |
| 6 | **Combined MFA Bypass Probability** | $P_{compromise} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} p_i$ | Joint probability of breaking *all* $n$ factors |
| 7 | **Biometric CER (Crossover Error Rate)** | $CER = \min_t \left( \dfrac{FAR(t) + FRR(t)}{2} \right)$ | Operating-point identifier for biometric system tuning |
| 8 | **Kerberos Clock Skew Bound** | $\Delta t \leq 5 \text{ min (default)}$ | Maximum allowed time difference between KDC and client |
| 9 | **TOTP Code Validity** | $T = \lfloor (t - t_0) / X \rfloor \bmod 10^d$ | $X$ = time step (30s), $d$ = digit count, $t_0$ = epoch |
| 10 | **Challenge-Response Resistance** | $R = 2^n \text{ attempts}$ | $n$ = nonce bit length — required to defeat replay |

> **Boundary Conditions & Assumptions:**
> - All log values are base-2 unless specified; entropy in **bits**.
> - FAR and FRR are dimensionless probabilities in $[0, 1]$.
> - For a perfectly secure system: $H \to \infty$, $FAR \to 0$, $FRR \to 0$.
> - $P_{compromise}$ assumes independent factors (rare in practice — true dependency requires Bayesian revision).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Sector | Dominant Authentication Scheme | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **Banking (e.g., SBI, Federal Bank)** | Password + OTP (2FA) | Low deployment cost, regulatory compliance (RBI mandate) |
| **Defense / Government** | Smart Card + Biometric (CAC/PIV) | High-assurance, FIPS 201 compliance |
| **Cloud (AWS, Azure, GCP)** | Certificate-based + MFA + Risk-Based | Federated SSO with adaptive trust |
| **Healthcare (HIPAA)** | Biometric + SSO | Audit traceability and non-repudiation |
| **IoT / Embedded** | Lightweight mutual auth (DTLS) | Constrained device capabilities |

The scheme chosen for a system is a **risk-treatment decision** — it directly maps to the *likelihood* and *impact* components of the KTU 2024 risk-assessment rubric (ISO 31000 / NIST 800-30).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Password Entropy and Search-Space Sizing

We derive the **information-theoretic strength** of a password policy and the minimum attacker workload.

**Step 1 — Single-Character Information Content.**
If a character is drawn uniformly from a charset of size $N$, the information per character is:

$$ H_1 = \log_2(N) \text{ bits} $$

**Step 2 — Multi-Character Extension.**
For a password of length $L$ with independent characters, total entropy is the sum of per-character entropies:

$$ H = \sum_{i=1}^{L} \log_2(N) = L \cdot \log_2(N) \text{ bits} $$

**Step 3 — Keyspace Conversion.**
The total number of candidate passwords (the brute-force keyspace) is the exponentiation of entropy:

$$ K = 2^H = 2^{L \cdot \log_2(N)} = N^L $$

**Step 4 — Expected Attack Workload.**
On average, an attacker must search half the keyspace:

$$ W_{avg} = \frac{K}{2} = \frac{N^L}{2} $$

**Worked Example (KTU Examination Style):**
Consider a policy: $L = 10$ characters, $N = 94$ (printable ASCII excluding space).

$$ H = 10 \cdot \log_2(94) $$

$$ \log_2(94) = \frac{\ln(94)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{4.5433}{0.6931} = 6.5546 \text{ bits} $$

$$ H = 10 \times 6.5546 = 65.546 \text{ bits} $$

$$ K = 94^{10} \approx 5.38 \times 10^{19} \text{ candidates} $$

$$ W_{avg} \approx 2.69 \times 10^{19} \text{ attempts} $$

> A GPU rig performing $10^{10}$ guesses/second would require approximately $2.69 \times 10^{9}$ seconds $\approx$ **85 years** to brute-force on average.

## 3.2 Derivation: Combined Multi-Factor Compromise Probability

Given $n$ independent factors with individual compromise probabilities $p_1, p_2, \dots, p_n$, derive the joint bypass probability.

**Step 1 — Independence Assumption.**
For an attacker to defeat MFA, **every** factor must be bypassed.

**Step 2 — Joint Probability via Multiplication Rule.**

$$ P_{compromise} = P(\text{Fail}_1 \cap \text{Fail}_2 \cap \cdots \cap \text{Fail}_n) $$

**Step 3 — Apply Independence.**

$$ P_{compromise} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} p_i $$

**Step 4 — Worked Numerical Evaluation.**
Consider a 3-factor system: Password ($p_1 = 10^{-6}$ — strong, hashed), OTP ($p_2 = 10^{-4}$ — vulnerable to SIM swap), Biometric ($p_3 = 10^{-5}$ — spoofable).

$$ P_{compromise} = 10^{-6} \times 10^{-4} \times 10^{-5} = 10^{-15} $$

This is astronomically smaller than the single-factor baseline, justifying the **risk-reduction ROI** of MFA deployment.

## 3.3 Derivation: Equal Error Rate (EER) and Optimal Threshold

The biometric decision threshold $t$ governs the FAR–FRR trade-off. The system accepts a sample if the similarity score $s \geq t$.

**Step 1 — Define FAR as a Function of Threshold.**
As $t$ decreases, more impostors are accepted:

$$ FAR(t) = \int_{t}^{\infty} f_{impostor}(s)\, ds $$

**Step 2 — Define FRR as a Function of Threshold.**
As $t$ decreases, fewer genuine users are rejected:

$$ FRR(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} f_{genuine}(s)\, ds $$

**Step 3 — Define CER (Crossover Error Rate).**

$$ CER = \min_{t} \frac{FAR(t) + FRR(t)}{2} $$

**Step 4 — EER Condition.**
The EER occurs where the two error curves intersect:

$$ FAR(t^*) = FRR(t^*) $$

Solving this equation for $t^*$ gives the threshold that minimizes total classification error — the recommended operating point for high-security biometric systems.

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU exam purposes, you are not required to derive the distribution functions $f_{genuine}$ and $f_{impostor}$ — simply state that they are typically modeled as **Gaussian distributions** with parameters $(\mu_g, \sigma_g)$ and $(\mu_i, \sigma_i)$ respectively.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Scheme Selection by Risk Profile

| Risk Profile | Recommended Scheme | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **Low** (public blog) | Password only | Cost-effective; minimal asset value |
| **Medium** (e-commerce) | Password + OTP (2FA) | Balanced UX and security |
| **High** (banking) | Password + OTP + Biometric (MFA) + Risk-Based | Multi-layer defense, regulatory compliance |
| **Very High** (military) | Smart Card (PKI) + Biometric + Hardware Token | Highest assurance (EAL 5+) |
| **Critical Infrastructure** | Mutual TLS + HSM + Continuous Authentication | Zero-trust architecture |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Authentication Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[User Principal] --> B[Authentication Client]
    B --> C{Scheme Selection Engine}
    C --> D[Knowledge Factor Module]
    C --> E[Possession Factor Module]
    C --> F[Inherence Factor Module]
    C --> G[Risk Engine Context]
    D --> H[Factor Aggregator]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I{Threshold Satisfied?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Session Token Issued]
    I -->|No| K[Access Denied / Step-up Auth]
    J --> L[Authorization Layer]
    K --> M[Audit Log + Alert]
```

## 4.2 Kerberos Authentication Sequence (Symmetric-Key Scheme)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User Client
    participant AS as Auth Server
    participant TGS as Ticket Granting Server
    participant S as Target Service

    U->>AS: 1. Request TGT (plaintext ID)
    AS->>U: 2. Encrypted TGT + Session Key
    U->>TGS: 3. Present TGT + Service Request
    TGS->>U: 4. Service Ticket (encrypted)
    U->>S: 5. Present Service Ticket
    S->>U: 6. Mutual Auth Confirmation
```

## 4.3 Biometric Authentication Architecture (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Biometric Sensor] --> S2[Pre-processing Module]
    S2 --> S3[Feature Extraction]
    S3 --> S4[Template Generator]
    S4 --> S5{Storage Path}
    S5 -->|Enrolment| S6[Template Database]
    S5 -->|Verification| S7[Matcher]
    S6 --> S7
    S7 --> S8[Decision Threshold t]
    S8 -->|Match| S9[Accept / Grant Access]
    S8 -->|Mismatch| S10[Reject / Log]
```

## 4.4 Multi-Factor Authentication Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Login Initiated] --> Ctx[Context Capture: IP, Device, Geo, Time]
    Ctx --> Risk{Risk Score Computation}
    Risk -->|Low Risk| Low[Password Only]
    Risk -->|Medium Risk| Med[Password + OTP]
    Risk -->|High Risk| High[Password + OTP + Biometric]
    Low --> Check1{Factor Valid?}
    Med --> Check2{All Factors Valid?}
    High --> Check3{All 3 Factors Valid?}
    Check1 -->|Yes| Grant[Session Granted]
    Check1 -->|No| Deny[Access Denied]
    Check2 -->|Yes| Grant
    Check2 -->|No| Deny
    Check3 -->|Yes| Grant
    Check3 -->|No| Deny
```

```mermaid
        X1[LDAP / Active Directory]
    end

    Decentralized --> R

```
