# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between authentication and authorization. List the three classical authentication factors with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Authentication | Authorization |
|---|---|---|
| **Question Answered** | "Who are you?" | "What can you do?" |
| **Phase** | Pre-session | Post-session |
| **Mechanism** | Credential verification | Policy/role enforcement |
| **Output** | Authenticated identity | Access decision |

**Three Authentication Factors (1 Mark each):**
1. **Knowledge Factor** — Password, PIN, security question
2. **Possession Factor** — Smart card, hardware token, mobile OTP
3. **Inherence Factor** — Fingerprint, iris scan, facial recognition

> [!NOTE]
> For full marks, students must explicitly state the order: *authentication precedes authorization*.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)? Why is using two passwords not considered MFA?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):** MFA is an authentication scheme that requires the user to present evidence from **two or more distinct factor categories** (Knowledge, Possession, Inherence) to gain access, thereby reducing the probability of unauthorized access through factor diversity.

**Justification (1 Mark):** Using two passwords fails the MFA definition because both belong to the **same factor class (Knowledge)**. If an attacker phishes one password, the same attack vector likely compromises the second. True MFA requires **factor heterogeneity** so that compromising one factor does not imply compromise of the others (e.g., password compromise does not leak the user's fingerprint).

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Common mark-loss areas include (a) failing to state the *threat model* the scheme defends against, (b) omitting the **false acceptance vs. false rejection trade-off** when describing biometric systems, and (c) presenting Kerberos without the **clock-skew assumption** that justifies its timestamp-based ticket validity.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the architecture of **Kerberos authentication scheme** with a clear sequence diagram. Discuss its strengths and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe **certificate-based authentication** using X.509 PKI. How does it differ from password-based authentication in terms of trust model? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Kerberos Architecture (4 Marks):**

Kerberos is a **trusted third-party authentication protocol** designed for client-server applications over insecure networks. It uses **symmetric-key cryptography** and timestamps to defeat replay attacks.

**Key Components:**
- **Client (C):** The user/workstation requesting service
- **Authentication Server (AS):** Verifies user identity, issues TGT
- **Ticket Granting Server (TGS):** Issues service-specific tickets
- **Service Server (S):** The target resource

**Sequence Diagram (must be drawn in exam):**

| Step | Message | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C \rightarrow AS$: $ID_C \vert ID_{TGS}$ | Initial authentication request |
| 2 | $AS \rightarrow C$: $E_{K_C}[K_{C,TGS} \vert ID_{TGS} \vert TS_2 \vert Lifetime_2 \vert TGT]$ | Encrypted TGT issued |
| 3 | $C \rightarrow TGS$: $ID_S \vert TGT \vert Authenticator$ | Service ticket request |
| 4 | $TGS \rightarrow C$: $E_{K_{C,S}}[K_{C,S} \vert ID_S \vert TS_4 \vert Ticket_S]$ | Service ticket granted |
| 5 | $C \rightarrow S$: $Ticket_S \vert Authenticator_2$ | Service access |
| 6 | $S \rightarrow C$: $E_{K_{C,S}}[TS_5 + 1]$ | Mutual authentication |

**Strengths (1.5 Marks):**
- Mutual authentication between client and server
- Replay attack resistance via timestamps and nonces
- Single Sign-On (SSO) capability across multiple services
- Passwords never traverse the network (only hashed via KDC)

**Limitations (1.5 Marks):**
- **Single point of failure** — KDC compromise = total breach
- **Clock synchronization requirement** ($\Delta t \leq 5$ minutes)
- Vulnerable to **offline password guessing** if KDC database is leaked
- Scalability constrained by symmetric-key distribution complexity

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**X.509 Certificate-Based Authentication (4 Marks):**

In PKI-based authentication, each principal holds a **public-private key pair**. The public key is embedded in an **X.509 digital certificate** signed by a trusted **Certificate Authority (CA)**.

**Authentication Flow:**
1. Client presents its X.509 certificate to the server.
2. Server validates the certificate chain up to a **trusted root CA**.
3. Server issues a random challenge (nonce) to the client.
4. Client signs the nonce with its **private key**.
5. Server verifies the signature using the **public key** from the certificate.
6. Upon success, mutual TLS session is established.

**Trust Model Comparison (3 Marks):**

| Dimension | Password-Based | Certificate-Based (PKI) |
|---|---|---|
| **Trust Anchor** | Server's password hash database | Trusted Root CA hierarchy |
| **Secret Location** | Server side (vulnerable to DB breach) | User side (private key never leaves user) |
| **Phishing Resistance** | Low (passwords are replayable) | High (signatures are non-replayable) |
| **Man-in-the-Middle Risk** | High | Low (mutual TLS) |
| **Revocation Mechanism** | Password reset | CRL or OCSP |
| **Deployment Cost** | Low | High (CA infrastructure required) |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** What is **biometric authentication**? Explain the metrics **False Acceptance Rate (FAR)** and **False Rejection Rate (FRR)**. Define the **Equal Error Rate (EER)** and explain its significance. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **Risk-Based Authentication (RBA)** with a suitable use case. How does it improve upon static authentication schemes? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Biometric Authentication (2 Marks):**
Biometric authentication verifies a user's identity by measuring and validating **unique physiological or behavioral characteristics** (fingerprint, iris, voice, gait, keystroke dynamics). It relies on the *inherence* factor and provides strong non-repudiation.

**FAR and FRR Definitions (2 Marks):**

$$ FAR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} \quad \text{(Security Metric — impostors accepted)} $$

$$ FRR = \frac{FN}{FN + TP} \quad \text{(Usability Metric — genuine users rejected)} $$

**FAR-FRR Trade-off (1 Mark):**
As the decision threshold $t$ is tightened, FAR decreases (more secure) but FRR increases (more false rejects). A loose threshold does the opposite. The system designer must select an operating point based on the use case (e.g., high security → low FAR tolerance).

**EER Definition and Significance (2 Marks):**
The **Equal Error Rate** is the specific threshold $t^*$ where $FAR(t^*) = FRR(t^*)$. It is the **single scalar metric** that best summarizes a biometric system's intrinsic accuracy — lower EER indicates a more capable sensor and algorithm. Industry-grade fingerprint systems target EER $< 0.01\%$.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Risk-Based Authentication (RBA) — Definition (3 Marks):**
RBA is an **adaptive authentication scheme** that dynamically adjusts the required authentication strength based on a real-time **risk score** computed from contextual signals such as:
- IP geolocation and known-device fingerprint
- Time-of-day access pattern
- Behavioral analytics (typing speed, mouse dynamics)
- Requested resource sensitivity
- Historical anomaly score

**RBA Decision Flow (2 Marks):**

$$ \text{Risk Score} = w_1 \cdot S_{IP} + w_2 \cdot S_{device} + w_3 \cdot S_{behavior} + w_4 \cdot S_{context} $$

| Risk Score Range | Required Factors |
|---|---|
| $0 - 30$ (Low) | Single factor (password) |
| $31 - 70$ (Medium) | 2FA (password + OTP) |
| $71 - 100$ (High) | MFA + step-up verification |

**Use Case (1 Mark):** **Online Banking Login** — A user logging in from their usual laptop in Kerala at 10 AM experiences low friction (password only). The same user logging in from an unknown device in Russia at 3 AM triggers a high-risk score, demanding OTP + biometric + transaction-time step-up authentication.

**Improvement over Static Schemes (1 Mark):**
- **Reduces user friction** in low-risk scenarios → better UX
- **Strengthens security** in anomalous scenarios → adaptive threat response
- **Cost-effective** — expensive factors are invoked only when justified
- **Continuous authentication** support via behavioral biometrics

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — Authentication Schemes**

- ✅ **Authentication ≠ Authorization** — always state this distinction in KTU answers.
- ✅ **Three Factors:** Knowledge, Possession, Inherence — MFA needs *different* classes.
- ✅ **Password Entropy:** $H = L \cdot \log_2(N)$ — increase $L$ over $N$ for better security.
- ✅ **Keyspace:** $K = N^L$ — sets brute-force difficulty.
- ✅ **FAR** = security metric; **FRR** = usability metric; **EER** = best single accuracy indicator.
- ✅ **Kerberos** uses symmetric keys, timestamps (≤ 5 min skew), KDC, and provides SSO.
- ✅ **PKI / X.509** uses asymmetric keys, CA hierarchy, CRL/OCSP for revocation.
- ✅ **OTP schemes:** TOTP (time-based, RFC 6238) and HOTP (counter-based, RFC 4226).
- ✅ **Risk-Based Authentication (RBA)** uses contextual signals for adaptive factor selection.
- ✅ **Zero-Knowledge Proofs** allow identity verification without revealing the secret.
- ✅ **Biometric systems** are tuned via threshold $t$; CER is the optimal operating point.
- ✅ **Federated schemes** (SAML, OpenID Connect) enable cross-domain trust via IdP.
- ✅ **Threat-Model Linkage:** Authentication scheme strength must be justified against identified threats in the risk register.
- ✅ **NIST SP 800-63B** is the de-facto reference for AAL (Authenticator Assurance Level) 1, 2, 3 definitions.
- ✅ **Defense in Depth:** Combine multiple authentication layers; never rely on a single factor for high-value assets.

> **Final KTU Mantra:** *"The strength of an authentication scheme is not measured by the cryptographic primitive alone, but by its resilience against the weakest link in the human-procedure-technology triad."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Access Control Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT418 - SECURITY ASSESSMENT AND RISK ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Access Control Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Access Control Model** is a formal, mathematically-grounded framework that defines **how subjects (users, processes, or systems) are authorized to interact with objects (resources, files, services)** within an information system. It specifies the policies, rules, and decision logic used by an access control mechanism to permit or deny a given operation.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, access control models are studied as the **policy-decision backbone** of any Risk Analysis exercise — they form the third pillar of the classic security triad **C-I-A (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition to Memorize:** *An Access Control Model is an abstract, formal specification of the access control policy that defines the universe of subjects, objects, rights, and the conditions under which a subject may exercise a right over an object.*

The four fundamental entities of any access control model are:

| Entity | Role | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Subject (S)** | Active entity requesting access | User, Process, Service Account |
| **Object (O)** | Passive resource being protected | File, Database row, API endpoint |
| **Right (R)** | The operation being attempted | Read, Write, Execute, Delete |
| **Permission (P)** | Authorized right tuple (S, O, R) | (Alice, /payroll, Read) |

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Hotel Key Card System

Imagine a luxury hotel:

- **You** are the **Subject**.
- The **guest room, pool, gym, executive lounge** are the **Objects**.
- **Your key card** is the **Access Token** — it is encoded with the rules of an access control model.
- The **front desk reception** acts as the **Reference Monitor** that checks your card against the **policy database** before the door lock (the **enforcement point**) opens.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for Risk Analysis:** In a Security Assessment, auditors do not just ask *"do you have a login screen?"*. They ask *"which access control model is enforced, and is it mathematically consistent with your threat model?"*. A weak or mismatched model is itself a **residual risk**.

### 1.3 The Three Primary Models at a Glance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — High-Priority Models (must know for ESE):**
> 1. **DAC — Discretionary Access Control**
> 2. **MAC — Mandatory Access Control** (Lattice-based, e.g., Bell-LaPadula, Biba)
> 3. **RBAC — Role-Based Access Control** (NIST standard)

Modern extensions include **ABAC (Attribute-Based)**, **RuBAC (Rule-Based)**, and **OrBAC (Organization-Based)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Discretionary Access Control (DAC)

In DAC, the **owner of an object** has full discretion to decide who can access it. The classic representation is the **Access Control Matrix (ACM)**.

**Operational logic:**
- Each object has an **owner** (usually its creator).
- The owner can **grant or revoke** rights to any other subject at will.
- Access decisions are made by **checking a row/column** of the ACM or by traversing a **Capability List (CL)** or **Access Control List (ACL)**.
- The model is **identity-centric** — every decision is keyed on the subject's unique identifier (UID).

**Strengths:** Flexible, easy to implement (file permissions in Unix/Linux/Windows are DAC).
**Weaknesses:** Susceptible to **Trojan Horse attacks**, **confused deputy problem**, and **insider threats** because the owner can inadvertently leak rights.

---

### 2.2 Mandatory Access Control (MAC)

In MAC, access is governed by a **system-wide central authority** and **security labels (clearances + classifications)**. The owner of an object **cannot** override the policy.

**Operational logic:**
- Every subject is assigned a **Clearance Level** (e.g., Unclassified, Confidential, Secret, Top Secret).
- Every object is assigned a **Classification Level** matching the lattice.
- Access decisions follow two well-known formal rules:

> [!NOTE]
> **Bell-LaPadula (BLP) — Confidentiality Model**
> - **No Read Up (NRU / Simple Security Property):** A subject at level $L_s$ can read an object at level $L_o$ **only if** $L_s \geq L_o$.
> - **No Write Down (NWD / \*-Property):** A subject at level $L_s$ can write to an object at level $L_o$ **only if** $L_s \leq L_o$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Biba Model — Integrity Model**
> - **No Read Down (NRD):** A subject may read an object only if its integrity is at least as high.
> - **No Write Up (NWU):** A subject may write only to objects of equal or lower integrity.

**Strengths:** Strongest confidentiality/integrity guarantee, formally verifiable.
**Weaknesses:** Rigid, expensive to administer, not user-friendly.

---

### 2.3 Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)

RBAC, standardized as **NIST INCITS 359**, shifts the access decision from **identity** to **role**.

**Operational logic:**
- Users are assigned to **Roles** (e.g., Doctor, Nurse, Cashier, Admin).
- Roles are assigned **Permissions** (e.g., view_record, prescribe_drug).
- Access is granted if the user's role set intersects the permission set of the resource.
- Supports **Role Hierarchies** (Senior Doctor inherits Junior Doctor's permissions).
- Supports **Separation of Duties (SoD)** — static and dynamic constraints to prevent fraud.

---

### 2.4 Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC)

The most modern and expressive model. Access is decided by evaluating **attributes of the subject, object, action, and environment** against a set of boolean **policy rules**.

**Typical ABAC Policy Form:**
> *"A user can read document D **IF** user.department = 'Finance' **AND** document.classification ≤ 'Confidential' **AND** current_time is between 09:00 and 18:00 **AND** device.trustLevel = 'High'."*

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formal Expression | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Access Control Triple | $\langle S, O, R \rangle$ | Subject, Object, Right |
| Permission Grant | $P = (s, o, r) \in \text{ACM}$ | A cell in the access matrix |
| DAC Owner Privilege | $\forall o \in O,\ \exists \text{owner}(o) \in S$ | Owner has full control |
| BLP — Simple Security | $L_s(s) \geq L_o(o)$ | No Read Up |
| BLP — \*-Property | $L_s(s) \leq L_o(o)$ | No Write Down |
| Biba — Read | $I_s(s) \geq I_o(o)$ | No Read Down |
| Biba — Write | $I_s(s) \leq I_o(o)$ | No Write Up |
| RBAC Permission Check | $u \in U,\ r \in R,\ p \in P:\ (u, r) \in UA \wedge (r, p) \in PA \Rightarrow (u, p) \in$ Access | UA = User-Role, PA = Permission-Assignment |
| ABAC Decision | $\text{decide}(s, o, a) = \text{eval}(\text{policy}(attr(s), attr(o), attr(a), attr(env)))$ | Returns Permit / Deny |
| Separation of Duties (Static) | $\forall u \in U,\ \lvert \{ r \in R \mid (u, r) \in UA \} \rvert \leq k$ | $k$ = mutually exclusive role limit |
| Lattice Dominance | $L_a \geq L_b \iff \forall i,\ a_i \geq b_i$ | Partial order in MAC lattice |
| Trust Zone Boundary | $T(s) \subseteq T(o) \Rightarrow \text{Access Permitted}$ | Used in Zero Trust models |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No vertical bars `|` inside table cells** — all set-cardinality expressions are wrapped using `\lvert` and `\rvert` to preserve markdown table parsing integrity.

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Healthcare (HIPAA):** RBAC for "Doctor / Nurse / Receptionist" — minimum necessary access.
- **Defense / Government (DoD):** MAC with Bell-LaPadula for classified information.
- **Cloud & Microservices (AWS IAM, Azure RBAC, OPA):** Hybrid RBAC + ABAC.
- **Banking:** RBAC + SoD prevents one employee from both *initiating* and *approving* a transaction.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Formal Derivation of the Bell-LaPadula Lattice

The Bell-LaPadula (BLP) model is built on a **lattice** of security levels. We define the lattice step-by-step.

**Step 1 — Define the Set of Security Levels.**
Let $\mathcal{L} = \{ L_0, L_1, L_2, L_3 \}$ be the discrete clearance levels:

$$
\mathcal{L} = \{ \text{Unclassified},\ \text{Confidential},\ \text{Secret},\ \text{Top Secret} \}
$$

**Step 2 — Establish the Dominance Partial Order.**
For any two levels $L_i, L_j \in \mathcal{L}$, we define dominance $L_i \geq L_j$ such that:

$$
L_i \geq L_j \iff \text{classification}(L_i) \geq \text{classification}(L_j)
$$

Example:
$L_3 \geq L_0$ is **true** (Top Secret dominates Unclassified).
$L_1 \geq L_3$ is **false** (Confidential does **not** dominate Top Secret).

**Step 3 — The Simple Security Property (No Read Up).**
A subject $s$ is allowed to read an object $o$ **iff** the subject's clearance dominates the object's classification:

$$
\text{canRead}(s, o) \iff L_s(s) \geq L_o(o)
$$

**Step 4 — The \*-Property (No Write Down).**
A subject $s$ is allowed to write to an object $o$ **iff** the subject's clearance is dominated by the object's classification. This prevents a high-clearance user from *downgrading* sensitive data into a low-classification file:

$$
\text{canWrite}(s, o) \iff L_s(s) \leq L_o(o)
$$

**Step 5 — Tranquility and the Trusted Subject Exception.**
A *Tranquility Principle* states that security labels do not change dynamically during a session:

$$
\forall t \in \text{session},\ L_s(s,t) = L_s(s, t_0) \quad \text{(Strong Tranquility)}
$$

A *Trusted Subject* is allowed to violate the \*-property under controlled conditions (e.g., a declassification officer), but such violations are logged:

$$
\text{TrustedWrite}(s, o) \iff \text{audit}(s, o, t) = \text{recorded}
$$

**Step 6 — Combine to form the Full BLP Access Decision.**

$$
\text{Access}(s, o, \text{read}) = 
\begin{cases}
\text{Permit} & \text{if } L_s(s) \geq L_o(o) \\
\text{Deny} & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

$$
\text{Access}(s, o, \text{write}) = 
\begin{cases}
\text{Permit} & \text{if } L_s(s) \leq L_o(o) \\
\text{Deny} & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

This is the canonical KTU derivation — examiners expect you to write the **two-property formulation** explicitly.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of RBAC Permission Resolution

**Step 1 — Define the Sets.**

$$
U = \{ u_1, u_2, \dots, u_n \} \quad \text{(Users)}
$$

$$
R = \{ r_1, r_2, \dots, r_m \} \quad \text{(Roles)}
$$

$$
P = \{ p_1, p_2, \dots, p_k \} \quad \text{(Permissions)}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the Assignment Relations.**

$$
UA \subseteq U \times R \quad \text{(User-Role Assignment)}
$$

$$
PA \subseteq R \times P \quad \text{(Permission-Assignment)}
$$

**Step 3 — Effective Permission Set for a User.**
For any user $u \in U$, the effective permission set $\mathcal{P}(u)$ is derived by transitively traversing UA and PA:

$$
\mathcal{P}(u) = \{\, p \in P \mid \exists\, r \in R:\ (u, r) \in UA \ \wedge\ (r, p) \in PA \,\}
$$

**Step 4 — Access Decision.**

$$
\text{Access}(u, o, \text{op}) = 
\begin{cases}
\text{Permit} & \text{if } (\text{op}, o) \in \mathcal{P}(u) \\
\text{Deny} & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 5 — Static Separation of Duty Constraint (SoD).**
No single user may hold a conflicting role pair. With conflicting role set $CR \subseteq R \times R$:

$$
\forall u \in U,\ \forall (r_a, r_b) \in CR:\ \lnot\bigl((u, r_a) \in UA \wedge (u, r_b) \in UA\bigr)
$$

This mathematically prevents fraud by enforcing that *one human cannot simultaneously hold two mutually-exclusive roles* (e.g., *Approver* and *Requester*).

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | DAC | MAC | RBAC | ABAC |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Decision basis | Identity of owner | Security labels | Role membership | Attributes + Policy |
| Owner can override policy? | Yes | **No** | No (admin-controlled) | No (policy-controlled) |
| Policy centralization | Low | Very High | High | Very High |
| Granularity | Low–Medium | High | High | Very High |
| Administration cost | Low | High | Medium | Medium–High |
| Formal verifiability | Weak | **Very Strong** | Strong | Strong |
| Trojan Horse resistance | Weak | Strong | Medium | Strong |
| NIST Standard | – | – | INCITS 359 | INCITS 499 (planned) |
| Typical Use | OS file permissions | Military / Classified | Enterprise / Healthcare | Cloud / Zero Trust |
| Exam weightage in KTU | Medium | **High** | **Highest** | Medium-High |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Reference Monitor — Core Enforcement Architecture

This block diagram shows how an access control model is enforced inside a Trusted Computing Base (TCB).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Subject User or Process] -->|Request access to object| B[Reference Monitor]
    B --> C{Policy Decision Point}
    C -->|Query| D[Policy Database ACL or Rules]
    D -->|Permit or Deny| C
    C -->|Permit| E[Enforcement Point]
    C -->|Deny| F[Reject and Log]
    E --> G[Object File or Resource]
    E --> H[Audit Trail]
    F --> H
    B -.->|Tamper proof| I[Trusted Computing Base]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Reference Monitor** is the *only* path through which any access request flows — this is the **complete mediation** principle.
- The **Policy Decision Point (PDP)** evaluates the request against the **Policy Database**.
- The **Enforcement Point (PEP)** physically permits the action.
- **Audit** happens for both permit and deny events.

---

### 4.2 RBAC Role Hierarchy and Permission Inheritance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SuperAdmin[Super Admin Role] --> SysAdmin[System Admin]
    SuperAdmin --> SecAuditor[Security Auditor]
    SysAdmin --> NetAdmin[Network Admin]
    SysAdmin --> DbAdmin[Database Admin]
    NetAdmin --> Helpdesk[Help Desk Operator]
    SecAuditor --> ReadOnlyAuditor[Read Only Auditor]

    SuperAdmin --> P1[Perm manage all users]
    SysAdmin --> P2[Perm create users]
    SecAuditor --> P3[Perm read all logs]
    NetAdmin --> P4[Perm configure firewall]
    DbAdmin --> P5[Perm backup databases]
    Helpdesk --> P6[Perm reset passwords]
    ReadOnlyAuditor --> P7[Perm view audit reports]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Arrows from role to role = **role inheritance** (e.g., SysAdmin inherits SuperAdmin permissions).
- Arrows from role to permission = **permission assignment**.
- A user assigned to *Help Desk* automatically gets *reset passwords* and (transitively) higher-level read rights.

---

### 4.3 Bell-LaPadula Lattice Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L3[Top Secret] --> L2[Secret]
    L2 --> L1[Confidential]
    L1 --> L0[Unclassified]
    L3 -.Read by Subject with clearance >= L3.-> L3
    L2 -.Read by Subject with clearance >= L2.-> L2
    L1 -.Read by Subject with clearance >= L1.-> L1
    L0 -.Read by Any cleared subject.-> L0
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Dominance flows **upward**; equal level is permitted (reflexive).
- A *Secret*-cleared subject **cannot read** a *Top Secret* object.
- A *Top Secret* subject **cannot write down** into a *Secret* or *Unclassified* object (the \*-property).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Discretionary Access Control (DAC) and Mandatory Access Control (MAC).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | DAC | MAC |
|---|---|---|
| Policy authority | Owner of the object decides | Central security authority decides |
| Override allowed? | Yes, by owner | No — labels are non-overridable |
| Mechanism | ACL / Capability lists | Security labels + lattice |
| Trojan-horse risk | High | Low (policy enforced by TCB) |
| Example | Linux file permissions `rwx` | Military classification levels |

**Marking split:** 1 mark for each difference × 2 differences + 1 mark for example = **3 marks**.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the two security properties of the Bell-LaPadula model and explain their purpose.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Simple Security Property (No Read Up):** A subject at clearance $L_s$ may read an object at level $L_o$ only if $L_s \geq L_o$. **Purpose:** Prevent unauthorized disclosure — protect **confidentiality**. (1.5 marks)
2. ***-Property (No Write Down):** A subject at clearance $L_s$ may write to an object at level $L_o$ only if $L_s \leq L_o$. **Purpose:** Prevent high-level data from being leaked to lower-level objects. (1.5 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A** (14 Marks)

> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Modified]`
> **(a)** Explain the **Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)** model in detail. With neat diagrams, describe the **role hierarchy** and **separation of duties** concepts. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **Bell-LaPadula confidentiality model** with the **Simple Security Property** and the **\***-Property. Show how access is decided using a labeled example. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**1. Definition (2 marks):** RBAC is an access control model where permissions are associated with **roles** rather than individual users. Users acquire permissions by being assigned to one or more roles. It is standardized as **NIST INCITS 359**.

**2. Core Components (2 marks):**

- **User (U):** Human or autonomous agent.
- **Role (R):** Job function or responsibility (e.g., Doctor, Cashier).
- **Permission (P):** Approval to perform an operation (e.g., view_record, prescribe_drug).
- **Session (S):** A mapping such that a user activates a subset of his assigned roles.

**3. Role Hierarchy (2 marks):**
A role hierarchy is a partial order $R$ where $r_1 \geq r_2$ means $r_1$ inherits all permissions of $r_2$. Example: *Chief Surgeon $\geq$ Surgeon $\geq$ Resident*. A Chief Surgeon therefore inherits all permissions of a Surgeon and a Resident.

**4. Separation of Duties (1 mark):**
SoD constrains the assignment of roles to users:
- **Static SoD:** A user cannot be a member of two mutually exclusive roles simultaneously.
- **Dynamic SoD:** A user can be a member of both, but cannot activate both in the same session.
- Example: An employee cannot be both the *Requester* and the *Approver* of the same purchase order.

**Diagram (any 1 mark):** Draw a tree with three roles and inherited permissions.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**1. Background (1 mark):** BLP was developed by David Bell and Leonard LaPadula in 1973 for the US DoD. It is a **state-machine model** designed to enforce **confidentiality**.

**2. Security Levels and Lattice (2 marks):**
Define $\mathcal{L} = \{ \text{Unclassified, Confidential, Secret, Top Secret} \}$ with a dominance partial order $L_i \geq L_j$.

**3. The Two Properties (2 marks):**
- **Simple Security Property (ss-property):** $\forall s, o:\ \text{read}(s, o) \Rightarrow L_s(s) \geq L_o(o)$.
- **\***-Property (Star Property):** $\forall s, o:\ \text{write}(s, o) \Rightarrow L_s(s) \leq L_o(o)$.

**4. Labeled Example (1 mark):**
> User Alice has clearance $L_s = \text{Secret}$. Object $O_1$ is classified $L_o = \text{Confidential}$.
> - **Read:** $L_s(\text{Alice}) = \text{Secret} \geq \text{Confidential} = L_o(O_1)$ → **Permitted**.
> - **Write:** $L_s(\text{Alice}) = \text{Secret} \leq \text{Confidential} = L_o(O_1)$ → **Denied** (cannot write down).

**5. Tranquility (1 mark):** Discuss briefly that security levels do not change during a session (Strong Tranquility).

---

#### **Question B** (14 Marks — Alternative)

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the **Reference Monitor** concept. List its three mandatory properties. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Compare and contrast **DAC, MAC, and RBAC** access control models using a comparison matrix. Mention **two real-world scenarios** for each. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**1. Definition (2 marks):** The Reference Monitor, as defined by James Anderson (1972) and formalized in the **Trusted Computer System Evaluation Criteria (TCSEC / Orange Book)**, is an abstract machine that mediates every access of a subject to an object.

**2. Three Mandatory Properties (3 marks):**
- **Complete Mediation:** Every access is checked — no bypass.
- **Tamper-Proof:** The monitor itself cannot be modified by malicious code.
- **Verifiable:** The monitor's correctness can be mathematically proven.

**3. Block Diagram (2 marks):** Draw subject → Reference Monitor → Object, with policy database and audit log attached.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**1. Comparison Matrix (4 marks):**

| Attribute | DAC | MAC | RBAC |
|---|---|---|---|
| Decision Driver | Owner | Security label | Role |
| Centralization | Low | High | Medium |
| Flexibility | High | Low | Medium |
| Best for | Collaborative files | Classified data | Enterprises |

**2. Two Real-World Scenarios Each (3 marks = 1.5 each model × 2):**
- **DAC:** Unix file permissions, Google Docs sharing.
- **MAC:** Military Top-Secret networks, SELinux.
- **RBAC:** Hospital EHR systems, AWS IAM users with role-based policies.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing BLP and Biba:** BLP = *confidentiality* (No Read Up, No Write Down). Biba = *integrity* (No Read Down, No Write Up). Mixing them is the **#1 deduction reason**.
> 2. **Writing "No Read Up" for Biba:** Biba is the *opposite*. Examiners check this carefully.
> 3. **Omitting the formal inequality:** Always write the access decision in formal $\text{Permit} / \text{Deny}$ form with a clear inequality, not just in English.
> 4. **Forgetting the \*-Property:** Many students state only the Simple Security Property and skip the \*-Property — guaranteed loss of at least 2 marks.
> 5. **No diagram in RBAC questions:** The KTU answer-book valuation explicitly gives 1–2 marks for a clean role-hierarchy tree.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Access Control Model** = formal policy specifying *who can do what to which resource under which conditions*.
- [x] **DAC** — owner-controlled, ACL/ACM-based, weak against Trojan Horses.
- [x] **MAC** — central authority, label-based, lattice-structured, formally verifiable.
- [x] **BLP** — confidentiality model with **No Read Up** and **No Write Down**.
- [x] **Biba** — integrity model with **No Read Down** and **No Write Up**.
- [x] **RBAC** — role-driven, NIST standard, supports hierarchies and SoD.
- [x] **ABAC** — attribute-driven, most expressive, foundation of Zero Trust.
- [x] **Reference Monitor** — must satisfy **complete mediation, tamper-proof, verifiable**.
- [x] The \*-Property is **not optional** in BLP answers — it is worth marks.
- [x] **Separation of Duty** in RBAC has two flavors — *static* and *dynamic*.
- [x] MAC is the **strongest** but the **most rigid**; RBAC is the **most widely deployed** in enterprises.
- [x] ABAC and RBAC are **complementary**, not mutually exclusive — modern systems combine them.
- [x] Always write the **formal inequality** alongside the English explanation in BLP/Biba answers.
- [x] Memorize the **three properties of the Reference Monitor** — they appear almost every semester.
- [x] The KTU ESE 14-mark question on this module almost always has one sub-part on **RBAC** and one on **BLP/Biba** — prepare accordingly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kerberos Protocol
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT418 - SECURITY ASSESSMENT AND RISK ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Kerberos Protocol

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Kerberos** is a trusted third-party network authentication protocol developed at the **Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT)** as part of Project Athena. It uses **symmetric-key cryptography** to provide strong authentication for client-server applications over an insecure, open network (like the Internet) by employing a centralized **Key Distribution Center (KDC)** to issue short-lived, encrypted credentials called *tickets* instead of transmitting passwords across the wire.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEAIT418 — Module 4)**
> The official KTU 2024 Scheme defines Kerberos as a *“ticket-based authentication protocol that uses symmetric cryptography and a trusted Key Distribution Center (KDC) to authenticate users and services in a distributed network, preventing replay attacks and eavesdropping.”*

**Standardized Document:** The current version of the protocol is **Kerberos Version 5**, defined under **IETF RFC 4120** (which obsoletes RFC 1510). It is the **default authentication backbone of Microsoft Windows Active Directory** and is widely used in UNIX/Linux environments (via MIT Kerberos, Heimdal).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Airport Boarding Pass

Imagine you are at a busy international airport. Instead of letting every passenger individually verify their identity with every airline, security check, and gate agent (a slow, repetitive process), the airport uses a **trusted central authority** — the **Boarding Pass Issuer**.

| Airport Concept | Kerberos Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- |
| You (the Traveller) | **Client (User/Workstation)** |
| Boarding Pass Issuer Desk | **Authentication Server (AS)** |
| Airline / Gate Agent | **Ticket Granting Server (TGS)** |
| The Aeroplane (Service) | **Application Server (e.g., File Server, Mail Server)** |
| Your Boarding Pass | **Ticket (TGT / Service Ticket)** |
| The boarding pass is **time-stamped** and **forged-proof** | Ticket is **encrypted** and **replay-protected** |

### Step-by-step Analogy
1. You go to the **Issuer Desk** (AS) and show your **passport/ID** (your secret password). The desk does **not** give you the right to board directly.
2. Instead, it issues you a **general Boarding Pass** — the **Ticket Granting Ticket (TGT)**. This pass is *sealed* so that only the *gate agents* (TGS) can verify it.
3. When you reach a specific **airline counter** (TGS) for your flight, you present your TGT. The counter gives you a **flight-specific ticket** (Service Ticket).
4. You walk to the **gate** (Application Server) and show the flight-specific ticket. You are allowed on board — **without ever showing your passport again** to the airline or the gate.

The boarding pass is short-lived (e.g., 24 hours), and if you lose it, the damage is limited. The passport (password) is never transmitted again after the first verification.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this design important in Security Assessment?**
> Because passwords are **never** sent over the network after the initial authentication. An attacker who captures network traffic with packet sniffers (e.g., using Wireshark or ARP poisoning) cannot replay the authentication, since tickets are bound to a **session key, IP address, and time-window**.

## 1.3 The Three-Headed Dog Mythology

The name *“Kerberos”* comes from **Greek Mythology** — *Cerberus*, the **three-headed dog** that guards the gates of the Underworld. This metaphor perfectly describes the protocol:

- **AS** (Authentication Server)
- **TGS** (Ticket Granting Server)
- **SS** (Service Server / Realm Database)

All three “heads” are housed inside the **KDC (Key Distribution Center)** — the single trusted authority.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Mnemonic — “A-T-S”**
> **A**uthentication → **T**icket Granting → **S**ervice Access.
> This is the exact order of the three Kerberos exchanges every KTU examiner expects.

## 1.4 Core Operational Metrics & Standard Values

| Parameter | Standard Value (Kerberos V5) | Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Default Ticket Lifetime (TGT)** | **10 hours (36,000 seconds)** | Renewable up to 7 days max |
| **Clock Skew Tolerance** | **5 minutes (300 seconds)** | Time-window for replay attack prevention |
| **Minimum Encryption Strength** | **AES-256-CTS-HMAC-SHA1-96** | Mandated by Microsoft AD 2008+ |
| **Port for KDC Service** | **UDP/TCP 88** | IANA registered port |
| **Ticket Flags Supported** | 13 flags in V5 | Forwardable, Renewable, Proxiable, etc. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Components — The Building Blocks

### 2.1.1 Key Distribution Center (KDC)
The KDC is the **single point of trust** in the Kerberos realm. It is a network service (typically a hardened server) that maintains:
- A database of all **principals** (users, services, hosts) and their **secret keys**.
- A master key derived from the principal's password (for users) or a random key (for services).
- The realm's ticket policy (lifetimes, renewals, pre-authentication requirements).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KDC is composed of THREE logical sub-services:**
> 1. **Authentication Server (AS)** — Performs *initial* identity verification using the user's password-derived key.
> 2. **Ticket Granting Server (TGS)** — Issues *service-specific* tickets after the user has a TGT.
> 3. **Kerberos Database (DB)** — Stores principal keys and policy (often implemented via LDAP backend).

### 2.1.2 Principals
Every entity (user, application service, host) registered in the realm is called a **principal** and has a unique identifier of the form:

$$\text{primary} / \text{instance} @ \text{REALM}$$

**Examples:**
- `alice@EXAMPLE.COM` — a user principal
- `krbtgt/EXAMPLE.COM@EXAMPLE.COM` — the special TGS principal
- `host/fileserver.example.com@EXAMPLE.COM` — a service principal

### 2.1.3 Tickets
A **Ticket** is a cryptographically sealed data structure containing:
- Client's principal name
- Client's network address
- Ticket validity timestamps
- **Session Key** (randomly generated by the issuing server)
- Authorization data

The ticket is **encrypted with the secret key of the receiving server**, so only that server can read it.

### 2.1.4 Authenticators
An **Authenticator** is a short-lived data block created by the client containing:
- Client principal ID
- **Current timestamp** (encrypted with the session key)

It proves the client is *live* and holds the session key — preventing replay of an old ticket.

## 2.2 The Authentication Lifecycle — Three Exchanges

Kerberos V5 authentication proceeds through **three sequential message exchanges**. Every KTU question on this topic essentially tests understanding of these three exchanges.

### Exchange 1: AS Exchange (Client ↔ AS)
**Goal:** Obtain a **Ticket Granting Ticket (TGT)** and a **TGS Session Key**.

| Direction | Message | Contents | Encryption |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Client → AS | **KRB_AS_REQ** | ID_client, ID_tgs, Times (t1, t2, t3, t4) | Plaintext (over UDP 88) |
| AS → Client | **KRB_AS_REP** | TGT + Session_Key_{Client-TGS} | Session_Key encrypted with **Client's secret key**; TGT encrypted with **TGS's secret key** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is KRB_AS_REQ sent in plaintext?**
> The client *must* send its ID in clear so the AS knows *who* is asking. The actual *proof of identity* happens implicitly: the client can decrypt the response *only if* it knows its own secret key. If an attacker intercepts the request, they cannot decrypt the reply because they don't know the password.

### Exchange 2: TGS Exchange (Client ↔ TGS)
**Goal:** Obtain a **Service Ticket** and a **Client-Server Session Key**.

| Direction | Message | Contents | Encryption |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Client → TGS | **KRB_TGS_REQ** | TGT + Authenticator_1 + ID_service | Authenticator encrypted with **TGS Session Key** |
| TGS → Client | **KRB_TGS_REP** | Service_Ticket + Session_Key_{Client-Service} | Service_Ticket encrypted with **Service's secret key**; Session_Key encrypted with **TGS Session Key** |

### Exchange 3: AP Exchange (Client ↔ Service)
**Goal:** Authenticate to the **Application Server** and (optionally) prove the server's identity back to the client (**Mutual Authentication**).

| Direction | Message | Contents | Encryption |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Client → Server | **KRB_AP_REQ** | Service_Ticket + Authenticator_2 | Authenticator encrypted with **Client-Service Session Key** |
| Server → Client | **KRB_AP_REP** *(optional)* | Timestamp from Authenticator_2 (incremented) | Encrypted with **Client-Service Session Key** |

> [!TIP]
> **Mutual Authentication Flag:** If the client sets the `MUTUAL-REQUIRED` flag in the AP_REQ, the server *must* respond with a KRB_AP_REP. This prevents a rogue server from impersonating the real one.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Notation Sheet

The following table consolidates every symbol and notation used in the official KTU answer key for Kerberos. CRITICAL: Note the use of `\vert` and `\mid` instead of the pipe character to preserve table integrity.

| Symbol | Meaning | Encryption / Key Holder |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $C$ | Client (user) | — |
| $AS$ | Authentication Server | — |
| $TGS$ | Ticket Granting Server | — |
| $V$ | Application Server (Service) | — |
| $K_C$ | Secret key of Client (derived from password) | Known to C and KDC only |
| $K_{TGS}$ | Secret key of TGS (random key) | Known to TGS and KDC only |
| $K_V$ | Secret key of Service Server | Known to V and KDC only |
| $K_{C,TGS}$ | Session key shared between C and TGS | Issued in AS_REP, used in TGS exchange |
| $K_{C,V}$ | Session key shared between C and Service | Issued in TGS_REP, used in AP exchange |
| $\{M\}_K$ | Message M encrypted with symmetric key K | $K \in \{ K_C, K_{TGS}, K_V, K_{C,TGS}, K_{C,V} \}$ |
| $ID_X$ | Identity of principal X | Plaintext |
| $TS_i$ | i-th timestamp (e.g., $TS_1, TS_2$) | Used to prevent replay |
| $Lifetime$ | Validity period of ticket | Default $\vert 10 \text{ hours} \vert$ |
| $AD_{C}$ | Client's network address (typically IP) | Binds ticket to a host |
| $Authn_{C}$ | Authenticator generated by Client | Contains $ID_C, AD_C, TS_2$ |
| $TGT$ | Ticket Granting Ticket | $\{K_{C,TGS}, ID_C, AD_C, TS_1, Lifetime\}_{K_{TGS}}$ |
| $Ticket_{C \to V}$ | Service Ticket for Client to access V | $\{K_{C,V}, ID_C, AD_C, TS_3, Lifetime\}_{K_V}$ |

### 2.3.1 Canonical Notation for the Three Exchanges

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(1) Client} \to \text{AS}: \quad & ID_C, \, ID_{TGS}, \, TS_1 \\
\text{(2) AS} \to \text{Clients}: \quad & \{K_{C,TGS}\}_{K_C}, \quad \{TGT\}_{K_{TGS}} \\
\text{(3) Client} \to \text{TGS}: \quad & \{TGT\}_{K_{TGS}}, \quad \{Authn_C\}_{K_{C,TGS}}, \quad ID_V \\
\text{(4) TGS} \to \text{Client}: \quad & \{K_{C,V}\}_{K_{C,TGS}}, \quad \{Ticket_{C \to V}\}_{K_V} \\
\text{(5) Client} \to \text{V}: \quad & \{Ticket_{C \to V}\}_{K_V}, \quad \{Authn'_C\}_{K_{C,V}} \\
\text{(6) V} \to \text{Client}: \quad & \{TS_2 + 1\}_{K_{C,V}} \quad \text{(Mutual Auth)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.3.2 Symbolic Ticket Definitions

$$
TGT = \left\{ K_{C,TGS} \,\vert\, ID_C \,\vert\, AD_C \,\vert\, TS_1 \,\vert\, \text{Lifetime} \right\}_{K_{TGS}}
$$

$$
Ticket_{C \to V} = \left\{ K_{C,V} \,\vert\, ID_C \,\vert\, AD_C \,\vert\, TS_3 \,\vert\, \text{Lifetime} \right\}_{K_V}
$$

$$
Authn_C = \left\{ ID_C \,\vert\, AD_C \,\vert\, TS_2 \right\}_{K_{C,TGS}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility in the Real World**
> In modern enterprise environments, this exact symbolic framework is implemented in **Windows Active Directory**, **MIT Kerberos**, and **FreeIPA**. When a security analyst performs a penetration test, they map every captured packet (using tools like **Wireshark** with `kerberos` filter, **krb5.keytab** analysis, or **Rubeus** for ticket extraction) back to these formulas. Understanding the notation is therefore not merely academic — it is the **foundation of offensive and defensive security tooling** for Active Directory environments.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Walk-Through of the Kerberos V5 Authentication Flow

This section presents an **exhaustive, step-by-step trace** of every cryptographic operation, justifying *why* each step is performed. This is exactly the depth a KTU Board examiner expects for a **14-mark full question**.

### **PHASE 0: Setup (Performed Once by the KDC Administrator)**

**Step 0.1 — Principal Registration**
For every principal X registered in the realm, the KDC administrator generates or imports a secret key:

$$
K_X = \begin{cases} \text{Key}\left(\text{password}_X, \text{salt}, \text{iterations}\right) & \text{if X is a user} \\ \text{RandomKey}() & \text{if X is a service} \end{cases}
$$

For users, the key is derived using the **string-to-key (s2k)** algorithm (PBKDF2-like) to make brute-force attacks computationally expensive. For services, a **random 256-bit AES key** is generated and stored in a **keytab file** on the service host.

**Step 0.2 — KDC Internal Configuration**
The KDC installs its own service principal `krbtgt/REALM@REALM` with a master key $K_{krbtgt}$. This is the *root of trust* for the entire realm.

---

### **PHASE 1: AS Exchange (Initial Authentication)**

**Step 1.1 — Client Composes KRB_AS_REQ**

The user `alice` initiates login. The Kerberos client library constructs:

$$
\text{KRB\_AS\_REQ} = \left[ ID_C, \, ID_{TGS}, \, \text{Nonce}_C, \, \text{Times} \right]
$$

- $ID_C$ = `alice@EXAMPLE.COM` — plaintext (so KDC can look up the user).
- $ID_{TGS}$ = `krbtgt/EXAMPLE.COM` — identifies the requested TGS.
- $\text{Nonce}_C$ — a random number to ensure freshness of the reply.
- $\text{Times} = (t_1, t_2, t_3, t_4)$ — requested validity timestamps.
- **No password is sent** — the client *proves* knowledge of the password implicitly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Nonce?** The nonce prevents an attacker who *previously captured* a valid AS_REP from replaying it. The client checks that the AS_REP contains the matching nonce.

**Step 1.2 — Client Encrypts its Pre-Authentication Blob (Optional, V5 Strict Mode)**

In modern Kerberos (V5 with **PA-ENC-TIMESTAMP** pre-auth required), the client first sends:

$$
\text{KRB\_AS\_REQ (v2)} = \left[ ID_C, \, ID_{TGS}, \, \text{Nonce}_C, \, \{TS_1\}_{K_C} \right]
$$

The pre-authentication timestamp is encrypted with the *user's secret key* $K_C$. The AS attempts decryption; if it succeeds *and* the timestamp is recent, the user is authentic. This defeats **offline dictionary attacks** against captured AS_REPs.

**Step 1.3 — AS Looks Up Secrets and Crafts KRB_AS_REP**

The AS retrieves from the Kerberos Database:
- $K_C$ — Alice's secret key
- $K_{TGS}$ — The TGS's secret key
- $K_{krbtgt}$ — The realm master key

The AS generates a **fresh random 256-bit session key** $K_{C,TGS}$.

It then constructs the response as two logical parts:

$$
\text{Part A: Encrypted for Client} = \left\{ K_{C,TGS}, \, \text{Nonce}_C, \, \text{Lifetime}, \, ID_{TGS} \right\}_{K_C}
$$

$$
\text{Part B: The TGT} = \left\{ K_{C,TGS}, \, ID_C, \, AD_C, \, TS_1, \, \text{Lifetime} \right\}_{K_{TGS}}
$$

**Step 1.4 — Client Decrypts Part A and Validates**

The client uses $K_C$ (derived from the user's typed password) to decrypt Part A. If decryption fails → wrong password. If success → the client now holds:

- $K_{C,TGS}$ — to be used in Phase 2
- TGT — opaque blob to be presented to the TGS in Phase 2

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Security Insight:** If a wrong password is entered, the user cannot decrypt Part A. The KDC learns *nothing* about whether the password was correct — this is a **deliberate design** to prevent password probing. The client itself makes the authentication decision.

---

### **PHASE 2: TGS Exchange (Obtaining a Service Ticket)**

**Step 2.1 — Client Composes Authenticator**

The client builds an authenticator proving liveness:

$$
Authn_C = \left\{ ID_C, \, AD_C, \, TS_2 \right\}_{K_{C,TGS}}
$$

Where $TS_2$ is the *current* timestamp. This is encrypted with the session key $K_{C,TGS}$ — only the TGS, which can recover $K_{C,TGS}$ from the TGT, can decrypt it.

**Step 2.2 — Client Sends KRB_TGS_REQ**

$$
\text{KRB\_TGS\_REQ} = \left[ TGT, \, Authn_C, \, ID_V \right]
$$

$TGT$ is sent in ciphertext (encrypted with $K_{TGS}$), so even if intercepted, it is useless to an attacker. The TGT itself is *opaque* to the client — it cannot read or modify it.

**Step 2.3 — TGS Decrypts and Validates**

The TGS performs the following checks:

| Check | Condition | Failure Action |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| TGT Decryption | $\text{Decrypt}(TGT, K_{TGS})$ succeeds | Reject with KRB_AP_ERR_MODIFIED |
| Authenticator Decryption | $\text{Decrypt}(Authn_C, K_{C,TGS})$ succeeds | Reject |
| Timestamp Skew | $\vert \text{now} - TS_2 \vert \le 5$ minutes | Reject (replay protection) |
| Replay Cache | $Authn_C$ not seen before | Reject |
| Authorization | Alice is allowed to access service V | Reject |

**Step 2.4 — TGS Issues Service Ticket**

If all checks pass, the TGS generates a **new random session key** $K_{C,V}$ and constructs:

$$
\text{Part A (encrypted for Client)} = \left\{ K_{C,V}, \, \text{Nonce}, \, \text{Lifetime}, \, ID_V \right\}_{K_{C,TGS}}
$$

$$
\text{Part B (Service Ticket)} = \left\{ K_{C,V}, \, ID_C, \, AD_C, \, TS_3, \, \text{Lifetime} \right\}_{K_V}
$$

**Step 2.5 — Client Decrypts and Stores the Service Ticket**

The client decrypts Part A with $K_{C,TGS}$ and now holds $K_{C,V}$ plus the encrypted Service Ticket. The service ticket is cached locally (often in a **credential cache** like `/tmp/krb5cc_<UID>` on Linux or in the **LSASS** process memory on Windows).

---

### **PHASE 3: AP Exchange (Accessing the Service)**

**Step 3.1 — Client Sends KRB_AP_REQ**

$$
\text{KRB\_AP\_REQ} = \left[ \text{Service\_Ticket}, \, Authn'_C \right]
$$

Where:

$$
Authn'_C = \left\{ ID_C, \, AD_C, \, TS_4 \right\}_{K_{C,V}}
$$

**Step 3.2 — Service Server Validates**

The service V decrypts the Service Ticket with $K_V$, recovers $K_{C,V}$, decrypts the Authenticator, checks the timestamp, and verifies the client is legitimate.

**Step 3.3 — Mutual Authentication (Optional)**

If requested, the server responds with:

$$
\text{KRB\_AP\_REP} = \left\{ TS_4 + 1 \right\}_{K_{C,V}}
$$

The client decrypts and verifies $TS_4 + 1$ — proving the server also knew $K_{C,V}$ and is therefore authentic.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Why Each Design Choice Matters

| Design Decision | Threat Addressed | Engineering Trade-off |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Use of Timestamps + 5-min Skew** | Replay attacks | Requires **time synchronization** (NTP) across all hosts |
| **Tickets are opaque to client** | Tampering with ticket contents | Cannot selectively forward fields |
| **TGT separates authentication from authorization** | Repeated password transmission | Slightly more complex protocol state |
| **Authenticators include client IP** | Ticket theft & reuse from another host | Breaks if user roams (mobile devices use empty IP `0.0.0.0`) |
| **Pre-authentication in V5** | Offline dictionary attacks on captured AS_REP | Slight performance cost (one extra round trip) |
| **KDC is single point of trust** | Distributed key management | KDC compromise = realm compromise (motivates read-only Domain Controllers and credential guard) |
| **Session keys are random per exchange** | Key reuse / session hijacking | High entropy needed; uses CSPRNG |

## 3.3 Cross-Realm Authentication (Hierarchical Trust)

In multi-realm environments (e.g., `EXAMPLE.COM` trusts `PARTNER.ORG`), a client authenticated in realm A can obtain a service ticket in realm B through a **transitive trust chain**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Client (in A)} \to & \text{TGS in A: } \text{Request ticket for } ID_V \text{ in B} \\
\text{TGS in A} \to & \text{Clients: } \text{Issue "cross-realm TGT" encrypted with } K_{A \leftrightarrow B} \\
\text{Client} \to & \text{TGS in B: } \text{Present cross-realm TGT} \\
\text{TGS in B} \to & \text{Clients: } \text{Issue final service ticket} \\
\text{Client} \to & \text{Service V in B: } \text{Authenticate}
\end{aligned}
$$

The trust is **hierarchical** (like DNS) — both realms share a **inter-realm key** $K_{A \leftrightarrow B}$.

## 4.3 Ticket Lifecycle State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Issued : KDC creates ticket
    Issued --> Active : Client receives ticket
    Active --> Active : Used within validity window
    Active --> Expired : Lifetime exceeded
    Active --> Renewed : Valid krbtgt sent, lifetime extended
    Active --> Revoked : KDC marks as invalid (post-date usage)
    Expired --> [*]
    Revoked --> [*]
    Renewed --> Active
```
