# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
**List any three principal components of Kerberos authentication and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer:**

The three principal components of the Kerberos authentication system are:

1. **Key Distribution Center (KDC):** It is the trusted third-party server that maintains the master secret keys of all principals in the realm. It comprises the Authentication Server (AS) and the Ticket Granting Server (TGS), and is responsible for issuing tickets and session keys.
2. **Authentication Server (AS):** The AS performs the *initial* verification of a user's identity during the first exchange (AS Exchange). Upon successful verification, it issues a Ticket Granting Ticket (TGT) and a session key encrypted with the user's secret key.
3. **Ticket Granting Server (TGS):** The TGS issues *service-specific* tickets (TGTs for the requested services) after the user presents a valid TGT. It functions as an intermediary that prevents the user from re-entering the password for every service access.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note (3 marks allocation):** 1 mark per component name + 1 mark for function. Students commonly lose marks by failing to *distinguish AS from TGS* — the AS is for *user* authentication, the TGS is for *service* ticket issuing.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Supplementary]**
**What is the role of an Authenticator in Kerberos? Why is it required when a ticket is already present?**

**Model Answer:**

An **Authenticator** is a short-lived cryptographic credential generated by the client to prove its *current* possession of a session key during a Kerberos exchange. It typically contains the client identity ($ID_C$), client network address ($AD_C$), and the current timestamp ($TS_2$), all encrypted with the relevant session key (e.g., $K_{C,TGS}$ or $K_{C,V}$).

**Why is it required even when a ticket is present?**
- A **ticket** is issued once by the KDC and can be used *multiple times* during its validity period. It proves the client's *initial* identity but does **not** prove the *current* session.
- Without an Authenticator, an attacker who steals a ticket could replay it from any machine during the validity window.
- The **Authenticator** binds the ticket to the *current* timestamp and the *presenting* client, providing **replay protection** with a default clock skew of **5 minutes (300 seconds)**.
- It also prevents the ticket from being used by an adversary even if intercepted, because the Authenticator requires the live session key to be created.

Thus, the combination of **Ticket (long-lived) + Authenticator (short-lived)** enforces both authentication and freshness — a classic KTU high-yield concept.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Each Part B question is internally optional. The student must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both questions carry equal marks (typically 7 + 7 sub-parts).

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Series 1]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of the Kerberos authentication system with a neat block diagram. List and briefly describe the responsibilities of the **Authentication Server (AS)** and the **Ticket Granting Server (TGS)** within the KDC. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe in detail the **three logical exchanges** that occur in Kerberos Version 5 authentication. For each exchange, specify the message names (KRB_AS_REQ, etc.), the cryptographic keys used for encryption, and the security goal achieved. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **MODEL SOLUTION — Question A**

##### **(a) Kerberos Architecture and KDC Responsibilities (7 Marks)**

**Architecture Overview:**
Kerberos uses a **client-server model with a trusted third party** (the KDC). All principals — users, services, and hosts — register their secret keys with the KDC. When a user wants to access a network service, the user does **not** send the password to the service. Instead, the user obtains *tickets* from the KDC, which are then presented to the target service.

**Block Diagram:**

```
                ┌──────────────────────────────────────┐
                │   KDC (Key Distribution Center)      │
                │  ┌────────────┐   ┌──────────────┐   │
                │  │  AS Module │   │ TGS Module   │   │
                │  │  • Verify  │   │ • Validate   │   │
                │  │    user ID │   │   TGT        │   │
                │  │  • Issue   │   │ • Issue      │   │
                │  │    TGT     │   │   Service    │   │
                │  └────────────┘   │   Tickets    │   │
                │       │            └──────────────┘   │
                │       ▼                  │            │
                │  ┌──────────────────────────────┐     │
                │  │  Kerberos Database (LDAP)    │     │
                │  │  • Principal Keys             │     │
                │  │  • Realm Policy               │     │
                │  └──────────────────────────────┘     │
                └──────────────────────────────────────┘
                          │                ▲
                  (AS_REQ) │                │ (AS_REP)
                          ▼                │
   ┌────────────┐                ┌────────────────┐
   │  Client    │ ────────────── │ Service Server │
   │ (Workstation)               │   (V)          │
   └────────────┘                └────────────────┘
```

**Responsibilities of the AS:**
1. Verifies the identity of the user during initial login (using pre-authentication timestamp in V5).
2. Generates a fresh **session key $K_{C,TGS}$** and a **Ticket Granting Ticket (TGT)**.
3. Encrypts the TGT with the TGS's secret key and the session key with the user's secret key.

**Responsibilities of the TGS:**
1. Receives the TGT + Authenticator from the client and validates them.
2. Enforces authorization policy (which users may access which services).
3. Issues **service tickets** encrypted with the target service's secret key.

*(Marks distribution: 3 marks for block diagram, 2 marks for AS responsibilities, 2 marks for TGS responsibilities.)*

##### **(b) The Three Logical Exchanges of Kerberos V5 (7 Marks)**

**Exchange 1 — AS Exchange (KRB_AS_REQ / KRB_AS_REP):**
- **Goal:** Authenticate the user and issue a TGT.
- **Client → AS (KRB_AS_REQ):** $ID_C, ID_{TGS}, \text{Nonce}_C, \text{Times}$. Sent in plaintext; the *real* authentication happens via PA-ENC-TIMESTAMP in V5.
- **AS → Client (KRB_AS_REP):** $\{K_{C,TGS}\}_{K_C}$ and $\{TGT\}_{K_{TGS}}$.
- **Key used:** Client's secret key $K_C$ and TGS's secret key $K_{TGS}$.
- **Security Goal:** Prevent password transmission; enable single-sign-on.

**Exchange 2 — TGS Exchange (KRB_TGS_REQ / KRB_TGS_REP):**
- **Goal:** Convert the TGT into a service-specific ticket.
- **Client → TGS (KRB_TGS_REQ):** $TGT, \{Authn_C\}_{K_{C,TGS}}, ID_V$.
- **TGS → Client (KRB_TGS_REP):** $\{K_{C,V}\}_{K_{C,TGS}}$ and $\{Ticket_{C \to V}\}_{K_V}$.
- **Key used:** TGS session key $K_{C,TGS}$ and service's secret key $K_V$.
- **Security Goal:** Provide replay-protected, authorized service access.

**Exchange 3 — AP Exchange (KRB_AP_REQ / KRB_AP_REP):**
- **Goal:** Authenticate to the application service and (optionally) establish mutual authentication.
- **Client → V (KRB_AP_REQ):** $Ticket_{C \to V}, \{Authn'_C\}_{K_{C,V}}$.
- **V → Client (KRB_AP_REP):** $\{TS_2 + 1\}_{K_{C,V}}$.
- **Key used:** Client-service session key $K_{C,V}$ and service's secret key $K_V$.
- **Security Goal:** Prevent server impersonation; bind session to a single client.

**Summary Table:**

| Exchange | Message Types | Session Key | Security Goal |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| AS | KRB_AS_REQ / REP | $K_{C,TGS}$ | Initial authentication |
| TGS | KRB_TGS_REQ / REP | $K_{C,V}$ | Service ticket issuance |
| AP | KRB_AP_REQ / REP | $K_{C,V}$ | Service access + mutual auth |

*(Marks distribution: 2 marks per exchange for goal + 1 mark for the final summary table.)*

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Series 2]**

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain how Kerberos Version 5 achieves **mutual authentication** between a client and an application server. How does this differ from one-way authentication? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **four common attack vectors** against Kerberos deployments (e.g., Kerberoasting, Golden Ticket, Pass-the-Ticket, AS-REP Roasting). For each attack, describe the **principle, impact, and primary mitigation**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **MODEL SOLUTION — Question B**

##### **(a) Mutual Authentication in Kerberos V5 (7 Marks)**

**Definition:** Mutual authentication means *both* the client *and* the server prove their identities to each other, ensuring neither party is communicating with an impostor.

**Mechanism in Kerberos V5:**

1. The client sets the **MUTUAL-REQUIRED** flag in the KRB_AP_REQ message.
2. The client sends the service ticket and the authenticator: $Ticket_{C \to V}, \{Authn'_C\}_{K_{C,V}}$.
3. The service server decrypts the ticket with $K_V$, recovers $K_{C,V}$, decrypts the Authenticator, and validates $TS_4$.
4. The service generates a response: $\{TS_4 + 1\}_{K_{C,V}}$ — i.e., it encrypts the *incremented* timestamp from the client's Authenticator with the session key.
5. The client decrypts this response and verifies that the timestamp is indeed $TS_4 + 1$. **If decryption succeeds, the client is convinced that the server knows $K_{C,V}$ — and therefore the server is genuine.**

**Sketch of the AP Exchange with Mutual Auth:**

```
   Client                                   Service V
     │                                          │
     │ ─── KRB_AP_REQ ─────────────────────────▶│
     │       (Ticket + Authenticator)            │
     │                                          │ (Validate ticket + auth)
     │ ◀── KRB_AP_REP ──────────────────────────│
     │       ({TS_4 + 1}_K_C_V)                 │
     │                                          │
   Verify TS_4 + 1 decryption OK
   → Server is AUTHENTIC
```

**Difference from One-Way Authentication:**
- In **one-way authentication** (no MUTUAL-REQUIRED flag), the server does not respond with KRB_AP_REP. The client cannot verify that the service is not a *rogue server* impersonating the real one.
- Mutual authentication is **mandatory in zero-trust networks** and in **any environment where the client may be redirected** (e.g., DNS spoofing, ARP poisoning).

*(Marks distribution: 4 marks for mechanism, 2 marks for sketch, 1 mark for one-way comparison.)*

##### **(b) Four Common Kerberos Attack Vectors (7 Marks)**

**1. Kerberoasting**
- **Principle:** Any authenticated user can request a service ticket for a service account. The service ticket is encrypted with the service account's NTLM hash. The attacker cracks this ticket *offline* to recover the plaintext password.
- **Impact:** Compromise of high-privilege service accounts (e.g., SQL service, backup service).
- **Mitigation:** Use *managed service accounts* (gMSAs) with 120+ character auto-rotated passwords, and enforce AES-only encryption.

**2. Golden Ticket Attack**
- **Principle:** If an attacker obtains the **krbtgt account hash** (e.g., via DCsync or DCSync), they can forge *arbitrary* TGTs offline with any group memberships and any validity period.
- **Impact:** Persistent, undetectable domain admin access for up to 10 years (until krbtgt password is rotated twice).
- **Mitigation:** Rotate the `krbtgt` account password **twice** every 6 months; enable **Credential Guard** on domain controllers; monitor for abnormal TGT issuance.

**3. Pass-the-Ticket (PtT)**
- **Principle:** The attacker extracts a Kerberos ticket (TGT or service ticket) from LSASS memory and injects it into another session using tools like **Mimikatz** or **Rubeus**.
- **Impact:** Lateral movement across the network without needing the user's password.
- **Mitigation:** Enable **Windows Defender Credential Guard**, use **Protected Users** security group, and restrict ticket caching on privileged accounts.

**4. AS-REP Roasting**
- **Principle:** Targets user accounts that have **"Do not require Kerberos pre-authentication"** set. The attacker requests an AS_REP for such a user and cracks the encrypted blob offline.
- **Impact:** Plaintext password recovery for the targeted account, leading to full account takeover.
- **Mitigation:** Ensure *all* user accounts have pre-authentication enabled (this is the default); use long, complex passwords; monitor for anomalous AS_REQ patterns.

*(Marks distribution: 1.5 marks per attack (Principle + Impact + Mitigation) + 1 mark for the introduction.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> Across the past five KTU examination cycles, students have **consistently lost 3–5 marks** in Kerberos questions due to the following recurring mistakes:
> 1. **Confusing TGT with Service Ticket** — The TGT is *only* for talking to the TGS, not for the application service. Many students incorrectly say "the TGT grants access to the file server."
> 2. **Forgetting the KRB_AP_REP message** in mutual authentication — the **+1 timestamp trick** is a high-yield point worth at least 2 marks.
> 3. **Omitting the Authenticator** when describing the TGS or AP exchange — a ticket *without* an authenticator is meaningless. The examiner specifically looks for "Authenticator" in your answer.
> 4. **Confusing $K_{C,TGS}$ and $K_{C,V}$** — the two session keys are *different* and serve *different* exchanges. Mark with arrows to make this unambiguous in your answer sheet.
> 5. **Not mentioning Pre-Authentication** — this is the *key* security improvement from V4 to V5, and is almost always asked as a sub-question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Read This 5 Minutes Before the Exam**

- **Kerberos = ticket-based, symmetric-key, trusted-third-party authentication protocol** from MIT, used in **Windows Active Directory** and **Linux/UNIX realms**.
- **Three entities in the KDC:** **AS** (verifies user), **TGS** (issues service tickets), and the **Kerberos Database** (stores master keys).
- **Three Exchanges (in order):** **AS Exchange → TGS Exchange → AP Exchange** — mnemonic: **"A-T-A"** (Auth → Ticket → Access).
- **Tickets are opaque to the client**; **Authenticators are short-lived** and use timestamps to prevent replay (5-minute skew).
- **Session keys:** $K_{C,TGS}$ for client↔TGS; $K_{C,V}$ for client↔service. Both are *random, per-session* AES-256 keys.
- **TGT = $\{K_{C,TGS}, ID_C, AD_C, TS, \text{Lifetime}\}_{K_{TGS}}$** — encrypted with TGS key, never readable by client.
- **Mutual Authentication** is achieved via the **+1 timestamp trick** in the KRB_AP_REP message.
- **V4 weaknesses fixed in V5:** pre-authentication, longer encryption algorithms, ticket forwarding via flags, ASN.1 encoding.
- **Default port: UDP/TCP 88.** Default ticket lifetime: **10 hours**. Default clock skew: **5 minutes**.
- **Cross-realm trust:** hierarchical; uses inter-realm key $K_{A \leftrightarrow B}$ for transit tickets.
- **Major attacks to know (for Security Assessment perspective):** Kerberoasting, AS-REP Roasting, Pass-the-Ticket, Golden Ticket, Silver Ticket, Unconstrained Delegation abuse, DCsync.
- **Mitigations:** AES-only encryption, **gMSA** service accounts, **Credential Guard**, **krbtgt** rotation, pre-auth enforcement, **Protected Users** group, anomaly-based SIEM detection.
- **The "Three-Headed Dog"** = AS + TGS + Service/DB = the entire KDC.
- **Most important rule of all:** **The user's password (or any derivation of it) is NEVER sent over the network after the initial login.** This is the central security claim of Kerberos.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Public Key Infrastructure (PKI)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT418 - SECURITY ASSESSMENT AND RISK ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Public Key Infrastructure (PKI)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Public Key Infrastructure (PKI)** is a comprehensive, framework-based system of hardware, software, policies, procedures, and cryptographic standards that enables the secure creation, management, distribution, storage, usage, and revocation of **digital certificates** and **asymmetric keys**. It is the foundational trust backbone that binds public keys to verified identities of entities (people, servers, devices, or applications) through a chain of trust anchored by trusted third parties called **Certificate Authorities (CAs)**.

As per the **ITU-T X.509 standard (v3)** — the internationally accepted specification that defines the format of public key certificates — PKI provides four critical security services:
1. **Authentication** — confirming the identity of a communicating party.
2. **Confidentiality** — ensuring data is readable only by intended recipients.
3. **Integrity** — verifying that data has not been altered in transit.
4. **Non-Repudiation** — preventing an entity from denying a performed action (e.g., signing a digital document).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** PKI is NOT just encryption. It is a *trust architecture* that solves the fundamental problem of "How do I know that a public key truly belongs to the person/entity claiming it?" — this is called the **key-to-identity binding problem**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Passport & Embassy System"

Imagine a global passport system for the internet:

| Real-World Passport Concept | PKI Digital Equivalent |
|---|---|
| A **Passport** (physical booklet) | A **Digital Certificate** (X.509) |
| The **Issuing Government / Embassy** | A **Certificate Authority (CA)** |
| The **Regional Passport Office** | A **Registration Authority (RA)** |
| **Notary / Identity Proof** | **Background Verification (domain, email, organization checks)** |
| **Visa Stamping** | **Certificate Signing by CA's Private Key** |
| **Passport Revocation List (Interpol)** | **Certificate Revocation List (CRL) / OCSP** |
| **Passport Number** | **Serial Number of Certificate** |
| **Photograph & Signature** | **Public Key of the Holder** |

Just as a passport issued by a government is trusted globally because of inter-government trust treaties, a certificate issued by a trusted CA (e.g., VeriSign/Symantec, DigiCert, Let's Encrypt) is trusted by your browser because the CA's root certificate is pre-installed in your operating system/browser's **trust store**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** PKI is the entire ecosystem — the *rules, players, processes, and technologies* — that allows two strangers on the internet to trust each other's digital identities without having met in person. The "infrastructure" part is key: PKI is the *roads, traffic lights, and police* of digital trust, not the *cars* (the certificates themselves).

---

## 1.3 Core PKI Components — At a Glance

- **Certificate Authority (CA):** The trusted root issuer of certificates. Signs certificates with its private key.
- **Registration Authority (RA):** Subordinate to CA; performs identity verification before certificate issuance.
- **Digital Certificate:** A digital document (X.509 format) binding a public key to an identity.
- **Certificate Repository / Directory:** A public database (often LDAP) where certificates and CRLs are stored.
- **Certificate Management System:** Tools for issuance, renewal, suspension, and revocation.
- **Key/Token Storage:** Hardware Security Modules (HSMs) or smart cards for protecting private keys.
- **PKI Users / Subscribers / Relying Parties:** End entities that hold certificates (subscribers) or use others' certificates for verification (relying parties).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The mathematical **asymmetric (public key) cryptography** primitives upon which PKI is built (RSA, ECC, DSA) provide the *mechanism*, but PKI provides the *trust framework*. Without PKI, public key cryptography is mathematically secure but practically useless because of impersonation risks (Man-in-the-Middle attacks).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Workflow of PKI — Step-by-Step Logic

PKI is governed by a **Certificate Management Protocol** (e.g., CMP, SCEP, EST) and operates through the following life-cycle:

### Step 1: **Key Pair Generation**
- The end entity generates a mathematically linked key pair: a **Private Key** (kept secret) and a **Public Key** (distributed openly).
- Key sizes for **RSA**: minimum **2048 bits** (KTU/NIST standard), recommended **3072 or 4096 bits**.
- For **Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC)**: minimum **256 bits** (equivalent to ~3072-bit RSA).

### Step 2: **Registration & Identity Verification**
- The entity submits a **Certificate Signing Request (CSR)** — a PKCS#10 formatted message containing its public key and identity information (CN, O, OU, C, S, L fields).
- The **Registration Authority (RA)** validates the request: domain ownership (for DV certs), organization legitimacy (for OV certs), or extended vetting (for EV certs).

### Step 3: **Certificate Issuance & Signing**
- The CA constructs an X.509 certificate with the entity's public key, identity, validity period, and extensions.
- The CA computes a **hash** of the certificate data, then signs it using **its own private key**:
  
$$\text{Signature} = H(\text{CertificateData})^{d_{CA}} \mod n_{CA}$$

- The signed certificate is published to a **Certificate Repository** (often an LDAP directory or HTTP-accessible file).

### Step 4: **Certificate Distribution & Use**
- The subscriber presents the certificate during a handshake (e.g., **TLS/SSL**).
- Relying parties verify the certificate chain up to a trusted **Root CA** in their trust store.

### Step 5: **Validation, Renewal & Revocation**
- A certificate is **valid** only if: (a) current date is within the validity period, (b) not revoked, (c) signature verifies, (d) chain leads to a trusted root.
- Revocation is handled via **Certificate Revocation Lists (CRL)** or the **Online Certificate Status Protocol (OCSP)**.

---

## 2.2 X.509 Certificate Structure — Anatomy

A **Version 3 X.509 certificate** contains the following fields (per **RFC 5280**):

| Field | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Version** | X.509 version (typically v3) | `2` (value of 2 = v3) |
| **Serial Number** | Unique identifier per CA | `0A:3B:7C:...` |
| **Signature Algorithm** | Hash + PK algorithm used by CA to sign | `sha256WithRSAEncryption` |
| **Issuer** | Distinguished Name (DN) of the CA | `CN=DigiCert, O=DigiCert Inc, C=US` |
| **Validity Period** | `Not Before` and `Not After` timestamps | `2024-01-01 to 2025-01-01` |
| **Subject** | DN of the certificate holder | `CN=www.ktu.edu.in, O=Kerala, C=IN` |
| **Subject Public Key Info** | Algorithm OID + the public key bits | `RSA 2048-bit key` |
| **Issuer Unique ID** (v2/v3) | Optional CA identifier | — |
| **Subject Unique ID** (v2/v3) | Optional subject identifier | — |
| **Extensions** (v3 only) | Key Usage, EKU, SAN, CRL Distribution, AIA | `subjectAltName = DNS:ktu.edu.in` |
| **Signature** | The CA's digital signature over the above | `A4:F2:...` (binary blob) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical X.509v3 Extensions for KTU:**
> - **Subject Alternative Name (SAN):** Modern browsers require SAN; CN alone is deprecated.
> - **Extended Key Usage (EKU):** Specifies allowed uses (serverAuth, clientAuth, codeSigning, emailProtection).
> - **CRL Distribution Points (CDP):** URL where CRL is published.
> - **Authority Information Access (AIA):** URL of the issuing CA's certificate (for chain building) and OCSP responder.
> - **Basic Constraints:** `CA:TRUE/FALSE` — vital for path validation.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

The following table consolidates **all critical PKI-related cryptographic formulas** required for the **End Semester Examination (ESE)** and internal assessments. **Print this — it is your last-minute revision gold.**

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Notation Explanation | KTU Exam Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| **RSA Public Exponent Condition** | $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\varphi(n)}$ | $e$ = public exponent, $d$ = private exponent | Key generation step |
| **Euler's Totient** | $\varphi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ | $n = p \cdot q$ (two large primes) | Step-1 of RSA setup |
| **RSA Encryption** | $C \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n}$ | $C$ = ciphertext, $M$ = plaintext (as integer) | Confidentiality |
| **RSA Decryption** | $M \equiv C^{d} \pmod{n}$ | Recovers original $M$ from $C$ | Confidentiality |
| **Digital Signature Generation** | $S \equiv H(M)^{d} \pmod{n}$ | $H(M)$ = hash of message, $d$ = sender's private key | Authentication/Integrity |
| **Digital Signature Verification** | $M' \equiv S^{e} \pmod{n}$ | Compare $M' \stackrel{?}{=} H(M)$ | Non-Repudiation |
| **Certificate Signature Verify** | $V = H(\text{CertData})^{e_{CA}} \pmod{n_{CA}}$ | Should match decrypted signature | Trust validation |
| **Hash Function Property (Collision Resistance)** | $H(x) = H(y) \Rightarrow x = y$ (computationally infeasible) | SHA-256 outputs 256 bits | Certificate integrity |
| **Fingerprint of Certificate** | $\text{FP} = \text{SHA-256}(\text{DER-encoded Cert})$ | Hexadecimal string shown in browsers | Quick identity check |
| **OCSP Response Validity** | $\text{Response is fresh if } t_{now} - t_{producedThisUpdate} < 10 \text{ min}$ (typical) | OCSP stapling preferred | Real-time revocation |
| **CRL Validity Period** | Recommended: nextUpdate − thisUpdate $\le$ 7 days (typical) | Updated periodically | Periodic revocation |
| **Elliptic Curve Key Equivalence** | 256-bit ECC $\approx$ 3072-bit RSA security | Based on discrete log problem | Modern PKI trend |
| **Trust Chain Length Validation** | Chain depth $\le$ 6 (RFC 5280 constraint) | Root → Intermediate → Leaf | Path validation step |
| **Public Key Fingerprint (SSH style)** | $\text{FP} = \text{MD5 Base64}(\text{PubKey})$ | Used in OpenSSH `known_hosts` | Trust-on-first-use |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these formulas matter in PKI:** Every certificate in the world is mathematically backed by these primitives. When you examine an SSL/TLS handshake in Wireshark, you are witnessing **formula execution in real time**. For KTU, you do NOT need to derive all of these from scratch, but you **MUST** be able to demonstrate a complete **RSA sign-verify cycle** with small primes (e.g., $p=5, q=11$) on the exam paper to score full marks.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of PKI

| Application Domain | PKI Usage |
|---|---|
| **HTTPS / TLS** | Server certificates + Client certificates (mutual TLS) |
| **Email Security (S/MIME)** | Signing and encrypting emails |
| **Code Signing** | Software authenticity (Windows, Apple, Android APKs) |
| **Document Signing** | PDF/A digital signatures (legal admissibility) |
| **IoT Device Identity** | Device certificates (Matter, Thread protocols) |
| **VPN Authentication** | IPsec/IKEv2 with certificate-based mutual auth |
| **Smart Card / National ID** | e-Governance, ePassports, Aadhaar PKI |
| **Blockchain / DIDs** | Decentralized PKI variants, X.509 still dominant in Web2 |
| **5G / Telecom** | Subscriber identity (SIM), network element authentication |
| **Industrial Control (OT)** | SCADA certificate-based device authentication |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU students should remember: **Every time you see 🔒 in a browser address bar, PKI is working silently in the background.** That's the real-world impact of this topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section demonstrates the **complete mathematical machinery** behind a PKI transaction. We use a deliberately small example (with $p=5, q=11$) so that you can replicate it by hand on the exam. **Follow every line.**

---

## 3.1 Complete RSA Key Pair Generation (Used for a Certificate's Key Pair)

**Step 1: Choose two large distinct primes $p$ and $q$.**

$$p = 5, \quad q = 11$$

**Step 2: Compute the modulus $n$.**

$$n = p \cdot q = 5 \times 11 = 55$$

**Step 3: Compute Euler's Totient $\varphi(n)$.**

$$\varphi(n) = (p-1)(q-1) = (5-1)(11-1) = 4 \times 10 = 40$$

**Step 4: Choose the public exponent $e$ such that $1 < e < \varphi(n)$ and $\gcd(e, \varphi(n)) = 1$.**

We try $e = 3$:

$$\gcd(3, 40) = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

So $e = 3$ is valid.

**Step 5: Compute the private exponent $d$ such that $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\varphi(n)}$.**

We need to solve $3d \equiv 1 \pmod{40}$.

Using the **Extended Euclidean Algorithm**:

$$40 = 13 \cdot 3 + 1$$
$$3 = 3 \cdot 1 + 0$$

Back-substitute:

$$1 = 40 - 13 \cdot 3$$

Therefore:

$$-13 \cdot 3 \equiv 1 \pmod{40}$$
$$d \equiv -13 \equiv 40 - 13 = 27 \pmod{40}$$

**Verification:** $3 \times 27 = 81 = 2 \times 40 + 1 \equiv 1 \pmod{40}$ $\checkmark$

**Final Key Pair:**

$$\text{Public Key } (n, e) = (55, 3)$$
$$\text{Private Key } (n, d) = (55, 27)$$

---

## 3.2 Digital Signature Generation & Verification (One PKI Transaction)

Suppose Alice (the subscriber) wants to digitally sign a message $M = 9$.

**Step 1: Hash the message** (in real PKI, this is SHA-256; for our small example, we skip the hash and operate on $M$ directly).

**Step 2: Alice signs $M$ using her private key $d_{Alice}$.**

$$S \equiv M^{d_{Alice}} \pmod{n_{Alice}} = 9^{27} \pmod{55}$$

Computing $9^{27} \pmod{55}$ using repeated squaring:

$$9^1 = 9$$
$$9^2 = 81 \equiv 81 - 55 = 26 \pmod{55}$$
$$9^4 \equiv 26^2 = 676 \equiv 676 - 12 \times 55 = 676 - 660 = 16 \pmod{55}$$
$$9^8 \equiv 16^2 = 256 \equiv 256 - 4 \times 55 = 256 - 220 = 36 \pmod{55}$$
$$9^{16} \equiv 36^2 = 1296 \equiv 1296 - 23 \times 55 = 1296 - 1265 = 31 \pmod{55}$$

Now $27 = 16 + 8 + 2 + 1$:

$$9^{27} = 9^{16} \cdot 9^{8} \cdot 9^{2} \cdot 9^{1} = 31 \times 36 \times 26 \times 9 \pmod{55}$$

Compute stepwise:

$$31 \times 36 = 1116 \equiv 1116 - 20 \times 55 = 1116 - 1100 = 16 \pmod{55}$$
$$16 \times 26 = 416 \equiv 416 - 7 \times 55 = 416 - 385 = 31 \pmod{55}$$
$$31 \times 9 = 279 \equiv 279 - 5 \times 55 = 279 - 275 = 4 \pmod{55}$$

Therefore the **signature is $S = 4$**.

**Step 3: Bob (relying party) receives $(M, S) = (9, 4)$ and Alice's certificate containing $e_{Alice} = 3$ and $n_{Alice} = 55$.**

**Step 4: Bob verifies by computing:**

$$M' \equiv S^{e_{Alice}} \pmod{n_{Alice}} = 4^{3} \pmod{55} = 64 \pmod{55} = 9$$

**Step 5: Bob compares $M'$ with the hash of received $M$.**

$$M' = 9 \quad \text{and} \quad H(M) = 9 \implies M' = H(M) \quad \checkmark$$

**Signature is VALID.** Alice's certificate was issued by a trusted CA, so Bob trusts that the key pair belongs to Alice → **Non-repudiation achieved.**

---

## 3.3 Certificate Chain Verification (Analytical Matrix)

When a browser verifies an HTTPS certificate, it executes the following **path validation algorithm** (per **RFC 5280**):

| Validation Step | Mathematical/Logical Check | Failure Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| 1. **Parse certificate** | Decode ASN.1 DER, extract subject, issuer, validity, public key, extensions | Certificate malformed → REJECT |
| 2. **Validity period check** | `NotBefore < NOW < NotAfter` | Expired/Not-yet-valid → REJECT |
| 3. **Critical extension check** | If `critical=TRUE` and app doesn't understand, REJECT | Mismatch → REJECT |
| 4. **Issuer match** | Subject of issuer cert == Issuer of current cert | Chain broken → REJECT |
| 5. **Signature verification** | $S_{parent}^{e_{parent}} \pmod{n_{parent}} = H(\text{Cert}_{child})$ | Bad signature → REJECT |
| 6. **Revocation check** | Query CRL or OCSP for serial number | Revoked → REJECT |
| 7. **Path length constraint** | Intermediate count $\le$ `pathLenConstraint` | Too long → REJECT |
| 8. **Key usage check** | `keyUsage`/`extKeyUsage` permits intended use | E.g., CA used for SSL only → REJECT |
| 9. **Trust anchor** | Top of chain must be in local trust store | Untrusted root → REJECT |
| 10. **Final acceptance** | All 9 steps pass | Certificate TRUSTED → Proceed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "Explain the steps involved in certificate path validation," reproduce this 10-step matrix. Examiners award marks for *structured presentation*. The chain trust logic — Root CA signs Intermediate CA, Intermediate CA signs End-Entity Certificate — is the **single most-tested PKI concept** in KTU papers.

---

## 3.4 Certificate Revocation — CRL vs OCSP Comparison

| Attribute | Certificate Revocation List (CRL) | Online Certificate Status Protocol (OCSP) |
|---|---|---|
| **Mode** | Pull-based (offline list) | Push-based (real-time query) |
| **Bandwidth** | High — full list download | Low — single response |
| **Latency** | Latency = CRL refresh period | Latency ≈ network round-trip |
| **Privacy** | Good (no per-cert query) | Poor (CA knows which sites you visit) |
| **Failure Handling** | Browser may allow if CRL unreachable | Browser may "soft-fail" |
| **Standard** | RFC 5280, X.509 v2 CRL | RFC 6960 |
| **Improvement** | Delta CRLs, partitioned CRLs | OCSP Stapling, Must-Staple |
| **Use Case** | Enterprise/closed networks | Public HTTPS at scale |

> [!NOTE]
> **OCSP Stapling** is the modern best practice: the *web server itself* periodically fetches its OCSP response and "staples" it to the TLS handshake, eliminating client privacy concerns and improving speed.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PKI High-Level Architecture — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[End Entity Subscriber Alice] -->|CSR PKCS10| B[Registration Authority RA]
    B -->|Verified Identity| C[Certificate Authority CA]
    C -->|Signed X509 Cert| D[Certificate Repository LDAP or HTTP]
    C -->|CRL and OCSP| E[Revocation Service]
    D -->|Cert Download| F[Relying Party Bob Browser]
    E -->|Status Check| F
    F -->|Validate Chain| G[Trust Store Root CAs]
    C -->|Root Cert| G
    H[Hardware Security Module HSM] -.->|Protects Private Keys| C
    H -.->|Protects Private Keys| A
    I[Policy Authority CP CPS] -->|Governs| C
    I -->|Governs| B
    J[Key Recovery Service] -.->|Escrow Backup| A
```

**Component Roles:**
- **A (Alice):** Generates key pair, submits CSR, holds private key.
- **B (RA):** Verifies Alice's identity per the **Certificate Policy (CP)**.
- **C (CA):** Issues, signs, and publishes certificates.
- **D (Repository):** Public directory (LDAP) where certificates are stored.
- **E (Revocation):** CRL issuer / OCSP responder.
- **F (Bob):** Relying party; uses the certificate to verify Alice.
- **G (Trust Store):** Pre-installed root CA certificates in OS/browser.
- **H (HSM):** FIPS 140-2 Level 3 device that stores CA's signing key.
- **I (Policy):** Documents **Certificate Policy (CP)** and **Certification Practice Statement (CPS)**.
- **J (Key Recovery):** For enterprise PKI; allows key escrow (controversial!).

---

## 4.2 Certificate Chain of Trust — Hierarchical PKI Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R[Root CA Self Signed] -->|Signs| I1[Intermediate CA 1 Issuing]
    R -->|Signs| I2[Intermediate CA 2 Policy]
    I1 -->|Signs| S1[Server Cert www ktu edu in]
    I1 -->|Signs| S2[Server Cert mail ktu edu in]
    I2 -->|Signs| U1[User Cert Alice Faculty]
    I2 -->|Signs| U2[User Cert Bob Student]
    I2 -->|Signs| C1[Code Signing Cert KTU App]
    I1 -->|Cross Cert| X[Bridge CA for Interop with Other PKIs]
    X -.->|Connects| O[Other PKI Roots]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Intermediate CAs?** The **Root CA's private key is kept offline** in a high-security vault. The Root is used only a few times a year to sign Intermediate CA certificates. The Intermediate CAs (online) handle day-to-day certificate issuance. This **two-tier model** is standard practice because if an Intermediate CA is compromised, only its issued certificates need to be revoked — the Root remains safe.

---

## 4.3 TLS Handshake Using PKI — Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client Browser
    participant S as Web Server
    participant CA as CA Repository
    participant O as OCSP Responder

    C->>S: 1 ClientHello (Supported Ciphers, Random)
    S->>C: 2 ServerHello (Chosen Cipher)
    S->>C: 3 Server Certificate X509
    S->>C: 4 ServerKeyExchange if needed
    S->>C: 5 ServerHelloDone
    C->>O: 6 OCSP Query for Server Cert Status
    O->>C: 7 OCSP Response Good / Revoked
    C->>C: 8 Verify Chain up to Trusted Root
    C->>S: 9 ClientKeyExchange Pre Master Secret
    C->>S: 10 ChangeCipherSpec
    C->>S: 11 Finished Encrypted
    S->>C: 12 ChangeCipherSpec
    S->>C: 13 Finished Encrypted
    Note over C,S: 14 Application Data Exchange with Confidentiality and Integrity
```

**Key Insight:** PKI is invoked in **steps 3, 6, 7, and 8** — a tiny fraction of the handshake, yet without it the entire secure channel is meaningless.

---

## 4.4 Certificate Life-Cycle States

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Pending: CSR Submitted
    Pending --> Active: CA Issues Cert
    Active --> Active: In Use Within Validity
    Active --> Expired: NotAfter Reached
    Active --> Suspended: Temporary Hold Optional
    Suspended --> Active: Reinstated
    Suspended --> Revoked: Permanent Decision
    Active --> Revoked: CA Initiated Key Compromise etc
    Expired --> [*]
    Revoked --> [*]
    note right of Active: Active certificates are also subject to OCSP Good status
    note right of Revoked: Serial number appears in CRL with reason code
```

**Revocation Reason Codes (per RFC 5280):**
- `0` = unspecified
- `1` = keyCompromise
- `2` = cACompromise
- `3` = affiliationChanged
- `4` = superseded
- `5` = cessationOfOperation
- `6` = certificateHold
- `8` = removeFromCRL
- `9` = privilegeWithdrawn
- `10` = aACompromise

---

## 4.5 Certificate Trust Models — Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hierarchical Strict PKI] --> A1[Single Root CA Top Down]
    B[Cross Certified PKI] --> B1[Multiple Roots Peer to Peer]
    C[Bridge CA Model] --> C1[Hub and Spoke Federated]
    D[Web of Trust PGP] --> D1[Decentralized User Signed]
    E[Mesh PKI] --> E1[Multiple Cross Certs]
    F[DPKI Decentralized] --> F1[Blockchain Anchored]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **hierarchical model** (Section 4.2) is what governs the **public internet (HTTPS)**. The **bridge CA model** is used by government federations (e.g., US Federal PKI). The **web of trust** is the original model used by **Pretty Good Privacy (PGP)** for email — each user signs the keys of people they trust, building a decentralized mesh.

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A1 (3 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define Public Key Infrastructure (PKI). List its primary components.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Public Key Infrastructure (PKI)** is a comprehensive framework of policies, procedures, hardware, software, and cryptographic mechanisms used to create, manage, distribute, store, and revoke **digital certificates** and **public-private key pairs**. It binds public keys to verified identities of users, systems, or services through a chain of trust rooted at a **Certificate Authority (CA)**, thereby enabling secure communication over insecure networks.

**Primary Components of PKI:**

1. **Certificate Authority (CA)** — The trusted entity that issues and signs digital certificates.
2. **Registration Authority (RA)** — Verifies the identity of entities requesting certificates.
3. **Digital Certificates (X.509)** — Bind a public key to an identity.
4. **Certificate Repository (Directory)** — Stores and distributes certificates and CRLs (often LDAP-based).
5. **Certificate Management System** — Tools for issuance, renewal, suspension, and revocation.
6. **Key/Token Storage (HSMs)** — Securely stores private keys.
7. **Certificate Policy (CP) and Certification Practice Statement (CPS)** — Governance documents.

*(Word count target for KTU answer: ~120 words for full marks. Use bullet points — examiners prefer structured answers.)*

---

### **Question A2 (3 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between CRL and OCSP. State one advantage of OCSP over CRL.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Certificate Revocation List (CRL) | Online Certificate Status Protocol (OCSP) |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Periodically published list of revoked certificates | Real-time query-response protocol |
| **Bandwidth** | Higher (entire CRL downloaded) | Lower (single status response) |
| **Latency** | Up to next CRL update (hours/days) | Real-time (sub-second) |
| **Privacy** | Better (no per-cert querying) | Poorer (CA knows which sites user visits) |
| **Reliability** | May be cached; works offline | Requires OCSP responder availability |

**Advantage of OCSP over CRL:** OCSP provides **real-time, near-instantaneous revocation status** of a certificate, whereas CRLs are only updated periodically, leaving a window of vulnerability between updates. Additionally, OCSP responses are smaller in size, making them bandwidth-efficient for mobile clients. *(Modern mitigation: OCSP Stapling, where the server itself provides the OCSP response during the TLS handshake.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.** You must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B (full 14 marks from your chosen option).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: Explain the X.509 certificate format in detail. List the important v3 extensions used in modern PKI.**

**Model Answer:**

**X.509 Certificate Format (ITU-T Standard, current version = v3, defined in RFC 5280):**

The X.509 certificate is encoded using **ASN.1 (Abstract Syntax Notation One)** and serialized using **DER (Distinguished Encoding Rules)** for binary representation, or **PEM (Privacy-Enhanced Mail)** for Base64-encoded text representation. The structure of an X.509v3 certificate contains the following fields:

1. **Version:** Indicates X.509 version. For v3, the value is `2` (0-indexed: v1=0, v2=1, v3=2).

2. **Serial Number:** A positive integer assigned by the CA; uniquely identifies the certificate within the CA's domain.

3. **Signature Algorithm Identifier:** Specifies the algorithm used by the CA to sign the certificate (e.g., `sha256WithRSAEncryption`, `ecdsa-with-SHA256`).

4. **Issuer Distinguished Name (DN):** The X.500 name of the issuing CA, comprising fields like CN, O, OU, C, S, L.

5. **Validity Period:** Two timestamps — `Not Before` (certificate becomes valid) and `Not After` (certificate expires). Encoded in **UTCTime** or **GeneralizedTime** formats.

6. **Subject Distinguished Name:** The X.500 name of the entity (person, server, organization) holding the certificate.

7. **Subject Public Key Information:** Contains the algorithm OID (e.g., `1.2.840.113549.1.1.1` for RSA) and the public key bits themselves.

8. **Issuer Unique Identifier (v2/v3 only):** Optional bit string for re-using issuer names.

9. **Subject Unique Identifier (v2/v3 only):** Optional bit string for re-using subject names.

10. **Extensions (v3 only):** A sequence of extension fields providing additional functionality (see below).

11. **Signature Value:** The CA's digital signature computed over the **DER-encoded TBSCertificate** (the portion containing fields 1–10). Computed as $\text{Sig} = H(\text{TBS})^{d_{CA}} \pmod{n_{CA}}$.

**Critical X.509v3 Extensions:**

| Extension | OID | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Subject Alternative Name (SAN)** | `2.5.29.17` | Lists additional identities (DNS names, IPs, emails) — modern browsers REQUIRE this |
| **Basic Constraints** | `2.5.29.19` | Specifies if the cert is a CA and maximum path length |
| **Key Usage** | `2.5.29.15` | digitalSignature, keyEncipherment, keyCertSign, cRLSign, etc. |
| **Extended Key Usage (EKU)** | `2.5.29.37` | serverAuth, clientAuth, codeSigning, emailProtection |
| **CRL Distribution Points (CDP)** | `2.5.29.31` | URL(s) for downloading the CRL |
| **Authority Information Access (AIA)** | `1.3.6.1.5.5.7.1.1` | URL of issuer's certificate (for chain building) and OCSP responder |
| **Subject Key Identifier (SKI)** | `2.5.29.14` | Hash of the subject's public key — for efficient chain construction |
| **Authority Key Identifier (AKI)** | `2.5.29.35` | Hash of the issuing CA's public key — links child to parent |
| **Certificate Policies** | `2.5.29.32` | OID identifying the policy under which the cert was issued |
| **Name Constraints** | `2.5.29.30` | In CA certs; restricts the namespace of descendant certs |

**Concluding Statement:** The combination of these fields and extensions makes X.509v3 the de-facto standard for digital certificates worldwide, underpinning the security of HTTPS, S/MIME, IPsec, and countless enterprise applications.

*(Target length: 7-mark answer = ~400-500 words. Use the table — examiners love it.)*

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: Describe the hierarchical PKI trust model with a suitable diagram. Explain the role of Root CA and Intermediate CAs.**

**Model Answer:**

**Hierarchical PKI Trust Model:**

The hierarchical model is the most widely deployed PKI architecture, especially on the public internet. It organizes Certificate Authorities into a **tree-like structure** with a single **Root CA** at the top, one or more **Intermediate CAs** in the middle, and the **End-Entity (Subscriber) Certificates** at the leaves.

```
                  [Root CA - Offline, Self-Signed]
                          |
              +-----------+-----------+
              |                       |
   [Intermediate CA - Policy]   [Intermediate CA - Issuing]
              |                       |
        +-----+-----+           +-----+-----+
        |     |     |           |     |     |
      User  User  Code        Server Server
      Cert  Cert  Signing     Cert   Cert
                                  (www.example.com)
```

**Step-by-Step Description:**

1. **Root CA:** The **trust anchor** of the entire PKI. It is **self-signed** (its own Issuer = Subject) and its certificate is pre-installed in the trust stores of all major browsers and operating systems (Mozilla, Microsoft, Apple, Android). The Root CA's private key is **kept offline** in a high-security physical vault (e.g., Faraday cage, multi-person key ceremony) and used **only a handful of times per year** — typically to sign Intermediate CA certificates or to renew the Root's own self-signature.

2. **Intermediate CAs:** These are **subordinate to the Root** and are used for **day-to-day certificate issuance**. The chain is: `Root CA → signs → Intermediate CA → signs → End-Entity Certificate`. There are typically two types:
   - **Policy CA:** Issues policies for an organization or domain.
   - **Issuing CA:** Performs the actual certificate issuance for end entities.

3. **Why Two-Tier Architecture?**
   - **Security:** The Root's private key is rarely used, minimizing exposure. If an Intermediate is compromised, only its subtree is affected — the Root remains trusted and only that specific Intermediate needs revocation.
   - **Scalability:** Different Intermediates can be assigned to different domains, geographic regions, or use cases (e.g., one Intermediate for SSL, another for S/MIME).
   - **Delegation:** Organizational policy can delegate issuance responsibilities to multiple Intermediates without compromising Root security.

4. **Chain Validation by a Relying Party:** When a browser receives `www.example.com`'s certificate, it walks the chain upward:
   - Verify the **leaf cert's** signature using the public key in the **Intermediate CA's certificate**.
   - Verify the **Intermediate CA's** certificate signature using the **Root CA's** public key.
   - Confirm the **Root CA's** certificate is in the local trust store.
   - If all steps succeed, the leaf certificate is **trusted**.

5. **Advantages:** Clear authority chain, simple validation, easy to revoke a single Intermediate, well-understood by all PKI software.

6. **Disadvantages:** Single point of failure (if Root compromised, entire PKI collapses); scalability limited by hierarchy depth (max 6 levels per RFC); cross-organizational trust requires **cross-certification** or **bridge CAs**.

**Concluding Statement:** The hierarchical PKI model provides the **structured, scalable, and operationally manageable** trust framework that the modern internet relies upon. Without it, HTTPS as we know it would not function.

*(Use the diagram above in your answer — visual aids earn bonus marks in KTU.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Alternative]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: With a neat diagram, explain the Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) architecture and the role of each component.**

**Model Answer:**

**PKI Architecture — Block Diagram:**

```
                 +-----------------------------------+
                 |      POLICY AUTHORITY              |
                 |   (Certificate Policy / CPS)       |
                 +-----------------+-----------------+
                                   |
                                   v
   +-------------+        +-------------------+        +-------------------+
   |  END ENTITY | <----> | REGISTRATION      | <----> | CERTIFICATE       |
   |  (Alice)    |  CSR   | AUTHORITY (RA)    | Verify| AUTHORITY (CA)    |
   |  + Key Pair |        +-------------------+  ID   |                   |
   +-------------+                                  +---------+---------+
        |                                                    |
        |                                                    | Signed Cert
        |                                                    v
        |                                           +-------------------+
        |                                           | CERTIFICATE       |
        |                                           | REPOSITORY (LDAP) |
        |                                           +---------+---------+
        |                                                     |
        |                                          +----------+----------+
        |                                          |                     |
        |                                  +-------v------+      +-------v------+
        |                                  |     CRL      |      |    OCSP      |
        |                                  |   Publisher  |      |  Responder   |
        |                                  +--------------+      +--------------+
        |                                          |                     |
        +--------------+                          Status               Status
                       |                            |                     |
                       v                            +----------+----------+
                +-------------+                                 |
                |  RELYING    | <-------------------------------+
                |  PARTY (Bob)|
                +-------------+
```

**Role of Each Component:**

1. **Policy Authority:** Defines the **Certificate Policy (CP)** — the rules governing certificate issuance, naming, key lengths, lifetimes, and validation procedures. Also defines the **Certification Practice Statement (CPS)** — how the CA implements the policy operationally. Together, these are the "constitution and bylaws" of the PKI.

2. **End Entity (Alice / Subscriber):** The user, device, or server that needs a digital identity. Alice generates her own key pair (or uses a hardware token) and submits a **Certificate Signing Request (CSR)** in **PKCS#10** format to the RA.

3. **Registration Authority (RA):** The **front desk** of the PKI. Performs **identity verification** — checking domain ownership, organization details, or individual identity documents (depending on certificate class: DV, OV, EV). Once verified, the RA forwards the request to the CA. The RA **does not sign certificates**; it only validates.

4. **Certificate Authority (CA):** The **cryptographic heart** of the PKI. The CA:
   - Constructs the X.509 certificate from the CSR and validated information.
   - Computes the hash of the certificate data.
   - Signs the hash using its own private key.
   - Publishes the signed certificate to the repository.
   - Maintains the CRL or OCSP responder for revocation.

5. **Certificate Repository:** A publicly accessible **directory** (typically LDAP, sometimes HTTP-based) where issued certificates and CRLs are stored. It is the "phone book" of the PKI.

6. **CRL Publisher & OCSP Responder:** The two standard mechanisms for revocation status. CRLs are periodically published lists; OCSP responders provide real-time per-certificate status.

7. **Relying Party (Bob):** Any entity that uses another entity's certificate to verify signatures or establish secure channels. Bob performs **path validation** back to a trusted root.

8. **Hardware Security Module (HSM):** A **FIPS 140-2** certified physical device that stores the CA's private key in tamper-resistant hardware. HSMs prevent key extraction even by an attacker with full server access.

9. **Key Recovery / Escrow Service:** (Optional, mainly in enterprise PKI.) Allows recovery of lost private keys. **Controversial in mass-surveillance contexts** and not used in consumer PKI.

**Concluding Statement:** PKI is a distributed system of trust where each component plays a specialized role. The CA is the cryptographic anchor, the RA is the verifier of identity, the repository is the public ledger, and the revocation services keep the system current. Together, they create a verifiable chain of trust from any end-entity certificate back to a globally recognized root.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: Explain the certificate life-cycle in detail. What is the role of Certificate Revocation Lists (CRL)?**

**Model Answer:**

**Certificate Life-Cycle Stages:**

The certificate life-cycle comprises **five main stages** that every digital certificate passes through, managed by the PKI's Certificate Management System:

**Stage 1: Certificate Request (Enrollment)**

The end entity generates a public-private key pair (often using a CryptoAPI, OpenSSL, or hardware token like a YubiKey) and constructs a **Certificate Signing Request (CSR)** in **PKCS#10** format. The CSR contains:
- The entity's public key.
- The entity's Distinguished Name (DN).
- A self-signature proving possession of the corresponding private key.

The CSR is sent to the **Registration Authority (RA)** for identity verification. The RA performs vetting per the **Certificate Policy (CP)**:
- For **DV (Domain Validation):** Checks domain control (email, DNS TXT, HTTP challenge).
- For **OV (Organization Validation):** Verifies business registration.
- For **EV (Extended Validation):** Rigorous legal, operational, and physical checks.

**Stage 2: Certificate Issuance**

Upon successful verification, the CA constructs the X.509v3 certificate, signs it with its private key, and:
- Sends the certificate to the subscriber.
- Publishes the certificate to the **repository**.
- Adds the certificate's serial number to its active database.

The subscriber installs the certificate along with the private key (often in a `.pfx` / `.p12` PKCS#12 file containing both cert and private key, password-protected).

**Stage 3: Certificate Usage (Active State)**

During the validity period (`NotBefore` to `NotAfter`), the certificate is used in:
- TLS/SSL handshakes (HTTPS).
- Email signing and encryption (S/MIME).
- Code signing, document signing, VPN authentication, etc.

The relying party performs **path validation** (as detailed in Section 3.3) on every use.

**Stage 4: Certificate Renewal (Pre-Expiration)**

Certificates have a finite lifetime (typically **90 days** for Let's Encrypt, **1–2 years** for commercial CAs, **3–5 years** for enterprise internal CAs). Before expiration, the subscriber can:
- **Re-key:** Generate a new key pair and request a new certificate (preferred — fresh cryptographic material).
- **Renew:** Reuse the existing key pair but obtain a new certificate with extended validity.

Automated renewal protocols like **ACME (Automatic Certificate Management Environment)** — used by Let's Encrypt — handle this transparently.

**Stage 5: Certificate Revocation and Expiration**

When a certificate must be **terminated before its natural expiration**, the CA **revokes** it. Common reasons for revocation (using RFC 5280 reason codes):
- `keyCompromise` (1) — The private key has been leaked.
- `caCompromise` (2) — The issuing CA's key was compromised.
- `affiliationChanged` (3) — The subject's relationship with the organization has changed.
- `superseded` (4) — A new certificate replaces this one.
- `cessationOfOperation` (5) — The subject no longer uses the certificate.
- `privilegeWithdrawn` (9) — The subject's privileges have been revoked.

**Role of Certificate Revocation List (CRL):**

A **CRL** is a digitally signed, time-stamped list issued by a CA containing the serial numbers of all certificates that have been revoked **before their scheduled expiration date** and that have not yet "aged out" of the CRL. The CRL itself is a signed X.509 structure with the following fields:

| CRL Field | Description |
|---|---|
| **Version** | CRL version (v2 supports extensions) |
| **Signature Algorithm** | Algorithm used by CA to sign the CRL |
| **Issuer** | DN of the issuing CA |
| **thisUpdate** | Time CRL was generated |
| **nextUpdate** | Time by which next CRL will be published |
| **Revoked Certificates List** | Array of (Serial Number, Revocation Date, CRL Entry Extensions) |
| **CRL Extensions** | CRL Number, Authority Key Identifier, Issuing Distribution Point, Delta CRL Indicator |
| **Signature Value** | CA's digital signature over the CRL body |

**How CRL Works in Practice:**

1. A relying party (e.g., browser) receives a certificate.
2. Before trusting it, the browser fetches the CRL from the **CRL Distribution Point (CDP)** URL embedded in the certificate's extensions.
3. The browser searches the CRL for the certificate's serial number.
4. If found → certificate is **revoked → REJECT the connection**.
5. If not found → certificate is presumed valid (in conjunction with OCSP for real-time checks).

**Variants of CRL:**
- **Complete CRL:** Lists all revoked certificates (large in size for busy CAs).
- **Delta CRL:** Lists only changes since the last complete CRL (bandwidth-efficient).
- **Indirect CRL:** Issued by a centralized authority for multiple CAs.
- **Partitioned/Redirected CRL:** Split into multiple smaller CRLs to improve efficiency.

**Concluding Statement:** The certificate life-cycle is a continuous, policy-driven process. CRLs play a critical role in maintaining the **integrity** of the PKI by ensuring that compromised or misused certificates are promptly flagged as untrustworthy, even before their natural expiration. Modern systems increasingly prefer **OCSP** (especially with **OCSP Stapling**) for real-time, low-latency revocation status, but CRLs remain a fundamental fallback mechanism required by the X.509 standard.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing PKI with Digital Signatures:** PKI is the *infrastructure*; digital signatures are one *application* built on it. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if you conflate them.
> 2. **Forgetting the 'I' in PKI:** Students often describe only the cryptographic primitives (RSA, hashing) and forget the *policy* and *procedural* aspects (CP, CPS, RA, repository, HSM). Always include non-technical components.
> 3. **Missing 'Intermediate CAs':** When drawing a PKI hierarchy, many students draw only Root → End-Entity, skipping the Intermediate layer. The two-tier architecture is what the examiner is testing.
> 4. **No Diagram:** Part B questions without a diagram or table usually score 1–2 marks less than comparable answers that include them. Always include a labeled block diagram.
> 5. **Confusing CRL with OCSP:** Many students mix up which is "real-time" and which is "periodic." Memorize the table from Section 3.4.
> 6. **Skipping Revocation Reason Codes:** When asked about CRL, mention at least 3 reason codes (keyCompromise, cACompromise, superseded) — this shows depth.
> 7. **X.509 version confusion:** Version field value `2` means X.509v3 (zero-indexed). Writing "X.509 version 3" is correct; writing "version field = 3" is wrong.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **PKI** = Framework of policies, procedures, hardware, software, and cryptography to manage digital certificates and public keys.
- [ ] **Core Components:** CA, RA, Repository, CRL/OCSP, End-Entities, HSM, CP/CPS.
- [ ] **X.509v3** is the standard certificate format (RFC 5280), encoded in ASN.1 DER or PEM (Base64).
- [ ] **Certificate Fields:** Version, Serial, Signature Algo, Issuer, Validity, Subject, Public Key, Extensions, Signature.
- [ ] **Critical v3 Extensions:** SAN, Basic Constraints, Key Usage, EKU, CDP, AIA, SKI, AKI, Certificate Policies.
- [ ] **Trust Models:** Hierarchical (Root → Intermediate → End-Entity) is dominant; Web of Trust (PGP) is decentralized; Bridge CA is federated.
- [ ] **Root CA:** Self-signed, offline, in trust store; signs Intermediate CAs.
- [ ] **Intermediate CA:** Online, signs end-entity certificates; if compromised, only its subtree is affected.
- [ ] **Path Validation Steps:** Parse → Validity Period → Critical Extensions → Issuer Match → Signature Verify → Revocation Check → Trust Anchor.
- [ ] **RSA Key Generation:** Choose $p, q$ → $n = p \cdot q$ → $\varphi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ → Choose $e$ with $\gcd(e, \varphi(n))=1$ → Compute $d$ with $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\varphi(n)}$.
- [ ] **Digital Signature:** Sign with private key ($S = M^d \mod n$), verify with public key ($M' = S^e \mod n$), compare $M' = H(M)$.
- [ ] **Encryption:** Encrypt with public key ($C = M^e \mod n$), decrypt with private key ($M = C^d \mod n$).
- [ ] **Certificate Revocation Methods:** CRL (periodic, list-based) vs OCSP (real-time, query-based). OCSP Stapling mitigates privacy.
- [ ] **Revocation Reason Codes:** keyCompromise (1), cACompromise (2), affiliationChanged (3), superseded (4), cessationOfOperation (5), certificateHold (6).
- [ ] **CRL Structure Fields:** Version, Issuer, thisUpdate, nextUpdate, Revoked Certificates, Extensions, Signature.
- [ ] **HSM (Hardware Security Module):** FIPS 140-2 device for storing CA private keys securely.
- [ ] **Key Lengths (KTU 2024 standard):** RSA $\geq$ 2048 bits (recommended 3072+), ECC $\geq$ 256 bits.
- [ ] **Path Length Constraint:** Max chain depth is 6 (Root at level 0).
- [ ] **Key Re-use:** Always prefer *re-keying* (new key pair) over *renewal* (same key pair) for security hygiene.
- [ ] **Real-World PKI Users:** Let's Encrypt (free DV), DigiCert, Sectigo, Entrust, GlobalSign, IdenTrust (US Federal).
- [ ] **Modern Alternatives:** DPKI (Decentralized PKI) using blockchain; TOFU (Trust On First Use) for SSH keys; Certificate Transparency (CT) logs to detect mis-issuance.
- [ ] **Sanity Rule:** When in doubt on the exam — draw a hierarchical diagram. It always scores.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy for PKI Questions:**
> 1. Always start with a **definition** (1 mark for terminological accuracy).
> 2. Always include at least **one diagram** (block diagram, hierarchy, or flow) — easy 1–2 marks.
> 3. Use **bullet points and tables** — examiners read ~120 answer sheets per hour; structure wins.
> 4. For mathematical questions (RSA), show **every modular arithmetic step** — no shortcuts.
> 5. Conclude with a one-line "Thus, PKI…" statement to anchor the answer's relevance.

---

> **End of Module 4 — Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) Notes**

topics.title: Protocols Specially Designed for E-Commerce and Web Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT418 - SECURITY ASSESSMENT AND RISK ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Protocols Specially Designed for E-Commerce and Web Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**E-Commerce and Web Application Security Protocols** are a specialized class of communication protocols engineered to provide **confidentiality, integrity, authentication, non-repudiation, and authorization** for transactions and data exchanges conducted over open, public networks such as the Internet. Unlike generic network protocols, these protocols are tailored to address the unique security challenges of electronic commerce, including cardholder data protection, merchant authentication, payment authorization, and cross-domain federated identity management.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus (Module 4):** This topic demands explicit coverage of *SSL/TLS*, *SET (Secure Electronic Transaction)*, *3-D Secure*, *HTTPS*, *S-HTTP*, *SAML*, *OAuth 2.0*, and *WS-Security*. Students are expected to compare, contrast, and trace the message flows of these protocols in the context of e-commerce and web applications.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Verified Courier with Sealed Briefcase"

Imagine a customer (Alice) wants to send a sealed briefcase containing her credit card details to a merchant (Bob), who must then hand it over to a bank for payment clearance. A plain courier can be intercepted, the seal broken, and the contents swapped or read.

A **security protocol** is the equivalent of:

- **Sealed, tamper-evident briefcase** → **Encryption + Integrity (MAC/HMAC)**
- **Courier's signed receipt** → **Digital Signature (Non-Repudiation)**
- **Verified identity badge of courier and recipient** → **Digital Certificates (PKI / X.509)**
- **An escrow bank that validates the contents before delivery** → **Payment Gateway / SET Acquirer**
- **A pre-arranged handshake and step-by-step verification procedure** → **Handshake Protocol (e.g., TLS)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Security Services Every E-Commerce Protocol Must Provide:**
> 1. **Confidentiality** – Hide transaction details from eavesdroppers (encryption).
> 2. **Integrity** – Detect any modification of messages in transit (hash + MAC).
> 3. **Authentication** – Verify the identities of all parties (certificates, tokens).
> 4. **Authorization** – Confirm that an authenticated entity has permission to perform an action.
> 5. **Non-Repudiation** – Prevent any party from denying that a transaction took place (digital signatures).

### 1.3 Classification of E-Commerce Security Protocols

| Layer / Domain | Representative Protocols | Primary Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Transport Layer** | **SSL 3.0 / TLS 1.2 / TLS 1.3** | Encrypt the HTTP channel (HTTPS) |
| **Application Layer (HTTP)** | **S-HTTP**, **HTTPS (HTTP+TLS)** | Secure individual web messages |
| **Payment-Specific** | **SET (Secure Electronic Transaction)**, **3-D Secure (3DS 2.0)** | Protect card-not-present (CNP) transactions |
| **Federated Identity / SSO** | **SAML 2.0**, **OpenID Connect**, **OAuth 2.0** | Cross-domain authentication and delegated authorization |
| **Web Services** | **WS-Security**, **XML Signature**, **XML Encryption** | SOAP/XML message-level security |
| **Token-Based** | **JWT (JSON Web Token)**, **PASETO** | Compact, signed API tokens |
| **API Authorization** | **OAuth 2.0 / OIDC**, **API Keys + mTLS** | Modern REST/GraphQL security |
| **Connection-Oriented** | **SSH (Secure Shell)** | Secure remote administration & tunneling |

### 1.4 Real-World Context

The **PCI-DSS (Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard)** compliance framework, which is mandatory for any entity that stores, processes, or transmits cardholder data, explicitly mandates the use of protocols such as **TLS 1.2+** and **3-D Secure** to protect cardholder data in transit. **End-to-End Encryption (E2EE)** at the application layer and **tokenization** complement these protocols.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 SSL / TLS — The Backbone of HTTPS

**Secure Sockets Layer (SSL)** was developed by Netscape in **1994** (SSL 2.0; SSL 3.0 released in 1996). The IETF subsequently took over the standardization effort and renamed it **Transport Layer Security (TLS)** with TLS 1.0 (1999), TLS 1.1 (2006), TLS 1.2 (2008), and **TLS 1.3 (RFC 8446, 2018)**. SSL is now considered deprecated due to known vulnerabilities (POODLE, BEAST); only **TLS 1.2 and TLS 1.3** are considered secure.

#### 2.1.1 TLS Architecture

TLS is composed of four sub-protocols:

1. **TLS Handshake Protocol** – Negotiates algorithms, authenticates peers, and establishes shared keys.
2. **TLS Change Cipher Spec Protocol** – A single-byte signal that subsequent records will be protected under the newly negotiated parameters.
3. **TLS Alert Protocol** – Conveys errors, warnings, and connection closures.
4. **TLS Record Protocol** – The lowest layer; it fragments, compresses (optional), applies a **MAC (Message Authentication Code)**, encrypts, and transmits data.

#### 2.1.2 TLS Handshake — Step-by-Step Logic (TLS 1.2)

1. **Client → Server:** `ClientHello` — supported cipher suites, compression methods, random nonce, and optional extensions (SNI, ALPN).
2. **Server → Client:** `ServerHello` — chosen cipher suite, server random nonce, session ID.
3. **Server → Client:** `Certificate` — server's X.509 certificate chain.
4. **Server → Client:** `ServerKeyExchange` — parameters for key agreement (e.g., Diffie-Hellman public value) if required.
5. **Server → Client:** `ServerHelloDone` — signals the server is finished.
6. **Client → Server:** `ClientKeyExchange` — pre-master secret (RSA encrypted to server's public key) or DH public value.
7. **Client → Server:** `ChangeCipherSpec` + `Finished` (encrypted, MAC'd, verifies the handshake).
8. **Server → Client:** `ChangeCipherSpec` + `Finished` — server confirms key agreement.
9. **Application Data** flows under the negotiated **bulk symmetric cipher** (e.g., AES-256-GCM).

#### 2.1.3 TLS 1.3 Improvement

- Handshake reduced to **1 round-trip (1-RTT)** typically, and **0-RTT** for resumed sessions.
- Removed weak algorithms (RC4, MD5, SHA-1, 3DES, static RSA key exchange).
- All key exchange is now based on **Diffie-Hellman (DHE/ECDHE)**, providing **forward secrecy**.

### 2.2 SET — Secure Electronic Transaction

**SET** was jointly developed by **Visa and Mastercard in 1996 (released 1997)** as an open specification for securing credit card transactions over open networks. Although largely superseded by TLS + 3-D Secure in practice, SET remains a **textbook KTU favorite** because it uniquely protects cardholder data from the merchant.

#### 2.2.1 SET Participants

| Role | Description |
|---|---|
| **Cardholder (C)** | The purchaser; owns the payment card. |
| **Merchant (M)** | Sells goods/services; receives the order. |
| **Issuer (I)** | The cardholder's bank; issued the card. |
| **Acquirer (A)** | The merchant's bank; processes the payment. |
| **Payment Gateway (PG)** | The acquirer's online system that connects to the payment network. |
| **Certification Authority (CA)** | Issues X.509 certificates binding identities to public keys. |

#### 2.2.2 SET Cryptographic Mechanisms

- **Dual Signatures (DS):** A cryptographic construct that proves two messages (Order Information OI and Payment Information PI) are linked, **without revealing OI to the payment gateway or PI to the merchant**.
- **Dual Signature Formula:**

  $$ DS = E_{PR_c}\Big[ H( H(PI) \;\Vert\; H(OI) ) \Big] $$

  where $PR_c$ is the cardholder's private signature key, $E$ is RSA signing, and $H$ is a one-way hash (SHA-1 in original SET).

- **The Payment Gateway verifies:**

  $$ D_{PU_c}\big[DS\big] = H\big( H(PI) \;\Vert\; H(OI) \big) $$

  by recomputing $H(PI)$ from PI, requesting $H(OI)$ from the merchant, and confirming the equality.

#### 2.2.3 SET Transaction Phases

1. **Cardholder Registration** — Obtain a digital certificate from the CA.
2. **Merchant Registration** — Obtain a digital certificate from the CA.
3. **Purchase Request** — Cardholder sends OI, PI, and Dual Signature to merchant.
4. **Payment Authorization** — Merchant forwards PI + DS + (OI hash) to payment gateway; gateway authorizes with issuer.
5. **Payment Capture** — After shipment, merchant requests funds transfer.

### 2.3 3-D Secure (3DS) — Verified by Visa, Mastercard SecureCode, RuPay PaySecure

**3-D Secure** was developed by **Visa (1999)** and later adopted by Mastercard, JCB, American Express, and RuPay. The current version is **EMV 3-D Secure (3DS 2.0 / 2.1 / 2.2)**, designed for both web and mobile (in-app) flows.

#### 2.3.1 Three Domains

- **Acquirer Domain (ACS — Access Control Server)** — The issuer's risk and authentication engine.
- **Issuer Domain (3DS Server / Access Control Server)** — Authenticates the cardholder.
- **Interoperability Domain (3DS MPI / Directory Server)** — The card brand's central routing infrastructure (Visa's VTS, Mastercard's DS, RuPay's RS).

#### 2.3.2 3DS 2.0 Flow (Frictionless + Challenge)

1. Customer initiates a transaction on the merchant site.
2. Merchant sends a **PAREQ (Payment Authentication Request)** to the **3DS Server / MPI** with enriched risk data.
3. MPI routes the request to the **Directory Server** → **ACS** of the issuer.
4. The ACS performs **Risk-Based Authentication (RBA)**:
   - If low risk → **Frictionless Flow** → Authentication Value (AV) returned immediately.
   - If high risk → **Challenge Flow** → Cardholder is prompted (OTP, biometric, knowledge-based).
5. ACS returns a **PARes (Payment Authentication Response)** with the AV (ECI, CAVV, ARes).
6. Merchant's acquirer attaches the AV to the authorization message (ISO 8583).
7. Issuer validates the AV and approves the transaction. The merchant receives a **liability shift** for any chargeback.

### 2.4 S-HTTP (Secure HTTP)

**S-HTTP (RFC 2660)** was proposed by Enterprise Integration Technologies in **1994** as an application-layer alternative to SSL. It secures individual HTTP messages using **CMS (Cryptographic Message Syntax)** and supports symmetric (DES, RC4) and asymmetric (RSA) encryption plus digital signatures. S-HTTP is **rarely deployed today**, having been eclipsed by **HTTPS (HTTP over TLS)**.

### 2.5 SAML 2.0

**Security Assertion Markup Language (SAML) 2.0 (OASIS, 2005)** is an XML-based framework for **federated identity and SSO** across web applications. The three central roles are:

- **Principal (User)** — The subject of the assertion.
- **Identity Provider (IdP)** — Authenticates the user and issues assertions.
- **Service Provider (SP)** — The application that consumes the assertion.

The core assertion types are `<Authentication>`, `<Attribute>`, and `<AuthorizationDecision>` statements, signed with XML Signature.

### 2.6 OAuth 2.0 and OpenID Connect (OIDC)

**OAuth 2.0 (RFC 6749, 2012)** is **not an authentication protocol** — it is a **delegated authorization framework**. It allows a user to grant a third-party client limited access to resources on a resource server, **without sharing credentials**.

- **Roles:** Resource Owner, Client, Authorization Server, Resource Server.
- **Grant Types:** Authorization Code, Implicit (deprecated), Client Credentials, Resource Owner Password Credentials (deprecated), PKCE-augmented Auth Code (RFC 7636).
- **Token:** Bearer Access Token (often a JWT) and an optional Refresh Token.

**OpenID Connect (OIDC)** is an **identity layer on top of OAuth 2.0**. It adds an **ID Token (JWT)**, a `/userinfo` endpoint, and a standardized `scope=openid`, making it an **authentication protocol**.

### 2.7 WS-Security and XML Security

**WS-Security (OASIS WSS 1.1.1, 2006)** is a SOAP extension specifying how to apply **XML Signature**, **XML Encryption**, and **SAML / Username / X.509 / Kerberos tokens** at the message level. It is critical for **SOAP-based web services** in B2B integrations.

### 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Concept | Expression | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Symmetric Encryption** | $C = E_K(P)$ | $K$ shared; AES-128/256 |
| **Asymmetric Encryption** | $C = E_{PU_B}(P)$ | Receiver's public key |
| **Digital Signature** | $S = E_{PR_A}\big[ H(M) \big]$ | Sender's private key |
| **Verification** | $H(M) = D_{PU_A}\big[ S \big]$ | Sender's public key |
| **Digital Envelope** | $C = E_K(P); \text{ and } E_{PU_B}(K)$ | Hybrid encryption |
| **HMAC** | $HMAC_K(M) = H\big( (K \oplus opad) \;\Vert\; H((K \oplus ipad) \Vert M) \big)$ | Message integrity with shared key |
| **SET Dual Signature** | $DS = E_{PR_c}\big[ H( H(PI) \;\Vert H(OI) ) \big]$ | Links OI and PI |
| **DH Shared Secret** | $K_{AB} = (g^b \mod p)^a \mod p = (g^a \mod p)^b \mod p$ | Forward secrecy |
| **RSA Key Generation** | $n = p \cdot q; \phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1); e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$ | $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$ |
| **JWT Structure** | $Base64URL(H) \cdot Base64URL(P) \cdot Base64URL(Sig)$ | $H$ = header, $P$ = payload |
| **HMAC-SHA256 Signature** | $Sig = HMAC\_SHA256(secret, H \Vert P)$ | HS256 algorithm |
| **ECDSA Signature** | $(r, s)$ with $r = (kG)_x \mod n$ | Elliptic curve variant |
| **Hash Properties** | Pre-image resistant, collision resistant | SHA-256, SHA-3 |
| **Forward Secrecy** | Compromised long-term key $\not\Rightarrow$ past session keys | Mandatory in TLS 1.3 |
| **Nonce Length** | $\geq 128$ bits | TLS 1.3 random |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These protocols protect **online banking, e-commerce checkouts, healthcare portals (HIPAA), and government e-services**. Compliance with **PCI-DSS, GDPR, HIPAA, and the RBI Card Storage Guidelines** is heavily dependent on correct deployment of TLS 1.2+, 3-D Secure, and tokenization.
